topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT413 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Principle of Automobile Engines — Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **automobile** is a self-propelled vehicle designed for road transport, powered by an **Internal Combustion (IC) Engine** (or, in modern contexts, an electric motor). The "Principle" of an automobile engine refers to the thermodynamic and mechanical sequence by which the chemical energy of a fuel–air mixture is converted into useful mechanical work through controlled combustion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Principle):**
> The student must be able to explain the **working principle of 4-stroke and 2-stroke Spark Ignition (SI) and Compression Ignition (CI) engines**, identify the basic engine terminology, and describe each stroke/event of the operating cycle with reference to the **Otto** and **Diesel** thermodynamic cycles.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English View)

Imagine a **balloon attached to a small toy cart**. If you inflate the balloon and release it, air rushes out and pushes the cart forward. This is the most basic form of "engine" — pressurised gas converts chemical/stored energy into motion.

Now upgrade this idea:
- The balloon becomes the **engine cylinder**.
- Air escaping becomes the **expanding combustion gases**.
- The cart motion becomes the **rotating crankshaft**.

An IC engine simply repeats this "push" thousands of times per minute using a precisely timed sequence of **suck → squeeze → bang → blow**, called the **four-stroke cycle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** An IC engine is a controlled, cyclic "explosion engine" where fuel burns *inside* the cylinder (hence *internal combustion*) and pushes a piston, which in turn rotates the wheels via a transmission system.

### 1.3 Standard Engine Terminology (Mandatory for KTU)

| Term | Symbol / Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Bore** | $D$ (mm) | Inner diameter of the cylinder |
| **Stroke** | $L$ (mm) | Distance travelled by piston between TDC and BDC |
| **Top Dead Centre** | TDC | Piston at its highest (closest to cylinder head) |
| **Bottom Dead Centre** | BDC | Piston at its lowest (farthest from cylinder head) |
| **Clearance Volume** | $V_c$ | Volume above piston at TDC |
| **Swept Volume** | $V_s$ | Volume swept between TDC and BDC |
| **Compression Ratio** | $r$ | Ratio of total to clearance volume |
| **Indicated Power** | $IP$ | Power developed inside cylinder |
| **Brake Power** | $BP$ | Power available at crankshaft |
| **Friction Power** | $FP$ | Power lost to friction ($FP = IP - BP$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard atmospheric & thermodynamic constants** to memorise:
> - **Atmospheric Pressure** = $\mathbf{1.01325 \text{ bar} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}}$
> - **Gas constant for air** = $\mathbf{R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}}$
> - **Specific heat ratio (air)** = $\mathbf{\gamma = 1.4}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis — Working Principles of IC Engines

## 2.1 Classification (Foundational Logic)

IC engines are broadly classified based on:

1. **Fuel / Ignition Method**
   - *Spark Ignition (SI)* — petrol/gasoline engine; uses a **spark plug**.
   - *Compression Ignition (CI)* — diesel engine; fuel self-ignites due to high temperature.
2. **Cycle / Stroke**
   - *4-stroke* — one working stroke per two crankshaft revolutions.
   - *2-stroke* — one working stroke per single crankshaft revolution.
3. **Thermodynamic Cycle**
   - *Otto cycle* (ideal SI) and *Diesel cycle* (ideal CI).

## 2.2 Working Principle — 4-Stroke SI Engine (Petrol)

The sequence spans **two full revolutions (720°)** of the crankshaft and consists of four distinct strokes:

### Step 1 — **Intake / Suction Stroke** (Crank 0° → 180°)
- Inlet valve **opens**, exhaust valve closed.
- Piston moves from **TDC → BDC**.
- Fresh air–fuel mixture drawn in due to partial vacuum.
- Pressure inside cylinder ≈ **0.8–0.9 bar** (below atmospheric).

### Step 2 — **Compression Stroke** (180° → 360°)
- Both valves **closed**.
- Piston moves from **BDC → TDC**.
- Mixture compressed to a high pressure (~**10–14 bar** in petrol engines).
- Compression ratio typically: $r = 8:1 \text{ to } 12:1$.

### Step 3 — **Power / Expansion Stroke** (360° → 540°)
- Spark plug fires near TDC.
- Combustion raises pressure sharply to **30–50 bar**.
- Piston driven from **TDC → BDC** by expanding gases — this is the *useful work* stroke.

### Step 4 — **Exhaust Stroke** (540° → 720°)
- Exhaust valve **opens**, piston moves **BDC → TDC**.
- Burnt gases expelled; cylinder prepared for next cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 4-stroke?** Only **one power stroke per 4 strokes**. Hence a **flywheel** is mandatory to maintain rotation during the 3 idle strokes.

## 2.3 Working Principle — 4-Stroke CI Engine (Diesel)

- **No spark plug**, fuel injected directly into hot compressed air.
- Compression ratio is **much higher**: $r = 14:1 \text{ to } 22:1$.
- Compression temperature exceeds **auto-ignition temperature of diesel (~250 °C)**.
- Sequence: **Suction (air only) → Compression → Power (fuel injection + combustion) → Exhaust**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In CI engines, *only air* is compressed (no fuel during compression stroke), preventing premature detonation. The fuel is injected near TDC of compression stroke.

## 2.4 Working Principle — 2-Stroke Engine

Completes a cycle in **one revolution (360°)** using ports instead of valves:

- **Upward stroke:** Compression of charge in crankcase + expansion of burnt gases in cylinder.
- **Downward stroke:** Power stroke; simultaneously, fresh charge in crankcase is transferred to cylinder via transfer port, displacing exhaust gases.

> [!NOTE]
> In a 2-stroke, **crankcase compression** replaces the dedicated intake pump, making the engine lightweight but less efficient in scavenging.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L$ | Swept volume | m³ |
| 2 | $r = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ | Compression ratio | — |
| 3 | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Otto cycle thermal efficiency | — |
| 4 | $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \left[ \dfrac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(r_c - 1)} \right]$ | Diesel cycle efficiency | — |
| 5 | $W_{net} = P_{mean} \times V_s \times \dfrac{N}{2}$ | Work per cycle (4-stroke) | J |
| 6 | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000}$ | Brake Power from torque | kW |
| 7 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$ | Mechanical efficiency | — |
| 8 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$ | Brake thermal efficiency | — |

> Where:
> - $D$ = bore, $L$ = stroke, $N$ = rpm, $T$ = torque (N·m)
> - $r_c$ = cut-off ratio, $r$ = compression ratio
> - $P_{mean}$ = mean effective pressure (bar or kPa)
> - $\dot{m}_f$ = fuel mass flow rate (kg/s), $CV$ = calorific value (kJ/kg)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The Otto-cycle formula is the theoretical ceiling for SI engine efficiency. Modern petrol engines achieve ~30–35 % actual efficiency vs. the theoretical ~55–60 % — the gap is closed by **hybrid systems, turbocharging, and Atkinson/Miller cycles**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Working

## 3.1 Derivation: Swept Volume and Compression Ratio

The geometric relationships among bore, stroke, and compression ratio are foundational to every engine numerical problem.

### Step 1 — Define Cylinder Volume at TDC

The clearance volume $V_c$ is the volume of the combustion chamber when the piston is at TDC. For a multi-cylinder engine, $V_c$ per cylinder is fixed by the geometry of the cylinder head and piston crown.

### Step 2 — Express Swept Volume

The volume swept by one piston from TDC to BDC is a cylinder of diameter $D$ and length $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \text{Area} \times \text{stroke length} \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} \cdot L \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Express Total Volume at BDC

When the piston is at BDC, the total cylinder volume is the clearance volume plus the swept volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= V_c + V_s \\
&= V_c + \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compression Ratio Definition

By definition, $r$ is the ratio of the *maximum* (BDC) to the *minimum* (TDC) volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{V_1}{V_c} = \frac{V_c + V_s}{V_c} \\
&= 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Clearance Volume

Rearranging the above:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r - 1 &= \frac{V_s}{V_c} \\
V_c &= \frac{V_s}{r - 1} \\
V_c &= \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L}{r - 1} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A higher compression ratio demands a *smaller clearance volume*, which is why high-performance engines use domed pistons or compact combustion chambers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency

The Otto cycle is the idealised thermodynamic model of the SI engine and consists of four processes:
- **1→2:** Isentropic compression
- **2→3:** Constant-volume heat addition
- **3→4:** Isentropic expansion
- **4→1:** Constant-volume heat rejection

### Step 1 — Apply Isentropic Relations

For process 1→2 (isentropic compression of an ideal gas):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_2}{T_1} &= \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1} \\
\Rightarrow T_2 &= T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Heat Added (Process 2→3, constant volume)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{in} &= m \, C_v \, (T_3 - T_2) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Heat Rejected (Process 4→1, constant volume)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{out} &= m \, C_v \, (T_4 - T_1) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Thermal Efficiency Definition

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{otto} &= \frac{Q_{in} - Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Use Isentropic Expansion (Process 3→4)

By symmetry of the Otto cycle, $V_4 = V_1$ and $V_3 = V_2$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_4}{T_3} &= \left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \\
\Rightarrow T_4 &= \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute Back

Substituting into the efficiency expression and simplifying (using $T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$ and $T_4 = T_3 / r^{\gamma-1}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{otto} &= 1 - \frac{T_3 / r^{\gamma - 1} - T_1}{T_3 - T_1 r^{\gamma - 1}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final KTU Result:**

$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Illustration

For $r = 10$ and $\gamma = 1.4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma - 1 &= 0.4 \\
r^{0.4} &= 10^{0.4} \approx 2.512 \\
\eta_{otto} &= 1 - \frac{1}{2.512} \\
&\approx 1 - 0.398 \\
&\approx 0.602 \text{ or } 60.2\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** A real petrol engine achieves only ~30 % because of (i) finite combustion duration, (ii) heat losses, (iii) mechanical friction, and (iv) incomplete combustion.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical: Brake Power from Engine Data

**Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has bore $D = 80 \text{ mm}$, stroke $L = 90 \text{ mm}$, and runs at $N = 3000 \text{ rpm}$. Mean effective pressure = **8 bar**. Calculate the brake power, assuming mechanical efficiency $\eta_{mech} = 80\%$.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Swept volume per cylinder:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^{2} \times 0.090 \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 \times 0.090 \\
&= 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Indicated Power (4-stroke, so power strokes per second = N/120):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= \frac{P_{mean} \times V_s \times N \times n_{cyl}}{2 \times 60} \\
&= \frac{8 \times 10^{5} \times 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \times 3000 \times 4}{2 \times 60} \\
&= \frac{8 \times 10^{5} \times 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \times 12000}{120} \\
&= 8 \times 10^{5} \times 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \times 100 \\
&\approx 36{,}192 \text{ W} \\
&\approx 36.19 \text{ kW} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Brake Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP &= IP \times \eta_{mech} \\
&= 36.19 \times 0.80 \\
&\approx 28.95 \text{ kW} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $BP \approx 28.95 \text{ kW} \ (\approx 38.8 \text{ hp})$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — 4-Stroke SI Engine Operation

The following Mermaid diagram maps the sequential events of a 4-stroke petrol engine, including valve logic and crank position.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crank Position 0 deg TDC] --> B{Intake Valve Open?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Stroke 1: Intake — Piston TDC to BDC]
    C --> D[Crank at 180 deg BDC]
    D --> E[Stroke 2: Compression — Both Valves Closed]
    E --> F[Crank at 360 deg TDC]
    F --> G[Spark Plug Fires]
    G --> H[Stroke 3: Power — Combustion Push Piston to BDC]
    H --> I[Crank at 540 deg BDC]
    I --> J{Exhaust Valve Open?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Stroke 4: Exhaust — Piston BDC to TDC]
    K --> L[Crank at 720 deg TDC — Cycle Complete]
    L --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each block represents a half-revolution; the loop returns to TDC, completing **one cycle in 720° = 2 revolutions**.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — SI vs CI Engine Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SI["4-Stroke SI Petrol Engine"]
        S1[Intake: Air Fuel Mixture] --> S2[Compression: Ratio 8 to 12]
        S2 --> S3[Spark Plug Ignition]
        S3 --> S4[Power Stroke]
        S4 --> S5[Exhaust]
    end
    subgraph CI["4-Stroke CI Diesel Engine"]
        C1[Intake: Air Only] --> C2[Compression: Ratio 14 to 22]
        C2 --> C3[Fuel Injection and Auto Ignition]
        C3 --> C4[Power Stroke]
        C4 --> C5[Exhaust]
    end
```

**Key Visual Takeaway:** Notice that the *only difference* between SI and CI lies in **Steps 1 and 3** — SI compresses a mixture and uses a spark, while CI compresses air only and uses high compression to ignite fuel.

## 4.3 Functional Mapping — Energy Conversion Chain in an Automobile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Chemical Energy in Fuel] --> B[Combustion in Cylinder]
    B --> C[High Pressure Gas Energy]
    C --> D[Linear Piston Motion]
    D --> E[Rotational Crankshaft Motion]
    E --> F[Transmission and Gearbox]
    F --> G[Drive Shaft]
    G --> H[Wheel Rotation]
    H --> I[Vehicle Translation]
```

**Engineering Insight:** Every arrow represents an *energy loss stage*. Modern automobiles use multiple gears and a differential to make the last two arrows as efficient as possible.

## 4.4 Schematic — Two-Stroke Crankcase Compression Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Piston Near TDC] --> Q1[Compression of Charge Above Piston]
    Q1 --> R1[Spark Fires — Power Stroke Begins]
    R1 --> S1[Piston Descends to BDC]
    S1 --> T1[Transfer Port Opens]
    T1 --> U1[Fresh Charge Enters from Crankcase]
    U1 --> V1[Exhaust Port Opens Simultaneously]
    V1 --> W1[Scavenging — Burnt Gases Expelled]
    W1 --> X1[Piston Reaches BDC — Power Stroke Ends]
    X1 --> Y1[Piston Rises Again — Compression Resumes]
    Y1 --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The 2-stroke engine performs the same thermodynamic steps in 360° (1 revolution), making it **lighter and more power-dense** but **less fuel-efficient** than its 4-stroke counterpart due to incomplete scavenging.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "compression ratio" of an IC engine and explain why diesel engines have a higher compression ratio than petrol engines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The compression ratio $r$ of an IC engine is defined as the ratio of the *total cylinder volume* (when piston is at BDC) to the *clearance volume* (when piston is at TDC):

$$
r = \frac{V_c + V_s}{V_c}
$$

**(1 mark)** for the definition and formula.

**(1 mark)** Diesel (CI) engines compress *only air* to a high ratio ($r = 14:1 \text{ to } 22:1$) so that the temperature at the end of compression exceeds the auto-ignition temperature of diesel (~250 °C). Petrol (SI) engines, on the other hand, compress a fuel–air mixture; excessive compression causes *detonation/knocking*. Hence SI engines operate at lower ratios ($r = 8:1 \text{ to } 12:1$).

**(1 mark)** for the numerical comparison and reasoning.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**List the four strokes of a 4-stroke SI engine in correct order and state the crank angles for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Stroke | Crank Angle | Valve Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Intake / Suction | 0°–180° | Inlet open, exhaust closed |
| 2 | Compression | 180°–360° | Both valves closed |
| 3 | Power / Expansion | 360°–540° | Both valves closed (after ignition) |
| 4 | Exhaust | 540°–720° | Exhaust open, inlet closed |

**(1 mark)** for listing all four strokes correctly.
**(1 mark)** for the crank-angle sequence.
**(1 mark)** for correct valve timing.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **4-stroke petrol (SI) engine** with a neat sketch and describe each stroke in detail. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has bore = **85 mm**, stroke = **100 mm**, and runs at **2800 rpm**. The mean effective pressure is **7.5 bar** and mechanical efficiency is **82%**. Calculate the **Brake Power** developed. **[(7 marks)]**

#### Model Solution (a) — Working Principle of 4-Stroke SI Engine

The 4-stroke SI engine completes its working cycle in **two full revolutions (720°)** of the crankshaft. The four strokes are:

1. **Suction Stroke (0°–180°):** The inlet valve opens and the exhaust valve remains closed. As the piston moves from TDC to BDC, a fresh charge of *air–fuel mixture* from the carburettor is drawn into the cylinder due to the partial vacuum created.

2. **Compression Stroke (180°–360°):** Both valves are closed. The piston moves from BDC to TDC, compressing the charge. Compression ratio is typically 8:1 to 12:1. Pressure rises to ~10–14 bar.

3. **Power/Expansion Stroke (360°–540°):** Just before TDC, the spark plug produces a high-voltage spark that ignites the mixture. Combustion raises pressure to **30–50 bar**, and the expanding gases push the piston from TDC to BDC — this is the only *useful work* stroke.

4. **Exhaust Stroke (540°–720°):** The exhaust valve opens; the piston moves from BDC to TDC, expelling burnt gases and preparing the cylinder for the next cycle.

> *Sketch: A standard 4-stroke engine diagram showing TDC/BDC, valves, and piston with crank rotation — credit 2 marks.*

**(7 marks distribution):** Diagram (2 marks) + Each stroke with valve timing (4 marks) + Correct cycle identification (1 mark).

#### Model Solution (b) — Brake Power Calculation

**Given:** $D = 85 \text{ mm} = 0.085 \text{ m}$; $L = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m}$; $N = 2800 \text{ rpm}$; $n = 4 \text{ cylinders}$; $P_{mean} = 7.5 \text{ bar} = 7.5 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$; $\eta_{mech} = 0.82$; 4-stroke engine.

**Step 1 — Swept volume per cylinder:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.085)^{2} \times 0.100 \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.007225 \times 0.100 \\
&\approx 5.675 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1 mark)** for this step.

**Step 2 — Indicated Power formula (4-stroke):**

For a 4-stroke engine, there is **one power stroke per 2 revolutions**, so working strokes per minute = $N/2$, and per second = $N/120$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= \frac{P_{mean} \times V_s \times N \times n}{2 \times 60} \\
&= \frac{7.5 \times 10^{5} \times 5.675 \times 10^{-4} \times 2800 \times 4}{120} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)** for formula and substitution.

**Step 3 — Numerator calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 7.5 \times 10^{5} \times 5.675 \times 10^{-4} \times 2800 \times 4 \\
&= 7.5 \times 5.675 \times 2800 \times 4 \times 10^{1} \\
&= 7.5 \times 5.675 \times 11200 \\
&\approx 4.767 \times 10^{5} \text{ W} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by 120:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= \frac{4.767 \times 10^{5}}{120} \\
&\approx 3972.5 \text{ W} \\
&\approx 3.97 \text{ kW} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)** for computation.

**Step 5 — Brake Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP &= IP \times \eta_{mech} \\
&= 3.97 \times 0.82 \\
&\approx 3.26 \text{ kW} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)** for final answer and units.

**Final Answer:** $BP \approx 3.26 \text{ kW}$.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the working of **2-stroke and 4-stroke petrol engines** under the following heads: number of revolutions per cycle, presence of valves, scavenging, power-to-weight ratio, lubrication requirement, and typical applications. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** With the help of pressure–volume and temperature–entropy diagrams, explain the **ideal Otto cycle**. Derive an expression for its thermal efficiency. **[(7 marks)]**

#### Model Solution (a) — Comparison Table

| Parameter | 2-Stroke Engine | 4-Stroke Engine |
|---|---|---|
| Revolutions per cycle | 1 (360°) | 2 (720°) |
| Power strokes per cycle | 1 | 1 |
| Power strokes per minute | $N$ | $N/2$ |
| Valves / Ports | Uses **ports** (no poppet valves) | Uses **mechanical valves** |
| Scavenging | Required; crankcase compression used | Not required; separate intake stroke |
| Power-to-weight ratio | **Higher** (lighter for same power) | Lower |
| Lubrication | Oil mixed with fuel (total-loss system) | Splash/forced lubrication with sump |
| Thermal efficiency | Lower (~20–25%) | Higher (~28–35%) |
| Emissions | Higher (unburnt hydrocarbons) | Lower, cleaner exhaust |
| Applications | Mopeds, chainsaws, outboard motors, scooters | Cars, trucks, buses, generators |

**(7 marks):** 1 mark per row × 7 rows. *(Examiner's discretion on row count)*

#### Model Solution (b) — Otto Cycle Diagram and Efficiency Derivation

**Otto Cycle Description (with P-V and T-S diagrams):**

The ideal Otto cycle models an SI engine and consists of four internally reversible processes:

1. **Process 1→2:** Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) **compression**.
2. **Process 2→3:** **Constant-volume** heat addition (combustion modelled as instantaneous heat input).
3. **Process 3→4:** Isentropic **expansion** (power stroke).
4. **Process 4→1:** **Constant-volume** heat rejection (exhaust modelled as instantaneous heat removal).

*On a P-V diagram:* Processes 1–2 and 3–4 appear as adiabats (steeper curves), and 2–3, 4–1 appear as vertical lines (constant volume).
*On a T-S diagram:* Processes 1–2 and 3–4 are vertical lines (isentropic = constant entropy), and 2–3, 4–1 are horizontal-ish curves (constant-volume heat transfer).

**(3 marks)** for description and diagrams.

**Derivation of Thermal Efficiency:**

Heat added at constant volume: $Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$.
Heat rejected at constant volume: $Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{otto} &= \frac{Q_{in} - Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For isentropic processes 1→2 and 3→4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_2}{T_1} &= r^{\gamma - 1}, \quad \frac{T_3}{T_4} = r^{\gamma - 1} \\
\Rightarrow T_2 &= T_1 r^{\gamma - 1}, \quad T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{otto} &= 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(4 marks):** 2 marks for setup, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final formula.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing 2-stroke with 4-stroke power-stroke count:** Forgetting that 2-stroke has $N$ power strokes/min and 4-stroke has $N/2$ — this alone costs **2–3 marks** in numericals.
> 2. **Using wrong denominator in IP formula:** Mixing up the factor of 2 (for 4-stroke) and 1 (for 2-stroke). Always state the formula clearly with justification.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Bore in metres (not mm) when using SI; otherwise the answer is off by a factor of $10^{6}$.
> 4. **In Otto-cycle derivations,** students often write $T_2/T_1 = r^{\gamma}$ instead of $r^{\gamma - 1}$ — this is a *very common error* and is penalised.
> 5. **Not labelling valve timings** in the 4-stroke diagram — examiners specifically look for **"intake open," "exhaust open," "both closed."**
> 6. **Writing "$BP = IP$"** without accounting for mechanical efficiency — a costly mistake.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **An IC engine converts chemical energy → thermal energy → mechanical work** via controlled combustion inside a cylinder.
- ✅ **4-stroke SI engine sequence:** Intake → Compression → Power (with spark) → Exhaust. One power stroke per **720°**.
- ✅ **4-stroke CI engine sequence:** Intake (air only) → Compression → Power (fuel injection) → Exhaust. Compression ratio is **14–22**, much higher than SI.
- ✅ **2-stroke engine:** Cycle completes in **360°**. Uses **crankcase compression** and **ports** instead of valves. Higher power-to-weight ratio but lower efficiency.
- ✅ **Compression ratio $r = (V_c + V_s)/V_c = 1 + V_s/V_c$** — directly affects thermal efficiency.
- ✅ **Otto efficiency formula:** $\eta_{otto} = 1 - 1/r^{\gamma - 1}$ with $\gamma = 1.4$.
- ✅ **Diesel efficiency formula:** $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(r_c - 1)}$.
- ✅ **IP formula (4-stroke):** $IP = \dfrac{P_{mean} \cdot V_s \cdot N \cdot n}{2 \cdot 60}$ Watts.
- ✅ **BP formula:** $BP = IP \times \eta_{mech}$ (or $BP = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$ kW).
- ✅ **SI engines use spark plug; CI engines rely on auto-ignition** due to high compression temperature.
- ✅ **Flywheel is essential** in 4-stroke engines to store rotational kinetic energy during the 3 idle strokes.
- ✅ **Standard constants to memorise:** $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $\gamma_{air} = 1.4$, $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dry Friction Clutches - Pull Type Diaphragm Clutch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT413 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dry Friction Clutches - Pull Type Diaphragm Clutch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pull Type Diaphragm Clutch** is a single-stage, dry-friction, mechanically-actuated automotive clutch in which a thin, conical diaphragm spring serves the dual function of (a) providing the axial clamping force on the pressure plate and (b) acting as a set of radial levers to operate the clutch mechanism. In the *pull* configuration, the release bearing *pulls* the central fingers of the diaphragm spring axially toward the flywheel to disengage the clutch — opposite to the push-type variant where the bearing pushes the fingers away from the flywheel.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Hook (PEMRT413 / Module 1):** *Dry Friction Clutches — construction and operation of diaphragm clutch (push and pull type), coaster and centrifugal clutches.* The pull-type diaphragm is the most commonly fitted clutch in modern front-engine, rear-wheel-drive and many transverse FWD passenger cars (e.g., Maruti, Hyundai, Tata passenger vehicles).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are holding a stack of papers flat on a table using a **soft drink bottle cap held upside down** (cone pointing up) and pressed down by your thumb. The cap tries to spring back upward (spring force). When you want to release the papers, instead of pushing your thumb *down* on the cap tip, you **hook your finger under the rim of the cap and lift it sideways toward you** — the papers fly free.

- The **bottle cap** = the **diaphragm spring** (conical disc spring).
- Your thumb pushing the tip = the **clamping force** that grips the clutch disc.
- Your finger pulling the rim toward you = the **release bearing pulling** the fingers of the diaphragm inward (toward the flywheel) to disengage.
- The **papers** = the **driven plate / friction disc**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The genius of the diaphragm clutch is that *one single part* (the diaphragm) replaces the many coil springs + levers + thrust bearing arrangement of older coil-spring clutches, giving lighter pedal effort, self-adjustment for wear, and a smaller axial package.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Coefficient of friction (μ), organic facings | **0.25 – 0.40** |
| Coefficient of friction (μ), ceramic facings | **0.30 – 0.45** |
| Mean effective friction radius (Rₘ) | **Rₘ = (Rₒ + Rᵢ) / 2** |
| Number of friction surfaces (n) | **2** (single plate) or **4** (double plate) |
| Diaphragm material | **Spring steel (EN 10132-4 / C75S / 51CrV4)** |
| Conical included angle of diaphragm | **Typically 8° – 15°** |
| Clamping force (W) | **3,000 N – 12,000 N** (passenger car) |
| Specific pressure (p) limit, organic | **≤ 2.0 MPa** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Layout (Pull Type)

The pull-type diaphragm clutch consists of the following parts, all mounted coaxially on the engine crankshaft:

1. **Flywheel** — bolted to the crankshaft, provides one friction face.
2. **Driven Plate (Clutch Disc)** — splined to the gearbox input shaft, carries friction linings on both sides.
3. **Pressure Plate** — bolted to a cover, rotates with the flywheel, presses the driven plate against the flywheel face.
4. **Diaphragm Spring** — a thin conical disc with radial fingers (or "tongues") cut into it; outer periphery rests on the cover, fingertips face the release bearing.
5. **Cover / Clutch Housing** — bolted to the flywheel, encloses the assembly.
6. **Release (Throw-out) Bearing** — slides on the gearbox input shaft bearing retainer; in the *pull* type, it **pulls the diaphragm fingers toward the flywheel** when the clutch pedal is depressed.
7. **Retracting Springs (3–4 small coil springs)** — pull the pressure plate back when the diaphragm is released.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

**ENGAGED (Pedal Released) State:**
- The diaphragm spring's outer rim is clamped between the cover and a fulcrum ring.
- The fingertips of the diaphragm bear against the pressure plate (or a thrust ring on the pressure plate fingers).
- The spring's natural tendency to flatten (return to its free conical shape) generates an **axial clamping force (W)** that pushes the pressure plate → driven plate → flywheel, locking them together.
- Engine torque is transmitted to the gearbox via friction.

**DISENGAGED (Pedal Depressed) State:**
- The release bearing moves axially (by the slave cylinder / clutch fork) and **pulls the diaphragm fingertips toward the flywheel**.
- The diaphragm pivots about the fulcrum ring — the fingertips move inward and the outer rim flexes.
- This action *lifts* the pressure plate off the driven plate, removing the clamping force.
- The driven plate is now free to slip on the input shaft splines → engine disconnected from gearbox.

> [!TIP]
> **The defining feature of PULL type:** the release bearing's axial motion is **toward the flywheel**, and the diaphragm fingertips move **inward and toward the flywheel** during disengagement.

## 2.3 Self-Reinforcing Effect (Crucial KTU Concept)

When the engine delivers torque, a tangential friction force (μW) acts on the pressure plate. This creates a torque that tends to:
- Rotate the pressure plate **further into** the driven plate (in the *pull* type) — the tangential friction drag *adds* to the axial clamping force.
- This is the **self-reinforcing (self-energizing) effect** of the pull-type diaphragm clutch.

$$\boxed{\text{Self-reinforcing effect} \Rightarrow W_{effective} = W_{spring} + \Delta W_{friction}}$$

This is one of the *most asked concepts* in KTU Module 1 and is why the pull-type diaphragm can transmit high torque with a relatively light diaphragm spring.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 exam-ready. Memorize the table; it covers ~80% of numerical questions on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Torque capacity (Uniform Pressure) | $T = n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot R_{m}$ | $R_{m} = \dfrac{R_{o} + R_{i}}{2}$ |
| 2 | Torque capacity (Uniform Wear) | $T = \dfrac{n \cdot \mu \cdot W}{2} \cdot (R_{o} + R_{i})$ | Most realistic for clutches |
| 3 | Mean radius | $R_{m} = \dfrac{R_{o} + R_{i}}{2}$ | Geometric mean |
| 4 | Average pressure (uniform pressure) | $p = \dfrac{2W}{n \cdot \pi \cdot (R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2})}$ | Used for checking p ≤ p_allow |
| 5 | Average pressure (uniform wear) | $p_{max} = \dfrac{2W}{n \cdot \pi \cdot (R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2})}$ | At inner radius; design limit |
| 6 | Number of friction surfaces | $n$ | = 2 (single plate), = 4 (double plate) |
| 7 | Axial clamping force from diaphragm | $W = k \cdot \delta$ | $k$ = diaphragm stiffness, $\delta$ = axial deflection |
| 8 | Diaphragm spring load (conical disc) | $P = \dfrac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot E \cdot t^{4} \cdot \sin\alpha}{R^{2} \cdot (1 - \nu^{2})} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\delta}{t}\right)\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{R_{i}}{R}\right)^{2}\right]^{-1}$ | Theoretical (rarely used in full) |
| 9 | Self-reinforcing incremental force | $\Delta W \approx \mu \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{r_{f}}{r_{p}}$ | $r_f$ = friction lever arm, $r_p$ = pivot radius |
| 10 | Energy absorbed per engagement | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} I \left(\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2}\right)$ | Slip-energy / heat |
| 11 | Rate of energy dissipation (avg.) | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{T \cdot (\omega_{1} - \omega_{2})}{2}$ | Used for thermal design |

> **Where:** $R_{o}$ = outer friction radius, $R_{i}$ = inner friction radius, $W$ = total axial clamping force, $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $n$ = number of friction surfaces, $E$ = Young's modulus, $t$ = diaphragm thickness, $\alpha$ = half-cone angle, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The pull-type diaphragm clutch is the **workhorse** of passenger-car drivelines because:

1. **Light pedal effort** — diaphragm acts as a lever with mechanical advantage ~3:1 between finger end and rim.
2. **Self-compensating for wear** — as friction linings thin, the diaphragm simply flattens a little more, automatically restoring clamping force (no manual adjustment).
3. **Compact axial length** — replaces 9–12 coil springs with one part.
4. **Self-reinforcing** — increases torque capacity under load without increasing pedal effort.
5. **Smooth engagement** — the diaphragm's nonlinear (rising then falling) characteristic gives a "soft" take-up.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Torque Transmitted by a Single-Plate Clutch (Uniform Pressure Assumption)

> **Starting principle:** The elementary ring of radius $r$ and width $dr$ on the friction surface carries a normal load $dW = p \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dr$. The elemental frictional force is $dF = \mu \cdot dW$ and the elemental torque is $dT = r \cdot dF$.

**Step 1 — Express the total axial load W in terms of pressure p:**

$$W = \int_{R_{i}}^{R_{o}} p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

**Step 2 — Integrate for constant pressure p:**

$$W = p \cdot 2\pi \cdot \left[ \dfrac{r^{2}}{2} \right]_{R_{i}}^{R_{o}} = p \cdot \pi \cdot (R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2})$$

**Step 3 — Express the elemental torque:**

$$dT = r \cdot (\mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr) = 2\pi \mu p \cdot r^{2} \, dr$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $R_{i}$ to $R_{o}$:**

$$T = 2\pi \mu p \int_{R_{i}}^{R_{o}} r^{2} \, dr = 2\pi \mu p \cdot \left[ \dfrac{r^{3}}{3} \right]_{R_{i}}^{R_{o}}$$

$$T = \dfrac{2\pi \mu p}{3} \cdot (R_{o}^{3} - R_{i}^{3})$$

**Step 5 — Eliminate p using Step 2: $p = \dfrac{W}{\pi(R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2})}$:**

$$T = \dfrac{2\pi \mu}{3} \cdot \dfrac{W}{\pi(R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2})} \cdot (R_{o}^{3} - R_{i}^{3})$$

$$\boxed{T = \dfrac{2 \mu W}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_{o}^{3} - R_{i}^{3}}{R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2}}}$$

**Step 6 — Factor as mean radius (uniform pressure form):**

Using the identity $\dfrac{R_{o}^{3} - R_{i}^{3}}{R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2}} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot R_{m}$, where $R_{m} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_{o}^{3} - R_{i}^{3}}{R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2}}$, we get:

$$\boxed{T_{UP} = n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot R_{m}}$$

with $n$ = number of friction surfaces (= 2 for single plate).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Torque Transmitted (Uniform Wear Assumption)

> **Starting principle:** Uniform wear ⇒ $p \cdot r = \text{const} = k$ (the wear rate is constant across the surface, so pressure falls off with radius). This is the **more realistic** assumption for steady-state clutch design.

**Step 1 — Pressure distribution:** $p(r) = \dfrac{k}{r}$

**Step 2 — Total axial load:**

$$W = \int_{R_{i}}^{R_{o}} p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \int_{R_{i}}^{R_{o}} \dfrac{k}{r} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi k (R_{o} - R_{i})$$

$$\Rightarrow k = \dfrac{W}{2\pi (R_{o} - R_{i})}$$

**Step 3 — Elemental torque:**

$$dT = r \cdot (\mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr) = r \cdot \mu \cdot \dfrac{k}{r} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi \mu k \cdot r \, dr$$

**Step 4 — Integrate:**

$$T = 2\pi \mu k \int_{R_{i}}^{R_{o}} r \, dr = 2\pi \mu k \cdot \dfrac{R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2}}{2} = \pi \mu k (R_{o} + R_{i})(R_{o} - R_{i})$$

**Step 5 — Substitute k from Step 2:**

$$T = \pi \mu \cdot \dfrac{W}{2\pi (R_{o} - R_{i})} \cdot (R_{o} + R_{i})(R_{o} - R_{i})$$

$$\boxed{T_{UW} = n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{R_{o} + R_{i}}{2} = n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot R_{m}}$$

Note: The mean radius for **uniform wear** is $R_{m,UW} = \dfrac{R_{o} + R_{i}}{2}$ (arithmetic mean), whereas for **uniform pressure** it is $R_{m,UP} = \dfrac{2(R_{o}^{3} - R_{i}^{3})}{3(R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2})}$ (a slightly larger value).

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU 2019-Style)

> **Problem:** A single-plate pull-type diaphragm clutch is required to transmit **250 N·m** of torque. The friction lining has an outer diameter of **200 mm**, inner diameter of **100 mm**, and coefficient of friction **0.3**. Using the **uniform pressure** theory, determine the axial clamping force $W$ and the maximum contact pressure $p$.

**Given:**
- $T = 250$ N·m
- $D_o = 0.200$ m $\Rightarrow R_o = 0.100$ m
- $D_i = 0.100$ m $\Rightarrow R_i = 0.050$ m
- $\mu = 0.3$, $n = 2$

**Step 1 — Compute mean radius (UP):**

$$R_{m,UP} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_{o}^{3} - R_{i}^{3}}{R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2}}$$

$$R_{o}^{3} = 0.100^{3} = 1.000 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}$$
$$R_{i}^{3} = 0.050^{3} = 1.250 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}$$
$$R_{o}^{2} = 0.0100 \text{ m}^{2}, \quad R_{i}^{2} = 0.0025 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$R_{m,UP} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{1.000\times10^{-3} - 1.250\times10^{-4}}{0.0100 - 0.0025}$$

$$= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{8.75 \times 10^{-4}}{7.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 0.11667 = 0.07778 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for W from $T = n \mu W R_m$:**

$$W = \dfrac{T}{n \mu R_{m,UP}} = \dfrac{250}{2 \times 0.3 \times 0.07778} = \dfrac{250}{0.04667}$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 5357 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute maximum pressure:**

$$p = \dfrac{W}{\pi(R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2})} = \dfrac{5357}{\pi \times (0.0100 - 0.0025)} = \dfrac{5357}{\pi \times 0.0075}$$

$$\boxed{p \approx 2.275 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 0.2275 \text{ MPa}}$$

> Since $0.2275$ MPa $\ll 2.0$ MPa (allowable for organic facings), the design is **safe**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pull vs. Push Diaphragm

| Parameter | Pull Type | Push Type |
|---|---|---|
| Release bearing motion | **Toward flywheel** | Away from flywheel |
| Self-reinforcing effect | **Yes — strong** | No / weak |
| Pedal effort | Lower (lever advantage + self-energizing) | Slightly higher |
| Axial space required | **Less** (bearing sits inboard) | More (bearing sits outboard) |
| Heat dissipation path | Slightly better | Adequate |
| Modern passenger-car usage | **Dominant (>85%)** | Mostly older / heavy commercial |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Pull-Type Diaphragm Clutch

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **physical / functional architecture** of the pull-type diaphragm clutch, showing all sub-systems, the flow of mechanical forces, and the actuation path. Use this as the *reference schematic* for KTU board-exam answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input["DRIVER INPUT"]
        A1["Clutch Pedal (Driver Foot)"]
    end

    subgraph Actuation["HYDRAULIC / MECHANICAL LINKAGE"]
        B1["Master Cylinder"]
        B2["Slave Cylinder"]
        B3["Clutch Fork (Lever)"]
    end

    subgraph ClutchAssembly["PULL-TYPE DIAPHRAGM CLUTCH ASSEMBLY"]
        direction TB
        C1["Flywheel (Crankshaft-mounted)"]
        C2["Driven Plate / Clutch Disc with Friction Linings (Splined to Gearbox Input Shaft)"]
        C3["Pressure Plate (Bolted to Cover)"]
        C4["Diaphragm Spring (Conical Disc with Radial Fingers)"]
        C5["Clutch Cover / Housing"]
        C6["Release / Throw-out Bearing (slides on guide sleeve)"]
        C7["Retracting Springs (3 to 4 small coil springs)"]
    end

    subgraph Output["DRIVELINE OUTPUT"]
        D1["Gearbox Input Shaft"]
        D2["Transmission Gears"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C6
    C6 -- "PULL motion toward flywheel" --> C4
    C4 -- "Finger pivots about fulcrum ring" --> C3
    C3 -- "Clamps" --> C2
    C2 -- "Friction contact" --> C1
    C1 -- "Rotates with" --> C5
    C2 -- "Torque to" --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    C7 -- "Retracts pressure plate when released" --> C3
    C4 -- "Generates axial clamping force W (engaged state)" --> C3
```

## 4.2 Operational State Machine (Engaged vs. Disengaged)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Engaged
    Engaged --> Disengaging : Pedal Pressed → Slave Cylinder pushes Fork → Fork pulls Release Bearing
    Disengaging --> Disengaged : Diaphragm fingers pulled inward → Pressure Plate lifts → Driven Plate free
    Disengaged --> Engaging : Pedal Released → Fork retracts → Diaphragm relaxes → Pressure Plate re-clamps
    Engaging --> Engaged : Self-reinforcing effect assists final lock-up
    Engaged --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Force & Motion Path (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["Driver Foot Force F_pedal"] --> F2["Pedal Lever (Mechanical Advantage ≈ 5:1)"]
    F2 --> F3["Master Cylinder (Hydraulic Pressure p)"]
    F3 --> F4["Slave Cylinder (Force ≈ p × A)"]
    F4 --> F5["Clutch Fork (Lever Arm ≈ 1.5:1)"]
    F5 --> F6["Release Bearing Axial Pull Force F_rb"]
    F6 --> F7["Diaphragm Spring (Lever Fingers, MA ≈ 3:1)"]
    F7 --> F8["Pressure Plate Clamping Force W (≈ 4000 to 8000 N)"]
    F8 --> F9["Friction Torque T = n·μ·W·R_m (Engaged)"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **In your KTU answer sheet**, the **overall mechanical advantage** from foot to pressure plate is approximately:
> $MA_{total} = 5 \times 1.5 \times 3 \approx 22.5$
> So a **200 N foot force** can deliver a **~4,500 N clamping force** — the secret of light clutch pedals.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between push-type and pull-type diaphragm clutches with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

| Feature | Push Type | Pull Type |
|---|---|---|
| Bearing motion at disengagement | Bearing pushes fingers **away** from flywheel | Bearing pulls fingers **toward** flywheel |
| Self-reinforcing | Absent | **Present** (clamping force increases with torque) |
| Axial length | Greater | **Less** |
| Pedal effort | Higher | Lower |
| Application | Older / heavy commercial | **Modern passenger cars** |
| Fingertip motion at disengage | Inward toward flywheel | Outward toward pressure plate (lifts it) |

*Sketch: Draw the cross-section, label (1) flywheel, (2) pressure plate, (3) diaphragm, (4) release bearing, and show arrow of bearing motion opposite to flywheel for push and toward flywheel for pull.* **(3 marks)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**List any four advantages of a diaphragm spring clutch over a conventional coil-spring clutch.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Compactness** — single spring replaces 9–12 coil springs; smaller axial length.
2. **Self-compensation for wear** — diaphragm flattens progressively as lining wears, automatically maintaining clamping force (no manual adjustment).
3. **Lighter pedal effort** — diaphragm acts as a lever giving 2.5–3× mechanical advantage.
4. **Better heat dissipation** — open construction; rotating cover induces airflow.
5. (Bonus) **Lower inertia** — fewer parts → smoother engagement.
6. (Bonus) **Self-reinforcing effect** in pull type → higher torque capacity. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]*

> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a pull-type diaphragm clutch** used in modern passenger cars. Clearly indicate the direction of release-bearing travel at disengagement. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A single-plate pull-type diaphragm clutch has friction surfaces of **outer radius 110 mm** and **inner radius 70 mm**. The maximum coefficient of friction is **0.32** and the axial clamping force on the pressure plate is **5,500 N**. Determine:
> (i) Torque capacity using **uniform pressure** theory.
> (ii) Torque capacity using **uniform wear** theory.
> (iii) Maximum contact pressure in each case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (3 marks):**
- Draw labelled cross-section (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid architecture in physical form): flywheel, driven plate with friction linings on both faces, pressure plate, diaphragm spring (conical with radial fingers), clutch cover, release bearing, retracting springs.
- **State the material** of the diaphragm (spring steel 51CrV4 / C75S, ~2–3 mm thick).
- **State the typical** outer diameter range of clutch disc (200–240 mm for passenger cars).

**Working (4 marks):**
- **Engaged:** diaphragm's natural conical shape presses pressure plate → driven plate → flywheel. Engine torque transmitted by friction. Self-reinforcing effect: friction drag on pressure plate adds to clamping force.
- **Disengaged:** when pedal is pressed, hydraulic fluid actuates slave cylinder → fork moves release bearing axially **toward flywheel (PULL action)** → bearing pulls diaphragm fingertips → diaphragm pivots about fulcrum ring at its outer periphery → pressure plate retracts (helped by retracting springs) → driven plate free to slip on input shaft splines.
- Highlight that the **release bearing does not rotate** with the clutch (it is a sliding contact, typically ball bearing with thrust face) — this is a frequent conceptual point.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $R_o = 0.110$ m, $R_i = 0.070$ m, $\mu = 0.32$, $W = 5500$ N, $n = 2$ (single plate, 2 friction surfaces).

**(i) Uniform Pressure Torque (3 marks):**

$$R_{m,UP} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}$$

$$R_o^{3} = 0.110^{3} = 1.331 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}, \quad R_i^{3} = 0.070^{3} = 3.430 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}$$
$$R_o^{2} = 0.0121 \text{ m}^{2}, \quad R_i^{2} = 0.0049 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$R_{m,UP} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{1.331\times10^{-3} - 3.430\times10^{-4}}{0.0121 - 0.0049} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{9.88\times10^{-4}}{7.2\times10^{-3}}$$

$$R_{m,UP} = \dfrac{2}{3} \times 0.1372 = 0.09148 \text{ m}$$

$$T_{UP} = n \mu W R_{m,UP} = 2 \times 0.32 \times 5500 \times 0.09148$$

$$\boxed{T_{UP} \approx 322.0 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(ii) Uniform Wear Torque (2 marks):**

$$T_{UW} = n \mu W \cdot \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2} = 2 \times 0.32 \times 5500 \times \dfrac{0.110 + 0.070}{2}$$

$$= 2 \times 0.32 \times 5500 \times 0.090 = 316.8 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{UW} \approx 316.8 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(iii) Maximum contact pressure (2 marks):**

Uniform pressure case: $p = \dfrac{W}{\pi(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})} = \dfrac{5500}{\pi \times 0.0072} = 2.43 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 0.243$ MPa.

Uniform wear case: $p_{max}$ at $R_i$: $p_{max} = \dfrac{k}{R_i}$ where $k = \dfrac{W}{2\pi(R_o - R_i)}$

$$k = \dfrac{5500}{2\pi \times 0.040} = 21,881 \text{ N/m}, \quad p_{max} = \dfrac{21{,}881}{0.070} = 3.126 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 0.313 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{p_{max,UP} = 0.243 \text{ MPa}, \quad p_{max,UW} = 0.313 \text{ MPa}}$$

Both well within the 2.0 MPa allowable limit → **design is safe**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper]*

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **self-reinforcing (self-energizing) effect** in a pull-type diaphragm clutch. Show how the effective clamping force is modified under transmitted torque. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A pull-type diaphragm clutch is to transmit **350 N·m** at **2,400 rpm**. The clutch has **two friction surfaces**, outer diameter **220 mm**, inner diameter **130 mm**, and the coefficient of friction may be taken as **0.30**. Using **uniform wear** theory, calculate:
> (i) The required axial clamping force $W$.
> (ii) The maximum intensity of pressure and check if the design is safe for organic facings ($p_{allow} = 0.35$ MPa). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition (2 marks):**
The self-reinforcing (or self-energizing) effect in a pull-type diaphragm clutch is the phenomenon whereby, when the clutch is transmitting torque, the tangential friction drag on the pressure plate **adds to the axial clamping force** supplied by the diaphragm spring, effectively *increasing* the clamping force and hence the torque-transmitting capacity.

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
- When the engine delivers torque, the friction force $\mu W$ acts tangentially on the contact between the pressure plate and the driven plate.
- This tangential force creates a moment about the diaphragm fulcrum ring (radius $r_f$).
- The moment is $\mu W \cdot r_f$, which acts in a sense that **rotates the pressure plate further into the driven plate** (in the pull-type geometry) — i.e., it *adds* to the spring's clamping moment.
- The effective clamping force becomes:

$$W_{eff} = W_{spring} + \Delta W = W_{spring} + \mu W_{spring} \cdot \dfrac{r_f}{r_p}$$

where $r_p$ is the pivot radius at the diaphragm fingertips.

**Significance (2 marks):**
- The pull-type diaphragm has this effect; the push type does not (its friction drag tends to *open* the clutch).
- This is why the pull-type is preferred for high-torque passenger-car applications with a relatively light pedal effort.
- **Note:** A downside is that during *disengagement* the effect is reversed — the driver must supply extra force to overcome the self-energizing residual. This is why pull-type clutches sometimes feel "heavier at the bite point."

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $T = 350$ N·m, $N = 2400$ rpm, $n = 2$, $D_o = 0.220$ m $\Rightarrow R_o = 0.110$ m, $D_i = 0.130$ m $\Rightarrow R_i = 0.065$ m, $\mu = 0.30$, $p_{allow} = 0.35$ MPa.

**(i) Required Clamping Force (3 marks):**

Using uniform wear: $T = n \mu W \cdot \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$

$$R_m = \dfrac{0.110 + 0.065}{2} = 0.0875 \text{ m}$$

$$W = \dfrac{T}{n \mu R_m} = \dfrac{350}{2 \times 0.30 \times 0.0875} = \dfrac{350}{0.0525}$$

$$\boxed{W = 6{,}666.67 \text{ N} \approx 6.67 \text{ kN}}$$

**(ii) Maximum pressure and safety check (4 marks):**

$$k = \dfrac{W}{2\pi (R_o - R_i)} = \dfrac{6666.67}{2\pi \times (0.110 - 0.065)} = \dfrac{6666.67}{2\pi \times 0.045}$$

$$k = \dfrac{6666.67}{0.28274} = 23{,}576.6 \text{ N/m}$$

Maximum pressure occurs at the inner radius $R_i$:

$$p_{max} = \dfrac{k}{R_i} = \dfrac{23{,}576.6}{0.065} = 3.627 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 0.3627 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{p_{max} = 0.3627 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{vs.} \quad p_{allow} = 0.35 \text{ MPa}}$$

> **Conclusion:** $p_{max} > p_{allow}$ by ~3.6%. **Design is marginally unsafe** — student should either (a) increase $W$ marginally, (b) increase $D_o$ or $D_i$, or (c) select a higher-μ lining. **(7 marks total)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks (KTU 2019–2024 trend):**
> 1. **Confusing "pull" with "push" direction.** If the examiner's diagram shows a push-type and you describe a pull-type (or vice versa), expect **2–3 marks deducted immediately**. *Always state explicitly:* "The release bearing moves axially **toward the flywheel** in a pull-type."
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $n$ (number of friction surfaces).** A single-plate clutch has **n = 2**, not 1. This error alone doubles/halves your torque answer.
> 3. **Using the wrong mean-radius formula.** $R_m = (R_o + R_i)/2$ is the *uniform-wear* mean. For *uniform pressure*, use $R_m = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$. Many students mix these up.
> 4. **Not stating the self-reinforcing effect** when asked about pull-type advantages — this is a **guaranteed 2-mark question** in Part A.
> 5. **Skipping units** in numerical answers. Always show N·m for torque, MPa (or N/m²) for pressure.
> 6. **Not drawing the diaphragm correctly** — examiners specifically look for the **fingered conical spring** and the **fulcrum ring** in the sketch.
> 7. **Using $D$ (diameter) instead of $R$ (radius)** in the torque formula. $R_m$ is a *radius*, not a diameter.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU revision sheet — go through this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ **Pull-type diaphragm clutch** = release bearing **pulls** diaphragm fingertips **toward the flywheel** at disengagement.
- ☐ **Single-plate dry friction clutch** has **n = 2** friction surfaces; **double-plate** has **n = 4**.
- ☐ **Uniform pressure torque:** $T = n \mu W R_{m,UP}$ where $R_{m,UP} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$.
- ☐ **Uniform wear torque:** $T = n \mu W \cdot \frac{R_o + R_i}{2}$ (the more realistic assumption for steady-state design).
- ☐ **Uniform wear** ⇒ $p \cdot r = \text{const}$; $p_{max}$ occurs at **inner radius** $R_i$.
- ☐ **Uniform pressure** ⇒ $p = \text{const}$; $p_{max} = p_{avg}$ everywhere.
- ☐ **Self-reinforcing effect** is the **defining advantage** of pull-type diaphragm clutches — friction drag adds to axial clamping force under torque.
- ☐ **Mechanical advantage** of diaphragm finger lever ≈ 2.5 – 3.5.
- ☐ **Diaphragm material:** spring steel (51CrV4, C75S) — NOT carbon steel, NOT stainless.
- ☐ **Diaphragm is dual-function:** spring *and* lever.
- ☐ **No manual adjustment needed** — diaphragm flattens as lining wears (self-compensating).
- ☐ **Allowable pressure** for organic facings ≈ 0.3 – 0.4 MPa; for ceramic ≈ 0.7 – 1.0 MPa.
- ☐ **Release bearing does not rotate** — it slides on a stationary guide sleeve (this is a frequent KTU trick question).
- ☐ **Retracting springs** (3 or 4 small coil springs) ensure the pressure plate returns when the diaphragm is pulled inward.
- ☐ **Energy absorbed per engagement** $E = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$ — used to design for thermal capacity (avoid fade).
- ☐ **Push type vs. Pull type — Key difference is the direction of bearing travel at disengage, NOT the spring orientation.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Diaphragm Clutch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT413 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multiple Diaphragm Clutch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — MULTIPLE DIAPHRAGM CLUTCH

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Multiple Diaphragm Clutch** (also termed a **Tandem Diaphragm Clutch** or **Dual Diaphragm Clutch**) is an advanced variant of the single-plate friction clutch in which **two or more conical diaphragm springs** are arranged coaxially (concentrically) and act in mechanical series on a common pressure plate. The arrangement is engineered to **multiply the axial clamping load** delivered to the friction disc without proportionally increasing the pedal effort or the overall clutch envelope dimensions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A multiple diaphragm clutch is a friction clutch employing **two Belleville-type diaphragm springs** stacked coaxially — a *primary (main) diaphragm* and an *auxiliary (secondary) diaphragm* — such that the leverage ratio of the assembly is the **product of the individual lever ratios**, producing an axial clamping force $F_c = n \cdot F_s$, where $n$ is the number of diaphragm stages and $F_s$ is the force from a single spring.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Handed Grip"

Imagine you are trying to press a heavy textbook flat against a table with one palm. Now try doing the same with **both palms stacked, one smaller on top of the other**, pushing in the same direction. The combined push is greater because the smaller palm is being squeezed by the larger one, which then transmits a *boosted* force onto the book.

In a multiple diaphragm clutch:
- The **larger (outer/rear) diaphragm** acts like the bigger palm.
- The **smaller (inner/front) diaphragm** sits concentric inside it.
- Both springs deflect together when the clutch pedal is pressed, but their combined mechanical advantage **multiplies the squeeze** on the pressure plate.
- The clutch therefore transmits **higher torque** for the **same pedal effort**, which is the key engineering advantage.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range (Automotive) |
|---|---|
| Number of diaphragm stages ($n$) | **2 (most common), occasionally 3** |
| Diaphragm cone angle ($\alpha$) | **8° – 15°** from horizontal |
| Lever ratio of each stage ($L_r$) | **2.5 – 4.0** |
| Combined (multiplied) lever ratio ($L_{total}$) | **$L_{r1} \times L_{r2} \approx 6$ – $12$** |
| Clamping force amplification | **1.8× – 2.5×** over single diaphragm |
| Material | **Spring steel (50CrV4 / 51CrMoV4 / SUP9)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use one bigger spring?**
> A single thicker diaphragm would require excessive **pedal effort** to deflect. Stacking two lighter diaphragms gives the **same clamping force** with **lower foot pressure** — a classic KTU Board "design rationale" question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Component Inventory of a Multiple Diaphragm Clutch

The assembly consists of the following parts, listed in the order of assembly from the engine flywheel outward:

1. **Engine Flywheel** — rotates with the crankshaft; its face is the driving member.
2. **Driven Friction Disc (Clutch Plate)** — carries friction linings on both faces, splined to the gearbox input shaft.
3. **Pressure Plate (Cover Assembly)** — bolted to a sheet-metal clutch cover; the driven clamping member.
4. **Primary Diaphragm Spring (Rear / Outer)** — a Belleville washer-type spring, larger in diameter; bears against the cover straps.
5. **Secondary Diaphragm Spring (Front / Inner)** — a smaller Belleville spring mounted concentrically inside the primary one; bears against a fulcrum ring attached to the primary spring's fingers.
6. **Release (Throw-out) Bearing** — slides on the input shaft sleeve; pushes on the inner fingers of the secondary diaphragm.
7. **Clutch Cover (Housing)** — sheet-metal casing bolted to the flywheel; houses all springs and linkages.
8. **Retracting Springs (3–4 small helical springs)** — pull the pressure plate back when the clutch is disengaged.
9. **Drive Straps / Tangential Drive Lugs** — transmit engine torque to the pressure plate so it rotates with the flywheel but can slide axially.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board:** In a *single* diaphragm clutch the release bearing pushes the **inner fingers** of the diaphragm. In a *multiple* diaphragm clutch the release bearing pushes only the **innermost diaphragm's fingers**, and its motion is then leveraged through the secondary diaphragm back to the primary.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Principle

**A — Clutch ENGAGED (Pedal Released):**
- The natural (undeflected) cone shape of the diaphragms pushes the pressure plate axially against the friction disc.
- Friction disc is firmly sandwiched between flywheel and pressure plate → **torque is transmitted**.
- Release bearing is pulled back by retracting springs; no contact with diaphragm fingers.

**B — Clutch DISENGAGED (Pedal Pressed):**
1. The driver's foot pushes the master cylinder → hydraulic fluid moves the slave cylinder.
2. The slave cylinder pushes the release fork → release bearing slides forward.
3. Release bearing contacts the **inner fingers of the secondary diaphragm only**.
4. Secondary diaphragm flattens slightly — its deflection pulls on the **fulcrum ring** shared with the primary diaphragm.
5. Primary diaphragm is now forced to flatten through this intermediate lever.
6. Pressure plate retracts axially, releasing the friction disc → **engine disconnected from gearbox**.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Multiplication

The genius of the arrangement lies in **compound lever action**.

- A single diaphragm acts as a **toggle lever**: a small radial finger movement produces a large axial clamping force, characterized by its *lever ratio* $L_r$.
- When **two diaphragms** are stacked concentrically, the secondary diaphragm's finger motion becomes the **input displacement** to the primary diaphragm's fingers.
- The total lever ratio becomes a **product**, not a sum:

$$L_{total} = L_{r1} \times L_{r2}$$

This non-linear multiplication is why two modestly sized springs can outperform one massive spring, while still requiring low pedal effort.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Viva:** "*Two diaphragms stacked = two toggle levers in series → lever ratios multiply, axial force multiplies.*"

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Axial clamping force of single diaphragm | $F_{s1} = F_{f1} \cdot L_{r1}$ | N | $F_{f1}$ = finger force |
| 2 | Axial clamping force, secondary diaphragm | $F_{s2} = F_{f2} \cdot L_{r2}$ | N | Acts on intermediate ring |
| 3 | Combined clamping force (tandem, both pressed simultaneously) | $F_c = F_{s1} + F_{s2}$ | N | When parallel-linked |
| 4 | Combined clamping force (compound leverage, common case) | $F_c = F_s \cdot n$ | N | $n$ = no. of diaphragms |
| 5 | Combined lever ratio | $L_{total} = L_{r1} \cdot L_{r2}$ | dimensionless | Multiplicative |
| 6 | Total torque capacity | $T_c = \mu \cdot F_c \cdot R_m$ | N·m | Standard friction-clutch form |
| 7 | Mean effective friction radius | $R_m = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}$ | m | $R_o$, $R_i$ = outer/inner lining radii |
| 8 | Effective spring rate (springs in series) | $\dfrac{1}{k_{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{k_1} + \dfrac{1}{k_2}$ | N/mm | Softer than either alone |
| 9 | Effective spring rate (springs in parallel) | $k_{eff} = k_1 + k_2$ | N/mm | Stiffer than either alone |
| 10 | Pedal effort (operator input) | $F_{pedal} = \dfrac{F_c}{L_{total} \cdot \eta_{hyd}}$ | N | $\eta_{hyd}$ ≈ 0.85 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** When two diaphragms are arranged *tandem/concentric* (which is the KTU-syllabus definition of *multiple diaphragm*), substitute $n = 2$ into the relevant formula. Do not confuse this with springs in mechanical series (deflection adds) versus parallel (force adds).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multiple diaphragm clutches are used in:

- **Heavy commercial trucks and buses** (Tata, Ashok Leyland, BharatBenz) where high torque at low pedal effort is mandatory.
- **Racing cars (Formula 1, rally)** — for ultra-high torque capacity in a compact package.
- **Tractors and off-highway equipment** — robust against vibration and thermal shock.
- **Motorcycles with high-torque engines** (some liter-class sport bikes).

The KTU-emphasised advantages are: **(i)** higher torque transfer, **(ii)** progressive engagement (the secondary diaphragm "tops up" the clamp progressively), **(iii)** self-reinforcing wear compensation, and **(iv)** compact packaging.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Combined Lever Ratio of Two Diaphragms in Compound Action

**Given:** A primary diaphragm with lever ratio $L_{r1}$ and a secondary diaphragm with lever ratio $L_{r2}$, both concentrically mounted.

**Find:** Total mechanical advantage $L_{total}$ and the axial clamping force $F_c$ in terms of the operator's finger force $F_f$.

**Step 1 — Define lever ratio of each diaphragm individually.**
A single diaphragm behaves as a **toggle**: a radial input displacement $\delta_r$ at the fingers produces an axial output displacement $\delta_a$ at the pressure-plate contact. The lever ratio is, by definition:

$$L_{r} = \dfrac{\delta_r}{\delta_a} = \dfrac{F_{axial}}{F_{radial}}$$

This is the mechanical advantage; equivalently, force is *amplified* by $L_r$ from finger to pressure plate.

**Step 2 — Analyse secondary diaphragm alone.**
The release bearing applies a radial (finger) force $F_f$ on the secondary diaphragm's inner fingers. The axial force generated by this diaphragm on the intermediate fulcrum ring is:

$$F_{s2} = L_{r2} \cdot F_f$$

**Step 3 — Analyse primary diaphragm with its input coming from the fulcrum ring.**
The axial motion of the secondary diaphragm's outer rim is communicated to the primary diaphragm's inner fingers via the shared fulcrum ring. This transmission effectively converts the axial motion back into a radial "finger force" on the primary diaphragm:

$$F_{f1} = F_{s2} \cdot \eta_{mech} \approx F_{s2}$$

where $\eta_{mech}$ is the mechanical efficiency of the fulcrum linkage (assume ≈ 1 for ideal analysis, or 0.95 for board marks).

**Step 4 — Combine.**
Substituting Step 2 into Step 3:

$$F_{f1} = L_{r2} \cdot F_f$$

The axial clamping force from the primary diaphragm on the pressure plate is:

$$F_{s1} = L_{r1} \cdot F_{f1} = L_{r1} \cdot L_{r2} \cdot F_f$$

**Step 5 — Write the final result.**

$$\boxed{\,F_c = L_{r1} \cdot L_{r2} \cdot F_f = L_{total} \cdot F_f \quad \text{where} \quad L_{total} = L_{r1} \cdot L_{r2}\,}$$

> The lever ratios multiply because the diaphragms are connected in **mechanical series (compound leverage)**, not in mechanical parallel.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque Capacity of a Multiple Diaphragm Clutch

**Given:** Friction coefficient $\mu$, friction lining outer radius $R_o$, inner radius $R_i$, clamping force $F_c$.

**Find:** Maximum transmissible torque $T_{max}$.

**Step 1 — Pressure distribution assumption.**
Assume uniform pressure $p$ over the annular friction lining area $A$:

$$p = \dfrac{F_c}{A} = \dfrac{F_c}{\pi (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})}$$

**Step 2 — Elemental torque from a ring of radius $r$, width $dr$.**
The elemental normal force on the ring is $dF = p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$. The elemental frictional torque is:

$$dT = \mu \cdot dF \cdot r = 2\pi \mu p \, r^{2} \, dr$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $R_i$ to $R_o$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi \mu p \, r^{2} \, dr \\[4pt]
&= 2\pi \mu p \left[ \dfrac{r^{3}}{3} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2\pi \mu p}{3} \left( R_o^{3} - R_i^{3} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $p = F_c / [\pi(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})]$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \dfrac{2\pi \mu}{3} \cdot \dfrac{F_c}{\pi (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})} \cdot (R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}) \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \mu \cdot F_c \cdot \dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Recognise the mean effective radius $R_m$.**

$$\boxed{\,T_{max} = \mu \cdot F_c \cdot R_m \quad \text{with} \quad R_m = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Insert $F_c = n \cdot F_s$ for a multiple diaphragm (n = 2 typical).**

$$\boxed{\,T_{max} = n \cdot \mu \cdot F_s \cdot R_m\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spring-Rate Analysis (Deflection & Pedal Travel)

**Given:** Two diaphragms with individual axial stiffnesses $k_1$ and $k_2$, stacked in **compound mechanical action** (not mechanical spring-series stacking).

**Find:** Effective stiffness $k_{eff}$ and resulting pedal travel.

**Step 1 — Spring stiffness definition.**
A diaphragm's axial stiffness is defined as:

$$k = \dfrac{F_s}{\delta_a}$$

where $\delta_a$ is the axial deflection under clamping force $F_s$.

**Step 2 — Displacement compatibility in compound linkage.**
Because the secondary diaphragm's output displacement drives the primary diaphragm's input, the **total axial displacement** seen at the pressure plate is the *sum* of the individual axial contributions (each diaphragm contributes its own axial deflection):

$$\delta_{total} = \delta_{a1} + \delta_{a2} = \dfrac{F_c}{k_1} + \dfrac{F_c}{k_2} = F_c \left( \dfrac{1}{k_1} + \dfrac{1}{k_2} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Effective spring rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{k_{eff}} &= \dfrac{1}{k_1} + \dfrac{1}{k_2} \\[6pt]
k_{eff} &= \dfrac{k_1 k_2}{k_1 + k_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Pedal travel.**
If the hydraulic slave-cylinder ratio is $A_s/A_p$ (slave/piston area) and the release-fork lever ratio is $L_f$:

$$\boxed{\,S_{pedal} = \dfrac{A_p}{A_s} \cdot L_f \cdot L_{total} \cdot \delta_{total}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedal Implication:** A *lower* $k_{eff}$ (because of series-style compounding) means *more pedal travel* is needed to disengage. This is the engineering trade-off board examiners often ask about.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board-Style)

> **Problem:** A heavy-vehicle clutch uses **two diaphragms in tandem**. Each diaphragm has lever ratio $L_{r1} = 3.0$ and $L_{r2} = 2.5$. The operator's finger force at the release bearing is $F_f = 200\,\text{N}$. Friction linings have $R_o = 150\,\text{mm}$, $R_i = 100\,\text{mm}$, $\mu = 0.35$.

**Step 1 — Combined lever ratio.**
$L_{total} = L_{r1} \cdot L_{r2} = 3.0 \times 2.5 = 7.5$

**Step 2 — Clamping force.**
$F_c = L_{total} \cdot F_f = 7.5 \times 200 = 1500\,\text{N}$

**Step 3 — Mean effective radius.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_m &= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{(0.150)^{3} - (0.100)^{3}}{(0.150)^{2} - (0.100)^{2}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{3.375 \times 10^{-3} - 1.000 \times 10^{-3}}{2.25 \times 10^{-2} - 1.00 \times 10^{-2}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{2.375 \times 10^{-3}}{1.25 \times 10^{-2}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2}{3} \times 0.190 = 0.1267\,\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum transmissible torque.**
$T_{max} = \mu \cdot F_c \cdot R_m = 0.35 \times 1500 \times 0.1267 = 66.5\,\text{N·m}$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multiple Diaphragm Clutch

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow of force** through the clutch assembly — an approved KTU-style schematic substitute for a physical cross-section drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Foot Pedal Force Fp] --> B[Master Cylinder Hydraulic Pressure]
    B --> C[Slave Cylinder Push Rod]
    C --> D[Release Fork Lever]
    D --> E[Release Bearing Axial Thrust]
    E --> F[Secondary Diaphragm Inner Fingers]
    F --> G[Secondary Diaphragm Toggle Action Lever Ratio Lr2]
    G --> H[Intermediate Fulcrum Ring]
    H --> I[Primary Diaphragm Inner Fingers]
    I --> J[Primary Diaphragm Toggle Action Lever Ratio Lr1]
    J --> K[Pressure Plate Axial Clamping Force Fc]
    K --> L[Friction Disc Clamped Between Flywheel and Pressure Plate]
    L --> M[Torque Transmitted to Gearbox Input Shaft]

    subgraph Return_Path ["Return Path (Engagement)"]
        N[Release Bearing Retracts] --> O[Helical Retracting Springs Pull Pressure Plate]
        O --> P[Diaphragms Re-expand to Cone Shape]
        P --> Q[Friction Disc Re-clamped]
    end

    Q -.-> L
```

### 4.2 Force-Amplification Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT ["Operator Input"]
        A1[Ff equals 200 N]
    end

    subgraph SECONDARY ["Secondary Diaphragm Stage"]
        B1[Lever Ratio Lr2 equals 2.5]
        B2[Axial Force Fs2 equals Ff times Lr2]
    end

    subgraph PRIMARY ["Primary Diaphragm Stage"]
        C1[Lever Ratio Lr1 equals 3.0]
        C2[Axial Force Fs1 equals Fs2 times Lr1]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT ["Final Clamping Force"]
        D1[Fc equals Ff times Lr1 times Lr2 equals 1500 N]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> C1 --> C2 --> D1
```

### 4.3 Schematic Component Layout (Axial Cross-Section — Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENG ["Engine Side"]
        F1[Crankshaft]
        F2[Flywheel with Driving Face]
    end

    subgraph CLUTCH ["Clutch Assembly"]
        G1[Friction Disc with Two-Sided Lining]
        G2[Pressure Plate]
        G3[Primary Diaphragm Spring Outer]
        G4[Secondary Diaphragm Spring Inner]
        G5[Intermediate Fulcrum Ring]
        G6[Release Bearing]
        G7[Clutch Cover Housing]
        G8[Retracting Helical Springs]
    end

    subgraph GB ["Gearbox Side"]
        H1[Input Shaft Spline]
        H2[Gearbox Input]
    end

    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> G4
    G4 --> G5
    G5 --> G6
    G7 --> G3
    G8 --> G2
    G1 --> H1 --> H2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Note:** When asked in the exam to "sketch a multiple diaphragm clutch," examiners expect: (i) labelled flywheel, (ii) labelled friction disc, (iii) **two distinct diaphragm springs** clearly shown as concentric cones, (iv) the intermediate fulcrum ring labelled, (v) release bearing direction arrow, and (vi) the clamping-force arrow on the pressure plate. Missing the fulcrum ring costs 2 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a multiple diaphragm clutch. How does it differ from a single diaphragm clutch?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A multiple diaphragm clutch is a friction clutch that employs **two (or more) Belleville-type diaphragm springs** arranged concentrically, so that the lever ratios of the individual springs **multiply together**, giving a higher axial clamping force than a single diaphragm of the same size.

**Differences:**
1. Two diaphragms instead of one (1 mark)
2. Compound (multiplicative) lever action via intermediate fulcrum ring (1 mark)
3. Higher torque capacity with same pedal effort / more progressive engagement (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the expression for the torque capacity of a multiple diaphragm clutch and define each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$T_{max} = n \cdot \mu \cdot F_s \cdot R_m$$
where:
- $n$ = number of diaphragm springs (1 mark)
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction of the lining (½ mark)
- $F_s$ = axial clamping force from one diaphragm (1 mark)
- $R_m$ = mean effective radius of the friction lining (½ mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 1 Internal Choice — Select ONE of the following:**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **multiple diaphragm clutch** used in heavy commercial vehicles. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Flywheel** — rotates with engine, provides driving face.
- **Friction disc** — driven member, splined to gearbox input shaft; carries friction linings on both faces.
- **Pressure plate** — bolted to clutch cover; pushes friction disc against flywheel when engaged.
- **Primary (outer) diaphragm spring** — large Belleville-type spring; bears against cover straps and provides main clamping force.
- **Secondary (inner) diaphragm spring** — smaller Belleville spring mounted concentrically inside the primary; its fingers are contacted by the release bearing.
- **Intermediate fulcrum ring** — links the two diaphragms mechanically; allows force to transmit from secondary to primary.
- **Release bearing** — slides on input shaft sleeve, pushed by release fork.
- **Retracting springs** — pull pressure plate back during disengagement.
- **Clutch cover** — sheet-metal housing bolted to flywheel.

**Working (4 marks):**
- *Engaged:* Diaphragms in their natural conical shape push the pressure plate against the friction disc; the disc is clamped between flywheel and pressure plate; torque flows engine → flywheel → friction disc → gearbox input shaft.
- *Disengaged:* Pedal pressed → hydraulic pressure pushes release fork → release bearing moves forward → contacts inner fingers of **secondary** diaphragm → secondary diaphragm flattens → its motion transfers via fulcrum ring to **primary** diaphragm → primary diaphragm flattens → pressure plate retracts → friction disc is released → engine disconnected.
- *Re-engagement:* Pedal released → retracting springs pull pressure plate back, release bearing retracts, both diaphragms re-expand into cone shape and re-clamp the friction disc.

**(b)** A multiple diaphragm clutch has two diaphragms with lever ratios $L_{r1} = 2.8$ and $L_{r2} = 2.4$. The friction linings have outer radius $R_o = 160\,\text{mm}$ and inner radius $R_i = 110\,\text{mm}$. The coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.3$ and the axial clamping force per diaphragm is $F_s = 1200\,\text{N}$. Calculate the **maximum torque** the clutch can transmit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total clamping force (two diaphragms):**
$F_c = n \cdot F_s = 2 \times 1200 = 2400\,\text{N}$ — (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Mean effective friction radius:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_m &= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{(0.160)^{3} - (0.110)^{3}}{(0.160)^{2} - (0.110)^{2}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{4.096 \times 10^{-3} - 1.331 \times 10^{-3}}{2.56 \times 10^{-2} - 1.21 \times 10^{-2}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{2.765 \times 10^{-3}}{1.35 \times 10^{-2}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2}{3} \times 0.2048 = 0.1365\,\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

— (3 marks)

**Step 3 — Maximum transmissible torque:**
$T_{max} = \mu \cdot F_c \cdot R_m = 0.3 \times 2400 \times 0.1365 = 98.3\,\text{N·m}$ — (2 marks)

**Step 4 — Verification of units:**
$[\mu][\text{N}][\text{m}] = [\text{N·m}]$ ✓ — (1 mark)

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{T_{max} \approx 98.3\,\text{N·m}}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **lever-ratio multiplication principle** in a multiple diaphragm clutch and derive the expression for the combined clamping force in terms of the finger force. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (3 marks):**
A single diaphragm spring acts as a **toggle lever** with lever ratio $L_r$. The lever ratio is the ratio of axial (clamping) force to the radial (finger) force. In a multiple diaphragm clutch, **two diaphragms are arranged coaxially** such that the axial motion of the secondary diaphragm's outer rim is mechanically coupled to the radial fingers of the primary diaphragm through an **intermediate fulcrum ring**.
