Because of this mechanical coupling, the lever ratios **multiply** rather than add.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Let $F_f$ be the operator's finger force at the release bearing.

Axial force from secondary diaphragm on the intermediate ring:
$$F_{s2} = L_{r2} \cdot F_f \quad \text{...(i)}$$

This force is transmitted (through the fulcrum ring) as a finger force on the primary diaphragm:
$$F_{f1} = F_{s2} = L_{r2} \cdot F_f \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Axial clamping force from primary diaphragm on the pressure plate:
$$F_{s1} = L_{r1} \cdot F_{f1} = L_{r1} \cdot L_{r2} \cdot F_f \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

Total clamping force (both diaphragms press simultaneously):
$$F_c = L_{r1} \cdot L_{r2} \cdot F_f = L_{total} \cdot F_f$$

$$\boxed{F_c = L_{total} \cdot F_f \quad \text{with} \quad L_{total} = L_{r1} \cdot L_{r2}}$$

**(b)** Compare a **single diaphragm clutch** with a **multiple diaphragm clutch** under the following heads: (i) torque capacity, (ii) pedal effort, (iii) complexity & cost, (iv) applications, (v) progressive engagement. **(7 marks — 1.4 marks each)**

**Model Solution (Tabular Comparison):**

| Parameter | Single Diaphragm | Multiple Diaphragm |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Torque Capacity** | Lower (one spring) | Higher ($n$-fold multiplied) |
| **(ii) Pedal Effort** | Higher for same torque | Lower for same torque |
| **(iii) Complexity & Cost** | Simple, cheaper | More parts (fulcrum ring, 2nd spring), costlier |
| **(iv) Applications** | Passenger cars, light vehicles | Heavy trucks, buses, tractors, racing |
| **(v) Progressive Engagement** | Single-stage curve | Two-stage; secondary "tops up" progressively |

— (5 × 1.4 ≈ 7 marks awarded proportionally)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Multiple Diaphragm Clutch Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "series" with "parallel":** Board answers frequently wrongly state that two diaphragms *add* their lever ratios. The correct relation is **multiplicative** because the diaphragms are in mechanical series (compound leverage), not in force-parallel.
> 2. **Forgetting the intermediate fulcrum ring:** A diagram without the fulcrum ring labelled loses **2 marks** in a sketch question.
> 3. **Sign errors in $R_m$ formula:** Students often write $R_m = (R_o + R_i)/2$, which is the *arithmetic* mean. The correct expression for friction-clutch torque uses the **friction-weighted** mean radius given above.
> 4. **Not converting mm to m:** Numerical answers where radii are kept in mm in the final torque calculation get **zero** for the numerical part — unit conversion is checked strictly.
> 5. **Omitting the "n" factor:** Using $F_c = F_s$ (single-diaphragm formula) when $n = 2$ diaphragms are present loses 1 mark per occurrence.
> 6. **Direction of pedal force / release bearing:** The arrow on the release bearing in the diagram must point **away from the engine**, i.e., toward the gearbox. Reversing this is a frequent but mark-losing error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **multiple diaphragm clutch** uses **two (or more) Belleville springs** arranged **concentrically / coaxially** to **multiply** the axial clamping force.
- ✅ The multiplication is through **compound leverage**, with the **intermediate fulcrum ring** as the mechanical link.
- ✅ Combined lever ratio: $L_{total} = L_{r1} \cdot L_{r2}$ (multiplicative, **not** additive).
- ✅ Clamping force: $F_c = L_{total} \cdot F_f = n \cdot F_s$ where $n$ is the number of diaphragms.
- ✅ Torque capacity: $T_{max} = n \cdot \mu \cdot F_s \cdot R_m$ with $R_m = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_o^{3} - R_i^{3}}{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}$.
- ✅ Effective spring rate for compound linkage: $k_{eff} = \dfrac{k_1 k_2}{k_1 + k_2}$ (softer → more pedal travel).
- ✅ The release bearing **only** contacts the **secondary (inner) diaphragm's fingers**; the primary diaphragm is actuated via the fulcrum ring.
- ✅ **Engaged state** = natural cone shape; **Disengaged state** = flattened diaphragms, retracted pressure plate.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Higher torque capacity, lower pedal effort, progressive engagement, compact packaging.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Higher cost, more parts, more complex assembly, slightly more pedal travel.
- ✅ **Applications:** Heavy commercial vehicles (trucks/buses), tractors, racing cars, high-torque motorcycles.
- ✅ **Always convert mm → m** in numerical torque calculations; **always state units** in final answer.
- ✅ **Draw the fulcrum ring** in any sketch — it is the single most important identifying feature of a *multiple* (vs. *single*) diaphragm clutch.
- ✅ Compare and contrast clearly with **single diaphragm** and **coil-spring clutches** when asked — KTU examiners love comparison tables.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi-Plate Hydraulically Operated Automatic Transmission Clutch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT413 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multi-Plate Hydraulically Operated Automatic Transmission Clutch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Multi-Plate Hydraulically Operated Automatic Transmission Clutch** is a friction-based torque-transmitting device used in modern automatic gearboxes that utilizes **multiple alternate driving and driven friction discs** (steel and friction-lined plates) immersed in automatic transmission fluid (ATF), with engagement and disengagement force generated by a **hydraulic actuator (piston-cylinder assembly)** that receives pressurized fluid from an **electronic/ hydraulic control valve body**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Term:** The system is classified as a *Wet Multi-Plate Clutch with Hydraulic Actuation* — the term **"wet"** refers to the fact that the clutch operates immersed in circulating transmission fluid, which provides cooling, lubrication, and smooth torque transfer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sandwich Press"

Imagine pressing a **stack of sandwiches** in a hydraulic sandwich press:

- The **bottom plate** = driving member (connected to engine/turbine).
- The **top plate** = driven member (connected to gearbox input shaft).
- **Alternating bread and filling** = alternating steel and friction plates (the "multi-plate" concept increases the number of friction surfaces).
- The **hydraulic ram pressing down** = hydraulic piston pushing the clutch pack together.
- The **hydraulic fluid** = automatic transmission fluid (ATF) supplied under regulated pressure.

When the press is OFF, the sandwiches (plates) can slide freely — the clutch is **disengaged**. When hydraulic pressure is applied, the ram clamps the stack together and **torque flows from bottom to top** — the clutch is **engaged**.

### 1.3 Why Multi-Plate in Automatic Transmissions?

A single-plate clutch of equivalent size cannot transmit sufficient torque within the limited **radial envelope (space)** of an automatic transmission. By stacking multiple plates, we multiply the effective friction surfaces without increasing the outer diameter of the clutch pack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Board Fact:** For a clutch pack with `n` alternate plates, the **number of active friction surfaces** = `n − 1` (e.g., 5 driven + 5 driving plates = **9 friction surfaces**). This is a *favourite 3-mark question* in KTU Automobile Engineering examinations.

### 1.4 Primary Engineering Functions

- **Transmit engine torque** from the input member to the gearbox input shaft smoothly.
- **Slip during engagement** to absorb torsional shock and damp vibrations.
- **Provide controlled, modulated clamping force** via hydraulic pressure for smooth, automated gear shifts without driver intervention.
- **Self-cool and self-lubricate** because of ATF circulation — allowing higher sustained slip energy.

### 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics Used in KTU Problems

| Metric | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Number of friction surfaces (n) | 3 to 12 (typically 5–10 in modern ATs) |
| Coefficient of friction (μ) — wet (ATF cooled) | **0.08 to 0.15** |
| Hydraulic actuation pressure | **3 to 8 bar** (line pressure up to 20 bar) |
| Plate material | Steel (driving) + Paper-based or woven-fibre friction lining (driven) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy (Component-by-Component Breakdown)

A wet multi-plate hydraulic clutch pack consists of the following critical components:

1. **Clutch Drum / Housing (Outer Member)** — A cylindrical shell that is splined to one rotating member (e.g., input shaft or sun gear). It carries the **outer splines** that engage with the **driving steel plates**.
2. **Hub / Inner Member** — A splined inner shaft that carries the **driven friction plates** (lined with friction material) on its splines.
3. **Friction Plates (Driven Plates)** — Thin steel plates coated on both faces with a porous, oil-resistant friction material (e.g., cellulose fibre, graphite-impregnated paper, or sintered bronze). Splined to the **hub**.
4. **Steel Separator Plates (Driving Plates)** — Smooth, flat steel plates (often with surface grooves for oil evacuation). Splined to the **drum/housing**.
5. **Apply Piston (Hydraulic Actuator)** — A sealed piston housed in a cylinder bore, usually located at one end of the clutch drum. Pressurized ATF acts on its face to generate the clamping force.
6. **Return Springs** — Coil or diaphragm springs that retract the piston and release the clutch pack when hydraulic pressure is removed.
7. **Sealing Rings** — Elastomeric O-rings/D-rings that seal the piston against fluid leakage.
8. **Snap Ring / Retainer** — Holds the clutch pack axially within the drum.
9. **Drain-back Orifices & Oil Feed Passages** — Allow ATF to enter the clutch pack to cool/lubricate it, and to drain quickly during disengagement for fast release.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Engagement Sequence

**Step 1 — Idle / Disengaged State**
- The transmission control module (TCM) or hydraulic valve body routes line pressure **away** from the apply piston.
- Return springs push the piston back.
- ATF circulates **between** the plates; the pack is loose and slips freely. **No torque is transmitted.**

**Step 2 — Fill Phase**
- The valve body directs pressurized ATF into the apply chamber.
- The piston slowly moves to take up the small clearance between plates (called the **"piston stroke"** or **"take-up"**). Pressure at this point is just enough to move the piston — called **"stroke pressure"** or **"line pressure × apply area − spring force"**.
- The plates are squeezed together with very low force; small **drag torque** exists.

**Step 3 — Apply Phase (Full Engagement)**
- Line pressure rises to its regulated value (modulated by a **pulse-width-modulated solenoid valve** in the valve body).
- The piston exerts a large axial clamping force `F` on the clutch pack.
- All plates are pressed together; friction surfaces lock. **Full engine torque is transmitted** to the gearbox input.

**Step 4 — Holding / Torque Transfer Phase**
- The TCM maintains the required clamping pressure to keep the pack locked.
- A small amount of controlled slip is permissible during shift transition (called **"slip-to-lock"** modulation) for smoothness.

**Step 5 — Release Phase**
- Line pressure is exhausted (vented to sump).
- Return springs retract the piston.
- Centrifugal force throws ATF out of the clutch drum via drain orifices.
- The pack is loose; **clutch is disengaged** — ready for the next gear.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `F = P × A_p` | Hydraulic clamping force = Pressure × Piston area | `F` in N, `P` in N/m², `A_p` in m² |
| 2 | `T = (n − 1) × μ × F × R_e` | Torque capacity (uniform wear — most used in AT) | `T` in N·m, `n` = number of plates |
| 3 | `R_e = (2/3) × (R³ − r³) / (R² − r²)` | Effective friction radius (uniform pressure) | `R` outer, `r` inner radius in m |
| 4 | `R_e = (R + r) / 2` | Effective friction radius (uniform wear) | `R` outer, `r` inner radius in m |
| 5 | `P_line × A_piston > F_spring + F_required` | Apply condition (must overcome spring + torque demand) | All in N |
| 6 | `τ_max = (n − 1) × μ × p × R_e` | Torque capacity using **uniform pressure `p`** | `p` = `F/A_plate` in Pa |
| 7 | `W_dot = T × ω × s` | Power dissipation during slip = Torque × angular velocity × slip ratio | `W_dot` in W, `ω` in rad/s |
| 8 | `Q_heat = m_dot × c_p × ΔT` | Heat absorbed by ATF (cooling requirement) | `Q` in W, `m_dot` in kg/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** For all multi-plate clutch problems in the Automobile Engineering course, KTU boards **default to the "uniform wear" assumption** because real clutch plates wear progressively to match the linear wear distribution. Always use `R_e = (R + r)/2` unless the question explicitly states "uniform pressure".

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smooth shift quality:** Hydraulic modulation allows infinitely variable clamping force, eliminating the jerky "step" feel of manual clutches.
- **Compact high-torque capacity:** A wet multi-plate pack of 100 mm diameter can transmit **>300 N·m** — equal to a dry single-plate clutch of 240 mm diameter.
- **Self-cooling for continuous slip:** During hill-launch or stop-and-go traffic, the wet design dissipates heat into the ATF sump.
- **Electronic control integration:** The TCM can apply precise PWM signals to the pressure-regulating solenoid, enabling **adaptive shift mapping** and **torque-on-demand lock-up**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Axial Clamping Force Generated by Hydraulic Piston

**Given:** Hydraulic line pressure `P` (Pa) acts uniformly on the apply piston of effective cross-sectional area `A_p` (m²). Return spring exerts a counter-force `F_s` (N).

**Find:** Net axial clamping force `F` transmitted to the clutch pack.

**Step 1:** Write the basic force equilibrium on the piston.

The piston is in static equilibrium when engaged; the net force pushing the piston toward the clutch pack equals hydraulic push minus spring return.

$$
F_{net} = P \cdot A_p - F_s
$$

**Step 2:** The net force on the piston equals the axial clamping force applied to the clutch pack (assuming frictionless seal friction is negligible, or that seal friction is included in `F_s`).

$$
F = P \cdot A_p - F_s
$$

**Step 3:** For typical AT systems, return spring force is small (often 30–80 N) compared to hydraulic force, so for a first-order analysis the simplified form is used.

$$
F \approx P \cdot A_p
$$

> **Conversion Logic for Step 3:** This simplification is valid because the **line pressure × area** product at 4–6 bar on a 30–50 cm² piston yields thousands of newtons — two orders of magnitude above the spring preload. KTU board solutions often use this approximation unless spring data is explicitly given.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque Capacity of a Multi-Plate Wet Clutch (Uniform Wear Theory)

**Given:** A clutch pack with `n` plates (alternate driving/driven) having outer radius `R` and inner radius `r`, operating in oil with coefficient of friction `μ`, under axial clamping force `F`.

**Find:** Maximum transmissible torque `T`.

**Step 1:** Establish the number of active friction surfaces.

Each contact zone between a steel plate and a friction plate is one friction surface. For `n` alternate plates, the number of contact zones is:

$$
N_{surfaces} = n - 1
$$

**Step 2:** State the elemental friction torque on an annular ring of radius `x` and width `dx`.

Consider a small ring at radius `x`, with area `dA = 2πx · dx`. The normal pressure on this ring, under uniform wear (pressure is inversely proportional to radius), is:

$$
p(x) = \frac{C}{x}
$$

where `C` is a constant determined by the total axial force.

**Step 3:** Apply the total axial force equilibrium to find `C`.

$$
F = \int_{r}^{R} p(x) \cdot dA = \int_{r}^{R} \frac{C}{x} \cdot 2πx \, dx = 2πC(R - r)
$$

Solving for the constant `C`:

$$
C = \frac{F}{2π(R - r)}
$$

**Step 4:** Write the elemental friction torque from the annular ring.

$$
dT = μ \cdot p(x) \cdot dA \cdot x = μ \cdot \frac{C}{x} \cdot 2πx \, dx \cdot x = 2πμ C \cdot x \, dx
$$

**Step 5:** Integrate from `r` to `R` to get the torque per friction surface.

$$
T_{one} = \int_{r}^{R} 2πμ C \cdot x \, dx = 2πμ C \cdot \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_{r}^{R} = πμ C (R^2 - r^2)
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute `C` from Step 3.

$$
T_{one} = πμ \cdot \frac{F}{2π(R - r)} \cdot (R^2 - r^2) = \frac{μ F (R^2 - r^2)}{2(R - r)}
$$

**Step 7:** Factor using `R² − r² = (R − r)(R + r)`.

$$
T_{one} = \frac{μ F (R + r)}{2}
$$

**Step 8:** Multiply by the number of friction surfaces `(n − 1)` to obtain total torque capacity.

$$
T = (n - 1) \cdot \frac{μ F (R + r)}{2}
$$

**Final Closed-Form Result:**

$$
\boxed{T = (n - 1) \cdot μ \cdot F \cdot R_e, \quad \text{where} \quad R_e = \frac{R + r}{2}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic for Step 8:** This is the **canonical multi-plate clutch torque equation** appearing in nearly every KTU Automobile Engineering problem. The factor `(n − 1)` is what makes a multi-plate clutch superior — torque capacity scales **linearly** with plate count for the same axial force and plate dimensions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydraulic Pressure Required for a Given Torque

**Given:** A multi-plate wet clutch must transmit torque `T` (N·m) using `n` plates, `μ`, `R`, and `r`, with piston area `A_p`.

**Find:** Required line pressure `P`.

**Step 1:** Start from the torque equation derived above.

$$
T = (n - 1) \cdot μ \cdot F \cdot \frac{(R + r)}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the required axial clamping force `F`.

$$
F = \frac{2T}{(n - 1) \cdot μ \cdot (R + r)}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute `F = P · A_p` (ignoring spring force for steady-state engagement).

$$
P \cdot A_p = \frac{2T}{(n - 1) \cdot μ \cdot (R + r)}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for `P`.

$$
\boxed{P = \frac{2T}{(n - 1) \cdot μ \cdot (R + r) \cdot A_p}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic for Step 4:** This is a *reverse-design* equation. Given a target torque (say, peak engine torque), the KTU board examiner may ask: *"What line pressure must the hydraulic pump deliver to engage this clutch pack?"* The derivation chain is `T → F → P`.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A multi-plate wet clutch in an automatic transmission has **6 plates** (3 driving + 3 driven), outer radius `R = 75 mm`, inner radius `r = 55 mm`. The wet coefficient of friction is **μ = 0.12**, and the hydraulic apply piston has area `A_p = 35 cm²`. The line pressure available is `P = 5 bar`. The return spring force is `F_s = 60 N`.

**Find:** (a) Net axial clamping force; (b) Maximum transmissible torque.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Clamping force**

Convert units: `P = 5 bar = 5 × 10⁵ N/m²`, `A_p = 35 × 10⁻⁴ m² = 3.5 × 10⁻³ m²`.

$$
F = P \cdot A_p - F_s = (5 \times 10^{5}) \times (3.5 \times 10^{-3}) - 60
$$

$$
F = 1750 - 60 = 1690 \, \text{N}
$$

**Part (b) — Torque capacity**

Active friction surfaces `n − 1 = 5`. Effective radius `R_e = (R + r)/2 = (0.075 + 0.055)/2 = 0.065 m`.

$$
T = (n - 1) \cdot μ \cdot F \cdot R_e = 5 \times 0.12 \times 1690 \times 0.065
$$

$$
T = 65.91 \, \text{N·m}
$$

> **Final Answer:** `F ≈ 1690 N` and `T ≈ 65.91 N·m`.

---

### 3.5 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix — Uniform Pressure vs Uniform Wear

| Aspect | Uniform Pressure Theory | Uniform Wear Theory (KTU default) |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure distribution `p(x)` | Constant across radius | `p(x) ∝ 1/x` (higher at inner radius) |
| Effective radius `R_e` | `(2/3) × (R³ − r³)/(R² − r²)` | `(R + r)/2` |
| Use case | **New clutch** at first engagement | **Worn clutch** in steady-state service |
| KTU preference | Used when question says "uniform pressure" | **Default for Automobile Engineering problems** |
| Torque magnitude | Slightly lower (for typical geometry) | Slightly higher (predicts the actual maximum sustainable torque) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Avoidance:** If the question gives `R` and `r` and asks for `R_e` *without* specifying the theory, use `(R + r)/2`. This single decision is worth **2 marks** in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hydraulic Multi-Plate Clutch System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of signals, fluid, and mechanical power through the system — from the driver's torque demand to the actual transmission of torque at the clutch pack.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    driverA[Driver Accelerator Pedal] --> tcmA[Transmission Control Module TCM]
    speedA[Vehicle Speed Sensor] --> tcmA
    rpmA[Engine RPM Sensor] --> tcmA
    tcmA -->|PWM Signal| solA[Pressure Control Solenoid]
    pumpA[Hydraulic Pump Driven by Engine] -->|High Pressure ATF| lineA[Line Pressure Circuit]
    solA -->|Modulates Pressure| lineA
    lineA -->|Regulated Fluid| pistonA[Apply Piston in Clutch Drum]
    pistonA -->|Axial Clamping Force F| packA[Multi-Plate Clutch Pack]
    packA -->|Torque Transmission| gearA[Planetary Gearset Input]
    springA[Return Springs] -->|Retracts When Vented| pistonA
    packA -->|Heat Generation| atfA[ATF Circulation and Cooling]
    atfA -->|Returns to Sump| pumpA
    tcmA -.->|Feedback Slip Monitoring| packA
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** = primary flow of fluid, force, or torque.
- **Dotted arrow** = electronic feedback (slip detection via turbine speed sensor).
- The **closed loop** of ATF (Pump → Clutch → Sump → Pump) shows the wet cooling circuit.
- The **TCM** is the brain that closes the slip-control loop.

---

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Functional Stack Diagram (Mermaid Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Drum1[Clutch Drum Outer Member]
        dir1[Driving Steel Plate 1]
        drv1[Driven Friction Plate 1]
        dir2[Driving Steel Plate 2]
        drv2[Driven Friction Plate 2]
        dir3[Driving Steel Plate 3]
        drv3[Driven Friction Plate 3]
    end
    piston1[Hydraulic Apply Piston] -->|Axial Force| dir1
    spring1[Return Spring] -.->|Retracts| piston1
    inlet1[ATF Inlet Port] -->|Pressurized Fluid| piston1
    drain1[Centrifugal Drain Orifices] -->|Releases ATF| sump1[ATF Sump]
    sump1 -.->|Re-pressurized| inlet1
    drv3 --> output1[Torque Output to Gearbox Input Shaft]
    dir1 --> output1
```

> **Interpretation:** The stack `dir → drv → dir → drv …` shows the *alternating* arrangement essential for multi-plate operation. The piston is at the **left end** of the drum, the output shaft connection is at the **right end** of the hub.

---

### 4.3 Force and Torque Flow Sankey-Style Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pP[Hydraulic Pressure P] -->|times Area A_p| fA[Axial Clamping Force F]
    fA -->|times Friction Coefficient mu| fT[Friction Traction at each Surface]
    fT -->|times Effective Radius R_e| tT[Engine Torque Transmitted T]
    fT -->|times Slip Velocity| hG[Heat Generation in ATF]
    pP -.->|minus Spring Force| fA
```

**Reading:** This is a **cause-to-effect chain** showing how a *fluid pressure signal* (input from the valve body) is transformed, through the *geometric* and *material* properties of the clutch, into the *mechanical torque* the gearbox needs to propel the vehicle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`**
**State the function of a multi-plate clutch in an automatic transmission. Why is a hydraulic actuator preferred over a mechanical lever-type actuator for engaging the clutch?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A multi-plate clutch in an automatic transmission **transmits engine torque from the input member to the planetary gearset** by clamping multiple alternate friction and steel plates together, using friction as the coupling medium.

A **hydraulic actuator is preferred** for the following reasons:

1. **Smooth, infinitely variable clamping force:** Hydraulic pressure can be modulated continuously by a control solenoid, enabling *creep control* and *smooth shift engagement* — a mechanical lever gives only discrete, abrupt actuation.
2. **Self-lubrication and cooling:** The hydraulic fluid (ATF) also serves as the lubricant and coolant for the wet clutch pack, removing frictional heat.
3. **Compact packaging:** Hydraulic pistons deliver very high force in a small radial envelope — ideal for the confined space inside a transmission housing.
4. **Automatic operation:** Allows *clutch-by-wire* and *adaptive shift logic* via the TCM, which a manual lever cannot provide.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**A wet multi-plate clutch operates with `μ = 0.10` and has 7 plates with outer radius 60 mm and inner radius 40 mm. If the axial clamping force is 1200 N, calculate the torque transmitted. Assume uniform wear.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: `μ = 0.10`, `n = 7` plates → friction surfaces = `n − 1 = 6`, `R = 0.060 m`, `r = 0.040 m`, `F = 1200 N`.

**Step 1:** Effective radius (uniform wear):

$$
R_e = \frac{R + r}{2} = \frac{0.060 + 0.040}{2} = 0.050 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 2:** Torque capacity:

$$
T = (n - 1) \cdot μ \cdot F \cdot R_e = 6 \times 0.10 \times 1200 \times 0.050 = 36 \, \text{N·m}
$$

**Final Answer:** `T = 36 N·m`.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often **forget the `(n − 1)` factor** and use `n` directly, leading to a **3-mark deduction** in Part B.
> 2. Mixing up **uniform pressure** and **uniform wear** effective radii is the **single most common** error — re-read the question stem carefully.
> 3. Failing to **convert bar → Pa and mm → m** before substituting into formulas costs a full **2 marks** for unit consistency.
> 4. Skipping a **neat free-body diagram** of the piston can cost **1 mark** even if the math is correct — KTU examiners reward presentation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a multi-plate hydraulically operated automatic transmission clutch**. List the components and describe the engagement sequence in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wet multi-plate clutch has **8 plates** (4 steel + 4 friction) with outer radius `R = 80 mm` and inner radius `r = 50 mm`. The wet coefficient of friction is `μ = 0.11`. The hydraulic apply piston has diameter `d = 70 mm` and the line pressure available is `P = 6 bar`. The return spring exerts a force of `80 N`.

Determine:
  (i) The net axial clamping force on the clutch pack.
  (ii) The maximum torque transmissible by the clutch, assuming uniform wear theory.
  (iii) The minimum line pressure required to transmit a torque of `250 N·m`. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:** A multi-plate hydraulically operated automatic transmission clutch consists of the following components:

1. **Clutch Drum (Outer Member):** A cylindrical shell with internal splines that engage the **driving steel plates**. The drum is connected to the engine input shaft (or turbine in a torque-converter-equipped AT).
2. **Hub (Inner Member):** A splined shaft carrying the **driven friction plates** (lined with friction material).
3. **Clutch Pack:** Alternating stack of `n` plates — `n/2` steel plates (driving) and `n/2` friction plates (driven).
4. **Apply Piston:** A hydraulic piston housed in a cylinder bore at one end of the drum, sealed by O-rings/D-rings.
5. **Return Springs:** Coil or diaphragm springs that retract the piston when hydraulic pressure is vented.
6. **Snap Ring / Retainer:** Holds the clutch pack axially inside the drum.
7. **ATF Inlet and Drain Orifices:** For oil circulation.

**Working (Engagement Sequence):**

- **Disengaged State:** Line pressure is vented. Return springs hold the piston back. ATF circulates freely between the plates. The pack is loose and slips.
- **Fill Phase:** The TCM commands a pressure-control solenoid to apply low line pressure. The piston slowly moves to take up the clearance between plates. Drag torque begins to develop.
- **Apply Phase:** Line pressure is increased to its regulated value. The piston exerts a large axial force, squeezing all plates together. Friction surfaces lock and full torque is transmitted.
- **Release Phase:** Line pressure is exhausted; the spring retracts the piston; centrifugal force drains the ATF out of the drum; the pack is loose again.

> **Examiner's note:** Drawing a labelled schematic earns **2 of the 7 marks**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** `n = 8` plates → `(n − 1) = 7` friction surfaces; `R = 0.080 m`; `r = 0.050 m`; `μ = 0.11`; piston diameter `d = 0.070 m`; `P = 6 × 10⁵ N/m²`; `F_s = 80 N`.

**(i) Net Axial Clamping Force:**

$$
A_p = \frac{π d^2}{4} = \frac{π (0.070)^2}{4} = 3.848 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2
$$

$$
F = P \cdot A_p - F_s = (6 \times 10^5)(3.848 \times 10^{-3}) - 80 = 2309 - 80 = 2229 \, \text{N}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Torque (Uniform Wear):**

$$
R_e = \frac{R + r}{2} = \frac{0.080 + 0.050}{2} = 0.065 \, \text{m}
$$

$$
T = (n - 1) \cdot μ \cdot F \cdot R_e = 7 \times 0.11 \times 2229 \times 0.065
$$

$$
T = 111.55 \, \text{N·m}
$$

**(iii) Minimum Line Pressure for T = 250 N·m:**

Use the reverse-design formula:

$$
F_{req} = \frac{2T}{(n - 1) \cdot μ \cdot (R + r)} = \frac{2 \times 250}{7 \times 0.11 \times (0.080 + 0.050)}
$$

$$
F_{req} = \frac{500}{7 \times 0.11 \times 0.130} = \frac{500}{0.1001} = 4995 \, \text{N}
$$

Required line pressure (including spring override):

$$
P_{req} = \frac{F_{req} + F_s}{A_p} = \frac{4995 + 80}{3.848 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{5075}{3.848 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
P_{req} = 1.319 \times 10^6 \, \text{N/m}^2 \approx 13.19 \, \text{bar}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) `F ≈ 2229 N`; (ii) `T ≈ 111.55 N·m`; (iii) `P_req ≈ 13.2 bar`.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]` — **Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **uniform pressure** and **uniform wear** theories used in the design of multi-plate clutches. Derive the expression for the **effective friction radius** under both theories. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A multi-plate wet clutch is required to transmit a torque of **400 N·m**. The plate dimensions are `R = 90 mm` and `r = 60 mm`. The wet coefficient of friction is `μ = 0.10`. The hydraulic apply piston has area `A_p = 40 cm²` and the return spring force is `F_s = 100 N`. The number of plates is `n = 6`. Determine:
  (i) The required axial clamping force.
  (ii) The required line pressure.
  (iii) Compare the result if **uniform pressure** theory is used instead of uniform wear. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Theory Derivations (7 marks):**

**Uniform Pressure Theory:**
In this theory, the normal pressure `p` is assumed to be **constant** across the entire contact surface. This is the case at the moment of first engagement, when the plate surfaces are new and not yet worn.

For uniform pressure `p` over the annular contact area, the total axial force is:

$$
F = p \cdot π (R^2 - r^2)
$$

The elemental torque is `dT = μ · p · (2πx dx) · x = 2πμ p x^2 dx`. Integrating from `r` to `R`:

$$
T_{one} = 2πμ p \int_{r}^{R} x^2 \, dx = \frac{2πμ p}{3}(R^3 - r^3)
$$

Substituting `p = F / [π(R² − r²)]`:

$$
T_{one} = \frac{2μ F}{3} \cdot \frac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2}
$$

Effective radius:

$$
R_e = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2}
$$

**Uniform Wear Theory:**
After prolonged use, the clutch plate wears such that the **rate of wear is uniform** at every point. Since wear rate is proportional to `(p · v) = (p · ωx)`, this implies `p · x = constant`. Hence `p(x) = C/x`.

Following the derivation in Section 3.2 above, the effective radius is:

$$
R_e = \frac{R + r}{2}
$$

**Comparison:**

| Theory | Pressure `p(x)` | Effective Radius | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform pressure | `p = constant` | `(2/3)(R³ − r³)/(R² − r²)` | New clutch |
| Uniform wear | `p ∝ 1/x` | `(R + r)/2` | **Worn clutch (real design basis)** |

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** `T = 400 N·m`; `R = 0.090 m`; `r = 0.060 m`; `μ = 0.10`; `A_p = 40 × 10⁻⁴ = 4.0 × 10⁻³ m²`; `F_s = 100 N`; `n = 6`.

**(i) Required Axial Clamping Force (Uniform Wear):**

$$
F = \frac{2T}{(n - 1) \cdot μ \cdot (R + r)} = \frac{2 \times 400}{5 \times 0.10 \times (0.090 + 0.060)} = \frac{800}{5 \times 0.10 \times 0.150}
$$

$$
F = \frac{800}{0.075} = 10{,}667 \, \text{N}
$$

**(ii) Required Line Pressure:**

$$
P = \frac{F + F_s}{A_p} = \frac{10{,}667 + 100}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{10{,}767}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.692 \times 10^6 \, \text{N/m}^2
$$

$$
P \approx 26.92 \, \text{bar}
$$

**(iii) Comparison Using Uniform Pressure Theory:**

For uniform pressure:

$$
R_e = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{(0.090)^3 - (0.060)^3}{(0.090)^2 - (0.060)^2}
$$

Compute numerator: `0.090³ = 7.29 × 10⁻⁴`, `0.060³ = 2.16 × 10⁻⁴`, difference = `5.13 × 10⁻⁴`.
Compute denominator: `0.090² = 8.1 × 10⁻³`, `0.060² = 3.6 × 10⁻³`, difference = `4.5 × 10⁻³`.

$$
R_e = \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{5.13 \times 10^{-4}}{4.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.1140 = 0.0760 \, \text{m}
$$

Required force:

$$
F_{UP} = \frac{T}{(n - 1) \cdot μ \cdot R_e} = \frac{400}{5 \times 0.10 \times 0.0760} = \frac{400}{0.038} = 10{,}526 \, \text{N}
$$

Required line pressure:

$$
P_{UP} = \frac{F_{UP} + F_s}{A_p} = \frac{10{,}526 + 100}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.657 \times 10^6 \, \text{N/m}^2 \approx 26.57 \, \text{bar}
$$

**Comparison Summary:**

| Parameter | Uniform Wear | Uniform Pressure |
|---|---|---|
| `R_e` | `0.0750 m` | `0.0760 m` |
| `F` required | `10,667 N` | `10,526 N` |
| `P` required | `26.92 bar` | `26.57 bar` |
| Difference | Higher force | Lower force (~1.3% less) |

> **Conclusion:** For typical plate geometries, the two theories yield very close results (within 1–2%), but **uniform wear theory is preferred for design** because it represents the actual long-term operating condition.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Memorize These:**

- [x] **Number of friction surfaces = `n − 1`** for `n` alternate plates in a clutch pack. (Most-forgotten step in exams.)
- [x] **Effective radius (uniform wear):** `R_e = (R + r)/2` — *KTU default*.
- [x] **Effective radius (uniform pressure):** `R_e = (2/3) × (R³ − r³)/(R² − r²)`.
- [x] **Master Torque Equation:** `T = (n − 1) × μ × F × R_e`.
- [x] **Hydraulic Clamping Force:** `F = P × A_piston − F_spring`.
- [x] **Reverse-Design Pressure Equation:** `P = 2T / [(n − 1) × μ × (R + r) × A_p]`.
- [x] **"Wet" clutch** = clutch pack immersed in ATF → cooling, lubrication, smoother engagement, lower μ (0.08–0.15).
- [x] **Components to draw in any diagram question:** Clutch drum, hub, steel plates, friction plates, apply piston, return spring, snap ring, oil inlet/drain.
- [x] **Engagement sequence:** Disengaged → Fill → Apply → Hold → Release.
- [x] **TCM controls** the pressure-control solenoid using **PWM signals** based on engine load, vehicle speed, and turbine speed.
- [x] **Advantages of hydraulic actuation:** smooth modulation, compact, self-lubricating, electronically controllable, suitable for automatic operation.
- [x] **Unit conversions to memorize:** `1 bar = 10⁵ N/m²`; `1 cm² = 10⁻⁴ m²`; `1 mm = 10⁻³ m`.
- [x] **For high-torque applications,** use **more plates** (not larger plates) — multi-plate geometry multiplies torque capacity by `(n − 1)` within the same envelope.
- [x] **Slip energy** is dissipated as heat in the ATF — cooling circuit (pump → cooler → sump) is critical for durability.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Multi-Plate Hydraulically Operated Automatic Transmission Clutch**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — Outcome-Based Education (OBE) / NEP 2020 Aligned*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semi-Centrifugal Clutch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT413 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Semi-Centrifugal Clutch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Semi-Centrifugal Clutch** is a friction clutch that transmits torque through a **combination of spring force (axial pressure) at low engine speeds and centrifugal force of weighted shoes at high engine speeds**. It is essentially a hybrid of a *single-plate friction clutch* and a *centrifugal clutch*, designed to enhance torque transmission capacity as the engine speed increases, without requiring excessively strong springs at low speeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Clutches):**
> The semi-centrifugal clutch is one of the most exam-relevant clutch types under KTU's *Clutch classification and design* unit. It tests the student's ability to integrate **axial friction mechanics** with **radial centrifugal dynamics** — a classic KTU board favourite.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine you are riding a bicycle up a hill. At the bottom (low pedaling speed), you don't need much force on the pedals. As you climb faster, your legs need to push harder because the hill requires more effort.

The **semi-centrifugal clutch behaves similarly**:

- At **low RPM** → the **springs** alone press the friction plate against the flywheel (gentle engagement, smooth starts).
- At **high RPM** → **centrifugal weights** fly outward, and through a **link-lever mechanism**, they *add* extra axial pressure on the friction plate (extra grip when the engine is producing high power).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:**
> "A semi-centrifugal clutch is a *smart* clutch. It uses light spring pressure for smooth take-off, but as the engine speeds up, it automatically increases its grip using centrifugal force — just like your legs naturally press harder when the bike goes uphill."

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Spring Force (C)** | Axial force pressing the friction plate at rest/low speed |
| **Centrifugal Shoe (Weight)** | A pivoted mass that flies outward at high rotational speed |
| **Link / Lever Mechanism** | Converts the *radial* outward motion of the shoe into *axial* pressure on the pressure plate |
| **Critical Speed (ωₛ)** | The angular velocity at which centrifugal force just overcomes spring force |
| **Mean Friction Radius (Rₘ)** | Average radius of the friction lining = (Rₒ + Rᵢ)/2 |
| **Mechanical Advantage (a)** | Ratio of axial thrust produced per unit radial centrifugal force, depending on link geometry |

> [!NOTE]
> The **centrifugal force** here is a *pseudo-force* observed in the rotating reference frame of the clutch. In the inertial frame, it is the inertia of the shoe mass resisting the change in direction — it is NOT a real force but the result of Newton's First Law applied to circular motion.

## 1.4 Why Not Pure Centrifugal or Pure Spring Clutch?

| Clutch Type | Low-Speed Engagement | High-Speed Capacity | Smoothness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-Plate (Spring)** | Excellent | Limited (spring fixed) | Excellent |
| **Pure Centrifugal** | Poor (no contact at idle) | Excellent | Poor at start |
| **Semi-Centrifugal** | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The semi-centrifugal clutch **inherits the smooth low-speed behaviour of a spring clutch** and **the high-torque capability of a centrifugal clutch** — the best of both worlds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details

A semi-centrifugal clutch consists of the following main parts:

1. **Flywheel** — mounted on the engine crankshaft; provides the driving friction surface.
2. **Pressure Plate (Driving Member)** — bolted to the flywheel via clutch springs.
3. **Friction Disc / Driven Plate** — splined to the gearbox input shaft; carries the friction lining.
4. **Clutch Springs** — placed circumferentially between the cover and pressure plate; provide the *base axial force* at low speeds.
5. **Centrifugal Weights (Shoes)** — pivoted on the pressure plate (or on levers attached to it). Free to move outward under centrifugal force.
6. **Links / Levers / Toggles** — transmit the radial motion of the weights into additional axial thrust on the pressure plate.
7. **Stop Pins / Adjustable Screws** — limit the outward movement of the shoes.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

- **At Engine Idle (ω low):**
  - Centrifugal force on the shoes is *less* than the inward spring force holding them in.
  - The shoes remain in the retracted (inward) position.
  - The **clutch springs alone** press the pressure plate onto the friction disc.
  - Engagement is smooth, slip is minimal, and the vehicle can crawl without jerking.

- **At Increasing Engine Speed (ω increases):**
  - The centrifugal force $F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ grows **quadratically** with speed.
  - Once $F_c$ equals the inward spring pull, the shoes begin to *pivot outward*.

- **At High Engine Speed (ω > ωₛ):**
  - The shoes press hard against the stop pins.
  - Through the link-lever system, each shoe exerts an **additional axial thrust** on the pressure plate.
  - This extra force **adds to** the spring force, dramatically increasing the frictional grip.
  - Torque capacity rises automatically, matching the rising engine torque.

- **During Declutching (Pedal Pressed):**
  - The pressure plate is pulled back axially.
  - The shoes are released, springs retract the weights inward.
  - Friction disc is disengaged, and the gearbox is interrupted.

## 2.3 Force Analysis (Core "Why" and "How")

For **each centrifugal shoe**, three forces act:

1. **Centrifugal force (radially outward):**
$$F_c = \frac{W}{g} \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$
   - where $W$ is the weight of the shoe (N), $r$ is the radial distance of its CG from the shaft axis (m), and $\omega$ is the angular speed (rad/s).

2. **Inward spring force (C):** Constant, designed to keep the shoe in at low speeds.

3. **Inward pull from the lever/articulation point** during the engagement phase.

**Critical (Threshold) Condition** — the speed at which the shoe just begins to move outward:
$$F_c = C \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{W \cdot r \cdot \omega_s^{\,2}}{g} = C$$

Solving for the critical speed:
$$\boxed{\omega_s = \sqrt{\frac{C \cdot g}{W \cdot r}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below $\omega_s$, the shoe stays in (only spring engagement).
> Above $\omega_s$, the shoe flies out, and the link mechanism converts the *excess* centrifugal force into *additional axial thrust* on the pressure plate.

## 2.4 Axial Thrust Generation (Link Mechanism)

When each shoe moves outward by a small radial distance, the connecting link transmits this motion to the pressure plate. The mechanical advantage of the link system, denoted by $a$, defines how much axial force is produced per unit of (F_c − C):

$$F_{axial,\,shoe} = a \cdot (F_c - C) \quad \text{for } F_c > C$$

If there are $N$ shoes, the **total additional axial thrust** is:

$$F_{add} = N \cdot a \cdot (F_c - C) = N \cdot a \cdot \left( \frac{W \cdot r \cdot \omega^2}{g} - C \right)$$

The **total axial force on the friction plate** becomes:

$$\boxed{F_{total} = F_{spring} + N \cdot a \cdot \left( \frac{W \cdot r \cdot \omega^2}{g} - C \right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Here $F_{spring}$ is the effective axial force delivered by the clutch springs alone. The term inside the bracket is **zero** below $\omega_s$ and **positive** above it.

## 2.5 Torque Capacity

Once $F_{total}$ is known, the torque transmitted by the friction surface is:

$$\boxed{T = \mu \cdot F_{total} \cdot R_m \cdot n_s}$$

where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (typically **0.25 to 0.4** for organic/cork linings)
- $R_m$ = mean radius of friction lining = $(R_o + R_i)/2$
- $n_s$ = number of friction surfaces (usually **2** for single-plate)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centrifugal force per shoe | $F_c = \dfrac{W \cdot r \cdot \omega^2}{g}$ | N | Always |
| 2 | Critical (threshold) speed | $\omega_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{C \cdot g}{W \cdot r}}$ | rad/s | $F_c = C$ |
| 3 | Speed for $F_c = C$ in rpm | $N_s = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{C \cdot g}{W \cdot r}}$ | rpm | Threshold |
| 4 | Excess radial force per shoe | $\Delta F = F_c - C$ | N | $F_c > C$ |
| 5 | Additional axial thrust (N shoes) | $F_{add} = N \cdot a \cdot (F_c - C)$ | N | $F_c > C$ |
| 6 | Total axial force | $F_{total} = F_{spring} + N \cdot a \cdot (F_c - C)$ | N | High speed |
| 7 | Friction torque | $T = \mu \cdot F_{total} \cdot R_m \cdot n_s$ | N·m | All speeds |
| 8 | Mean friction radius | $R_m = \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$ | m | Geometry |
| 9 | Torque below $\omega_s$ (spring only) | $T = \mu \cdot F_{spring} \cdot R_m \cdot n_s$ | N·m | Low speed |
| 10 | Torque above $\omega_s$ (spring + centrifugal) | $T = \mu \cdot [F_{spring} + N \cdot a \cdot (F_c - C)] \cdot R_m \cdot n_s$ | N·m | High speed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Marker Hint:** Always state *which speed regime* (below or above $\omega_s$) applies before picking the torque formula. Examiners specifically check this distinction in 7-mark sub-parts.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Motorcycles and Scooters** — The most common application. Allows smooth start at idle and prevents stalling; extra grip appears at higher revs.
- **Old-era Heavy Trucks and Buses** — Tata, Leyland, and Ashok vehicles used semi-centrifugal clutches before hydraulic assist became standard.
- **Stationary Engines / Pumps / Compressors** — Where the driven load increases with speed, semi-centrifugal design self-matches torque.
- **Race Cars (Historical)** — Used in pre-1960s race cars to provide a "boost" in clutch capacity without a heavy pedal effort.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Centrifugal Force on a Pivoted Shoe

**Step 1 — Set up the rotating reference frame:**
Consider a shoe of mass $m$ pivoted at a point on the rotating pressure plate. Its centre of gravity lies at a radial distance $r$ from the axis of rotation.

**Step 2 — Relate mass to weight:**
By Newton's second law applied in the rotating frame, the centrifugal (pseudo) force on a body of mass $m$ moving in a circle of radius $r$ at angular velocity $\omega$ is:
$$F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$

**Step 3 — Express mass in terms of weight:**
Weight $W = m \cdot g$, so $m = W / g$. Substituting:
$$F_c = \frac{W}{g} \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$

**Step 4 — Critical (threshold) condition:**
The shoe begins to move outward the instant the outward centrifugal force equals the inward spring force $C$:
$$F_c = C$$
$$\frac{W \cdot r \cdot \omega_s^{\,2}}{g} = C$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the critical angular speed:**
$$\omega_s^{\,2} = \frac{C \cdot g}{W \cdot r}$$
$$\boxed{\omega_s = \sqrt{\frac{C \cdot g}{W \cdot r}}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to rpm:**
$$N_s = \frac{60}{2\pi} \cdot \omega_s$$
$$\boxed{N_s = \frac{60}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{C \cdot g}{W \cdot r}} = 9.549 \sqrt{\frac{C \cdot g}{W \cdot r}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **quadratic dependence** of $F_c$ on $\omega$. This is why the clutch "kicks in" very quickly once a threshold is crossed — small RPM increases produce large increases in additional thrust.

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Axial Thrust on the Pressure Plate

**Step 1 — Identify the two contributors to axial force:**
- The **clutch springs** provide a constant force $F_{spring}$.
- The **shoes** (above $\omega_s$) provide an additional radial force $\Delta F = F_c - C$.

**Step 2 — Apply the mechanical advantage of the link:**
Each unit of excess centrifugal force $\Delta F$ produces an axial thrust of $a \cdot \Delta F$ on the pressure plate, where $a$ is the lever-arm ratio of the link mechanism.

**Step 3 — Sum over all shoes:**
For $N$ identical shoes:
$$F_{add} = N \cdot a \cdot \Delta F = N \cdot a \cdot (F_c - C)$$
$$F_{add} = N \cdot a \cdot \left( \frac{W \cdot r \cdot \omega^2}{g} - C \right)$$

**Step 4 — Add the spring contribution:**
$$F_{total} = F_{spring} + F_{add}$$
$$\boxed{F_{total} = F_{spring} + N \cdot a \cdot \left( \frac{W \cdot r \cdot \omega^2}{g} - C \right)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Torque Transmitted (Friction Mechanics)

**Step 1 — Friction force on a differential ring:**
A small ring of radius $r$, width $dr$, on the friction plate has area $2\pi r \, dr$. The pressure is uniform and equals $p = F_{total} / (2\pi R_m \cdot b)$ (where $b$ is the lining width).

**Step 2 — Friction force on the ring:**
$$dF = \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\mu \cdot F_{total}}{R_m \cdot b} \cdot r \, dr$$

**Step 3 — Friction torque on the ring:**
$$dT = r \cdot dF = \frac{\mu \cdot F_{total}}{R_m \cdot b} \cdot r^2 \, dr$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $R_i$ to $R_o$:**
$$T = \frac{\mu \cdot F_{total}}{R_m \cdot b} \int_{R_i}^{R_o} r^2 \, dr = \frac{\mu \cdot F_{total}}{R_m \cdot b} \cdot \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{3}$$

**Step 5 — Use the relation $R_o^3 - R_i^3 = (R_o - R_i)(R_o^2 + R_o R_i + R_i^2)$.** For uniform wear (which is the design assumption for clutches), the integral simplifies to:
$$\int_{R_i}^{R_o} r \, dr = \frac{R_o^2 - R_i^2}{2}$$
and the analysis yields the **uniform-pressure formula**:
$$\boxed{T = \mu \cdot F_{total} \cdot R_m \cdot n_s}$$
where $R_m = (R_o + R_i)/2$ and $n_s$ is the number of friction surfaces.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU Part B format):**

A semi-centrifugal clutch has **4 centrifugal shoes**, each weighing **15 N**, with its CG at a radius of **120 mm**. The inward spring force on each shoe is **25 N**, and the link mechanism has a mechanical advantage of **a = 1.5**. The friction lining has an outer radius of **150 mm**, inner radius of **75 mm**, coefficient of friction **μ = 0.3**, and there are **2 friction surfaces**. The spring delivers an axial force of **$F_{spring}$ = 1200 N** on the pressure plate.

**(a)** Find the critical speed at which the centrifugal effect begins.
**(b)** Find the total torque transmitted when the engine runs at **2400 rpm**.

---

### Solution — Part (a): Critical Speed

**Given:**
- $W = 15$ N, $r = 120$ mm $= 0.12$ m, $C = 25$ N, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Apply threshold formula:**
$$\omega_s = \sqrt{\frac{C \cdot g}{W \cdot r}}$$

**Substitute values:**
$$\omega_s = \sqrt{\frac{25 \times 9.81}{15 \times 0.12}}$$

**Compute numerator:** $25 \times 9.81 = 245.25$

**Compute denominator:** $15 \times 0.12 = 1.8$

$$\omega_s = \sqrt{\frac{245.25}{1.8}} = \sqrt{136.25}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_s \approx 11.67 \text{ rad/s} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N_s \approx 111.4 \text{ rpm}}$$

---

### Solution — Part (b): Torque at 2400 rpm

**Step 1 — Convert rpm to rad/s:**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 2400}{60} = 80\pi \approx 251.33 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Centrifugal force on one shoe:**
$$F_c = \frac{W \cdot r \cdot \omega^2}{g} = \frac{15 \times 0.12 \times (251.33)^2}{9.81}$$

**Compute $\omega^2$:**
$$(251.33)^2 = 63166.78$$

**Compute numerator:** $15 \times 0.12 \times 63166.78 = 113700.2$

**Compute $F_c$:**
$$F_c = \frac{113700.2}{9.81} \approx 11590.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Excess radial force per shoe:**
$$\Delta F = F_c - C = 11590.6 - 25 = 11565.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Additional axial thrust (4 shoes, a = 1.5):**
$$F_{add} = N \cdot a \cdot \Delta F = 4 \times 1.5 \times 11565.6 = 69393.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Total axial force on the friction plate:**
$$F_{total} = F_{spring} + F_{add} = 1200 + 69393.6 = 70593.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Mean friction radius:**
$$R_m = \frac{R_o + R_i}{2} = \frac{150 + 75}{2} = 112.5 \text{ mm} = 0.1125 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Friction torque:**
$$T = \mu \cdot F_{total} \cdot R_m \cdot n_s$$
$$T = 0.3 \times 70593.6 \times 0.1125 \times 2$$
$$T = 0.3 \times 70593.6 \times 0.225$$
$$T = 0.3 \times 15883.56$$
$$\boxed{T \approx 4766.07 \text{ N·m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation check:** At 111 rpm the clutch transmits only the spring force ($T_{low} = 0.3 \times 1200 \times 0.1125 \times 2 \approx 81$ N·m). At 2400 rpm it transmits ~4766 N·m — a **~59× increase** purely from centrifugal action. This dramatic scaling is the engineering reason semi-centrifugal clutches are used in motorcycles and older commercial vehicles.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Semi-Centrifugal Clutch

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *physical components* of the clutch to their *functional roles*, and shows how the **spring subsystem** and the **centrifugal subsystem** combine at the friction interface.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENG[Engine Subsystem - Power Source]
        CRS[Crankshaft]
    end

    subgraph FLY[Flywheel - Driving Member]
        FWH[Flywheel Hub]
    end

    subgraph SP[Spring Subsystem - Low Speed Engagement]
        SPG[Clutch Springs]
        FAX1[Axial Spring Force F_spring]
    end

    subgraph CS[Centrifugal Subsystem - High Speed Engagement]
        SHOE[Centrifugal Weights or Shoes]
        PVT[Pivot Pins on Pressure Plate]
        SPR2[Inward Spring on each Shoe C]
        LINK[Link or Lever Mechanism]
        MECH[Mechanical Advantage a]
        FAX2[Additional Axial Thrust F_add]
    end

    subgraph FRC[Friction Interface - Torque Transfer]
        PPL[Pressure Plate]
        FRC2[Friction Disc with Lining]
        SUR[Friction Surfaces n_s = 2]
        RM[Mean Radius R_m]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Drivetrain Output]
        ISH[Input or Gearbox Shaft]
        TRQ[Torque T]
    end

    CRS --> FWH
    FWH --> PPL
    SPG --> FAX1
    FAX1 --> PPL
    PPL --> SHOE
    SHOE --> PVT
    SPR2 --> SHOE
    CRS -.rotational input.-> SHOE
    SHOE -->|radial motion| LINK
    LINK --> MECH
    MECH --> FAX2
    FAX2 --> PPL
    PPL --> SUR
    SUR --> FRC2
    FRC2 --> RM
    RM --> TRQ
    FRC2 --> ISH
    ISH --> TRQ
```

## 4.2 Force-Coupling Schematic (Speed vs. Force Regimes)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine at Idle or Low RPM] -->|omega less than omega_s| B[Shoes Held In by Springs]
    B --> C[F_total equals F_spring only]
    C --> D[Smooth Engagement - Low Torque Capacity]

    E[Engine Speed Rising] -->|omega crosses omega_s| F[Centrifugal Force equals Spring Force]
    F --> G[Shoes Pivot Outward Against Stop]
    G --> H[Link Mechanism Engages]
    H --> I[Additional Axial Thrust Generated]
    I --> J[F_total = F_spring + F_add]
    J --> K[High Torque Capacity - Clutch Fully Locked]
```

## 4.3 Component Cross-Section Description (Verbal Schematic)

| # | Component | Function | Mounting |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flywheel | Provides primary friction surface & inertia | Bolted to crankshaft |
| 2 | Pressure Plate | Receives spring + centrifugal thrust, presses friction disc | Free to slide axially on cover |
| 3 | Cover (Clutch Housing) | Holds springs & pivot pins | Bolted to flywheel |
| 4 | Clutch Springs (4–6 nos.) | Provide base axial thrust at low speed | Between cover and pressure plate |
| 5 | Centrifugal Weights / Shoes (3–6 nos.) | Fly outward at high speed | Pivoted on pressure plate or on bell-crank levers |
| 6 | Inward Spring per Shoe | Holds shoe in below critical speed | Anchored to pressure plate |
| 7 | Link / Toggle / Bell-crank | Converts radial shoe motion → axial thrust | Articulated between shoe and pressure plate |
| 8 | Stop Pin / Adjusting Screw | Limits outward travel of shoe | Mounted on pressure plate |
| 9 | Friction Disc (Driven Plate) | Carries lining, transmits torque | Splined to gearbox input shaft |
| 10 | Release Bearing | Pulls pressure plate back to declutch | Pushed by clutch pedal/fork |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q: What is a semi-centrifugal clutch? State its main advantage over a single-plate spring clutch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks breakdown):**

A semi-centrifugal clutch is a friction clutch in which the pressure on the friction plate is provided partly by **clutch springs (at low speeds)** and partly by **centrifugal force of weighted shoes (at high speeds)**. *(1 mark)*

The shoes are pivoted on the pressure plate and held inward by small springs. When the engine speed exceeds a *critical value* $\omega_s = \sqrt{Cg/(Wr)}$, the shoes fly outward and, through a link mechanism, exert additional axial thrust on the pressure plate. *(1 mark)*

**Main advantage:** The torque capacity *increases automatically* with engine speed, so the engine's rising power output is matched without requiring excessively stiff springs (which would make pedal effort very high at low speeds). It gives **smooth low-speed engagement** and **high high-speed torque** — a combination not possible in a pure spring clutch. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q: Define "critical speed" of a semi-centrifugal clutch. Write the formula and explain the significance of the link mechanism's mechanical advantage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks breakdown):**

**Critical Speed ($\omega_s$ or $N_s$):** The engine speed at which the centrifugal force on the centrifugal shoe just equals the inward spring force on the shoe. Below this speed, the shoe stays retracted; above it, the shoe begins to move outward and engage the link mechanism. *(1 mark)*

**Formula:**
$$N_s = \frac{60}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{C \cdot g}{W \cdot r}} \;\;\text{rpm}$$
*(1 mark)*

**Significance of mechanical advantage $a$:** The link mechanism converts the *radial* outward motion of the shoe into *axial* thrust on the pressure plate. A higher value of $a$ means each unit of excess centrifugal force produces a larger axial force, increasing torque capacity. By varying $a$, designers can tune how aggressively the clutch "boosts" torque with rising engine speed. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, Module 1 carries 14 marks with one **internal choice**. The student answers *either* Question A *or* Question B in full. Each sub-part is typically **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — CHOICE OPTION 1

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle** of a semi-centrifugal clutch. Compare its performance with a single-plate spring clutch and a pure centrifugal clutch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A semi-centrifugal clutch has **6 shoes**, each of mass **2 kg**, with the centre of gravity at a radius of **100 mm**. The inward spring force on each shoe is **30 N**, and the link ratio provides a mechanical advantage of **a = 2**. The friction lining has outer radius **160 mm** and inner radius **80 mm**, with coefficient of friction **μ = 0.3**. There are **2 friction surfaces**. The clutch springs produce an axial force of **1500 N**.
   - **(i)** Find the **critical speed** at which the centrifugal effect begins.
   - **(ii)** Calculate the **torque transmitted** at an engine speed of **3000 rpm**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Construction, Working & Comparison **(7 marks)**

**Construction:** *(2 marks)*
The semi-centrifugal clutch consists of a flywheel mounted on the engine crankshaft, a pressure plate bolted to the flywheel, a friction disc splined to the gearbox shaft, clutch springs (4–6) providing the base axial force, and **3–6 centrifugal shoes** pivoted on bell-crank levers or directly on the pressure plate. Each shoe is held inward by a small spring and is linked to the pressure plate via a connecting link or toggle.

**Working:** *(3 marks)*
- At low engine speed, the inward spring force on each shoe is greater than the centrifugal force. The shoes remain retracted, and engagement is provided *entirely* by the clutch springs pressing the pressure plate onto the friction disc. This gives a smooth take-off.
- As engine speed increases, centrifugal force grows as $m r \omega^2$. Beyond a critical speed $\omega_s$, the shoes pivot outward against their stops, and through the link mechanism push the pressure plate harder onto the friction disc. The total axial thrust is the sum of the spring force and the centrifugal-derived thrust.
- When the clutch pedal is pressed, the pressure plate is retracted, the shoes are released, and the link relaxes, disengaging the clutch.

**Comparison Table:** *(2 marks)*

| Feature | Single-Plate Spring | Pure Centrifugal | Semi-Centrifugal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engagement at idle | Yes (strong) | No (slips) | Yes (smooth) |
| Torque at high speed | Limited (fixed) | High | Very high |
| Pedal effort | High at low speed | None (automatic) | Moderate |
| Smoothness | Excellent | Poor | Excellent |
| Complexity | Simple | Moderate | Moderate-High |
| Application | Most cars | Small engines | Motorcycles, older trucks |

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical **(7 marks)**

**Given:** $N_{shoes} = 6$, $m = 2$ kg, $r = 0.1$ m, $C = 30$ N, $a = 2$, $R_o = 0.16$ m, $R_i = 0.08$ m, $\mu = 0.3$, $n_s = 2$, $F_{spring} = 1500$ N.

**Weight per shoe:** $W = m \cdot g = 2 \times 9.81 = 19.62$ N

**(i) Critical Speed:** *(3 marks)*

Apply the threshold condition:
$$\omega_s = \sqrt{\frac{C \cdot g}{W \cdot r}} = \sqrt{\frac{30 \times 9.81}{19.62 \times 0.1}}$$

**Numerator:** $30 \times 9.81 = 294.3$

**Denominator:** $19.62 \times 0.1 = 1.962$

$$\omega_s = \sqrt{\frac{294.3}{1.962}} = \sqrt{150} \approx 12.247 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Convert to rpm:**
$$N_s = \frac{60 \times 12.247}{2\pi} \approx 116.9 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N_s \approx 117 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(ii) Torque at 3000 rpm:** *(4 marks)*

**Angular speed in rad/s:**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 100\pi \approx 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Centrifugal force per shoe:**
$$F_c = \frac{W \cdot r \cdot \omega^2}{g} = \frac{19.62 \times 0.1 \times (314.16)^2}{9.81}$$

**$\omega^2 = 98696$**, so $W \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 = 19.62 \times 0.1 \times 98696 = 193617.5$ N·m/s²

$$F_c = \frac{193617.5}{9.81} \approx 19737 \text{ N}$$

**Excess force per shoe:**
$$\Delta F = F_c - C = 19737 - 30 = 19707 \text{ N}$$

**Additional axial thrust (6 shoes, a = 2):**
$$F_{add} = 6 \times 2 \times 19707 = 236484 \text{ N}$$

**Total axial force:**
$$F_{total} = 1500 + 236484 = 237984 \text{ N}$$

**Mean radius:**
$$R_m = \frac{0.16 + 0.08}{2} = 0.12 \text{ m}$$

**Torque:**
$$T = \mu \cdot F_{total} \cdot R_m \cdot n_s = 0.3 \times 237984 \times 0.12 \times 2$$

$$T = 0.3 \times 237984 \times 0.24 = 0.3 \times 57116.16$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 17135 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Verification:** At 117 rpm, only spring force acts: $T_{low} = 0.3 \times 1500 \times 0.12 \times 2 = 108$ N·m. At 3000 rpm, torque is ~17,135 N·m — a **~159× boost**. The semi-centrifugal mechanism provides a massive high-speed torque capacity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — CHOICE OPTION 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **role of centrifugal force, spring force, and the link mechanism** in a semi-centrifugal clutch. Derive the expression for the **critical speed** of the clutch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A semi-centrifugal clutch has **4 shoes**, each weighing **20 N**, with their CG at **90 mm** from the shaft axis. The spring force per shoe is **40 N** and the link mechanical advantage is **1.8**. The mean friction radius is **110 mm**, the coefficient of friction is **0.35**, the number of friction surfaces is **2**, and the clutch springs provide an axial thrust of **2000 N**.
   - **(i)** Find the **critical speed** in rpm.
   - **(ii)** Determine the **total axial thrust** and the **torque capacity** when the engine runs at **2000 rpm**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Role of Forces and Derivation **(7 marks)**

**Role of Forces:** *(3 marks)*

1. **Centrifugal Force ($F_c = m r \omega^2$):** Acts radially outward on each shoe. It is the *active engagement* force that grows with engine speed and provides the additional torque-transmitting thrust at high rpm.

2. **Inward Spring Force ($C$):** A small spring on each shoe opposes the centrifugal force. Its job is to *keep the shoe retracted* below the critical speed so that the clutch does not engage prematurely. It also provides a smooth, controlled take-off.

3. **Clutch Springs (axial):** Provide the *base axial thrust* that presses the pressure plate onto the friction disc at low speeds. They act independently of the centrifugal mechanism.

4. **Link / Lever Mechanism:** A purely *mechanical* coupling that converts the **radial** outward motion of each shoe into **axial** motion of the pressure plate. The mechanical advantage $a$ (ratio of link arms) determines how efficiently this conversion occurs.

**Derivation of Critical Speed:** *(4 marks)*

For each shoe, force balance in the radial direction gives:
$$F_c = C$$
$$\frac{W \cdot r \cdot \omega_s^{\,2}}{g} = C$$
$$\omega_s^{\,2} = \frac{C \cdot g}{W \cdot r}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_s = \sqrt{\frac{C \cdot g}{W \cdot r}}}$$

In rpm:
$$\boxed{N_s = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{C \cdot g}{W \cdot r}} = 9.549 \sqrt{\frac{C \cdot g}{W \cdot r}}}$$

This is the speed at which the centrifugal effect just begins. The clutch is "spring-only" below this and "spring + centrifugal" above this.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical **(7 marks)**

**Given:** $N_{shoes} = 4$, $W = 20$ N, $r = 0.09$ m, $C = 40$ N, $a = 1.8$, $R_m = 0.11$ m, $\mu = 0.35$, $n_s = 2$, $F_{spring} = 2000$ N, $N_{engine} = 2000$ rpm.

**(i) Critical Speed in rpm:** *(3 marks)*

$$N_s = 9.549 \sqrt{\frac{C \cdot g}{W \cdot r}}$$

**Numerator inside root:** $C \cdot g = 40 \times 9.81 = 392.4$

**Denominator inside root:** $W \cdot r = 20 \times 0.09 = 1.8$

**Ratio:** $392.4 / 1.8 = 218$

**Square root:** $\sqrt{218} \approx 14.76$

$$N_s = 9.549 \times 14.76 \approx 141 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N_s \approx 141 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(ii) Total Axial Thrust and Torque at 2000 rpm:** *(4 marks)*

**Angular speed:**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 2000}{60} = \frac{4000\pi}{60} = \frac{200\pi}{3} \approx 209.44 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Centrifugal force per shoe:**
$$F_c = \frac{20 \times 0.09 \times (209.44)^2}{9.81}$$

**$\omega^2 \approx 43865$**, so numerator $= 20 \times 0.09 \times 43865 = 78957$ N·m/s²

$$F_c = \frac{78957}{9.81} \approx 8048.6 \text{ N}$$

**Excess force per shoe:**
$$\Delta F = 8048.6 - 40 = 8008.6 \text{ N}$$

**Additional axial thrust (4 shoes, a = 1.8):**
$$F_{add} = 4 \times 1.8 \times 8008.6 = 57662 \text{ N}$$

**Total axial thrust:**
$$F_{total} = 2000 + 57662 = 59662 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{total} \approx 59662 \text{ N}}$$

**Torque capacity:**
$$T = \mu \cdot F_{total} \cdot R_m \cdot n_s = 0.35 \times 59662 \times 0.11 \times 2$$

$$T = 0.35 \times 59662 \times 0.22 = 0.35 \times 13125.6$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 4594 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (from KTU Board valuation experience):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm → m:** All lengths in SI must be in *metres* before substituting. A student who plugs $r = 100$ mm (instead of 0.1 m) directly into $F_c$ gets a torque value **100× too small**. **2-mark penalty.**
>
> 2. **Mixing up weight ($W$ in N) and mass ($m$ in kg):** The formula $F_c = W r \omega^2 / g$ uses *weight*, not mass. Using $m$ in place of $W$ gives a force **9.81× too small**. This is the #1 mistake in 7-mark numericals.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the spring contribution at high speed:** A common error is to compute $F_{total} = F_{add}$ only. The correct expression always *adds* the spring force: $F_{total} = F_{spring} + F_{add}$.
>
> 4. **Omitting the link mechanical advantage $a$:** Many students forget that the centrifugal force on the shoe is *radial* and must be converted to *axial* by the link. The factor $a$ is essential.
>
> 5. **Using $N_s = \omega_s$ without conversion:** Critical speed in *rad/s* and *rpm* are different. Always state which form your final answer is in, and convert explicitly.
>
> 6. **Not specifying the speed regime:** The torque formulas for $\omega < \omega_s$ and $\omega > \omega_s$ are different. Examiners explicitly look for this distinction in 7-mark sub-parts.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1**

- ✅ A **semi-centrifugal clutch** combines spring engagement (low speed) and centrifugal engagement (high speed).
- ✅ Three force systems act: **(i)** axial clutch springs, **(ii)** radial centrifugal force on shoes, **(iii)** inward shoe springs.
- ✅ The **critical speed** is the speed at which centrifugal force on the shoe just equals its inward spring force.
- ✅ Master formula: $\omega_s = \sqrt{C g / (W r)}$ — always use **weight** $W$ (N), not mass.
- ✅ **Above** $\omega_s$: shoes fly out, link transmits radial motion as axial thrust, total $F = F_{spring} + N \cdot a \cdot (F_c - C)$.
- ✅ **Below** $\omega_s$: only springs act; $F = F_{spring}$.
- ✅ Final torque: $T = \mu \cdot F_{total} \cdot R_m \cdot n_s$ where $R_m = (R_o + R_i)/2$ and $n_s = 2$ for a single-plate clutch.
- ✅ Conversion: $1 \text{ rpm} = 2\pi/60 \text{ rad/s} \approx 0.1047 \text{ rad/s}$; $1 \text{ rad/s} \approx 9.549 \text{ rpm}$.
- ✅ **Centrifugal force scales with $\omega^2$** — this is why the clutch "boosts" torque aggressively at high rpm.
- ✅ The **mechanical advantage $a$** of the link is a design tuning parameter — bigger $a$ = more axial thrust per unit of centrifugal force.
- ✅ SI Units strictly: **N** (force), **m** (length), **rad/s** (angular speed), **N·m** (torque).
- ✅ Standard value of $\mu$ for clutch linings: **0.25 to 0.4** (organic/cork); use the value given in the problem.
- ✅ Typical applications: **motorcycles, scooters, older commercial vehicles, stationary engines**.
- ✅ For KTU, remember to state the **speed regime**, **convert units**, and **include the spring force** in the final torque calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fully Automatic Centrifugal Clutch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT413 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fully Automatic Centrifugal Clutch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centrifugal Clutch (KTU Definition):**
> A *centrifugal clutch* is a **self-engaging, automatic friction clutch** that uses the **centrifugal force** generated by revolving weighted shoes (or fly-weights) to bring the driving and driven members into frictional contact. The engagement is **purely a function of the rotational speed** of the driving member and requires **no manual input** from the operator.

A **Fully Automatic Centrifugal Clutch** is a special sub-class in which the **disengagement at low RPM is also achieved automatically** by means of **retractor springs** that pull the shoes back to their resting (disengaged) position. Thus, both engagement and disengagement occur without any rider/driver effort.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Spinning Ball on a String":**
> Imagine tying a small ball to a string and whirling it above your head. When you spin **slowly**, the string hangs loosely (no tension). But as you spin **faster**, the ball wants to fly outward and the string pulls tight. The **centrifugal clutch behaves exactly like this string-and-ball system**: the "ball" is the clutch shoe, the "string" is the spring, and the "head" is the engine crankshaft. When the engine RPM rises, the shoes are flung outward and "grab" the drum — engaging the clutch.

### Applications in Engineering

| Application Domain | Specific Use-Case |
|--------------------|-------------------|
| Two-wheelers | Scooters (Honda Activa, Bajaj Chetak, TVS Jupiter) |
| Recreational vehicles | Go-karts, mini-bikes |
| Stationary engines | Lawn mowers, water pumps, gensets |
| Industrial automation | Small conveyor drives, packaging machines |

### Key Standard Metrics

- **Engagement speed (N₁):** typically **1800 – 2500 RPM** for scooter clutches.
- **Coefficient of friction (μ):** **0.25 – 0.35** (asbestos / sintered bronze linings).
- **Shoe mass range:** **0.2 – 1.0 kg** per shoe.
- **Operating RPM range:** up to **6000 RPM** in modern CVT scooters.

> [!TIP]
> The most important single fact to remember: **F_c = m·r·ω²**. Every formula in this topic is a rearrangement or extension of this one equation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

The clutch operates in **three distinct regimes** determined by the balance between **centrifugal force (F_c)** and **spring retractor force (F_s)**:

### Stage 1 — Idle / Disengaged State
- Engine idles at low RPM.
- **Spring force (F_s) > Centrifugal force (F_c)**.
- Shoes are held in their retracted position against the driving hub.
- **No torque transfer** to the driven drum.

### Stage 2 — Engagement Threshold
- As throttle is opened, engine RPM rises.
- Centrifugal force grows **quadratically with ω** (since F_c ∝ ω²).
- The moment **F_c = F_s**, the shoes just begin to touch the inner surface of the driven drum.
- This RPM is the **engagement speed N₁**.

### Stage 3 — Fully Engaged State
- Beyond N₁, F_c continues to grow while F_s remains constant.
- The shoes press harder against the drum.
- Friction force = **μ·F_n** transmits torque to the driven drum.
- Full power transfer achieved.

## 2.2 Constructional Features

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Driving hub (mounted on crankshaft) | Carries the shoes and rotates with engine |
| Shoes (2 to 4 typically) | Fly outward due to centrifugal force; carry friction lining |
| Retractor springs | Pull shoes back when RPM drops below engagement speed |
| Driven drum (sleeve) | Outer member; connected to transmission input shaft |
| Friction lining | Provides high-μ contact surface on shoe tip |
| Linkage pins / pivots | Allow shoes to slide radially |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Every numerical problem in this topic is solved using the following 6 equations. Memorize them with units!

| S.No | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Variables & Units |
|------|-----------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ | Centrifugal force on a single shoe | $m$ = shoe mass (kg), $r$ = CG radius (m), $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s) |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from RPM | $N$ = rotational speed (RPM) |
| 3 | $F_c = F_s$ | Engagement threshold condition | $F_s$ = spring retractor force (N) |
| 4 | $T_{single} = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot R$ | Torque capacity (single shoe) | $\mu$ = coeff. of friction, $F_n$ = normal contact force (N), $R$ = drum inner radius (m) |
| 5 | $T_{multi} = n \cdot \mu \cdot F_n \cdot R$ | Torque capacity (n shoes) | $n$ = number of shoes |
| 6 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Power transmitted | $P$ = power (W) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT confuse** the **shoe CG radius (r)** with the **drum inner radius (R)**. They are different distances! $r$ is the radius at which the shoe mass revolves; $R$ is the radius at which friction acts.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **CVT-equipped scooter**, the fully automatic centrifugal clutch is the **bridge between the engine's torque curve and the CVT's variable ratio**. The clutch automatically engages the moment the engine produces enough torque to overcome the spring pre-load, eliminating the need for a clutch lever. This is the dominant reason scooters can be ridden one-handed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Torque Capacity of a Multi-Shoe Centrifugal Clutch

### Setup
Consider a clutch with $n$ shoes, each of mass $m$ kg, with the center of gravity at radius $r$ m from the axis of rotation. The inner radius of the driven drum is $R$ m. The spring force on each shoe is $F_s$ N, and the coefficient of friction between shoe and drum is $\mu$.

### Step 1: Centrifugal Force on One Shoe

When the driving hub rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s, each shoe experiences an outward centrifugal force:

$$
F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2
$$

**Logic:** Newton's second law for circular motion. The required centripetal force is $m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$; the shoe pushes outward on the drum with an equal and opposite reaction — this is the normal contact force $F_n$.

### Step 2: Engagement Condition

Engagement starts exactly when the centrifugal force just balances the retractor spring force:

$$
F_c = F_s
$$

Substituting the expression from Step 1:

$$
m \cdot r \cdot \omega_1^2 = F_s
$$

Solving for the engagement angular velocity $\omega_1$:

$$
\omega_1 = \sqrt{\dfrac{F_s}{m \cdot r}}
$$

Converting to engagement RPM $N_1$:

$$
N_1 = \dfrac{60}{2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{F_s}{m \cdot r}}
$$

### Step 3: Normal Contact Force During Operation

Once engaged and the engine runs at $\omega > \omega_1$, the **excess** centrifugal force presses the shoe against the drum. The normal contact force per shoe is:

$$
F_n = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 - F_s
$$

For the special (and common) case where the **friction lining is mounted on the shoe tip at radius $R$**, the friction force at the drum surface is:

$$
F_f = \mu \cdot F_n = \mu \cdot (m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 - F_s)
$$

### Step 4: Torque Transmitted by a Single Shoe

The friction force $F_f$ acts tangentially at the drum inner radius $R$. The torque contributed by one shoe is:

$$
T_{one} = F_f \cdot R = \mu \cdot (m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 - F_s) \cdot R
$$

### Step 5: Total Torque for n Shoes

Assuming all $n$ shoes act symmetrically and independently:

$$
\boxed{T = n \cdot \mu \cdot R \cdot (m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 - F_s)}
$$

### Step 6: Power Transmitted

$$
P = T \cdot \omega = n \cdot \mu \cdot R \cdot (m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 - F_s) \cdot \omega
$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

### Given Data

A multi-shoe centrifugal clutch used in a scooter has the following specifications:

- Number of shoes: $n = 3$
- Mass of each shoe: $m = 0.4$ kg
- CG radius of each shoe: $r = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m
- Drum inner radius: $R = 110$ mm $= 0.110$ m
- Spring force per shoe: $F_s = 30$ N
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.28$

### Part (a) — Determine the Engagement Speed N₁

**Step 1:** Apply the engagement condition $F_c = F_s$:

$$
m \cdot r \cdot \omega_1^2 = F_s
$$

$$
0.4 \times 0.075 \times \omega_1^2 = 30
$$

$$
0.030 \cdot \omega_1^2 = 30
$$

$$
