P/W affects top speed, but only via cube root, so aerodynamic improvements have a larger impact at high speeds. **Part (b) — Numerical Computation:** Given: - $m = 12$ tonnes = 12000 kg - Gradient = 15% → $\sin\theta \approx 0.15$, $\theta \approx 8.63°$ - $v = 30 \, \text{km/h} = 8.33 \, \text{m/s}$ - $f = 0.015$, $\eta_t = 0.88$ (i) Resistance forces: Weight: $W = 12000 \times 9.81 = 117720 \, \text{N}$ Rolling resistance: $$F_r = f \cdot W = 0.015 \times 117720 = 1765.8 \, \text{N}$$ Gradient resistance: $$F_g = W \cdot \sin\theta = 117720 \times 0.15 = 17658 \, \text{N}$$ (ii) Propelling force required (neglecting inertia at constant speed): $$F_p = F_r + F_g = 1765.8 + 17658 = 19423.8 \, \text{N}$$ (iii) Minimum engine power at wheels: $$P_w = F_p \cdot v = 19423.8 \times 8.33 = 161800 \, \text{W} = 161.8 \, \text{kW}$$ Engine power required (before transmission losses): $$P_{\text{engine}} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_t} = \frac{161.8}{0.88} = 183.86 \, \text{kW}$$ P/W ratio: $$\frac{P}{W} = \frac{161800}{117720} = 1.374 \, \text{W/kg} = 1.374 \, \text{kW/tonne}$$ Equivalent in hp/ton: $$P = 161.8 \, \text{kW} = \frac{161800}{745.7} = 217 \, \text{hp}$$ $$\frac{P}{W} = \frac{217}{12} = 18.07 \, \text{hp/ton}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Heavy Loss Zone:** > 1. **Unit conversion oversight:** Students often forget to convert km/h to m/s when computing power, producing answers off by a factor of **3.6** (≈ 36 marks scaled loss). > 2. **Forgetting transmission efficiency:** Many skip dividing by $\eta_t$, computing only $P_w$ but labelling it as engine power. Examiner deducts **2 marks** explicitly. > 3. **Confusing gradient percentage with angle:** A 15% gradient means $\tan\theta = 0.15$ (NOT $\theta = 15°$). Mark $\theta$ correctly as $\arctan(0.15) = 8.53°$ and use $\sin\theta = 0.149$ for accuracy. > 4. **Sign error in gradeability formula:** When gradient resistance *adds* to rolling resistance, both must be on the *same side* of the equation with the same sign. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2 / Power-to-Weight Ratio:** - **Definition:** P/W is engine power divided by vehicle weight (or mass). Units: **W/kg**, **kW/tonne**, or **hp/ton**. - **Conversion constant:** **1 hp = 745.7 W**, **1 tonne = 1000 kg**, **g = 9.81 m/s²**. - **Core governing equation:** $a = \dfrac{(P/W) \cdot g}{v}$ — *the single most important formula for KTU exams*. - **Top speed (drag-limited):** $V_{\max} = \left(\dfrac{2P}{\rho C_d A}\right)^{1/3}$ — depends on **cube root** of power. - **Maximum grade angle:** $\sin\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{(P/W)}{v_{\text{crawl}} \cdot g} - f$ where $f$ is rolling resistance coefficient. - **Power at wheels:** $P_w = \eta_t \cdot P_{\text{engine}}$; always check whether the problem gives engine power or wheel power. - **Trivial acceleration** assumption: drag-free, level road, $F_a = F_p$ — valid only at low speeds (typically < 30 km/h). - **Inverse v relationship:** Acceleration decreases hyperbolically as speed rises because $P = F \cdot v$ means $F = P/v$. - **Vehicle P/W benchmarks to memorise:** F1 ≈ 1000+ W/kg, Supercar ≈ 400 W/kg, Sedan ≈ 60–70 W/kg, Truck ≈ 10–15 W/kg. - **Common exam trap:** Always convert **km/h to m/s** before substitution (multiply by $\frac{5}{18}$). - **Engineering insight:** Hypercar designers prioritise lowering $C_d \cdot A$ (aerodynamics) over raising P/W for top speed gains, due to cube-root dependence. > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** *"**P**ush **W**eight — Power Wins."* — A higher P/W means the engine can *push* each unit of vehicle *weight* more effectively, yielding faster acceleration, better gradeability, and higher top speed.

topics.title: Speed Operating Range
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT413 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Speed Operating Range

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Automobile Engineering (PEMRT413), the **Speed Operating Range** of an automobile is defined as the band of vehicle speeds lying between the **maximum attainable vehicle speed** (V_max) and the **minimum practical vehicle speed** (V_min) — typically evaluated in top gear (overdrive or direct drive) where the vehicle operates most of the time on highways and flat roads.

Mathematically, the operating range is expressed as:

$$
\text{Speed Operating Range} = V_{max} - V_{min} \quad [\text{km/h}]
$$

where:

- **V_max** = Maximum speed limited by engine power, aerodynamic drag, and rolling resistance.
- **V_min** = Lowest stable speed in top gear (above which the engine does not stall and the vehicle does not judder), often governed by the **maximum tractive effort** required to overcome gradients at low speeds.

The operating range is a critical parameter that determines the **flexibility** of a vehicle's performance: a wider range implies the vehicle can cruise at both very low and very high speeds in top gear without frequent gear shifts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Speed Operating Range" in Module 2 of PEMRT413 is conventionally interpreted as the **top-gear operating window**, i.e., the spread between maximum and minimum usable speeds in the highest gear. Always state this assumption explicitly in board answers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Breathing Range" Intuition

Imagine driving a bicycle with a **single fixed gear**. There is a sweet spot of pedalling cadence (rotational speed) at which your legs feel comfortable. If you pedal too slowly on an incline, you lose balance and stop. If you pedal too fast on a flat road, your legs spin uselessly while the bicycle barely accelerates further.

- **V_min** is like the slowest cadence at which you can still push the bicycle uphill without losing balance.
- **V_max** is like the fastest cadence at which your leg speed matches the road speed and additional pedalling produces no further gain.
- The **Operating Range** is the cadence *band* where the bicycle remains usable and stable.

A car with a **wide operating range** behaves like a bicycle whose single gear is sized so that you can climb a hill *and* sprint on a flat road without changing gears. A car with a **narrow operating range** requires frequent gear changes — it is "fussy."

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

The following constants and metrics are conventionally used in KTU Automobile Engineering problems:

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Air density (ρ) | **1.25 kg/m³** |
| Coefficient of drag (C_d) | **0.30 – 0.45** for passenger cars |
| Frontal area (A) | **1.8 – 2.5 m²** |
| Coefficient of rolling resistance (f) | **0.012 – 0.018** |
| Gravitational acceleration (g) | **9.81 m/s²** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** When solving numericals in the university ESE, always carry units through every step. Marks are explicitly awarded for *unit consistency* and *correct final units*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of the Operating Range Concept

The operating range is governed by **two limiting speeds**, each derived from a distinct set of resistance forces:

### Step 1 — Identifying V_max (Top Speed)
At V_max, the **engine delivers peak usable power**, but the **resistive forces (aerodynamic drag + rolling resistance + gradient resistance)** are exactly equal to the tractive force at the wheels. Beyond V_max, drag rises as the cube of velocity, and the engine cannot supply any more power.

### Step 2 — Identifying V_min (Minimum Speed in Top Gear)
V_min corresponds to the lowest vehicle speed at which the engine can still develop enough **tractive force** to:
- Overcome total road resistance (rolling + gradient + air),
- Without the engine RPM dropping below the **stall / lugging limit** (typically 60–70 % of rated RPM in petrol engines, and 30–40 % in diesels).

### Step 3 — Operating Range Width
The width:

$$
\Delta V = V_{max} - V_{min}
$$

A **larger ΔV** implies:
- Better cruising flexibility in top gear.
- Less frequent gear shifting on undulating terrain.
- Higher mechanical/thermal stress during lugging near V_min.

### Step 4 — Why V_min is Reduced by Gradient
On a positive gradient (climb angle θ), the **grade resistance** component $W \sin\theta$ rises dramatically at low speeds because tractive effort at low engine RPM falls faster than resistance rises. Hence V_min increases with gradient, *shrinking* the operating range.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — Speed Operating Range (Module 2, PEMRT413)**
> CRITICAL: All pipe symbols are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \dfrac{2\pi N r}{60 \, R_{total}}$ | Vehicle speed from engine RPM | m/s |
| 2 | $R_{total} = R_g \times R_f$ | Overall gear ratio (gear × final drive) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $F_t = \dfrac{T_e \, \eta_t \, R_{total}}{r}$ | Tractive force at wheels | N |
| 4 | $F_r = f \, W \, \cos\theta$ | Rolling resistance force | N |
| 5 | $F_g = W \, \sin\theta$ | Gradient resistance force | N |
| 6 | $F_a = \dfrac{\rho \, C_d \, A \, V^2}{2}$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N |
| 7 | $P = F_t \times V$ | Power required at vehicle speed V | W |
| 8 | $\Delta V = V_{max} - V_{min}$ | Speed operating range | km/h |
| 9 | $V_{max}: P_e = F_r + F_a + F_g$ | Equilibrium at maximum speed | — |
| 10 | $V_{min}: F_t \geq F_r + F_a + F_g$ | Tractive limit at minimum speed | — |

### Where:

- **N** = engine speed (RPM)
- **r** = effective wheel radius (m)
- **R_g** = transmission gear ratio in the engaged gear
- **R_f** = final drive (axle) ratio
- **T_e** = engine torque (N·m)
- **η_t** = transmission efficiency (typically **0.85 – 0.92**)
- **W** = vehicle weight (N)
- **θ** = gradient angle (radians or degrees)
- **P_e** = engine power available at wheels (W)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Transmission Design:** Automakers tune first-gear ratio and final-drive ratio jointly so that ΔV in top gear is wide enough to handle moderate undulations (2–4 % gradient) without downshifting.
2. **Electric Vehicles:** EVs have an inherently infinite operating range (single gear) because electric motors deliver near-constant torque from 0 RPM — this is precisely the engineering principle Tesla exploited with single-speed transmissions.
3. **Heavy Commercial Vehicles (HCVs):** Operate in narrow speed bands because their engines are tuned for torque, not power. Hence HCVs have many gears (9, 12, 16-speed gearboxes).
4. **Racing Cars:** Use *close-ratio* gearboxes that deliberately shrink ΔV in each gear to keep the engine in its peak power band.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Vehicle Speed from Engine RPM

**Given:**
- Engine rotational speed: N (RPM)
- Wheel radius: r (m)
- Overall gear ratio (top gear): R_total = R_g × R_f

**Step 1 — Convert engine RPM to angular velocity:**

The engine rotates at N revolutions per minute, so its angular velocity in rad/s is:

$$
\omega_e = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad [\text{rad/s}]
$$

**Step 2 — Reduce angular velocity through the drivetrain:**

Each revolution of the engine produces **R_total** revolutions of the wheel. Therefore the wheel angular velocity is:

$$
\omega_w = \frac{\omega_e}{R_{total}} = \frac{2\pi N}{60 \, R_{total}} \quad [\text{rad/s}]
$$

**Step 3 — Convert wheel angular velocity to linear vehicle speed:**

The vehicle moves forward by the circumference of the wheel ($2\pi r$) for every wheel revolution:

$$
V = \omega_w \times r = \frac{2\pi N \, r}{60 \, R_{total}} \quad [\text{m/s}]
$$

**Step 4 — Convert m/s to km/h (KTU standard for speed):**

$$
V_{km/h} = V_{m/s} \times 3.6 = \frac{2\pi N \, r \times 3.6}{60 \, R_{total}} = \frac{0.377 \, N \, r}{R_{total}}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{km/h} = \frac{0.377 \, N \, r}{R_{total}}}
$$

This is the **master equation** linking engine RPM to road speed. Every KTU numerical on speed ultimately reduces to manipulating this equation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Speed (V_max)

**Step 1 — Total resistance at V_max:**

The vehicle is on a flat road at maximum speed, so $\theta = 0$, meaning $\cos\theta = 1$ and $\sin\theta = 0$. Total resistance is:

$$
F_{res} = F_r + F_a = f \, W + \frac{\rho \, C_d \, A \, V_{max}^2}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium condition at V_max:**

At maximum speed, tractive force exactly balances resistance (no acceleration):

$$
F_t = F_r + F_a
$$

$$
\frac{T_e \, \eta_t \, R_{total}}{r} = f \, W + \frac{\rho \, C_d \, A \, V_{max}^2}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for V_max:**

The drag term dominates at high speed. Rearranging into a quadratic in V_max:

$$
\frac{\rho \, C_d \, A}{2} V_{max}^2 - \frac{T_e \, \eta_t \, R_{total}}{r} + f \, W = 0
$$

Using the standard quadratic formula $x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:

$$
V_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{\dfrac{T_e \, \eta_t \, R_{total}}{r} - fW}{\dfrac{\rho \, C_d \, A}{2}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \left( \dfrac{T_e \, \eta_t \, R_{total}}{r} - fW \right)}{\rho \, C_d \, A}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Check:** V_max is real (not imaginary) only when the numerator is positive, i.e., when the tractive force at the wheels exceeds rolling resistance. Otherwise the vehicle cannot reach V_max in that gear and must downshift.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A passenger car has the following data. Determine (i) the maximum speed in top gear, and (ii) the speed operating range if V_min = 35 km/h.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Gross vehicle weight, W | 15,000 N |
| Wheel radius, r | 0.32 m |
| Top gear ratio, R_g | 1.0 |
| Final drive ratio, R_f | 3.7 |
| Transmission efficiency, η_t | 0.9 |
| Peak engine torque at 4000 RPM, T_e | 120 N·m |
| Rolling resistance coefficient, f | 0.015 |
| Frontal area, A | 2.0 m² |
| Drag coefficient, C_d | 0.35 |
| Air density, ρ | 1.25 kg/m³ |
| V_min in top gear | 35 km/h |

**Step 1 — Compute overall gear ratio:**

$$
R_{total} = R_g \times R_f = 1.0 \times 3.7 = 3.7
$$

**Step 2 — Compute rolling resistance:**

$$
F_r = f \, W = 0.015 \times 15{,}000 = 225 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute available tractive force at wheels (at peak torque):**

$$
F_t = \frac{T_e \, \eta_t \, R_{total}}{r} = \frac{120 \times 0.9 \times 3.7}{0.32}
$$

$$
F_t = \frac{399.6}{0.32} = 1248.75 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator for V_max:**

$$
F_t - F_r = 1248.75 - 225 = 1023.75 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator for V_max:**

$$
\frac{\rho \, C_d \, A}{2} = \frac{1.25 \times 0.35 \times 2.0}{2} = 0.4375 \text{ N·s²/m²}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute V_max:**

$$
V_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1023.75}{0.4375}} = \sqrt{\frac{2047.5}{0.4375}} = \sqrt{4679.99}
$$

$$
V_{max} \approx 68.41 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to km/h:**

$$
V_{max} = 68.41 \times 3.6 = 246.3 \text{ km/h}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute speed operating range:**

$$
\Delta V = V_{max} - V_{min} = 246.3 - 35 = 211.3 \text{ km/h}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** This vehicle has a top-gear operating range of **211.3 km/h**, indicating it can cruise comfortably anywhere between 35 km/h and 246 km/h in top gear without gear shifting.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Operating Range

| Factor | Effect on V_max | Effect on V_min | Net Effect on ΔV |
|---|---|---|---|
| Higher final drive ratio (R_f) | ↓ (decreases) | ↓ (decreases) | Roughly constant |
| Higher engine torque (T_e) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (widens) |
| Lower C_d × A product | ↑ | No change | ↑ (widens) |
| Higher rolling resistance f | ↓ (slightly) | ↑ (increases) | ↓ (narrows) |
| Positive gradient θ > 0 | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ (narrows sharply) |
| Lower transmission efficiency η_t | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ (narrows) |
| Smaller wheel radius r | ↑ | No change | ↑ (widens) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Speed Operating Range Determination

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the logical sequence used to compute the speed operating range, mapping the causal relationships between engine parameters, drivetrain ratios, resistances, and the resulting V_max and V_min bounds.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Torque T_e and RPM N] --> B[Transmission Gear Ratio R_g]
    B --> C[Final Drive Ratio R_f]
    C --> D[Overall Ratio R_total]
    D --> E[Tractive Force at Wheels F_t]
    E --> F{Vehicle State}
    F --> G[Flat Road V_max]
    F --> H[Gradient theta V_min]
    I[Rolling Resistance fW] --> J[Total Resistance F_res]
    K[Aerodynamic Drag rho C_d A V squared by 2] --> J
    L[Gradient Force W sin theta] --> J
    J --> M[Equilibrium Check F_t versus F_res]
    M --> N[V_max computed]
    M --> O[V_min computed]
    N --> P[Operating Range Delta V]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Decision: Wide Range or Narrow Range]
    Q --> R1[Good: Flexible cruising, fewer shifts]
    Q --> R2[Bad: Frequent downshifts, driver fatigue]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force Balance During Operating Range

This diagram visualises the **force balance at the wheels** as vehicle speed varies from V_min to V_max, showing how tractive force and resistance forces intersect to define the operating window.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        ENG[Engine Torque and RPM]
        GR[Gearbox R_g]
        FD[Final Drive R_f]
        WH[Wheel Radius r]
    end
    ENG --> CALC1[Tractive Force F_t]
    GR --> CALC1
    FD --> CALC1
    WH --> CALC1
    subgraph Resistances
        RR[Rolling Resistance fW]
        AD[Aerodynamic Drag kV squared]
        GR2[Gradient W sin theta]
    end
    RR --> SUM[Total Resistance F_res]
    AD --> SUM
    GR2 --> SUM
    CALC1 --> LIMIT{F_t versus F_res}
    SUM --> LIMIT
    LIMIT --> VMAX[V_max: F_t equals F_res]
    LIMIT --> VMIN[V_min: F_t equals F_res on gradient]
    VMAX --> RANGE[Operating Range Delta V]
    VMIN --> RANGE
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Effect of Gradient on Operating Range

The block diagram below shows how increasing the gradient angle θ progressively shrinks the operating range by raising V_min and reducing V_max.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Gradient 0 percent: Wide range] --> L1[Gradient 3 percent: Slight narrowing]
    L1 --> L2[Gradient 6 percent: Moderate narrowing]
    L2 --> L3[Gradient 10 percent: Heavy narrowing]
    L3 --> L4[Gradient 15 percent: Driver must downshift]
    L0 -.-> NOTE1[V_max around 246 km per h, V_min around 35 km per h]
    L4 -.-> NOTE2[V_max drops, V_min rises, Delta V collapses]
    NOTE1 --- DASH[Dashed indicator lines]
    NOTE2 --- DASH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When drawing free-body / block diagrams in the answer script, always label force directions and indicate the *equilibrium point* with a clear dot or circle. Half-mark deductions occur for unlabelled arrows.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Speed Operating Range" of an automobile. State the two limiting speeds that bound it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The speed operating range is the band of vehicle speeds within which a vehicle can operate in top gear without requiring a gear change. It is bounded by:
1. **V_max** — the maximum attainable speed, limited by the balance between available engine power and total road resistance (rolling + aerodynamic drag).
2. **V_min** — the minimum usable speed, governed by the maximum tractive effort the engine can deliver at low RPM while still overcoming road and gradient resistance.

The range is mathematically defined as $\Delta V = V_{max} - V_{min}$, expressed in km/h. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two bounds.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List four factors that affect the speed operating range of a vehicle in top gear.**

**Model Answer (4 points × ~0.75 marks):**
1. Overall drivetrain ratio (gear ratio × final drive ratio).
2. Engine torque characteristics and rated power.
3. Aerodynamic drag coefficient and frontal area ($C_d \times A$).
4. Rolling resistance coefficient and vehicle weight.
5. Gradient angle of the road.
6. Transmission efficiency.

*(Any four correct factors with brief descriptions — 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Never mix up V_max and V_min derivations.** V_max uses *power-balance* (P_e = F_res × V), while V_min uses *force-balance* (F_t ≥ F_res). Many students incorrectly apply the power equation at V_min. **Loss: up to 4 marks.**
> - **Always convert final speed to km/h** when the question states km/h. Marks are awarded for *correct units*.
> - **Do not forget transmission efficiency η_t.** Omitting it costs 2 marks in 14-mark problems.
> - **Show the quadratic solution step** when computing V_max — examiners specifically look for the step "solving for V using $V = \sqrt{(F_t - F_r) / (k_a)}$".

---

### **Question A — Module Internal Choice Option (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

A four-wheeled passenger car weighing **18,000 N** is to be analysed for top-gear performance. Given:
- Wheel radius r = 0.33 m
- Top gear ratio R_g = 1.0, Final drive ratio R_f = 3.5
- Engine peak torque T_e = 130 N·m at 3800 RPM
- Transmission efficiency η_t = 0.88
- Rolling resistance coefficient f = 0.013
- Frontal area A = 2.1 m², drag coefficient C_d = 0.36
- Air density ρ = 1.25 kg/m³
- Minimum speed in top gear on a 4 % gradient V_min = 40 km/h

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Derive the expression for maximum speed V_max in top gear on a level road and compute its value for the given data.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Overall gear ratio:**

$$
R_{total} = R_g \times R_f = 1.0 \times 3.5 = 3.5
$$

**Step 2 — Rolling resistance:**

$$
F_r = f \, W = 0.013 \times 18{,}000 = 234 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Tractive force at wheels:**

$$
F_t = \frac{T_e \, \eta_t \, R_{total}}{r} = \frac{130 \times 0.88 \times 3.5}{0.33} = \frac{400.4}{0.33} = 1213.33 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Drag constant:**

$$
k_a = \frac{\rho \, C_d \, A}{2} = \frac{1.25 \times 0.36 \times 2.1}{2} = 0.4725 \text{ N·s²/m²}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply equilibrium for V_max (level road, no gradient):**

$$
V_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{F_t - F_r}{k_a}} = \sqrt{\frac{1213.33 - 234}{0.4725}}
$$

$$
V_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{979.33}{0.4725}} = \sqrt{2072.66} = 45.53 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to km/h:**

$$
V_{max} = 45.53 \times 3.6 = 163.9 \text{ km/h}
$$

**Answer:** $V_{max} \approx 163.9$ km/h *(7 marks: 2 for derivation setup, 3 for substitution, 1 for arithmetic, 1 for unit conversion.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Compute the speed operating range of the vehicle and comment on its suitability for highway cruising.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify V_min (given):**

$$
V_{min} = 40 \text{ km/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Operating range:**

$$
\Delta V = V_{max} - V_{min} = 163.9 - 40 = 123.9 \text{ km/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Suitability comment:**

A range of **123.9 km/h** is wide enough for highway cruising between 60 km/h and 130 km/h without any gear change. The vehicle can comfortably absorb minor undulations (2–4 % gradient) in top gear. However, on steeper gradients (>6 %), the driver may experience lugging and should downshift.

**Answer:** $\Delta V \approx 123.9$ km/h, suitable for highway cruising with occasional downshifts on steep climbs. *(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for arithmetic, 2 for engineering comment, 1 for units.)*

---

### **Question B — Module Internal Choice Alternative (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

A mini-truck has the following specifications:
- Kerb weight W = 22,000 N
- Wheel radius r = 0.40 m
- Top gear ratio R_g = 1.0, Final drive ratio R_f = 4.2
- Engine peak torque T_e = 250 N·m at 2200 RPM
- Transmission efficiency η_t = 0.85
- Rolling resistance f = 0.018
- Frontal area A = 3.5 m², C_d = 0.55
- Air density ρ = 1.25 kg/m³
- On a 5 % gradient, V_min = 22 km/h in top gear

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Compute the maximum speed of the mini-truck in top gear on level road.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Overall gear ratio:**

$$
R_{total} = 1.0 \times 4.2 = 4.2
$$

**Step 2 — Rolling resistance:**

$$
F_r = 0.018 \times 22{,}000 = 396 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Tractive force:**

$$
F_t = \frac{250 \times 0.85 \times 4.2}{0.40} = \frac{892.5}{0.40} = 2231.25 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Drag constant:**

$$
k_a = \frac{1.25 \times 0.55 \times 3.5}{2} = 1.203125 \text{ N·s²/m²}
$$

**Step 5 — V_max:**

$$
V_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{2231.25 - 396}{1.203125}} = \sqrt{\frac{1835.25}{1.203125}}
$$

$$
V_{max} = \sqrt{1525.41} = 39.06 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert:**

$$
V_{max} = 39.06 \times 3.6 = 140.6 \text{ km/h}
$$

**Answer:** $V_{max} \approx 140.6$ km/h. *(7 marks.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Determine the speed operating range and assess whether the mini-truck is suitable for expressway operation at 80 km/h cruising.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Operating range:**

$$
\Delta V = V_{max} - V_{min} = 140.6 - 22 = 118.6 \text{ km/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Check cruise feasibility:**

The cruise speed of 80 km/h lies **between V_min (22) and V_max (140.6)**, so the truck can theoretically maintain 80 km/h in top gear. The *reserve tractive force* at 80 km/h determines whether it can climb moderate gradients:

**Step 3 — Convert 80 km/h to m/s:**

$$
V = 80 / 3.6 = 22.22 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Aerodynamic drag at 80 km/h:**

$$
F_a = 1.203125 \times (22.22)^2 = 1.203125 \times 493.83 = 594.16 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Rolling resistance:**

$$
F_r = 396 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Total resistance at 80 km/h (level road):**

$$
F_{res} = 396 + 594.16 = 990.16 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 7 — Available tractive force (already 2231.25 N) > 990.16 N**, so the truck has a reserve of ~1241 N for gradient climbing.

**Conclusion:** The mini-truck is **suitable for 80 km/h expressway cruising** in top gear, with sufficient reserve to handle gradients up to ~6 % without downshifting.

**Answer:** $\Delta V \approx 118.6$ km/h; **Yes, suitable.** *(7 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Speed Operating Range (Module 2, PEMRT413)**

- **Definition:** $\Delta V = V_{max} - V_{min}$ in **top gear** only.
- **Master speed equation:** $V_{km/h} = 0.377 \, N \, r \, / \, R_{total}$.
- **V_max** is found by force/power balance on a *level road* — drag dominates.
- **V_min** is found by force balance on the *steepest gradient* the vehicle must climb in top gear.
- **Overall ratio:** Always $R_{total} = R_g \times R_f$. For top gear in many cars, $R_g = 1.0$.
- **Tractive force:** $F_t = T_e \, \eta_t \, R_{total} \, / \, r$. Don't forget $\eta_t \approx 0.85 - 0.92$.
- **Drag constant:** $k_a = \rho \, C_d \, A \, / \, 2 \approx 0.4 - 0.5$ for typical sedans.
- **Gradient resistance:** $F_g = W \sin\theta \approx W \theta$ for small angles; for 4 % gradient, $\theta \approx 2.29°$.
- **Conversion:** $1 \text{ m/s} = 3.6 \text{ km/h}$.
- **Operating range widens with** higher torque, smaller $C_d A$, lower weight, lower gradient.
- **Operating range narrows with** higher gradient, higher rolling resistance, lower transmission efficiency.
- **Always state the assumption** that the analysis is for top gear unless specified otherwise — board examiners award 1 mark for explicit assumptions.
- **EV vs ICE:** EVs have infinite operating range in principle because electric motors deliver peak torque at 0 RPM.
- **HCVs vs passenger cars:** HCVs have narrow ranges → many gears; passenger cars have wide ranges → 5–6 gears.
- **Quadratic formula check:** Ensure $F_t > F_r$ before computing V_max; otherwise the vehicle cannot reach V_max in that gear.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constant Mesh Gear Boxes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT413 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constant Mesh Gear Boxes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Constant Mesh Gear Box** is a type of automotive transmission in which **all pairs of gears (on the main shaft and the countershaft/layshaft) remain permanently in mesh** with one another throughout the operation of the vehicle. Power transmission to the output (main) shaft is achieved by means of **sliding dog clutches** or **synchromesh synchronizing devices** that positively lock the selected gear to the shaft, rather than by physically sliding the gears into mesh.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT413 – Module 2):**
> Constant mesh gear boxes are studied as a major evolution over sliding mesh gear boxes. The student must be able to (i) describe the construction, (ii) trace the power flow in each gear, and (iii) compare it with sliding mesh and synchromesh arrangements.

**Standard Engineering Terminology (KTU 2024 Scheme Glossary):**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Main Shaft (Output Shaft)** | Shaft delivering power to the propeller shaft / final drive. |
| **Countershaft (Layshaft)** | Intermediate shaft carrying fixed gears that mesh with main-shaft gears. |
| **Dog Clutch** | A positive-engagement clutch with projecting teeth that slides along splines on the main shaft. |
| **Synchromesh Unit** | A device that equalizes the speed of two gears *before* dog engagement to allow clash-free shifting. |
| **Gear Reduction Ratio ($i$)** | Ratio of driven gear speed to driver gear speed, used to compute torque multiplication. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine two parallel railways (the two shafts). On each rail, several **locomotive wheels (gears) of different sizes** are placed, and the wheels on one rail are *always* touching the wheels on the other rail — they roll together all the time. However, the wheels on the output rail are mounted on **free-spinning axles (bearings)** so they rotate without driving the rail.

Now, to actually move the train (i.e., to drive the output shaft), a **mechanical pin (the dog clutch)** is inserted through a selected wheel, locking it to the rail. Once locked, that wheel forces the rail to turn at its speed.

- Different wheel sizes → different speeds (different gears).
- Switching the pin to a different wheel → gear change.
- Since wheels are *always* in contact, there is no grinding, no clashing, and far less wear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a 4-speed ceiling fan regulator. The fan motor's shaft has several resistor coils (gears) in continuous contact with the supply. Selecting a speed is analogous to choosing which coil to "lock" into the circuit. The coils are always connected; only the active path is changed.

## 1.3 Why Constant Mesh Was Invented — The Engineering Motivation

In **sliding mesh gear boxes** (the earlier design), gears had to be physically slid along splines until they meshed with the mating gear. This produced:

- Loud **clashing noise** during engagement,
- **Rapid wear** of gear teeth,
- **Difficulty in shifting** at high rpm,
- Limitation on the number of forward speeds.

The constant mesh design solved these problems by separating the functions of *transmitting motion* (done by the permanently meshed gears) and *selecting the gear* (done by the dog clutch).

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Mnemonic — "CM = Always Meshed, Clutch = Always Sliding":**
> In a Constant Mesh box, the **Gears** are always meshed; it is the **Dog Clutch** that slides to engage different ratios.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details

A typical 4-speed + reverse constant mesh gearbox has the following components:

1. **Clutch Shaft (Input Shaft):** Driven by the engine via the clutch. Carries a pinion that drives the countershaft gear.
2. **Countershaft (Layshaft):** Mounted parallel to the main shaft. All countershaft gears are **rigidly keyed or shrunk-fitted** to this shaft and rotate with it. The countershaft gear cluster typically includes:
   - 1st gear (largest diameter on countershaft, smallest on main shaft)
   - 2nd gear
   - 3rd gear
   - 4th/Top gear (smallest on countershaft, largest on main shaft)
3. **Main Shaft (Output Shaft):** All gears on this shaft rotate **freely on needle roller bearings or bronze bushings**. Each gear has an integral dog clutch ring (or a separate dog clutch sleeve is fitted between gears).
4. **Sliding Dog Clutches:** Externally splined sleeves that slide on the splined portion of the main shaft to engage the dog teeth of the chosen gear, locking it to the shaft.
5. **Reverse Idler Gear:** A small extra gear on a separate idler shaft that reverses the direction of rotation for the reverse gear.
6. **Gear-shift Lever & Fork Mechanism:** Actuates the sliding of the dog clutches.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Gear teeth are typically case-hardened (55–62 HRC) and precision-ground to AGMA **Quality Class 6–8** tolerances to handle the continuous meshing load.

## 2.2 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Why the gears can stay meshed without transmitting power:**

Because the gears on the main shaft are mounted on **bushings/needle bearings**, they can spin freely on the shaft even while being driven by the countershaft gears. Power flows through a gear only when it is **mechanically locked to the main shaft via its dog clutch**.

**How gear selection occurs:**

1. The driver operates the gear lever, which moves a selector fork.
2. The fork slides the appropriate dog clutch sleeve along the main shaft.
3. The dog teeth of the sleeve mesh with the dog teeth of the chosen gear.
4. That gear, previously free-wheeling, is now locked to the main shaft.
5. Since the gear is already being driven by its countershaft mate, the main shaft is now driven at the corresponding speed and torque.

**Why the gears don't clash:**

Because the dog clutch engages a gear that is **already rotating at exactly the right speed** (driven by the countershaft), there is no relative speed difference to cause a clash — *provided the driver matches engine speed to road speed* (which is what the modern synchromesh assists automatically).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable from gear kinematics** and are essential for KTU ESE numericals. The vertical bar $\vert$ is rendered using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Description | Standard Unit | Application in Constant Mesh Box |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $i = \dfrac{N_{driven}}{N_{driver}} = \dfrac{T_{driver}}{T_{driven}} = \dfrac{D_{driven}}{D_{driver}}$ | $i$ = gear ratio, $N$ = rpm, $T$ = teeth, $D$ = pitch circle diameter | dimensionless | Compute ratio of a single meshed pair |
| 2 | $N_{main} = N_{countershaft} \times \dfrac{T_{cs}}{T_{m}}$ | Speed relation across one mesh | rpm | Power-flow speed calculations |
| 3 | $T_{out} = T_{in} \times i \times \eta_{gear}$ | $T$ = torque, $\eta_{gear}$ = gear mesh efficiency (typically **0.96–0.98** per mesh) | N·m | Available torque at main shaft |
| 4 | $i_{total} = i_{gb} \times i_{fd} \times i_{diff}$ | $fd$ = final drive, $diff$ = differential | dimensionless | Overall drivetrain ratio |
| 5 | $V_{vehicle} = \dfrac{0.377 \times N_{engine} \times r_{wheel}}{i_{total}}$ (m/s) | $r$ = effective wheel radius | m/s | Vehicle speed for a given gear |
| 6 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{gb} \times \eta_{fd} \times \eta_{diff}$ | Combined drivetrain efficiency | dimensionless | Usually **0.85–0.90** for rear-wheel-drive cars |
| 7 | Tooth velocity $v = \dfrac{\pi \times D \times N}{60}$ | Pitch line velocity | m/s | Check for scuffing/noise limits |
| 8 | Module $m = \dfrac{D}{T} = \dfrac{p}{\pi}$ | $p$ = circular pitch | mm | Standard gear design |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** These same formulas are used by Tata Motors, Mahindra, Maruti (and their gearbox suppliers like Aisin, ZF) to design transmission ratios for BS-VI compliant vehicles. Engineers target a **1st-gear ratio of ~3.5:1** and a **top-gear ratio near 1:1 (direct drive)** for optimal acceleration and fuel economy.

## 2.4 Comparison: Constant Mesh vs. Sliding Mesh

| Feature | Sliding Mesh Box | Constant Mesh Box |
|---|---|---|
| Gears in mesh? | Only when engaged | **Always meshed** |
| Noise during shifting | Loud (clash) | **Quiet** |
| Gear material | Usually unhardened | **Case-hardened, ground** |
| Gear life | Shorter | **Longer** |
| Shifting effort | High | **Lower** |
| Manufacturing cost | Lower | **Higher** |
| Use today | **Obsolete** (vintage cars) | **Standard in modern manual cars** |
| Synchronization | None (or baulk rings in some) | **Synchromesh in modern versions** |

## 2.5 Power Transmission Path (Per Gear)

The "power flow" refers to tracing the path of torque from the clutch shaft, through the engaged gear pair, to the main shaft and out to the propeller shaft.

| Gear | Power Flow Path |
|---|---|
| **1st (Lowest)** | Clutch → CS gear → CS → 1st CS gear → 1st main gear (largest reduction) → dog clutch → main shaft. |
| **2nd** | Clutch → CS → 2nd CS gear → 2nd main gear → dog clutch → main shaft. |
| **3rd** | Clutch → CS → 3rd CS gear → 3rd main gear → dog clutch → main shaft. |
| **4th / Top** | Clutch → CS → 4th CS gear → 4th main gear → dog clutch → main shaft (smallest reduction). |
| **Direct Drive (5th in some)** | Clutch shaft **directly coupled** to main shaft via dog clutch; CS not used. |
| **Reverse** | Clutch → CS → reverse idler (reverses direction) → reverse main gear → dog clutch → main shaft. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fundamental Speed–Torque Relationship Across a Meshed Gear Pair

For two external spur gears in mesh, the linear pitch-line velocity must be equal at the point of contact (no slip in pure rolling contact).

**Step 1 — Equal pitch-line velocity condition:**

$$
v_{driver} = v_{driven}
$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of pitch-circle diameter and angular speed:**

$$
\frac{\pi \cdot D_{driver} \cdot N_{driver}}{60} = \frac{\pi \cdot D_{driven} \cdot N_{driven}}{60}
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the common factor $\pi / 60$:**

$$
D_{driver} \cdot N_{driver} = D_{driven} \cdot N_{driven}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain the gear ratio $i$:**

$$
i = \frac{N_{driven}}{N_{driver}} = \frac{D_{driver}}{D_{driven}}
$$

**Step 5 — Use the geometric relation $D = m \cdot T$ (where $m$ is the module and $T$ is the number of teeth):**

$$
i = \frac{m \cdot T_{driver}}{m \cdot T_{driven}} = \frac{T_{driver}}{T_{driven}}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the conservation of power (neglecting losses) to relate torque and speed:**

$$
P_{in} = P_{out} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_{in} \cdot \omega_{in} = T_{out} \cdot \omega_{out}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$ and simplify:**

$$
T_{in} \cdot N_{in} = T_{out} \cdot N_{out}
$$

**Step 8 — Conclude the torque relationship:**

$$
T_{out} = T_{in} \cdot \frac{N_{in}}{N_{out}} = T_{in} \cdot i
$$

**Step 9 — Include the gear-mesh efficiency $\eta_{gear}$ (since real gears lose ~2–4% per mesh):**

$$
\boxed{\,T_{out} = T_{in} \cdot i \cdot \eta_{gear}\,}
$$

This is the master relationship used in **all** KTU numericals on gearbox output torque and vehicle tractive effort.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> **Problem [Model Question]:**
> A 4-speed constant mesh gearbox has the following tooth counts on the main shaft and countershaft respectively: **1st: 40 / 20, 2nd: 35 / 25, 3rd: 30 / 30, 4th: 25 / 35.** The engine delivers a maximum torque of **150 N·m** at **3000 rpm**. The gear-mesh efficiency is **0.97 per mesh**. The final drive ratio is **4.1:1** and the differential ratio is **3.73:1**. The effective wheel radius is **0.32 m**. Determine:
> (i) The maximum vehicle speed in 4th gear.
> (ii) The torque available at the rear wheels in 1st gear.

**Given Data:**

$$
T_{engine} = 150 \text{ N·m}, \quad N_{engine} = 3000 \text{ rpm}, \quad \eta_{gear} = 0.97, \quad i_{fd} = 4.1, \quad i_{diff} = 3.73, \quad r_{wheel} = 0.32 \text{ m}
$$

**Tooth counts and corresponding gear ratios:**

$$
i_{1} = \frac{40}{20} = 2.00, \quad i_{2} = \frac{35}{25} = 1.40, \quad i_{3} = \frac{30}{30} = 1.00, \quad i_{4} = \frac{25}{35} \approx 0.714
$$

> **Part (i) — Maximum Vehicle Speed in 4th Gear**

**Step 1 — Compute the speed of the main shaft in 4th gear:**

$$
N_{main} = N_{cs} \times \frac{T_{cs,4}}{T_{m,4}}
$$

**Step 2 — Countershaft speed equals the clutch-shaft speed (since clutch pinion drives CS gear at 1:1 in most passenger cars):**

$$
N_{cs} \approx N_{clutch} = N_{engine} = 3000 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 4th-gear tooth ratio:**

$$
N_{main} = 3000 \times \frac{35}{25} = 3000 \times 1.4 = 4200 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the propeller-shaft speed (main shaft drives it directly):**

$$
N_{prop} = 4200 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute wheel speed using final drive and differential ratios:**

$$
N_{wheel} = \frac{N_{prop}}{i_{fd} \times i_{diff}} = \frac{4200}{4.1 \times 3.73} = \frac{4200}{15.293} \approx 274.7 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert wheel rpm to linear vehicle speed:**

$$
V = \frac{2 \pi r_{wheel} N_{wheel}}{60} = \frac{2 \times \pi \times 0.32 \times 274.7}{60}
$$

$$
V = \frac{552.5}{60} \approx 9.21 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to km/h:**

$$
\boxed{V_{max, 4^{th}} \approx 9.21 \times 3.6 \approx 33.1 \text{ km/h}}
$$

> **Part (ii) — Rear-Wheel Torque in 1st Gear**

**Step 1 — Apply torque multiplication through the gearbox in 1st gear:**

$$
T_{main} = T_{engine} \times i_{1} \times \eta_{gear} = 150 \times 2.00 \times 0.97 = 291.0 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply final drive torque multiplication:**

$$
T_{fd} = T_{main} \times i_{fd} \times \eta_{fd} = 291.0 \times 4.1 \times 0.97 \approx 1157.4 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply differential torque multiplication:**

$$
\boxed{T_{wheel} = T_{fd} \times i_{diff} \times \eta_{diff} = 1157.4 \times 3.73 \times 0.97 \approx 4188 \text{ N·m}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check (KTU Examiner Expectation):** A ~28× torque multiplication from engine to wheels in 1st gear is typical for a compact passenger car. The tractive force is $F_t = T_{wheel} / r_{wheel} = 4188 / 0.32 \approx 13{,}088$ N — enough to propel a 1200 kg vehicle up a moderate slope.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Power Flow Summary

| Gear | Engaged Dog Clutch Position | Active Gear Pair | Direction of Main Shaft | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1st | Engages 1st-gear dog | Largest CS / smallest main | Same as engine | Moving off, steep climb |
| 2nd | Engages 2nd-gear dog | Mid-large CS / mid-small main | Same as engine | City driving, gentle incline |
| 3rd | Engages 3rd-gear dog | Mid CS / mid main | Same as engine | Highway entry, overtaking |
| 4th | Engages 4th-gear dog | Small CS / large main | Same as engine | Cruising, fuel economy |
| 5th (overdrive, if fitted) | Direct-couple dog | None (countershaft idle) | Same as engine | High-speed cruise |
| Reverse | Engages reverse dog | CS → idler → reverse main gear | **Opposite** to engine | Reversing the vehicle |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Constant Mesh Gearbox

The Mermaid diagram below traces the power-flow architecture and decision logic of a constant-mesh transmission. It maps the relationship between the **input (clutch shaft)**, the **countershaft (intermediate)**, the **sliding dog-clutch selector**, and the **output (main shaft)** for each gear.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Flywheel] --> B[Clutch Plate]
    B --> C[Clutch Shaft / Input Pinion]
    C --> D[Countershaft Cluster]
    D --> D1[CS 1st Gear - Largest]
    D --> D2[CS 2nd Gear]
    D --> D3[CS 3rd Gear]
    D --> D4[CS 4th Gear - Smallest]
    D1 --> M1[Main 1st Gear - Free on bush]
    D2 --> M2[Main 2nd Gear - Free on bush]
    D3 --> M3[Main 3rd Gear - Free on bush]
    D4 --> M4[Main 4th Gear - Free on bush]
    M1 --> S{Dog Clutch Selector Fork}
    M2 --> S
    M3 --> S
    M4 --> S
    S -->|Position 1 - 1st Gear| P1[Lock M1 to Main Shaft]
    S -->|Position 2 - 2nd Gear| P2[Lock M2 to Main Shaft]
    S -->|Position 3 - 3rd Gear| P3[Lock M3 to Main Shaft]
    S -->|Position 4 - 4th Gear| P4[Lock M4 to Main Shaft]
    S -->|Position R - Reverse| P5[Engage Idler - Reverse Direction]
    P1 --> OUT[Main Output Shaft]
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
    P5 --> OUT
    OUT --> Q[Propeller Shaft]
    Q --> R[Final Drive and Differential]
    R --> W[Driving Wheels]
    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style OUT fill:#9CD3A6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S fill:#F8B4B4,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
    style W fill:#7FB3D5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Component Cross-Section Schematic (Text-Based Layout)

The figure below is an ASCII cross-section of a typical 4-speed constant-mesh gearbox, viewed along the axis of the main shaft. Read each row from **left (clutch end)** to **right (propeller-shaft end)**.

```
        CLUTCH END                                                PROP-SHAFT END
        ===========                                                ================
        | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |
   (A) ( 1 ) ( 2 ) ( 3 ) ( 4 ) [DC] ( R ) ( DRV ) ( 5 ) ......... (B)
        | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |

Legend (left to right):
  (A)  Clutch shaft input splines
   1   1st-gear sliding dog (engaged in 1st)
   2   2nd-gear sliding dog (engaged in 2nd)
   3   3rd-gear sliding dog (engaged in 3rd)
   4   4th-gear sliding dog (engaged in 4th)
  [DC] Direct-coupling dog (engaged in 5th / overdrive)
   R   Reverse sliding dog (engaged in R)
  (DRV) Drive flange coupling to propeller shaft
   5   Output speed pickup / speedometer drive gear
  (B)  Output flange / pinion to differential
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a real gearbox, the **countershaft** lies **below** the main shaft, parallel to it. All CS gears are **fixed** to the CS and mesh continuously with the corresponding free-running gears on the main shaft. The diagram above shows only the main-shaft layout for clarity.

## 4.3 Power-Flow Diagram — 2nd Gear (Selected)

```
[Engine] --> [Clutch] --> [Clutch Shaft] =pinion=>
                                    |
                                    v
                            [Countershaft rotates]
                                    |
                            CS 2nd Gear (fixed)
                                    | (always meshed)
                                    v
                            Main 2nd Gear (free)
                                    |
                            [Dog Clutch slides + locks]
                                    |
                                    v
                            [Main Shaft rotates] ---> [Propeller Shaft]
```

## 4.4 Power-Flow Diagram — Reverse Gear (Selected)

```
[Engine] --> [Clutch] --> [Clutch Shaft] =pinion=>
                                    |
                                    v
                            [Countershaft rotates]
                                    |
                            CS Reverse Pinion (fixed)
                                    |
                                    v
                            [REVERSE IDLER GEAR - external]
                                    | (rotates in OPPOSITE direction)
                                    v
                            Main Reverse Gear (free)
                                    |
                            [Dog Clutch slides + locks]
                                    |
                                    v
                            [Main Shaft rotates in REVERSE] ---> [Prop Shaft]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reverse Idler Trick:** Note that the idler gear is *not* fixed to the countershaft — it is mounted on a separate stationary idler pin. Its sole purpose is to **reverse the direction of rotation** without changing the magnitude of the ratio.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a constant mesh gear box. List any two advantages over the sliding mesh type.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A constant mesh gear box consists of:
1. A **clutch shaft** carrying the input pinion.
2. A **countershaft (layshaft)** on which all countershaft gears are **rigidly fixed**.
3. A **main (output) shaft** on which all gears are mounted on **bushings/needle bearings** and rotate freely.
4. **Sliding dog clutches** that engage the chosen gear to the main shaft.

All gear pairs remain **permanently in mesh**.

**Advantages over sliding mesh:**
- Quiet gear shifting (no clashing).
- Gears can be case-hardened and precision-ground, giving **longer life**.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for components, 1 mark for diagram explanation, 1 mark for advantages.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is the function of a dog clutch in a constant mesh gear box? Why is it preferred over sliding gears in modern vehicles?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **dog clutch** is a positive-locking device with projecting teeth on its inner circumference. It slides along the splined main shaft and engages the matching dog teeth of the selected gear, **locking that gear to the main shaft** to transmit power.

It is preferred over sliding gears because:
- The dog clutch engages a gear that is **already rotating at matched speed** → no clashing noise.
- The main-shaft gears can remain **always meshed** → easier shifting, less wear, smoother operation.

*(Allocation: 1.5 marks for function, 1.5 marks for advantages.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** (full 14 marks).

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working** of a constant mesh gear box. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Clutch shaft** carries the input pinion from the engine.
- **Countershaft (layshaft)** has all gears **fixed** to it: typically one each for 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th, and reverse.
- **Main shaft (output shaft)** has gears mounted on **bushings/needle roller bearings** so they spin freely.
- **Sliding dog clutches** are positioned between adjacent gears and slide on the main-shaft splines.
- **Reverse idler** on a separate stationary pin reverses direction.
- A **gear-shift lever** with selector forks moves the dog clutches.

**Working (4 marks):**
- Engine torque → clutch → clutch shaft → pinion → countershaft (which rotates as a unit).
- Since all gear pairs are always meshed, the main-shaft gears also rotate but are **free-wheeling** (no torque transmitted).
- When the driver selects a gear (say 3rd), the dog clutch slides and **positively engages** the dog teeth of the 3rd-gear main-shaft gear.
- That gear, which is already rotating at the correct speed (driven by its CS mate), is now **locked to the main shaft**, transmitting torque to the propeller shaft.
- Different gear sizes give different speed-torque combinations.
- For reverse, the **idler gear** is engaged in the path, reversing the direction of rotation of the main-shaft gear.

---

**(b)** Draw and explain the **power flow diagram for 2nd gear and reverse gear** of a constant mesh gearbox. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**2nd Gear Power Flow (3.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Engine} \rightarrow \text{Clutch} \rightarrow \text{Clutch shaft} \rightarrow \text{CS pinion} \rightarrow \text{Countershaft (rotates)} \rightarrow \text{2nd-gear CS gear (fixed)} \rightarrow \text{2nd-gear Main gear (free)} \rightarrow \text{Dog clutch locks} \rightarrow \text{Main shaft} \rightarrow \text{Propeller shaft}
$$

**Reverse Gear Power Flow (3.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Engine} \rightarrow \text{Clutch} \rightarrow \text{Clutch shaft} \rightarrow \text{Countershaft (rotates)} \rightarrow \text{Reverse CS gear} \rightarrow \text{Reverse Idler gear (reverses direction)} \rightarrow \text{Reverse main gear} \rightarrow \text{Dog clutch locks} \rightarrow \text{Main shaft (rotates REVERSE)} \rightarrow \text{Propeller shaft}
$$

**Key Difference:** In 2nd gear, the power flows through a *single mesh* (CS-to-Main); in reverse, an *extra idler gear* is inserted, which adds one more mesh and **reverses the direction of rotation**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **sliding mesh and constant mesh gear boxes** in detail. Mention typical applications of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Sliding Mesh Box | Constant Mesh Box |
|---|---|---|
| Gear meshing | Slid into mesh only when selected | **Always meshed** |
| Engagement method | Sliding gear itself engages | Dog clutch engages free gear |
| Noise | Loud clashing | **Quiet** |
| Gear material | Mild steel, unhardened | **Case-hardened, ground** |
| Gear life | Short | **Long** |
| Shifting effort | High | **Lower** |
| Manufacturing cost | Low | Higher |
| Power loss | Higher (impact loading) | **Lower** (steady loading) |
| Application today | **Vintage / off-road heavy machinery** | **All modern manual cars & LCVs** |
| Maximum speeds | Limited by impact | **Higher sustained speeds** |

*(Allocation: 5 marks for tabular comparison, 1 mark for applications, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

**(b)** Explain the **working of a synchromesh gear box** with a neat sketch. How does it overcome the limitation of a basic constant mesh box? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

A **synchromesh gear box** is an advanced form of the constant mesh box in which **synchronizing devices** are fitted on the main shaft between adjacent gear pairs.

**Components (2 marks):**
- Main-shaft gears (free on bushings).
- **Synchronizer assembly:** consisting of a **synchronizing hub** (splined to main shaft), a **sleeve** (slides on hub splines), and **baulk rings** (with internal dog teeth and external friction cone).

**Working (4 marks):**
1. When the driver moves the gear lever toward a target gear, the sleeve pushes against the baulk ring of the target gear.
2. Friction between the **cone of the baulk ring** and the **cone of the gear** brings the sleeve speed and the gear speed to **the same angular velocity** (synchronization).
3. Once speeds match, the chamfered dog teeth align, and the sleeve slides over the dog teeth of the gear → positive engagement.
4. Power now flows from the gear to the main shaft via the synchronizer.

**Overcoming the constant-mesh limitation (1 mark):**
- In a basic constant-mesh box, mismatched gear and shaft speeds cause a **rattle or clash** at the dog clutch.
- The synchromesh **automatically equalizes speeds before engagement**, giving **smooth, clash-free shifting** at all engine speeds — a key reason it is the universal standard in modern cars (e.g., Maruti Swift, Hyundai Creta manual variants).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "constant mesh" with "synchromesh":** A constant-mesh box *may* or may not have synchromesh units. Mentioning synchromesh is optional unless the question specifically asks.
> 2. **Forgetting the efficiency term:** In numericals, students often write $T_{out} = T_{in} \times i$ and **lose 2 marks** for omitting $\eta_{gear}$ (typically 0.97).
> 3. **Wrong direction for reverse gear:** The reverse idler must be drawn as a **separate gear on a separate shaft**, NOT as a gear on the countershaft. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this is missed.
> 4. **Missing the role of the dog clutch:** Several students describe only the gears and forget to explain *how* a free gear is locked to the main shaft. Always mention the **dog clutch engagement** as the final step in power flow.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency in vehicle speed:** KTU expects the final answer in **m/s OR km/h — be consistent**. Do not mix units mid-calculation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Constant Mesh Gear Box**

- **Definition:** All gear pairs are *always meshed*; gear selection is done by **sliding dog clutches** (or synchromesh units).
- **Key components:** Clutch shaft, countershaft (fixed gears), main shaft (free gears on bushings), dog clutches, reverse idler, shift forks.
- **Two functions separated:** (1) Motion transmission → always-meshed gears; (2) Ratio selection → sliding dog clutch.
- **Master formula for output torque:**

$$
T_{out} = T_{in} \times i_{gearbox} \times i_{fd} \times i_{diff} \times \eta_{overall}
$$

- **Vehicle speed formula:**

$$
V = \frac{2 \pi r_{wheel} N_{engine}}{60 \times i_{total}} \quad [\text{m/s}]
$$

- **Standard gear-mesh efficiency:** 0.96–0.98 per mesh; overall drivetrain efficiency ≈ 0.85–0.90.
- **Reverse gear principle:** Achieved by inserting an **idler gear** that adds an extra mesh and reverses rotation direction.
- **Direct drive (5th gear / overdrive):** Countershaft may be **disengaged**; clutch shaft couples *directly* to the main shaft.
- **Advantages over sliding mesh:** Quiet, less wear, longer gear life, easier shifting, higher speed capability.
- **Disadvantage vs. sliding mesh:** More complex construction, higher cost.
- **Modern evolution:** Almost all modern manual cars use **synchromesh constant-mesh** boxes (e.g., 5-speed and 6-speed manuals).
- **Common KTU numerical traps:**
  - Always multiply by $\eta$ for each mesh (typically 0.97).
  - In the reverse path, the **idler gear itself does not change the magnitude** of the ratio — it only reverses direction.
  - Direct-drive 5th gear has $i_{gb} = 1$ but the **countershaft still rotates**; only the main-shaft dog clutch locks directly to the clutch shaft.
- **Famous real-world examples:** Maruti Alto 5-speed, Hyundai i20 6-speed manual, Tata Nexon AMT — all derivatives of the constant-mesh architecture (with synchromesh).
- **Mnemonic:** **"C-M = Constantly Meshed, D-C = Dogs Clutch to Select"**.

---

✅ **End of Module 2 — Constant Mesh Gear Boxes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PEMRT413)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchromesh Gear Boxes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT413 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Synchromesh Gear Boxes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Synchromesh Gear Box** (also called a *Synchromesh Transmission* or *Synchronized Transmission*) is a refined form of a **constant-mesh gear box** in which the speeds of the **gears to be engaged and the driven shaft are first automatically synchronized (equated) by a friction device** before the actual positive locking of the gears takes place through dog clutches. The mechanism that performs this speed-equalization function is called a **Synchronizer** or **Synchromesh Device**.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The synchronizer unit is the single most examinable component in a modern automobile gear box. It is responsible for:
> 1. **Speed Synchronization** (Friction Phase) — using blocking / baulk rings.
> 2. **Positive Locking** (Engagement Phase) — using dog clutch teeth on the sleeve.
> 3. **Reverse Gear Lock-out** — preventing accidental reverse engagement.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Elevator Door" Intuition

Imagine you are trying to board a moving escalator:

- **Sliding Mesh Gear Box (Old Technology):** You must *time your jump* perfectly to match the escalator's speed, otherwise you will stumble and the gears will clash and grind.
- **Synchromesh Gear Box (Modern Technology):** An invisible, gentle **"magnetic friction brake"** automatically slows the moving steps of the escalator to match *your* walking speed. Once speeds match, you step on smoothly and lock into place. The escalator steps (synchronizer) ensure a clash-free, effortless transition.

In engineering terms: the **synchronizer ring (baulk ring)** acts as a friction clutch that pre-rotates the gear to match the shaft speed, *before* the dog teeth ever touch.

### 1.3 Need for Synchromesh — Why It Was Invented

| Problem in Sliding/Constant Mesh | How Synchromesh Solves It |
|---|---|
| Gear clash and grinding noise during shifting | Friction pre-synchronization eliminates clash |
| Driver must double-declutch (skill-dependent) | Automatic synchronization — no skill needed |
| Wear of dog teeth due to impact loading | Soft, gradual friction engagement → long life |
| High noise during engagement | Near-silent gear changes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** Modern synchromesh units can synchronize speeds up to **3,000 rpm differential** in approximately **0.1 to 0.3 seconds** before the dog teeth engage. The friction torque generated during this phase must overcome the inertia of the gear-set.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Three Sequential Phases

The operation of a synchromesh unit is divided into **three distinct, sequential phases**. KTU examiners frequently test these phases in 7-mark sub-questions.

**Phase 1 — Neutral Position (Disengaged)**
- The sliding sleeve is in the central neutral position.
- The synchronizer hub is splined to the main shaft (output shaft).
- The gear is free to rotate on the shaft via needle rollers.
- No power transmission occurs; no friction is applied.

**Phase 2 — Synchronization (Friction Phase)**
- The driver pushes the gear lever → the shift fork moves the sliding sleeve laterally.
- The internal chamfered cone of the sleeve contacts the external cone of the **blocking ring (baulk ring)**.
- Friction between the cone surfaces causes the **gear and the baulk ring to be accelerated/decelerated** until their angular velocities equal the sleeve's rotational speed.
- This is also called the **"pre-synchronization"** or **"friction phase."**

**Phase 3 — Positive Locking (Engagement Phase)**
- Once speeds are equal, the **"key slots"** or **recesses** in the blocking ring align with the **dog teeth** on the side of the gear.
- The sleeve slides over the dog teeth, locking the gear rigidly to the shaft.
- Power is now transmitted positively through the splines.

### 2.2 Detailed Component Breakdown

| Component | Function | Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Synchronizer Hub** | Splined to main shaft; carries the sliding sleeve | Hardened steel |
| **Sliding Sleeve** | Moves axially via shift fork; has internal dog teeth | Hardened steel with bronze inserts |
| **Blocking Ring (Baulk Ring)** | Friction cone ring; prevents dog engagement until sync is complete | Brass or sintered bronze |
| **Synchronizer Springs** | Centering springs that return sleeve to neutral | Spring steel |
| **Shift Fork** | Actuated by gear lever; moves sleeve axially | Forged steel |
| **Dog Teeth** | Positive locking teeth on gear side / sleeve internal | Case-hardened steel |
| **Strut / Keys (in some designs)** | Sliding keys for torque transmission in pin-type synchronizers | Hardened steel |

### 2.3 Types of Synchronizers

#### A. Single Cone (Single Plate) Synchronizer
- Uses **one** friction cone surface.
- Common in light to medium-duty vehicles.
- Simpler design, lower cost.

#### B. Double Cone (Double Plate) Synchronizer
- Uses **two** friction cone surfaces in series.
- Common in heavy-duty trucks and high-performance cars.
- Higher friction torque → faster synchronization.
- Better heat dissipation.

#### C. Triple Cone Synchronizer
- Used in very high-torque applications (heavy commercial vehicles).

#### D. Pin-Type Synchronizer (e.g., Porsche design)
- Uses sliding pins/keys instead of dog teeth.
- Smaller axial length, more compact.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The formulas below are derived from first principles and are universally valid for any friction-based synchronizer design.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_f = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot r_m$ | Friction torque developed at cone surface | N·m |
| 2 | $T_i = I \cdot \alpha = I \cdot \dfrac{\Delta\omega}{\Delta t}$ | Inertial torque of the gear-set | N·m |
| 3 | $T_f \geq T_i$ | **Condition for synchronization** (friction must overcome inertia) | N·m |
| 4 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{I \cdot \Delta\omega}{\mu \cdot F_n \cdot r_m}$ | Time required to synchronize | s |
| 5 | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from rpm | rad/s |
| 6 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Power transmitted after engagement | W (Watts) |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{F_n}{A_c}$ | Contact pressure on cone surface | N/m² (Pa) |
| 8 | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\mu}{\sec\theta}\right)$ | Self-locking condition of cone (where $\theta$ is half-cone angle) | rad |

**Where:**

- $\mu$ = Coefficient of friction (brass-on-steel ≈ **0.08 to 0.12**)
- $F_n$ = Normal force applied by shift fork (typically **200–600 N**)
- $r_m$ = Mean radius of friction cone (≈ **30–60 mm**)
- $I$ = Mass moment of inertia of gear-set
- $\Delta\omega$ = Angular speed difference (rad/s)
- $\theta$ = Half-angle of friction cone (typically **6° to 8°**)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Passenger Cars:** Maruti Suzuki Alto, Hyundai Creta, Tata Nexon — all use 5-speed/6-speed synchromesh manual transmissions.
- **Commercial Vehicles:** Ashok Leyland, Tata Trucks — use double/triple cone synchronizers in 6 to 9-speed gear boxes.
- **Racing:** Formula 1 cars use **paddle-shift sequential synchromesh** boxes with hydraulic actuation for shift times under **20 ms**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Synchronization Time

> **Problem Statement:** A synchromesh unit must synchronize a gear rotating at $N_1 = 3000 \text{ rpm}$ to the shaft speed of $N_2 = 1500 \text{ rpm}$. The gear-set has a mass moment of inertia $I = 0.05 \text{ kg·m}^2$. The friction coefficient between the brass blocking ring and steel cone is $\mu = 0.10$, the mean cone radius is $r_m = 0.045 \text{ m}$, and the shift fork applies a normal force of $F_n = 400 \text{ N}$. Find the synchronization time $\Delta t$.

**Step 1 — Convert speeds to angular velocities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_1 &= \frac{2\pi N_1}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 100\pi \approx 314.159 \text{ rad/s} \\
\omega_2 &= \frac{2\pi N_2}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.080 \text{ rad/s} \\
\Delta\omega &= \omega_1 - \omega_2 = 50\pi \approx 157.080 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The difference in angular velocity is what the friction cone must "absorb" to equate the two speeds.

**Step 2 — Compute the friction torque developed at the cone:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= \mu \cdot F_n \cdot r_m \\
&= 0.10 \times 400 \times 0.045 \\
&= 1.80 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The friction torque is the product of the tangential friction force ($\mu F_n$) acting at the mean radius. This is the only retarding/accelerating torque available to the system.

**Step 3 — Compute the inertial torque required to stop the gear:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_i &= I \cdot \alpha = I \cdot \frac{\Delta\omega}{\Delta t} \\
0.05 \times \frac{157.080}{\Delta t} &= \frac{7.854}{\Delta t} \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* By Newton's second law for rotation, the inertial torque is the inertia times the angular deceleration. This must be exactly matched by the friction torque for synchronization to occur.

**Step 4 — Apply the synchronization condition $T_f \geq T_i$ and solve for $\Delta t$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= T_i \\
1.80 &= \frac{7.854}{\Delta t} \\
\Delta t &= \frac{7.854}{1.80} \\
\Delta t &\approx 4.363 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This synchronization time is quite long, indicating that for a real automotive application, the design parameters ($F_n$, $\mu$, or $r_m$) would need to be increased, OR a double-cone synchronizer would be used to effectively double $T_f$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Self-Locking Cone Condition

> **Problem Statement:** For a synchromesh cone with half-angle $\theta = 7°$ and friction coefficient $\mu = 0.10$, determine if the cone is self-locking (i.e., does it tend to release or grip under axial load?).

**Step 1 — Write the self-locking condition:**

For a cone to be self-locking, the friction angle $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$ must be greater than the half-cone angle $\theta$:

$$
\phi > \theta \;\;\text{(Self-locking)} \quad ; \quad \phi < \theta \;\;\text{(Self-releasing)}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the friction angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \tan^{-1}(\mu) = \tan^{-1}(0.10) \\
\phi &\approx 5.711° 
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the half-cone angle:**

$$
\phi = 5.711° < \theta = 7°
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result:**

Since $\phi < \theta$, the cone is **self-releasing**. This means the axial force $F_n$ from the shift fork will naturally tend to **push the cone surfaces apart**, which is *exactly* what we want during the synchronization phase — the gear can "slip" freely to equalize speeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** If the cone were self-locking ($\phi > \theta$), the friction would "grip" and prevent synchronization, causing the cone to lock and the dog teeth to clash — defeating the entire purpose of the synchromesh unit. Hence, designers deliberately choose $\theta \approx 6°$ to $8°$ and brass/bronze blocking rings to ensure $\mu$ remains low.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Synchromesh vs. Sliding Mesh vs. Constant Mesh

| Parameter | Sliding Mesh | Constant Mesh | Synchromesh |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gears in constant mesh? | No | Yes | Yes |
| Dog clutch / sliding gear used? | Sliding gear | Dog clutch | Synchronizer sleeve + baulk ring |
| Speed synchronization? | No (manual) | No (manual via double-clutching) | **Yes (automatic)** |
| Noise during shifting | Very High | Moderate | **Very Low** |
| Driver skill required | High | Moderate | **Low** |
| Wear of dog teeth | Very High | High | **Low** |
| Power transmission efficiency | 95–96% | 97–98% | **97–98%** |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | **Highest** |
| Application | Old vehicles (1940s) | Older trucks | **All modern manual cars** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Synchromesh Unit Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Input Gear Lever] --> B[Shift Fork Actuation]
    B --> C[Sliding Sleeve Axial Motion]
    C --> D{Phase 1 Neutral}
    D --> E[Phase 2 Friction Contact]
    E --> F[Blocking Ring Cone Friction]
    F --> G[Speed Equalization Omega1 = Omega2]
    G --> H{Phase 3 Dog Teeth Alignment}
    H --> I[Positive Locking via Sleeve]
    I --> J[Power Transmission to Main Shaft]
    J --> K[Output to Propeller Shaft]
    D --> L[Reverse Lockout Plunger]
    L --> M[Reverse Idler Engagement]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Cross-Sectional Schematic (Mermaid Block Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    MainShaft[Main Output Shaft] --> Hub[Synchronizer Hub Splined to Shaft]
    Hub --> Sleeve[Sliding Sleeve with Internal Dog Teeth]
    Sleeve -->|Axial Force from Shift Fork| Fork[Shift Fork]
    Fork -->|Driver Force| Lever[External Gear Lever]
    
    GearA[Drive Gear Rotating Freely] -->|External Dog Teeth| Sleeve
    GearA -->|External Friction Cone| BaulkRing[Blocking Ring Baulk Ring]
    BaulkRing -->|Internal Friction Cone| Sleeve
    
    SpringA[Centering Spring 1] --> Sleeve
    SpringB[Centering Spring 2] --> Sleeve
    
    KeySlot[Key Slot in Baulk Ring] -->|Aligns after sync| DogTeeth[External Dog Teeth on Gear]
```

### 4.3 Power Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Engine[Engine Crankshaft] --> Clutch[Clutch Plate]
    Clutch --> InputShaft[Transmission Input Shaft]
    InputShaft --> ConstGearSet[Constant Mesh Gear Pair]
    ConstGearSet --> SyncUnit[Synchromesh Unit on Main Shaft]
    SyncUnit --> MainShaft[Main Output Shaft]
    MainShaft --> PropShaft[Propeller Shaft to Differential]
    PropShaft --> RearAxle[Rear Axle and Wheels]
```

### 4.4 Synchronization Sequence Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Gear Lever Engaged]) --> S1[Move Sleeve Toward Target Gear]
    S1 --> S2[Chamfered Cone Contacts Baulk Ring]
    S2 --> S3{Friction Torque Sufficient?}
    S3 -->|No| S4[Blocking Ring Prevents Dog Engagement]
    S4 --> S5[Driver Applies More Force on Lever]
    S5 --> S3
    S3 -->|Yes| S6[Speeds Equalize via Friction]
    S6 --> S7[Blocking Ring Recedes Sideways]
    S7 --> S8[Dog Teeth Align Through Key Slots]
    S8 --> S9[Sleeve Locks Gear to Shaft]
    S9 --> End([Power Transmitted Smoothly])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a synchromesh gear box. Mention any two advantages it offers over a constant-mesh gear box.*

**Model Answer:**
A synchromesh gear box is a type of constant-mesh gear box fitted with a **synchronizing device (synchronizer)** that automatically equates the speeds of the gear to be engaged and the main shaft by means of friction, before positive locking is achieved through dog teeth.

**Two advantages over constant-mesh gear box:**
1. **Smooth and clash-free gear shifting** — since speeds are pre-synchronized by friction, no gear clash occurs.
2. **No double-clutching required** — synchronization is automatic, reducing driver effort and skill dependency.

*(Alternative advantages: quieter operation, longer dog-tooth life, easier and faster shifting.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the function of a blocking ring (baulk ring) in a synchromesh unit?*

**Model Answer:**
The **blocking ring (baulk ring)** is a friction ring with an internal cone surface made of brass or sintered bronze. Its primary functions are:
1. To **create friction** with the gear's external cone surface during the pre-synchronization phase, thereby equalizing speeds.
2. To **mechanically block** the sleeve from sliding over the dog teeth until synchronization is complete — this is the "blocking" action that prevents gear clash and protects the dog teeth from impact damage.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below are two alternative full questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a single-cone synchromesh gear box**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
The single-cone synchromesh unit consists of the following components (refer to Section 4 diagrams):

1. **Synchronizer Hub:** Splined to the main shaft, with external splines.
2. **Sliding Sleeve:** Has internal splines that engage with the hub's external splines. Has internal dog teeth at both ends.
3. **Blocking Ring (Baulk Ring):** Located on each side of the sleeve, has an internal cone surface and external dog teeth.
4. **Shift Fork:** Engages the groove in the sleeve to move it axially.
5. **Centering Springs:** Located in the hub grooves, push the sleeve back to neutral.
6. **Dog Teeth:** External teeth on the gear side, internal teeth on the sleeve.

**Working (Three Phases):**

*Phase 1 — Neutral:* The sleeve sits in the central position held by the centering springs. The gear rotates freely on the main shaft via needle roller bearings. No power flows.

*Phase 2 — Synchronization (Friction):* When the driver moves the lever, the shift fork pushes the sleeve toward the target gear. The **internal chamfered cone of the sleeve** contacts the **external cone of the blocking ring**, which is keyed to the gear. Friction is generated, and the gear's speed is gradually matched to the shaft speed.

*Phase 3 — Engagement (Locking):* Once speeds are equal, the **"blocking" effect** of the ring (which was resisting sleeve motion) disappears. The external dog teeth of the gear align with the key slots in the ring, and the sleeve slides over them, locking the gear positively to the main shaft. Power flows from gear → dog teeth → sleeve → hub → main shaft.

---

**(b)** A synchromesh unit must synchronize a gear of mass moment of inertia $I = 0.08 \text{ kg·m}^2$ from $N_1 = 2500 \text{ rpm}$ to the shaft speed of $N_2 = 1000 \text{ rpm}$. The friction coefficient is $\mu = 0.09$, the mean cone radius is $r_m = 0.05 \text{ m}$, and the shift fork applies a normal force of $F_n = 500 \text{ N}$. Calculate the **synchronization time**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Angular velocities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_1 &= \frac{2\pi \times 2500}{60} = 261.799 \text{ rad/s} \\
\omega_2 &= \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.720 \text{ rad/s} \\
\Delta\omega &= 261.799 - 104.720 = 157.080 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Friction torque:**

$$
T_f = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot r_m = 0.09 \times 500 \times 0.05 = 2.25 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Synchronization time:**

$$
\Delta t = \frac{I \cdot \Delta\omega}{T_f} = \frac{0.08 \times 157.080}{2.25} = \frac{12.566}{2.25} \approx 5.585 \text{ s}
$$

**Result:** $\Delta t \approx 5.59 \text{ seconds}$ (indicates the design needs optimization for real-time automotive use).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Compare **single-cone, double-cone, and pin-type synchronizers**. State two applications of each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Single-Cone | Double-Cone | Pin-Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Friction surfaces | 1 | 2 (in series) | 1 (pin friction) |
| Synchronization torque | Lowest | Highest (≈ 2× single) | Moderate |
| Axial length | Longest | Longest | **Shortest** |
| Heat dissipation | Poor | Good (more area) | Moderate |
| Cost | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex | Moderate |
| Application 1 | Maruti Alto (1st-gen) | Tata LPT trucks (6-speed) | Porsche manual boxes |
| Application 2 | Older Hyundai Santro | Ashok Leyland Dost | BMW M-series (older) |

**Conclusion:** Double-cone is preferred for **high-torque heavy vehicles**; pin-type for **compact sports cars**; single-cone for **economy passenger cars**.

---

**(b)** Explain with a sketch the **working of a synchromesh gear box** when shifting from **3rd gear to 4th gear**. What is the role of the centering spring? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Working — 3rd to 4th gear shift:**
