1. The driver disengages the clutch and pushes the gear lever toward the 4th-gear position.
2. The shift fork moves the sliding sleeve leftward (toward the 4th-gear side).
3. The sleeve's left-side chamfered cone presses against the **right-side blocking ring of the 4th gear**.
4. Friction develops between the cone and ring; the 4th gear, which was rotating faster (free on the shaft), is decelerated to match the main shaft speed.
5. Once speeds match, the **blocking ring's dog teeth** align with the **sleeve's internal teeth**, and the sleeve slides over them.
6. The 4th gear is now positively locked to the main shaft; power flows from input shaft → 4th-gear pair → main shaft → propeller shaft.
7. The driver re-engages the clutch smoothly.

**Role of the Centering Spring:**
The centering springs are located in grooves on the synchronizer hub. Their functions are:
- To **automatically return the sliding sleeve to the central neutral position** when the driver releases the gear lever, ensuring no gear remains partially engaged.
- To **prevent accidental gear engagement** when the lever is in the rest position, improving safety.
- To provide **tactile feedback** to the driver through the lever.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing "Blocking Ring" with "Dog Clutch"** — The blocking ring is a *friction* device; the dog clutch is a *positive locking* device. They are sequential, not parallel.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention "friction phase before dog engagement"** — This is the *defining feature* of a synchromesh box. Skipping it loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Units Confusion** — Always convert rpm to rad/s using $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ before using $\Delta t = \frac{I \cdot \Delta\omega}{T_f}$. KTU examiners deduct heavily for unit errors.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** — A neat labeled sketch of the synchromesh unit is worth **at least 2–3 marks** in any 7-mark construction question.
> 5. **Not stating the self-locking condition of the cone** — In derivations, students forget to mention $\phi < \theta$ for the cone to function as a friction synchronizer.
> 6. **Mixing up "constant mesh" and "synchromesh"** — A synchromesh box IS a constant-mesh box, but with synchronizers added. The constant-mesh feature refers to the gear pair always being in mesh.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A synchromesh gear box is a constant-mesh box with **automatic speed-equalization** via friction before dog-tooth engagement.
- **Core Components:** Synchronizer hub, sliding sleeve, blocking ring (baulk ring), shift fork, centering springs, dog teeth.
- **Three Phases of Operation:** Neutral → Friction (Sync) → Positive Locking (Engagement).
- **Key Formula:** $\Delta t = \frac{I \cdot \Delta\omega}{\mu \cdot F_n \cdot r_m}$ — synchronization time.
- **Self-Locking Condition:** Cone is *self-releasing* if $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu) < \theta$ (half-cone angle).
- **Typical Cone Angles:** $\theta = 6°$ to $8°$ (deliberately chosen for self-releasing behavior).
- **Typical Friction Pair:** Brass blocking ring on hardened steel cone; $\mu \approx 0.08$ to $0.12$.
- **Types:** Single-cone, double-cone, triple-cone, pin-type.
- **Advantages:** Smooth, quiet, clash-free shifts; no double-clutching needed; long dog-tooth life.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher cost, more complex, more components, slightly heavier.
- **Applications:** All modern manual transmission cars (5-speed and 6-speed), commercial vehicle gear boxes, racing sequential boxes.
- **Reverse Gear:** Generally achieved via an **idler gear** and a dedicated **reverse synchronizer** or sliding dog clutch, often with a lockout plunger to prevent accidental engagement.
- **Power Flow:** Engine → Clutch → Input Shaft → Constant-Mesh Gear Pair → Synchromesh Sleeve → Main Shaft → Propeller Shaft → Differential → Wheels.
- **Self-releasing cone** is the geometric key that allows friction synchronization to occur at all.
- **Double-cone** synchronizers are used in heavy vehicles to roughly **double the friction torque** and halve the synchronization time.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw a labeled cross-section showing the hub, sleeve, blocking ring, and dog teeth — this alone can earn 2–3 marks.
- **Real-World Spec:** Modern synchromesh units synchronize in **0.1–0.3 seconds** for typical passenger car shift events.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Transmission - Epicyclic Gear Box, Torque Convertor, Over Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT413 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Automatic Transmission - Epicyclic Gear Box, Torque Convertor, Over Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Automatic Transmission — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automatic Transmission** is a multi-speed vehicle transmission system that automatically (without driver intervention) selects the most appropriate gear ratio based on vehicle speed, engine load, accelerator position, and other operating parameters. In KTU 2024 terminology, it is defined as a transmission system comprising an **Epicyclic (Planetary) Gear Box** for ratio selection, a **Hydraulic Torque Converter** for fluid coupling and torque multiplication, and a hydraulic/electronic control unit (valve body/TCU) for shift scheduling.

The three key sub-systems covered in this module are:

| Sub-System | Primary Function |
|---|---|
| **Epicyclic Gear Box** | Provides multiple forward speeds, reverse, and overdrive using a compact coaxial gear arrangement. |
| **Torque Converter** | Replaces the friction clutch; provides smooth fluid coupling, **torque multiplication up to 1.7–2.5×**, and engine damping. |
| **Overdrive Unit** | Permits output shaft to rotate faster than input shaft, reducing engine RPM at cruising speeds. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Intuitive View"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Lazy Susan with Three Gears** 🌀
>
> Imagine a flat round plate (the *ring gear*) lying on a turntable, with a small spinning disc in its center (the *sun gear*), and three small rolling balls (the *planet gears*) sandwiched between them, all held in place by a rotating arm (the *planet carrier*). 
>
> - If you **hold the plate still** and spin the central disc, the arm rotates slowly in the same direction — like reducing a hand-crank to a slower, more powerful motion. 
> - If you **hold the central disc** and spin the plate, the arm rotates the *opposite* way — useful for **reverse gear**. 
> - If you **lock the arm** and spin the plate, the central disc spins very fast in the opposite direction — this is **overdrive**, like a bicycle's high gear.

This single "lazy Susan" mechanism gives a car **all its gears**, **reverse**, and **overdrive**, all in a package shorter than a manual gearbox. The torque converter acts like the smooth fluid coupling that lets the engine idle without stalling (think of two fans facing each other — pushing air to transmit power).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

- **Standard API Gear Designation:** $T_R = T_S + 2T_P$ (must be an integer for a standard planetary set).
- **Typical Torque Multiplication (Stall Torque Ratio):** $T_{ratio} = 1.7$ to $2.5$.
- **Stall Speed:** **1800–2400 rpm** for passenger-car torque converters.
- **Coupling Speed (Speed ratio at which stator releases):** approximately **80–90%** of impeller speed.
- **Working Fluid:** Automatic Transmission Fluid (**ATF**) — typically **Dexron III/Mercon** or modern low-viscosity variants.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Epicyclic (Planetary) Gear Box — Operating Principle

An epicyclic gear train has its gear axes **moving** relative to a fixed reference frame. The three rotating members are:

1. **Sun Gear (S)** — central gear with external teeth.
2. **Planet Gears (P)** — typically 3 (sometimes 4) small gears meshed with both sun and ring; their axes are mounted on the **planet carrier (C)**.
3. **Ring Gear / Annulus (R)** — outer gear with internal teeth.
4. **Planet Carrier (C)** — the arm that holds the planet pinions; it is itself a rotating member.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Tooth Count Relationship:**
> $$T_R = T_S + 2T_P$$
> This geometric constraint guarantees the planet gear meshes simultaneously with the sun (external) and the ring (internal).

### 2.1.1 The Three Operating Conditions

By fixing (holding stationary) any one of the three members — Sun, Ring, or Carrier — and driving the other two, we obtain **three distinct reductions**, all packaged in the same housing. The fourth condition (any one member free, none fixed) gives **direct drive**.

| Case | Fixed Member | Input | Output | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ring (R) | Sun (S) | Carrier (C) | **Forward Reduction (1st gear)** |
| 2 | Sun (S) | Ring (R) | Carrier (C) | **Reverse Gear** |
| 3 | Carrier (C) | Sun (S) | Ring (R) | **Overdrive** |
| 4 | None fixed; one input, one output | — | — | **Direct Drive (1:1)** |

### 2.1.2 The Fundamental Willis Equation (Speed Ratio)

By imagining the **carrier as stationary** and analyzing the gear train as a simple fixed-axis gear train, Willis derived the famous relationship:

$$\frac{N_S - N_C}{N_R - N_C} = -\frac{T_R}{T_S}$$

Or equivalently (in terms of speed ratio $i$):

$$\boxed{\;i = \frac{N_{input}}{N_{output}} = \frac{T_{driven}}{T_{driver}}\;}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that the sun and ring rotate in **opposite directions relative to the carrier** in the equivalent fixed-axis representation. When we apply the constraint that one member is stationary ($N = 0$), the direction of the actual output is determined case by case.

## 2.2 Torque Converter — Operating Principle

The torque converter is a **hydrodynamic** coupling device that:

- Transmits engine torque to the transmission via a fluid (ATF).
- **Multiplies torque** during stall/launch conditions.
- Provides a soft, **slip-based** start (no clutch pedal required).
- Damps torsional vibrations from the engine.

### 2.2.1 Three-Element Construction

| Element | Role | Connection |
|---|---|---|
| **Impeller (Pump)** | Driven by engine (crankshaft) | Bolted to engine flywheel housing |
| **Turbine** | Drives the transmission input shaft | Splined to gearbox input |
| **Stator (Reactor)** | Redirects returning fluid to amplify torque | Mounted on a **one-way (overrunning) clutch**; reaction ground via transmission housing |

### 2.2.2 Operating Stages

1. **Stall Condition** — Turbine is stationary (vehicle not moving). Fluid is flung outward by the impeller, strikes the stationary stator at a sharp angle, the stator redirects it back to the impeller. **Maximum torque multiplication (1.7–2.5×)** occurs here.
2. **Acceleration Phase** — Turbine begins to rotate. Fluid follows a vortex path; torque multiplication decreases as turbine speed rises.
3. **Coupling Point** — Turbine reaches ~80–90% of impeller speed. Fluid strikes the stator blades axially (no angle of attack), stator begins to **freewheel** on its one-way clutch. Torque multiplication = 1.0.
4. **Cruise Phase** — Torque converter behaves as a **fluid coupling** (~95–98% efficient); stator freewheels, no torque multiplication.

### 2.2.3 Key Performance Parameters

- **Speed Ratio (SR):** 
$$SR = \frac{N_{Turbine}}{N_{Impeller}}$$

- **Torque Ratio (TR):** 
$$TR = \frac{T_{Turbine}}{T_{Impeller}}$$

- **Slip:** 
$$\text{Slip} = \frac{N_{Impeller} - N_{Turbine}}{N_{Impeller}} \times 100\%$$

- **Stall Torque Ratio:** Max value of $TR$ at $SR = 0$, typically $1.7$ to $2.5$.

## 2.3 Overdrive — Concept & Need

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overdrive** is a transmission condition in which the **output shaft rotates faster than the input shaft**, i.e., speed ratio $< 1$. For an internal combustion engine, this is highly desirable at cruising speeds because it allows the engine to operate at lower RPM for a given road speed, reducing fuel consumption, noise, vibration, and wear.

### 2.3.1 Methods of Achieving Overdrive

1. **Epicyclic Overdrive** — using the planetary gear set in *Case 3* (Carrier fixed, Sun input, Ring output). A typical overdrive ratio is **0.7:1** (output 1.43× input speed).
2. **Lay-shaft Overdrive** — in a manual gearbox, the lay-shaft top gear is taller than 1:1.
3. **Separate Overdrive Unit (Borg-Warner type)** — a two-speed epicyclic box attached behind the main gearbox, with electrical/ hydraulic activation.
4. **Modern AMT/DCT** — overdrive is simply a tall ratio in the constant-mesh set.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_R = T_S + 2T_P$ | Geometric tooth constraint for standard planetary set | Teeth count, dimensionless |
| 2 | $\dfrac{N_S - N_C}{N_R - N_C} = -\dfrac{T_R}{T_S}$ | **Willis fundamental equation** | Speeds in rpm; same direction sign convention |
| 3 | $\dfrac{N_S}{N_C} = 1 + \dfrac{T_R}{T_S}$ (Ring fixed) | Forward reduction ratio | Sun input, Carrier output |
| 4 | $\dfrac{N_R}{N_C} = 1 + \dfrac{T_S}{T_R}$ (Sun fixed) | Reverse / alternate reduction | Ring input, Carrier output |
| 5 | $\dfrac{N_R}{N_S} = -\dfrac{T_R}{T_S}$ (Carrier fixed) | Reverse-rotation direct mesh | Sun input, Ring output |
| 6 | $SR = \dfrac{N_{Turbine}}{N_{Impeller}}$ | Torque converter speed ratio | $0 \leq SR \leq 1$ |
| 7 | $TR = \dfrac{T_{Turbine}}{T_{Impeller}}$ | Torque converter torque ratio | $1.0 \leq TR \leq 2.5$ |
| 8 | $\eta = SR \times TR$ | Torque converter efficiency (peak ~95%) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Slip} = \dfrac{N_I - N_T}{N_I} \times 100\%$ | Slip percentage | % |
| 10 | $i_{overdrive} = \dfrac{N_{in}}{N_{out}} < 1$ | Definition of overdrive | Typically $0.7$ to $0.8$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The epicyclic gear box is the *backbone* of every modern automatic transmission (AT, AMT, CVT, DCT). The torque converter, invented by Hermann Föttinger (1908), is responsible for the smooth launch that makes automatic cars effortless to drive. Overdrive ratios (e.g., 0.7:1 in the Maruti Suzuki Swift AMT) directly translate into measurable **fuel-economy gains of 8–15%** on highways.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Willis Equation

> [!NOTE]
> The Willis equation is the **single most important** result in planetary gear analysis. Board examiners frequently award 4–6 marks just for its derivation. We will derive it rigorously.

### Step 1 — Imagine the Carrier is Held Fixed

If we conceptually "freeze" the planet carrier $C$ and observe the system from the carrier's reference frame, the gears S, P, and R appear to rotate about **fixed axes** — exactly like an ordinary simple gear train. The relative speeds become:

$$\omega_S^{rel} = \omega_S - \omega_C \quad ; \quad \omega_P^{rel} = \omega_P - \omega_C \quad ; \quad \omega_R^{rel} = \omega_R - \omega_C$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Sun–Planet Mesh (External Mesh)

For an **external** gear mesh, the two gears rotate in **opposite directions**, and the speed ratio equals the inverse ratio of teeth:

$$\frac{\omega_S^{rel}}{\omega_P^{rel}} = -\frac{T_P}{T_S}$$

Cross-multiplying (this is the **first train-value equation**):

$$T_S \cdot (\omega_S - \omega_C) = -T_P \cdot (\omega_P - \omega_C) \tag{1}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Planet–Ring Mesh (Internal Mesh)

For an **internal** gear mesh (planet and ring both have their pitch circles on the *same side* of the contact point), the two gears rotate in the **same direction**:

$$\frac{\omega_P^{rel}}{\omega_R^{rel}} = \frac{T_R}{T_P}$$

So:

$$T_P \cdot (\omega_P - \omega_C) = T_R \cdot (\omega_R - \omega_C) \tag{2}$$

### Step 4 — Eliminate the Planet Speed

From equation (1):

$$-T_P \cdot (\omega_P - \omega_C) = T_S \cdot (\omega_S - \omega_C)$$

Substituting this into equation (2):

$$T_S \cdot (\omega_S - \omega_C) = T_R \cdot (\omega_R - \omega_C)$$

### Step 5 — Rearrange into the Standard Willis Form

Dividing both sides by $(N_R - N_C) \cdot T_S$ and inverting:

$$\boxed{\; \frac{\omega_S - \omega_C}{\omega_R - \omega_C} = \frac{T_R}{T_S} \cdot \frac{(\omega_R - \omega_C)}{(\omega_R - \_C) \cdot 1} = -\frac{T_R}{T_S}\;}$$

*(Note: the negative sign arises when sign convention of external mesh is strictly maintained; many textbooks write the equation as positive when both ratios are stated in magnitude and direction is assessed case-by-case.)*

$$\frac{N_S - N_C}{N_R - N_C} = \pm \frac{T_R}{T_S} \quad \text{(Willis Equation)}$$

## 3.2 Speed Ratio — Case-by-Case Expansion

Let us assume a representative planetary set with $T_S = 20$, $T_P = 10$, $T_R = T_S + 2T_P = 40$.

### Case 1: Ring Fixed, Sun Input, Carrier Output (Forward Reduction)

Substitute $N_R = 0$:

$$\frac{N_S - N_C}{0 - N_C} = -\frac{T_R}{T_S} = -\frac{40}{20} = -2$$

Solving:

$$N_S - N_C = -2 \cdot (-N_C) = 2 N_C$$
$$N_S = 3 N_C \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{N_S}{N_C} = 3$$

**Speed reduction ratio = 3:1**; sun and carrier rotate in the **same direction** (positive ratio). This is typical **1st gear**.

### Case 2: Sun Fixed, Ring Input, Carrier Output (Reverse / Alternate)

Substitute $N_S = 0$:

$$\frac{0 - N_C}{N_R - N_C} = -\frac{T_R}{T_S} = -2$$
$$-N_C = -2(N_R - N_C) = -2 N_R + 2 N_C$$
$$3 N_C = 2 N_R \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{N_C}{N_R} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$$

Speed ratio ≈ 1.5:1 reduction. The direction: ring input rotates one way; carrier output rotates the **same way** (since the ratio is positive). However, the *output* is on the opposite side of the gear set from where it would be in Case 1, yielding a **reversed rotational sense** for the wheels. This is the **reverse gear**.

### Case 3: Carrier Fixed, Sun Input, Ring Output (Overdrive / Max Reduction in Opposite Sense)

Substitute $N_C = 0$:

$$\frac{N_S - 0}{N_R - 0} = -\frac{T_R}{T_S} = -2$$

So $N_S = -2 N_R$. The ring rotates at **half the speed of the sun, in the opposite direction**. Used in **overdrive** configurations and in some manual reverse setups.

### Case 4: Direct Drive (All Members Locked Together)

When the friction bands/clutches lock any two members together, all three rotate as a single unit at speed $N$:

$$N_S = N_R = N_C \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ratio} = 1:1 \;\; (\text{Direct Drive, usually 3rd or 4th gear})$$

## 3.3 Torque Converter — Analytical Performance Curves

The performance of a torque converter is typically represented by three curves plotted against Speed Ratio $SR = N_T/N_I$:

- **Torque Ratio curve:** $TR$ vs $SR$ — decreasing from stall value (~2.0) to 1.0.
- **Efficiency curve:** $\eta = SR \times TR$ vs $SR$ — rises to a peak of ~95% near coupling, then drops sharply.
- **Slip curve:** $1 - SR$ vs $SR$ — decreases from 100% (stall) to 5–10% (coupling).

### 3.3.1 Derivation of the Coupling Condition

At the **coupling point**, fluid strikes the stator blades purely **axially** — there is no tangential force component on the stator, so the stator is free to rotate (it freewheels on its one-way clutch). 

Applying the **angular momentum theorem** (Euler's pump equation) to the fluid in the three elements:

$$T = \dot{m} \cdot (V_{tangential, outlet} - V_{tangential, inlet}) \cdot r$$

For the **stator** at coupling: $T_{stator} = 0$ because $V_{tangential, inlet} = V_{tangential, outlet}$ (axial flow). The moment balance for the impeller-turbine loop gives:

$$T_I = T_T \quad \Rightarrow \quad TR = 1.0 \;\;\text{(coupling condition)}$$

This condition is met at a specific speed ratio (typically $SR_{couple} \approx 0.85$).

### 3.3.2 Sample Numerical Problem (KTU pattern)

> **Problem:** A torque converter has a stall torque ratio of **2.0** and the impeller rotates at **2000 rpm** while the turbine rotates at **1200 rpm**. Calculate: (a) the speed ratio, (b) the slip, (c) the torque ratio at this operating point (assuming a linear characteristic), and (d) the converter efficiency.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Speed Ratio:**
$$SR = \frac{N_T}{N_I} = \frac{1200}{2000} = 0.6$$

**Step (b) — Slip:**
$$\text{Slip} = \frac{N_I - N_T}{N_I} \times 100\% = \frac{2000 - 1200}{2000} \times 100\% = 40\%$$

**Step (c) — Torque Ratio (linear interpolation):** 
Assume linear variation of $TR$ from stall ($TR=2.0$ at $SR=0$) to coupling ($TR=1.0$ at $SR=1$):
$$TR = 1.0 + (2.0 - 1.0)(1 - SR) = 1.0 + 1.0 \times 0.4 = 1.4$$
So $T_T = 1.4 \times T_I$.

**Step (d) — Efficiency:**
$$\eta = SR \times TR = 0.6 \times 1.4 = 0.84 = 84\%$$

## 3.4 Overdrive — Mathematical Justification

Consider an epicyclic set with $T_S = 20$, $T_R = 40$ and the **carrier held fixed**. From Case 3 above:

$$\frac{N_S}{N_R} = -\frac{T_R}{T_S} = -\frac{40}{20} = -2$$

If we now take the **sun as input** at $N_S$ and the **ring as output** at $N_R$, then the output is **half** the input speed — a *reduction*, not an overdrive. To get an **overdrive**, we instead take the **carrier as input** and the **ring or sun as output**. For example, in a rear-mounted Borg-Warner overdrive:

- **Sun** is driven by the gearbox output shaft.
- **Ring** is connected through a one-way clutch to the propeller shaft.
- **Carrier** is held by a brake band when overdrive is engaged.

With the carrier fixed:
$$N_S : N_R = -T_R : T_S = -2:1$$

So with the sun as input ($N_S = 2000$ rpm) and the ring as output ($N_R = -1000$ rpm). The output is therefore at **half the input speed in the opposite direction**, which is then passed through an idler to reverse the direction, giving a final **overdrive ratio of 0.5:1**. (In practice, gears are chosen for ~0.7:1, e.g., 0.7 means output rotates 1.43× input.)

$$\boxed{\;i_{OD} = \frac{N_{in}}{N_{out}} = \frac{1}{1 + T_S/T_R} = \frac{T_R}{T_S + T_R}\;}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Epicyclic Gear Train — Functional Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below is a *functional block representation* of an epicyclic gear train showing the three rotating members, the planet carrier, and the standard tooth-constraint relationship. (A physical radial diagram with sun, planets, and ring is best drawn by hand; the block diagram below captures the kinematic structure unambiguously for KTU exam answers.)

```mermaid
graph TD
    InputShaft[Input Shaft] -->|Torque In| SunNode[Sun Gear S]
    SunNode -->|External Mesh Tp Teeth| PlanetNode[Planet Gears P 3 or 4 units]
    PlanetNode -->|Internal Mesh Tr Teeth| RingNode[Ring Gear R Annulus]
    PlanetNode -->|Mounted On| CarrierNode[Planet Carrier C]
    CarrierNode --> OutputShaft[Output Shaft]
    RingNode -.->|Optional Clutch Lock| DirectLockNode[Direct Drive Lockup]

    SunNode -.->|Band Brake A| BrakeA[Brake A Fixes S]
    RingNode -.->|Band Brake B| BrakeB[Brake B Fixes R]
    CarrierNode -.->|Band Brake C| BrakeC[Brake C Fixes C]

    ToothConst[Tooth Constraint Tr = Ts + 2Tp] -.-> SunNode
    ToothConst -.-> PlanetNode
    ToothConst -.-> RingNode

    classDef mem fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ctl fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rel fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    class SunNode,PlanetNode,RingNode,CarrierNode mem
    class BrakeA,BrakeB,BrakeC,DirectLockNode ctl
    class ToothConst rel
```

## 4.2 Torque Converter — Functional Power-Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    Engine[Engine Flywheel] -->|Drives| ImpellerNode[IMPELLER PUMP]
    ImpellerNode -->|High Velo Fluid| StatorNode[STATOR REACTOR]
    StatorNode -->|Redirected Flow| TurbineNode[TURBINE]
    TurbineNode -->|Torque Out| Gearbox[Transmission Input Shaft]
    Gearbox -.->|Reaction Path| StatorNode

    ImpellerNode -.->|Shell Blades| ImpellerDetail[Housing Filled With ATF]
    StatorNode -.->|One Way Clutch| OWC[Overrunning Clutch Freewheels at Coupling]
    TurbineNode -.->|Spline Coupling| Gearbox

    StallOp[Stall TR = 2 point 0 max torque] -.-> ImpellerNode
    AccOp[Acceleration TR drops as SR rises] -.-> StatorNode
    CoupleOp[Coupling TR = 1 point 0 stator freewheels] -.-> OWC
    CruiseOp[Cruise Acts as Fluid Coupling 95 percent eff] -.-> Gearbox

    classDef comp fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    classDef stage fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:1px
    classDef cond fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:1px

    class ImpellerNode,StatorNode,TurbineNode,OWC comp
    class StallOp,AccOp,CoupleOp,CruiseOp stage
    class Engine,Gearbox cond
```

## 4.3 Overdrive Engagement — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Driver Demands High Speed Cruise] --> SwitchOD[Engage Overdrive Solenoid]
    SwitchOD --> ApplyBrake[Brake Band Applied to Carrier]
    ApplyBrake --> LockCarrier[Planet Carrier Held Stationary]
    LockCarrier --> SunDrive[Sun Driven by Gearbox Output]
    SunDrive --> RingSpin[Ring Gear Spins Faster than Sun]
    RingSpin --> ReverseIdler[Idler Gear Reverses Direction]
    ReverseIdler --> PropShaft[Propeller Shaft Receives 0 point 7 ratio]
    PropShaft --> Econ[Engine RPM Reduced 30 percent Fuel Saved]

    Disengage[Solenoid Off] --> ReleaseBrake[Brake Band Releases]
    ReleaseBrake --> FreeCarrier[Carrier Free One Way Clutch Engages]
    FreeCarrier --> DirectDrive[All Members Free to Rotate as Unit]
    DirectDrive --> NormalDrive[Normal 1 to 1 Drive Resumes]

    classDef act fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px
    classDef std fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:1px

    class SwitchOD,ApplyBrake,LockCarrier,Disengage,ReleaseBrake act
    class PropShaft,Econ,NormalDrive out
    class Start,SunDrive,RingSpin,ReverseIdler,FreeCarrier,DirectDrive std
```


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMRT413]**

**Q1. State and explain the fundamental equation of an epicyclic gear train. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The fundamental equation of an epicyclic (planetary) gear train, also known as the **Willis Equation**, relates the speeds of the three rotating members (Sun $S$, Ring $R$, and Carrier $C$) to their teeth numbers. When the carrier is treated as the reference frame, the system reduces to a simple fixed-axis gear train, giving:

$$\frac{N_S - N_C}{N_R - N_C} = \pm \frac{T_R}{T_S}$$

where $T_S$ and $T_R$ are the number of teeth on the sun and ring gears, and $N_S, N_R, N_C$ are their respective speeds. The equation is used to compute speed ratios in automatic transmissions by holding one member fixed (using a brake band or clutch) and supplying torque to another.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper PEMRT413]**

**Q2. What is "stall torque ratio" in a torque converter, and what is its typical range? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **stall torque ratio** is the ratio of output (turbine) torque to input (impeller) torque when the turbine is **stationary** ($N_T = 0$), i.e., at vehicle launch or "stall" condition. At this point, the stator is held stationary by the one-way clutch, redirecting returning fluid to assist the impeller. Typical values for passenger-car torque converters range from **1.7 to 2.5**, with modern designs closer to **2.0**. This is the maximum torque multiplication the converter can deliver; it falls to 1.0 at the coupling point.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Question Pattern]**

---

### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**Q. (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of an **Epicyclic Gear Box** used in automatic transmissions. Derive the Willis equation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An **epicyclic gear box** (also called a planetary gear box) is a compound gear system in which one or more gear axes **revolve about a central axis**. It consists of three main rotating members:

- **Sun Gear (S):** A central gear with external teeth, mounted on the input shaft.
- **Planet Gears (P):** 3 (or sometimes 4) small gears whose teeth mesh simultaneously with the sun and the ring. Their pins are mounted on the planet carrier.
- **Ring Gear / Annulus (R):** An outer gear with **internal teeth**, coaxial with the sun.
- **Planet Carrier (C):** A rotating arm that carries the planet pinions and provides the output.

**Working Principle:** By selectively **holding one of the three members** (using a brake band or multi-plate clutch) and driving the second, the third member becomes the output with a fixed reduction ratio. This is the basis of all gear shifts in an automatic transmission.

**Willis Equation Derivation:**
Consider the system in the carrier's reference frame. The gears now appear to rotate about fixed axes with relative speeds $\omega_S - \omega_C$, $\omega_P - \omega_C$, $\omega_R - \omega_C$.

For the **sun–planet** external mesh:
$$T_S(\omega_S - \omega_C) = -T_P(\omega_P - \omega_C) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

For the **planet–ring** internal mesh:
$$T_P(\omega_P - \omega_C) = T_R(\omega_R - \omega_C) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Eliminating the planet speed from (1) and (2):
$$T_S(\omega_S - \omega_C) = T_R(\omega_R - \omega_C)$$

Rearranging:
$$\frac{N_S - N_C}{N_R - N_C} = -\frac{T_R}{T_S}$$

This is the **Willis fundamental equation**, used to compute speed ratios in any operating condition by substituting the constraint $N = 0$ for the fixed member.

**Operating Configurations:**
- Ring fixed → forward reduction
- Sun fixed → reverse (with idler for direction)
- Carrier fixed → overdrive
- Two members locked → direct drive

---

**Q. (b)** Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working of a three-element hydraulic torque converter**. Discuss its performance curves. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **torque converter** is a hydrodynamic device that transmits engine torque to the transmission through Automatic Transmission Fluid (ATF), providing smooth launch, torque multiplication, and torsional damping. It has three main elements:

1. **Impeller (Pump):** Driven by the engine flywheel. Its curved blades fling oil outward by centrifugal force.
2. **Turbine:** Splined to the transmission input shaft. Oil strikes its blades, causing it to rotate and deliver output torque.
3. **Stator (Reactor):** A stationary reaction member mounted on a **one-way (overrunning) clutch** between impeller and turbine. It redirects returning fluid to assist the impeller, multiplying torque.

**Working Stages:**

- **Stall (vehicle not moving):** Oil leaving the turbine strikes the stator at a sharp angle. The stator redirects this oil back into the impeller, adding to its driving force. **Torque is multiplied by a factor of 1.7–2.5**.
- **Acceleration:** As turbine speed rises, oil strikes the stator at a smaller angle. Torque multiplication falls.
- **Coupling point (~85% speed ratio):** Oil returns axially; stator is free to rotate on its one-way clutch. **Torque ratio = 1.0**.
- **Cruise:** Stator freewheels; converter behaves as a simple fluid coupling with ~95% efficiency.

**Performance Curves (plotted against Speed Ratio SR = $N_T/N_I$):**
- Torque Ratio curve: starts at ~2.0 (stall), drops to 1.0 (coupling).
- Efficiency curve: $\eta = SR \times TR$ rises to peak ~95% just before coupling, then drops.
- Slip curve: $(1 - SR) \times 100\%$ falls from 100% (stall) to ~10% (coupling).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert ⚠️**
> Common mistakes students make in this question:
> 1. **Confusing fluid coupling with torque converter** — fluid coupling has *no stator* and cannot multiply torque. Always mention the stator and the **one-way clutch** explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the directional sense** in the Willis derivation — the negative sign MUST be justified by the external-mesh direction reversal.
> 3. **Missing the standard tooth relationship** $T_R = T_S + 2T_P$ — KTU examiners expect this in epicyclic derivations.

---

### 🟢 Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**Q. (a)** Explain the construction and working of an **Overdrive unit** with a neat sketch. State two methods of achieving overdrive. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** An **overdrive** is a transmission condition in which the **output shaft rotates faster than the input shaft**, i.e., the speed ratio is less than 1 (typically **0.7:1**). It is used at cruising speeds to reduce engine RPM, improving fuel economy and reducing wear.

**Construction (Epicyclic Overdrive Type — Borg-Warner):**
- A compact epicyclic gear set is mounted behind the main gearbox.
- **Sun gear** is connected to the main gearbox output.
- **Ring gear** is connected via a **one-way (overrunning) clutch** to the propeller shaft.
- **Planet carrier** is held by an **electromagnetic or hydraulic brake band** when overdrive is engaged.
- A **solenoid** controls the brake band engagement based on vehicle speed sensor input.

**Working:**
- When overdrive is **OFF**, the brake band is released, the carrier is free, and the one-way clutch locks — all members rotate together as a unit. The unit acts as a **1:1** connector.
- When overdrive is **ON** (at speeds above ~50 km/h), the solenoid applies the brake band, holding the **carrier stationary**. The sun input drives the ring at a *faster* (relative) speed in the opposite direction. The idler gear reverses this direction, so the propeller shaft turns at **0.7 times** the gearbox output speed (i.e., 1.43× the input shaft speed at the sun).

**Mathematical expression:** With $T_S = 20$, $T_R = 40$ and the carrier fixed:
$$\frac{N_S}{N_R} = -\frac{T_R}{T_S} = -2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N_R = -\frac{N_S}{2}$$

After direction reversal by the idler, the output to the propeller shaft is $N_S / 0.7$.

**Two Methods of Achieving Overdrive:**
1. **Lay-shaft Top Gear in Manual Gearbox:** The 5th gear pair is chosen with more teeth on the output (driven) gear, e.g., 25T driving 35T, giving ratio 0.71:1.
2. **Epicyclic Overdrive Unit (Borg-Warner or similar):** A separate two-speed epicyclic box attached behind the main gearbox, with electronic/hydraulic shift.

---

**Q. (b)** A planetary gear set has **20 teeth** on the sun, **15 teeth** on each planet, and **50 teeth** on the ring. The sun is the **input** at **1000 rpm**, and the **ring is held fixed**. Calculate: (i) the speed of the planet carrier, (ii) the speed reduction ratio, and (iii) the direction of rotation of the carrier relative to the sun. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $T_S = 20$, $T_P = 15$, $T_R = 50$
- $N_S = 1000$ rpm (input)
- $N_R = 0$ (ring fixed)
- $N_C = ?$ (carrier output)

**Verify the tooth constraint:**
$$T_R = T_S + 2T_P = 20 + 2(15) = 50 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step (i) — Apply the Willis Equation:**
$$\frac{N_S - N_C}{N_R - N_C} = -\frac{T_R}{T_S} = -\frac{50}{20} = -2.5$$

**Step (ii) — Substitute $N_R = 0$:**
$$\frac{N_S - N_C}{0 - N_C} = -2.5$$
$$\frac{1000 - N_C}{-N_C} = -2.5$$
$$1000 - N_C = 2.5 N_C$$
$$1000 = 3.5 N_C$$
$$N_C = \frac{1000}{3.5} = 285.71 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

**Step (iii) — Speed Reduction Ratio:**
$$i = \frac{N_S}{N_C} = \frac{1000}{285.71} = 3.5 : 1$$

**Step (iv) — Direction:**
The ratio $N_S/N_C$ is **positive** (3.5), so the carrier rotates in the **same direction** as the sun.

**Final Answer:** $N_C = 285.71$ rpm; reduction ratio = **3.5:1**; same direction as sun.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert ⚠️**
> Common mistakes in planetary gear numericals:
> 1. **Sign error** — students often write the Willis equation as $\frac{N_S - N_C}{N_R - N_C} = +\frac{T_R}{T_S}$ and forget the negative sign, leading to a wrong final ratio.
> 2. **Forgetting the tooth constraint** — if $T_R \neq T_S + 2T_P$, the problem is ill-posed; always verify.
> 3. **Inverting the reduction ratio** — the speed *reduction* ratio is $N_{in}/N_{out}$, not the other way around.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Automatic Transmission** = Epicyclic Gear Box + Torque Converter + Hydraulic/Electronic Control Unit (valve body).
- [x] **Planetary Gear Set** has 3 rotating members: **Sun, Planets, Ring**, all carried by the **Planet Carrier**.
- [x] **Standard tooth constraint:** $T_R = T_S + 2T_P$ (always verify before solving).
- [x] **Willis Equation:** $\dfrac{N_S - N_C}{N_R - N_C} = -\dfrac{T_R}{T_S}$ — derive by fixing the carrier and analyzing two simple mesh equations.
- [x] **Four operating conditions:** (1) Ring fixed → forward reduction, (2) Sun fixed → reverse, (3) Carrier fixed → overdrive/reverse rotation, (4) Two members locked → direct drive.
- [x] **Typical forward gear ratios:** 1st = 3.5:1, 2nd = 2.1:1, 3rd = 1.4:1, 4th = 1.0:1, 5th = 0.7:1 (overdrive).
- [x] **Torque Converter has 3 elements:** **Impeller (driving), Turbine (driven), Stator (reactor)**. Stator is mounted on a **one-way clutch**.
- [x] **Stall Torque Ratio** = 1.7 to 2.5; **Coupling** occurs at speed ratio ≈ 0.85 with TR = 1.0; **Efficiency** peaks ≈ 95% near coupling.
- [x] **Slip** = $(N_I - N_T)/N_I \times 100\%$; high slip at stall, low slip at coupling.
- [x] **Overdrive** = output > input; achieved by **epicyclic set with carrier fixed** OR by **tall top gear** in manual gearboxes.
- [x] **Real-world overdrive ratio** is typically **0.7:1** (input:output), giving ~30% engine RPM reduction at cruise.
- [x] **Modern trend:** Many ATs now use a **lock-up clutch** in the torque converter to eliminate slip at cruise, raising efficiency to 98–99%.
- [x] **Always mention** the one-way (overrunning) clutch when describing the stator's role — it is the single most-tested KTU concept.
- [x] **Practical exam tip:** In long answers, *always* include a labelled block diagram and the Willis derivation — together they earn 8+ of 14 marks.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT413 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Automated Manual Transmission (AMT) - Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

An **Automated Manual Transmission (AMT)**, also referred to as a *clutchless manual transmission* or *semi-automatic transmission*, is a type of motor vehicle transmission that mechanically resembles a conventional manual gearbox but uses an **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** integrated with **electro-mechanical, electro-hydraulic, or electro-pneumatic actuators** to automatically perform the functions of clutch engagement/disengagement and gear selection, thereby eliminating the need for a driver-operated clutch pedal and mechanical gear lever.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "An Automated Manual Transmission (AMT) is a manual transmission in which the clutch and gear shift operations are automated using electronic actuators and a Transmission Control Unit (TCU), combining the mechanical efficiency of a manual gearbox with the driving comfort of a conventional automatic."

### Key Terminology
- **TCU (Transmission Control Unit)**: The brain of the AMT that processes sensor inputs and commands the actuators.
- **EMA (Electro-Mechanical Actuator)**: Converts electrical signals into mechanical motion to actuate the gear shift forks and clutch.
- **Shift-by-wire**: A system where gear selection is performed electronically, removing the physical mechanical linkage between the gear lever and gearbox.
- **Torque Interruption**: A momentary loss of torque delivery to the drive wheels during a single-clutch AMT gear change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why AMT exists in the market:**
> AMT bridges the cost-performance gap between a manual transmission (cheap, efficient, but laborious) and a conventional torque-converter automatic (comfortable but costly and less efficient). In emerging markets like India, AMT is offered on entry-level and mid-segment vehicles (e.g., Maruti Suzuki Alto K10 AMT, Hyundai Grand i10 Nios AMT) for fuel economy at affordable pricing.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Imagine teaching a robot to drive a stick-shift car for you.**

You are sitting in the driver's seat of a car equipped with a manual transmission. Instead of your left foot pressing the clutch pedal and your right hand moving the gear lever, there is a small **robotic system** under the hood that does it for you.

- When you press the accelerator, the **robot's "brain" (TCU)** reads your intent.
- The robot **presses the clutch** using an actuator arm.
- The robot **moves the gear lever** to the next gear.
- The robot **releases the clutch** smoothly.
- All this happens in a fraction of a second — without your feet or hands ever touching a pedal or lever.

> **In essence:** An AMT is a manual gearbox with a "robotic chauffeur" operating the clutch and shifter, governed by sensors and an electronic controller.

### Real-World Example
The **Maruti Suzuki Swift AMT** uses a 5-speed AMT. When the driver moves the gear lever from "D" to "R", the TCU commands the clutch actuator to disengage the clutch plate, the gear actuator to engage reverse gear, and then to re-engage the clutch — all in approximately **300–500 milliseconds** (ms).

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recall Tip for KTU Exams:**
> *AMT = Manual Gearbox + Electronic Brain + Actuators (No Clutch Pedal)*
> Think of it as a *smart robot driver* operating a *conventional manual gearbox*.

---

## Classification of AMT

There are **two main categories** of Automated Manual Transmissions:

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Single-Clutch AMT** | Uses one clutch actuator; gear shifts involve a brief torque interruption. | Entry-level passenger cars, commercial vehicles, two-wheelers (e.g., TVS Scooty). |
| **Dual-Clutch AMT (DCT)** | Uses two wet clutches; pre-selects the next gear for seamless, nearly instant shifts. | Premium passenger cars, sports cars (e.g., Volkswagen DSG, Porsche PDK). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** Modern single-clutch AMTs perform a complete gear shift in **200 ms to 500 ms**, while dual-clutch AMTs execute shifts in **100 ms to 200 ms**.

---

## Advantages and Limitations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advantages of AMT (High-Yield for KTU 2-Mark Questions):**
> 1. **Higher fuel efficiency** than conventional automatic transmissions (similar to manual).
> 2. **Lower cost** of manufacturing and maintenance.
> 3. **No clutch pedal** — reduced driver fatigue in city traffic.
> 4. **Compact and lightweight** design.
> 5. Easy integration with existing manual transmission platforms (lower R&D cost for OEMs).

> [!WARNING]
> **Disadvantages of AMT (Common Interview/Exam Question):**
> 1. **Torque interruption** during gear shifts in single-clutch AMT (causes a "head-nod" effect).
> 2. **Jerky low-speed manoeuvring** (especially during creeping in traffic).
> 3. **Limited torque handling** compared to torque-converter automatics (typically up to **350 Nm**).
> 4. Heat generation in the clutch during repeated engagements.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Working Principle of AMT — Structured Step-by-Step

The operation of an Automated Manual Transmission is governed by a closed-loop feedback control system. The TCU continuously monitors vehicle dynamics and commands the actuators. The process can be broken down into the following sequential logic steps:

### Step 1: Driver Input Acquisition
- The driver selects a mode (typically **D-Drive**, **R-Reverse**, or **N-Neutral**) via the gear selector.
- The accelerator pedal position sensor (APP) reads the throttle input.
- Additional inputs include: **brake pedal switch**, **vehicle speed sensor (VSS)**, and **engine RPM sensor**.

### Step 2: Sensor Data Fusion by TCU
- The TCU aggregates data from:
  - Throttle Position Sensor (TPS) — *measures % pedal depression*
  - Engine Speed Sensor (Ne) — *measures engine RPM*
  - Vehicle Speed Sensor (V) — *measures wheel speed*
  - Gear Position Sensor — *identifies current engaged gear*
  - Clutch Position Sensor — *tracks clutch plate engagement*
  - Brake Switch — *confirms brake application*
  - Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor — *modifies shift map during cold starts*

### Step 3: Decision Making (Shift Logic)
- The TCU refers to a pre-calibrated **shift map** (a 2D lookup table of engine RPM vs. throttle position).
- If the current operating point crosses a shift threshold, a gear change is initiated.
- Algorithms used include: **fuzzy logic**, **PID control**, and **adaptive learning algorithms**.

### Step 4: Actuator Command
- **Clutch Actuator** receives a command to disengage the clutch (using an EMA or hydraulic cylinder).
- **Gear Actuator** rotates the selector fork to disengage the current gear and engage the target gear.
- A brief **synchronization phase** allows the dog clutch/synchronizer ring to match speeds.

### Step 5: Clutch Re-engagement
- The clutch actuator progressively re-engages the clutch plate in a **proportional-integral (PI) controlled ramp** to avoid shock loading.
- Torque is gradually transmitted from the engine to the transmission input shaft.

### Step 6: Continuous Monitoring
- The TCU monitors for **clutch slip**, **misfire**, and **shift quality** using real-time feedback.
- Adaptive algorithms refine shift timing and clutch engagement curves over time.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
> The closed-loop architecture is essential because clutch engagement is a **non-linear, time-varying** process. Without feedback, the system cannot compensate for clutch wear, temperature variations, or driver aggression.

---

## System Architecture — Key Components

| Component | Function | Type |
|-----------|----------|------|
| **Transmission Control Unit (TCU)** | Brain of the system; runs shift logic and actuator control algorithms. | Electronic |
| **Clutch Actuator** | Engages/disengages the clutch plate automatically. | Electro-mechanical / Electro-hydraulic |
| **Gear Actuator (Shift Actuator)** | Selects and engages the target gear. | Electro-mechanical / Electro-hydraulic |
| **Hydraulic Control Unit (HCU)** | Provides pressurized fluid to the actuators (in hydraulic AMT). | Hydraulic |
| **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** | Reports accelerator pedal position to TCU. | Sensor (Potentiometric / Hall-effect) |
| **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)** | Measures output shaft/wheel rotational speed. | Sensor (Magnetic / Hall-effect) |
| **Engine Speed Sensor (Ne)** | Measures engine crankshaft rotational speed. | Sensor (Inductive / Hall-effect) |
| **Gear Position Sensor** | Identifies which gear is currently engaged. | Sensor (Hall-effect) |
| **Clutch Position Sensor** | Monitors clutch plate travel and engagement. | Sensor (Potentiometric) |
| **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** | Detects vehicle pitch, yaw, and acceleration (used in hill-start assist). | Sensor (MEMS) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"CSST-GPS"* — **C**lutch actuator, **S**hift actuator, **S**ensors, **T**CU, **G**ear position sensor, **P**edal sensors, **S**peed sensors.

---

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are essential for solving KTU numerical problems on transmission systems, including AMT.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|-----------|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | **Overall Drive Ratio** | $i_{total} = i_g \times i_f$ | Product of transmission gear ratio and final drive ratio. | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **Vehicle Speed (V)** | $V = \dfrac{0.377 \times N_e \times r}{i_{total}}$ | Where $N_e$ = engine RPM, $r$ = wheel rolling radius, $i_{total}$ = overall ratio. | km/h |
| 3 | **Wheel Torque (T_w)** | $T_w = T_e \times i_{total} \times \eta_{tr}$ | Where $T_e$ = engine torque, $\eta_{tr}$ = transmission efficiency (typically **0.92–0.96**). | N·m |
| 4 | **Tractive Force (F_t)** | $F_t = \dfrac{T_e \times i_{total} \times \eta_{tr}}{r}$ | Force available at the tyre-road contact. | N |
| 5 | **Engine Power to Wheel Power** | $P_w = P_e \times \eta_{tr}$ | Power loss across transmission. | kW |
| 6 | **Clutch Engagement Time (t_c)** | $t_c = t_{dis} + t_{sync} + t_{eng}$ | Sum of disengagement, synchronizer, and re-engagement times. | s |
| 7 | **Clutch Slip Energy (E_s)** | $E_s = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} T_{clutch} \cdot \omega_{rel} \cdot dt$ | Energy dissipated during slip phase; affects clutch life. | J |
| 8 | **Gear Ratio for Maximum Gradeability** | $i_{max} = \dfrac{F_{tr,max} \times r}{T_{e,max} \times \eta_{tr}}$ | Determines first gear ratio for steep inclines. | Dimensionless |
| 9 | **Shift Time Penalty (Δt)** | $\Delta t = t_{shift} - t_{ideal}$ | Additional time taken by AMT vs. skilled manual driver. | s |
| 10 | **Slope Climbing Force (F_g)** | $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Force needed to overcome gravity on a slope of angle $\theta$. | N |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Standard Values for KTU Problems:**
> - Rolling resistance coefficient ($\mu_{rr}$): **0.012–0.018** for car tyres on asphalt.
> - Air density ($\rho$): **1.225 kg/m³** at standard atmospheric conditions.
> - Mechanical efficiency of manual/AMT gearbox: **0.94–0.96** (typically use **0.95** unless stated).

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Application Insight:**
> - **Bosch, Continental, ZF, and Getrag** are the global market leaders in AMT and DCT systems.
> - AMT technology is widely used in **Formula 1 cars** (pre-2014 era, 7-speed semi-automatic) and **World Rally Championship (WRC)** cars, where shift times of **20–50 ms** are achieved using pneumatic actuators.
> - In **commercial vehicles (buses and trucks)**, AMT is preferred for fuel economy on long-haul routes, often paired with predictive cruise control using GPS-based terrain maps.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Vehicle Speed from Engine Speed

### Given Data
- Engine speed at the operating point: $N_e$ (in RPM)
- Overall transmission ratio: $i_{total}$
- Effective wheel rolling radius: $r$ (in metres)

### Objective
Derive the relationship between engine RPM and the corresponding vehicle speed $V$ (in km/h).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert engine speed from RPM to rad/s.**

The angular velocity of the engine crankshaft is given by:

$$
\omega_e = \dfrac{2\pi N_e}{60} \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate engine speed to wheel speed using the overall ratio.**

Since the overall gear ratio reduces the engine speed to the wheel speed:

$$
\omega_w = \dfrac{\omega_e}{i_{total}}
$$

Substituting the value of $\omega_e$:

$$
\omega_w = \dfrac{2\pi N_e}{60 \cdot i_{total}} \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Relate wheel angular velocity to linear vehicle speed.**

The linear velocity of the vehicle equals the product of wheel angular velocity and rolling radius:

$$
V_{m/s} = \omega_w \times r
$$

Substituting $\omega_w$:

$$
V_{m/s} = \dfrac{2\pi N_e \cdot r}{60 \cdot i_{total}}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert velocity from m/s to km/h.**

Since $1 \text{ m/s} = 3.6 \text{ km/h}$:

$$
V_{km/h} = V_{m/s} \times 3.6 = \dfrac{2\pi N_e \cdot r \times 3.6}{60 \cdot i_{total}}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the constant.**

$$
\dfrac{2\pi \times 3.6}{60} = \dfrac{7.2 \pi}{60} = 0.377
$$

Therefore, the **final governing equation** is:

$$
V = \dfrac{0.377 \times N_e \times r}{i_{total}} \text{ km/h}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This equation is the **backbone of KTU transmission numerical problems.** It allows the student to calculate vehicle speed given any combination of engine RPM, gear ratio, and wheel size.

---

## Derivation 2: Tractive Force at the Tyre-Road Interface

### Given Data
- Engine torque: $T_e$ (in N·m)
- Overall transmission ratio: $i_{total}$
- Transmission efficiency: $\eta_{tr}$
- Wheel rolling radius: $r$ (in m)

### Objective
Derive the expression for tractive force $F_t$ available at the tyre contact patch.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Express the torque at the wheel hub.**

The transmission multiplies the engine torque by the gear ratio, with efficiency losses accounted for:

$$
T_w = T_e \times i_{total} \times \eta_{tr}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert wheel torque to tractive force.**

The tractive force is the torque divided by the effective rolling radius:

$$
F_t = \dfrac{T_w}{r}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $T_w$.**

$$
F_t = \dfrac{T_e \times i_{total} \times \eta_{tr}}{r}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Governing Equation.**

$$
\boxed{F_t = \dfrac{T_e \cdot i_{total} \cdot \eta_{tr}}{r} \text{ N}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Insight:** The tractive force equation shows that the *lowest* gear (largest $i_{total}$) produces the *highest* tractive force — this is why 1st gear is used for starting from rest and climbing steep inclines.

---

## Derivation 3: Power Flow Through the AMT Drivetrain

### Objective
Establish the relationship between engine power and wheel power, and compute the power loss.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Engine power is the product of torque and angular velocity.**

$$
P_e = T_e \times \omega_e
$$

**Step 2 — Wheel power after transmission losses.**

The power reaching the wheels is:

$$
P_w = T_w \times \omega_w = (T_e \cdot i_{total} \cdot \eta_{tr}) \times \dfrac{\omega_e}{i_{total}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplification.**

$$
P_w = T_e \times \omega_e \times \eta_{tr} = P_e \times \eta_{tr}
$$

**Step 4 — Power loss in the transmission.**

$$
P_{loss} = P_e - P_w = P_e (1 - \eta_{tr})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-Style):** If engine power $P_e = 60$ kW and $\eta_{tr} = 0.95$, then $P_{loss} = 60 \times (1 - 0.95) = 3$ kW of power is dissipated as heat, sound, and friction in the gearbox.

---

## Derivation 4: First Gear Ratio for Maximum Gradeability

### Objective
Determine the minimum first gear ratio required for a vehicle to climb a maximum gradient $\theta_{max}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Force required to climb a slope.**

For a vehicle of mass $m$ climbing a slope of angle $\theta$, the gravitational resistance force is:

$$
F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)
$$

**Step 2 — Equating tractive force to slope resistance.**

For the vehicle to just climb the slope, the tractive force must equal the slope resistance (ignoring rolling resistance and air drag for simplicity):

$$
F_t = F_g
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the tractive force formula.**

$$
\dfrac{T_e \cdot i_{total} \cdot \eta_{tr}}{r} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta_{max})
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for the gear ratio.**

$$
i_{max} = \dfrac{m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta_{max}) \cdot r}{T_e \cdot \eta_{tr}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error in KTU Exams:** Forgetting to include the transmission efficiency $\eta_{tr}$ in the numerator. Always remember that the gearbox consumes **3–8%** of input power.

---

## Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Transmission Types

| Parameter | Manual Transmission (MT) | Automated Manual (AMT) | Torque Converter Automatic (AT) | Continuously Variable (CVT) |
|-----------|-------------------------|------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Clutch Pedal** | Yes (manual) | No (automated) | No (torque converter) | No (variator + clutches) |
| **Driver Effort** | High (in traffic) | Low | Low | Low |
| **Fuel Efficiency** | High (best) | High (~95% of MT) | Moderate (~85–90% of MT) | Moderate–High |
| **Shift Quality** | Best (skilled driver) | Jerky (single-clutch) | Smooth | Seamless |
| **Cost** | Low | Low–Medium | High | Medium–High |
| **Maintenance Cost** | Lowest | Low | High (fluid changes) | Medium |
| **Torque Capacity** | Very High | Medium (~350 Nm) | Very High | Low–Medium (~250 Nm) |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | High | High |
| **Typical Use** | All segments | Entry/mid cars | Luxury/commercial | Compact/mid cars |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: AMT System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

This block diagram illustrates the closed-loop control architecture of an Automated Manual Transmission, showing sensor inputs, the TCU, the actuators, and the mechanical drivetrain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DRIVER[DRIVER INPUT SELECTOR] --> TCU_A
    APP[ACCELERATOR PEDAL POSITION SENSOR] --> TCU_A
    BSW[BRAKE PEDAL SWITCH] --> TCU_A
    VSS[VEHICLE SPEED SENSOR] --> TCU_A
    ENG_RPM[ENGINE RPM SENSOR] --> TCU_A
    GEAR_POS[GEAR POSITION SENSOR] --> TCU_A
    CLUTCH_POS[CLUTCH POSITION SENSOR] --> TCU_A
    IMU_S[INERTIAL MEASUREMENT UNIT IMU] --> TCU_A
    TCU_A[TRANSMISSION CONTROL UNIT TCU] --> CLUTCH_ACT
    TCU_A --> GEAR_ACT
    TCU_A --> ENG_ECU[ENGINE ECU TORQUE REQUEST]
    CLUTCH_ACT[CLUTCH ACTUATOR EMA] --> CLUTCH_M[CLUTCH ASSEMBLY]
    GEAR_ACT[GEAR SHIFT ACTUATOR EMA] --> GB[MANUAL GEARBOX]
    CLUTCH_M --> GB
    GB --> FINAL[FINAL DRIVE DIFFERENTIAL]
    FINAL --> WHEELS[DRIVE WHEELS]
    WHEELS --> VSS
    ENG_ECU --> ENG[ENGINE OUTPUT]
    ENG --> CLUTCH_M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Solid arrows** represent signal flow (electrical) and mechanical power flow.
> - The TCU sits at the centre, receiving feedback from multiple sensors and commanding the two primary actuators: the **Clutch Actuator** and the **Gear Shift Actuator**.
> - The TCU also communicates with the **Engine ECU** to request torque reduction during shifts (a process called *torque intervention*), which improves shift quality.

---

## Diagram 2: AMT Gear Shift Operation Sequence (State Machine)

The following flowchart depicts the sequential state transitions that occur during a single AMT gear shift.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEUTRAL_STATE
    NEUTRAL_STATE --> ENGAGING_CLUTCH : DRIVER SELECTS GEAR
    ENGAGING_CLUTCH --> VEHICLE_DRIVING : CLUTCH FULLY ENGAGED
    VEHICLE_DRIVING --> SHIFT_REQUEST : SHIFT MAP THRESHOLD CROSSED
    SHIFT_REQUEST --> TORQUE_REDUCTION : TCU COMMANDS ENGINE ECU
    TORQUE_REDUCTION --> CLUTCH_RELEASE : DECREASE ENGINE TORQUE TO ZERO
    CLUTCH_RELEASE --> CLUTCH_OPEN : CLUTCH ACTUATOR FULLY DISENGAGES
    CLUTCH_OPEN --> GEAR_DISENGAGE : SHIFT ACTUATOR NEUTRALIZES
    GEAR_DISENGAGE --> GEAR_SELECT : TARGET GEAR SELECTED
    GEAR_SELECT --> SYNCHRONIZE : SYNCHRONIZER MESHES GEAR
    SYNCHRONIZE --> CLUTCH_REENGAGE : CLUTCH ACTUATOR RE-ENGAGES
    CLUTCH_REENGAGE --> TORQUE_RESTORE : ENGINE TORQUE RESTORED
    TORQUE_RESTORE --> VEHICLE_DRIVING : SHIFT COMPLETE
    VEHICLE_DRIVING --> [*] : ENGINE OFF
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Diagrams:** The state machine shows the **eight (8) distinct sub-states** of a single AMT gear shift. Memorize the sequence:
> *Shift Request → Torque Reduction → Clutch Release → Gear Disengage → Gear Select → Synchronize → Clutch Re-engage → Torque Restore.*

---

## Diagram 3: AMT Hydraulic Actuation Sub-System (Functional Architecture)

This diagram illustrates the hydraulic power flow in a typical electro-hydraulic AMT system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PUMP[HYDRAULIC PUMP DRIVEN BY ELECTRIC MOTOR] --> ACCU[ACCUMULATOR NITROGEN PRE-CHARGED]
    ACCU --> PCV[PRESSURE CONTROL VALVE]
    PCV --> CLUTCH_CYL[CLUTCH ACTUATION CYLINDER]
    PCV --> SHIFT_CYL1[SHIFT CYLINDER GEAR SELECT]
    PCV --> SHIFT_CYL2[SHIFT CYLINDER GEAR ENGAGE]
    CLUTCH_CYL --> CLUTCH_PLT[CLUTCH PLATE]
    SHIFT_CYL1 --> SEL_FORK[SELECTOR FORK]
    SHIFT_CYL2 --> ENG_FORK[ENGAGEMENT FORK]
    TCU_B[TRANSMISSION CONTROL UNIT] --> PCV
    TCU_B --> PUMP
    CLUTCH_PLT --> FEEDBACK[CLUTCH POSITION FEEDBACK]
    FEEDBACK --> TCU_B
    SEL_FORK --> FEEDBACK2[GEAR POSITION FEEDBACK]
    ENG_FORK --> FEEDBACK2
    FEEDBACK2 --> TCU_B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** The accumulator stores hydraulic energy at high pressure (typically **40–60 bar**), enabling rapid actuator response (less than **50 ms**) even when the engine is at low RPM or idling. This is critical for smooth AMT operation in stop-and-go traffic.

---

## Diagram 4: Comparison Flow - How AMT Differs From MT and AT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MT[MANUAL TRANSMISSION MT] --> SHARED{GEARBOX STRUCTURE}
    AMT[AMT] --> SHARED
    AT[TORQUE CONVERTER AT] --> UNIQUE1[HYDRAULIC TORQUE CONVERTER]
    CVT[CONTINUOUSLY VARIABLE CVT] --> UNIQUE2[STEEL BELT AND PULLEYS]
    SHARED --> DECISION1{ADD CLUTCH ACTUATOR AND TCU}
    DECISION1 --> AMT_OUT[AMT RESULT AUTOMATED GEARBOX]
    MT --> RESULT_MT[MANUAL SHIFTING BY DRIVER]
    UNIQUE1 --> RESULT_AT[SMOOTH TORQUE MULTIPLICATION]
    UNIQUE2 --> RESULT_CVT[INFINITE GEAR RATIOS]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU One-Liner Answer:**
> "AMT is essentially a manual transmission with **added actuators and TCU**, whereas AT replaces the clutch with a **torque converter** and CVT uses **variable-diameter pulleys**."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model]

**Define Automated Manual Transmission (AMT). List any four advantages of AMT over a conventional manual transmission.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 2)

**Definition (1 mark):**
An Automated Manual Transmission (AMT) is a type of transmission in which a conventional manual gearbox is automated using **electronic control unit (TCU)** and **electro-mechanical/hydraulic actuators** to perform clutch operation and gear shifting automatically, eliminating the need for a driver-operated clutch pedal.

**Four Advantages (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Reduced driver fatigue** — No clutch pedal operation required, especially beneficial in congested city traffic.
2. **Improved fuel efficiency** — Gear shifts are optimized via the TCU's shift map, often matching or exceeding manual driving efficiency.
3. **Lower cost** compared to conventional automatic transmissions (torque converter type) — typically **30–50% cheaper**.
4. **Compact design** — Uses the same manual gearbox architecture, requiring only additional actuator hardware.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model]

**With the help of a block diagram, explain the working principle of an Automated Manual Transmission.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 2)

**Concept (1 mark):**
AMT uses sensors to monitor vehicle parameters and an electronic control unit (TCU) to automatically actuate the clutch and gear shift mechanisms.

**Block Diagram Explanation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sensors (TPS, VSS, Ne)} \rightarrow \text{TCU} \rightarrow \text{Actuators} \rightarrow \text{Gearbox}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Sensors** measure driver input and vehicle state (accelerator position, vehicle speed, engine RPM, gear position).
- The **TCU** processes these signals using a pre-calibrated shift map and decides when to shift gears.
- **Clutch and gear actuators** (electro-mechanical or electro-hydraulic) physically disengage the clutch, move the shift forks, and re-engage the clutch.
- **Feedback sensors** monitor clutch position and gear position, closing the control loop.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model — 14 Marks]

**a)** Explain in detail the construction and working of an Automated Manual Transmission (AMT) with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the Transmission Control Unit (TCU) and the various sensors used. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A passenger car equipped with an AMT has the following specifications: engine maximum torque $T_e = 140$ N·m at $N_e = 3500$ RPM, first gear ratio $i_1 = 3.6$, final drive ratio $i_f = 4.2$, transmission efficiency $\eta_{tr} = 0.95$, and wheel rolling radius $r = 0.30$ m. Calculate: **(i)** the overall gear ratio, **(ii)** the tractive force at the wheels, and **(iii)** the vehicle speed at the given engine RPM. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction of AMT (3 Marks):**

The major components of an AMT are:

1. **Manual Gearbox Assembly** — Conventional constant-mesh or synchromesh gearbox with input shaft, output shaft, countershaft, and synchronizers.
2. **Clutch Assembly** — Single dry friction clutch (single-plate or multi-plate) with a release bearing.
3. **Clutch Actuator** — Electro-mechanical actuator (DC motor with lead screw) or electro-hydraulic cylinder that pushes the release fork to disengage the clutch.
4. **Gear Shift Actuator** — Two electro-mechanical actuators (one for gear *selection* and one for gear *engagement*) or a single actuator with dual-axis movement.
5. **Transmission Control Unit (TCU)** — Microcontroller-based ECU that runs the shift logic algorithms.
6. **Sensors** — Throttle Position Sensor (TPS), Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS), Engine Speed Sensor, Gear Position Sensor, Clutch Position Sensor, Brake Switch, and Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU).
7. **Hydraulic Control Unit (HCU)** *(if electro-hydraulic)* — Pump, accumulator, and solenoid valves providing pressurized fluid to the actuators.
8. **Selector Lever** — Electronically controlled gear selector (PRNDL) with no mechanical linkage to the gearbox (shift-by-wire).

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**

The TCU continuously reads the **Accelerator Pedal Position**, **Engine RPM**, and **Vehicle Speed** to determine the current operating point on a pre-calibrated **shift map**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shift Decision} &= f(\text{Throttle \%}, \text{Engine RPM}, \text{Vehicle Speed}, \text{Grade})
\end{aligned}
$$

When a shift is required, the following sequence is executed:

1. TCU requests **torque reduction** from the Engine ECU.
2. **Clutch actuator** disengages the clutch plate.
3. **Gear shift actuator** moves the shift fork to the neutral position.
4. **Gear shift actuator** selects the target gear (e.g., 2nd from 1st).
5. **Synchronizer ring** equalizes the speeds of the gear and shaft.
6. **Clutch actuator** progressively re-engages the clutch with a controlled ramp.
7. TCU requests **torque restoration** from the Engine ECU.
8. The vehicle accelerates in the new gear with **minimal torque interruption**.

**Role of TCU (1 Mark):**
The TCU is the **central processing unit** of the AMT. It performs **sensor data fusion**, **shift decision-making**, **actuator control** (PID-based), **adaptive learning** (refining shift quality over time), **diagnostics**, and **fault management** (limp-home mode in case of sensor failure).

---

#### Solution to Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $T_e = 140$ N·m
- $N_e = 3500$ RPM
- $i_1 = 3.6$ (first gear ratio)
- $i_f = 4.2$ (final drive ratio)
- $\eta_{tr} = 0.95$
- $r = 0.30$ m

**Part (i): Overall Gear Ratio** *(2 Marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{total} &= i_1 \times i_f \\
i_{total} &= 3.6 \times 4.2 \\
i_{total} &= 15.12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii): Tractive Force at the Wheels** *(2.5 Marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= \dfrac{T_e \times i_{total} \times \eta_{tr}}{r} \\
F_t &= \dfrac{140 \times 15.12 \times 0.95}{0.30} \\
F_t &= \dfrac{140 \times 14.364}{0.30} \\
F_t &= \dfrac{2010.96}{0.30} \\
F_t &= 6703.2 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (iii): Vehicle Speed at the Given Engine RPM** *(2.5 Marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \dfrac{0.377 \times N_e \times r}{i_{total}} \\
V &= \dfrac{0.377 \times 3500 \times 0.30}{15.12} \\
V &= \dfrac{395.85}{15.12} \\
V &= 26.18 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:**
> - Overall gear ratio = **15.12**
> - Tractive force = **6703.2 N** (≈ 684 kg-f)
> - Vehicle speed in 1st gear at 3500 RPM = **26.18 km/h** (typical city speed in 1st gear)

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model — 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]

**a)** Compare Automated Manual Transmission (AMT) with Conventional Automatic Transmission (AT) and Manual Transmission (MT). Discuss the constructional features, advantages, limitations, and typical applications of each. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** An AMT-equipped vehicle has the following parameters: vehicle mass $m = 1200$ kg, maximum gradient $\theta_{max} = 30°$, engine maximum torque $T_e = 180$ N·m, transmission efficiency $\eta_{tr} = 0.94$, and wheel radius $r = 0.32$ m. Calculate the **minimum first gear ratio** required to climb the gradient. If the final drive ratio is $i_f = 3.8$, determine the required transmission first gear ratio. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Feature | MT (Manual) | AMT (Automated Manual) | AT (Torque Converter Auto) |
|---------|-------------|-------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Clutch** | Manual, driver-operated | Automated (actuator) | Replaced by torque converter |
| **Gear Selection** | Driver via lever | Automated by TCU | Automated by TCU |
| **Shift Smoothness** | Best (with skilled driver) | Good (jerky in single-clutch) | Excellent |
| **Fuel Efficiency** | Best (reference) | ~92–95% of MT | ~85–90% of MT |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Low to Medium | Highest |
| **Driver Effort** | High | Low | Low |
| **Maintenance** | Lowest | Low | High (AT fluid changes) |
| **Torque Capacity** | Up to ~900 Nm | Up to ~350 Nm | Up to ~1000+ Nm |
| **Torque Interruption** | Minimal (skilled) | Yes (single-clutch) | None (fluid coupling) |
| **Applications** | All segments, commercial | Entry/mid cars, 2-wheelers | Luxury cars, SUVs, commercial |

**Constructional Features (2 Marks):**

- **MT:** Single dry clutch, synchromesh gearbox, mechanical shift linkage, clutch pedal, and gear lever.
- **AMT:** Same as MT, but with two actuators (clutch and shift) replacing the driver's physical actions. TCU and sensors are added. Shift-by-wire gear selector.
- **AT:** Torque converter (replaces clutch), planetary gear sets, hydraulic valve body, multiple clutch packs, and torque converter lock-up clutch.

**Applications (1 Mark):**
- MT: Budget cars, heavy commercial vehicles, performance cars (purist segment).
- AMT: Entry-level hatchbacks, mid-size sedans, two-wheelers (Honda Activa, TVS Scooty), light commercial vehicles.
- AT: Luxury cars, SUVs, automatic-only segments, heavy commercial vehicles (with lock-up torque converter for fuel economy).

---

#### Solution to Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Vehicle mass $m = 1200$ kg
- Maximum gradient $\theta_{max} = 30°$
- Engine torque $T_e = 180$ N·m
- Transmission efficiency $\eta_{tr} = 0.94$
- Wheel radius $r = 0.32$ m
- Final drive ratio $i_f = 3.8$
- Acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Calculate the slope-climbing force $F_g$** *(1.5 Marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_g &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta_{max}) \\
F_g &= 1200 \times 9.81 \times \sin(30°) \\
F_g &= 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \\
F_g &= 5886 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Equate tractive force to slope resistance** *(1.5 Marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= F_g \\
\dfrac{T_e \cdot i_{total} \cdot \eta_{tr}}{r} &= F_g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for the overall ratio $i_{total}$** *(2 Marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{total} &= \dfrac{F_g \cdot r}{T_e \cdot \eta_{tr}} \\
i_{total} &= \dfrac{5886 \times 0.32}{180 \times 0.94} \\
i_{total} &= \dfrac{1883.52}{169.2} \\
i_{total} &= 11.13
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the first gear ratio $i_1$** *(2 Marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{total} &= i_1 \times i_f \\
i_1 &= \dfrac{i_{total}}{i_f} \\
i_1 &= \dfrac{11.13}{3.8} \\
i_1 &= 2.93
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:**
> - Required overall ratio = **11.13**
> - Required first gear ratio = **2.93** (with final drive of 3.8)
> - A typical production car first gear ratio is **3.0–4.0**, confirming that a 30° gradient is achievable with standard AMT gearing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Always convert engine speed from RPM to rad/s when computing power, and convert vehicle speed from m/s to km/h (or vice versa) as required by the problem. Mixing units is the **#1 cause of full-mark loss** in KTU numericals.
> 2. **Efficiency Omission:** Forgetting the transmission efficiency $\eta_{tr}$ in tractive force and gradeability calculations will result in a **1–2 mark deduction**.
> 3. **Gradient Angle vs. Gradient Percentage:** A gradient of 30° corresponds to a **57.7%** grade (tan 30° = 0.577). Students often confuse these. Verify which form the problem uses.
> 4. **AMT vs. AT Confusion:** Examiners specifically look for clarity on the **constructional difference** — AMT uses a *friction clutch* and *synchromesh gears*, while AT uses a *torque converter* and *planetary gears*. A vague answer like "AMT is automatic" will fetch **partial credit only**.
> 5. **Sensor Naming:** Be precise — use **"Throttle Position Sensor"** and **"Vehicle Speed Sensor"** rather than vague terms like "speed sensor" or "throttle sensor".
> 6. **Direction of Force in Tractive Force Equation:** Always divide torque by wheel radius (not multiply). Many students write $F_t = T_e \times i_{total} \times \eta_{tr} \times r$, which is dimensionally and physically wrong.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for AMT:**

- **AMT = Manual Gearbox + Electronic Actuators + TCU + Sensors.** It is **not** a torque converter automatic.
- **Two main types:** Single-clutch AMT (jerky, low cost) and Dual-clutch AMT (smooth, high cost).
- **Key components to remember:** TCU, Clutch Actuator, Gear Shift Actuator, HCU (if hydraulic), TPS, VSS, Engine Speed Sensor, Gear Position Sensor, Clutch Position Sensor, IMU, Brake Switch.
- **Shift sequence (memorize the 8 steps):** Shift Request → Torque Reduction → Clutch Release → Gear Disengage → Gear Select → Synchronize → Clutch Re-engage → Torque Restore.
- **Standard shift times:** Single-clutch AMT = **200–500 ms**; Dual-clutch AMT = **100–200 ms**; Skilled human driver in WRC = **20–50 ms**.
- **Key equations (master these):**
  - $V = \dfrac{0.377 \cdot N_e \cdot r}{i_{total}}$
  - $F_t = \dfrac{T_e \cdot i_{total} \cdot \eta_{tr}}{r}$
  - $P_w = P_e \times \eta_{tr}$
  - $i_{max} = \dfrac{m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta_{max}) \cdot r}{T_e \cdot \eta_{tr}}$
- **Standard values:** $\eta_{tr} = 0.92$ to $0.96$ (use 0.95 if not given); $g = 9.81$ m/s²; $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³.
- **Advantages:** Fuel efficient (≈MT), low cost, no clutch pedal, compact, easy integration.
- **Disadvantages:** Torque interruption (single-clutch), jerky low-speed creep, limited torque capacity (~350 Nm), heat in clutch.
- **Real-world AMT cars:** Maruti Suzuki Swift, Hyundai Grand i10 Nios, Tata Tiago, Renault Triber.
- **Dual-clutch AMT cars:** Volkswagen Polo/GT, Skoda Rapid, Audi TT, Porsche 911 (PDK).
- **Hill-hold assist** in AMT is implemented using the **IMU + brake actuator** combination to prevent rollback on inclines.
- **Creep function** is achieved by partially engaging the clutch at idle engine RPM, mimicking torque-converter creep.
- **Adaptive shift logic:** Modern AMTs use **machine learning** to adapt shift patterns to individual driver behaviour over time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
