> **Sequential MPFI (SFI)** is the industry standard today. The injector fires **once per two crank revolutions (720°)**, precisely synchronized to the intake stroke of its cylinder.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle of CRDI — Step-by-Step

The CRDI operating sequence has **8 logical stages** due to its higher complexity:

1. **Pre-Supply Stage** — A **lift pump (transfer pump)** in the tank draws fuel through a fine filter and delivers it to the high-pressure pump at ~5 – 7 bar.
2. **Pressure Generation Stage** — A **radial-piston high-pressure pump** (e.g., Bosch CP1, CP3, CP4) compresses the fuel in 3 pistons (or more) to 1500 – 2000 bar.
3. **Rail Pressure Stabilization Stage** — The high-pressure fuel flows into the **common rail**, a tubular accumulator with a volume of ~20 – 40 cm³. A **pressure control valve (PCV / DRV)** at the pump and a **rail pressure sensor** form a closed loop with the ECU, holding rail pressure within ±2% of target.
4. **Pressure Holding Stage** — The rail maintains fuel at the commanded pressure independent of engine speed — this *decoupling* is the soul of CRDI.
5. **Injection Control Stage** — The ECU decides the **injection timing (T_inj)**, **injection pressure (P_rail)**, and **injection quantity (Q_inj)**. The injector solenoid (or piezo actuator) opens the nozzle.
6. **Multi-Event Injection Stage** — A single combustion cycle can have:
   - **Pilot injection** (1 – 2 mm³) — reduces combustion noise
   - **Main injection** (bulk of fuel) — produces torque
   - **After injection** — regenerates DPF, raises exhaust temp
7. **Combustion Stage** — The atomized fuel auto-ignites due to high compression temperatures (≈ 600 °C).
8. **Pressure Relief Stage** — Excess fuel is returned to tank through a cooler, completing the loop.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **routinely tested** in KTU ESE papers. Memorize the variables, units, and sign conventions.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{AFR_{stoich} \cdot \lambda}$ | Mass fuel flow from air mass and lambda | $\dot{m}$ in kg/s, $\lambda$ dimensionless |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{inj} = C_d \cdot A_{noz} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{fuel} \cdot \Delta P_{rail}}$ | Injector mass flow rate (Bernoulli-derived) | $C_d$ ≈ 0.85 – 0.95, $A_{noz}$ in m², $\Delta P$ in Pa, $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 3 | $Q_{inj} = \dot{m}_{inj} \cdot t_{inj} \cdot N_{inj}$ | Total injected fuel per cycle | $Q$ in mg, $t_{inj}$ in s, $N_{inj}$ = number of injections/cycle |
| 4 | $P_{rail} = K \cdot n_{eng}$ (approx) | Rail pressure vs. engine speed (linear regime) | $K$ = pump constant, $n_{eng}$ in rpm |
| 5 | $P_{injection\ line} = P_{rail} - \Delta P_{line}$ | Pressure at injector inlet | $\Delta P_{line}$ = line loss |
| 6 | $t_{inj} = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{C_d A_{noz} \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}}$ | Injector pulse width | $t_{inj}$ in seconds |
| 7 | $SMD = A \cdot \left(\dfrac{\mu_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel}}\right)^{0.5} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho_{air}}{\Delta P}\right)^{0.25} \cdot d_{noz}^{0.5}$ | Sauter Mean Diameter of spray | $SMD$ in μm; smaller = better atomization |
| 8 | $P_{crank} = \dfrac{n_{cyl} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot N_{eng}}{2 \cdot 60 \cdot \eta_{vol}}$ | Theoretical displacement pump rate (geometry) | $V_{disp}$ in m³ |
| 9 | $V_{rail} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{pump} \cdot \Delta t_{inj}}{\Delta P_{allowed}}$ | Required rail accumulator volume | $V_{rail}$ in m³ |
| 10 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{actual}}{\rho_{air} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot n_{eng}/2}$ | Volumetric efficiency of engine | dimensionless |
| 11 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh |
| 12 | $\tau_{inj\ delay} = \dfrac{L_{line}}{c_{sound\ in\ fuel}}$ | Hydraulic delay between command and injection | $L$ in m, $c$ ≈ 1300 m/s in diesel |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** For exam answers, NEVER use `|` (vertical pipe) inside any table cell — use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value / conditional expressions to avoid breaking markdown tables.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MPFI** dominates passenger petrol cars in India due to lower cost, BS-VI compliance via 3-way catalytic converter, and adequate atomization for SI combustion.
- **CRDI** dominates diesel commercial vehicles, tractors, and SUVs because of its 40%+ thermal efficiency advantage and ability to support advanced after-treatment (DPF, SCR, EGR).
- The **electronic controls** in both systems form the foundation for **drive-by-wire throttle**, **cylinder deactivation**, **stop-start systems**, and **mild hybrid (48V)** architectures — all of which are part of KTU Module 5 (Modern Trends).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Injector Mass Flow Rate (Bernoulli Application)

The injector is essentially a **pressurized orifice**. Applying Bernoulli's equation between the rail (point 1) and the combustion chamber (point 2), and accounting for viscous losses through the **discharge coefficient** $C_d$:

**Starting Equation (Steady, Incompressible Bernoulli):**

$$
\dfrac{P_1}{\rho_{fuel}} + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho_{fuel}} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Assume rail velocity ≈ 0** (fuel is essentially static in the rail):

$$
v_1 \approx 0
$$

So the equation reduces to:

$$
\dfrac{P_1}{\rho_{fuel}} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho_{fuel}} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for velocity at nozzle exit:**

$$
\dfrac{v_2^2}{2} = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_{fuel}}
$$

Taking the square root of both sides:

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho_{fuel}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the pressure differential $\Delta P$:**

$$
\Delta P = P_{rail} - P_{combustion}
$$

For MPFI, $P_{combustion} \approx P_{manifold} \approx 0.5 \text{ bar (gauge)}$.
For CRDI, $P_{combustion}$ varies during the cycle (3 – 80 bar).

**Step 4 — Apply the discharge coefficient** (accounts for vena contracta and friction):

$$
v_{actual} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_{fuel}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by nozzle area and density to get mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_{inj} = \rho_{fuel} \cdot A_{noz} \cdot v_{actual}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{inj} = C_d \cdot A_{noz} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho_{fuel} \cdot \Delta P}}
$$

This is **Formula #2 in the cheat sheet** — it is the most tested equation in MPFI/CRDI problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Injector Pulse Width

Given the required fuel quantity $Q_{req}$ (in mg per injection event), the ECU must compute the **injector energization time** $t_{inj}$.

**Starting Point:**

$$
Q_{req} = \dot{m}_{inj} \cdot t_{inj}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the Bernoulli-derived mass flow rate:**

$$
Q_{req} = C_d \cdot A_{noz} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{fuel} \cdot \Delta P} \cdot t_{inj}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $t_{inj}$:**

$$
t_{inj} = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{C_d \cdot A_{noz} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{fuel} \cdot \Delta P}}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the **static flow rate** $Q_{static}$** (manufacturer-specified, measured at $\Delta P_{ref}$):

$$
Q_{static} = C_d \cdot A_{noz} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{fuel} \cdot \Delta P_{ref}}
$$

So the actual pulse width becomes:

$$
t_{inj} = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{Q_{static}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_{ref}}{\Delta P}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ECU firmware contains a **3-D lookup table** indexed by $\Delta P$ and battery voltage (since solenoid pull-in time depends on voltage), and it corrects $t_{inj}$ accordingly. This is the essence of **injector dead time / offset** compensation in modern ECUs.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

### 🔢 Problem Statement
A 4-cylinder MPFI engine runs at **3000 rpm**. Each cylinder displaces **500 cm³** with a **volumetric efficiency of 85%**. The fuel rail pressure is **3.5 bar (gauge)** and manifold pressure is **0.5 bar (gauge)**. Injector nozzle diameter is **1.6 mm**, $C_d = 0.85$, fuel density $\rho_{fuel} = 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Compute:
1. Mass of air per cylinder per cycle.
2. Mass of fuel per cylinder per cycle (assume stoich, $AFR = 14.7$).
3. Required injector pulse width.

### ✍️ Solution

#### Part 1 — Mass of Air per Cylinder

The displacement per cylinder is $V_{disp} = 500 \text{ cm}^3 = 500 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$.

Air density at manifold conditions (assume $\rho_{air} = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$):

$$
\dot{m}_{air/cyl} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot \dfrac{n_{eng}}{2 \cdot 60}
$$

(Note: divide by 2 for 4-stroke — one intake event per 2 revolutions.)

$$
\dot{m}_{air/cyl} = 0.85 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \dfrac{3000}{120}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{air/cyl} = 0.85 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 25
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{air/cyl} = 0.85 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.0125
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{air/cyl} = 0.01275 \text{ kg/s per cylinder}
$$

> Note: this is the *flow rate* into one cylinder. The mass per cycle is obtained by multiplying by the cycle time (2/3000 min = 0.04 s):

$$
m_{air/cycle} = 0.01275 \cdot 0.04 = 5.1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg} = 510 \text{ mg}
$$

#### Part 2 — Mass of Fuel per Cycle (Stoich, $\lambda = 1$)

$$
m_{fuel/cycle} = \dfrac{m_{air/cycle}}{AFR_{stoich}}
$$

$$
m_{fuel/cycle} = \dfrac{510}{14.7} = 34.69 \text{ mg/cycle/cylinder}
$$

#### Part 3 — Injector Pulse Width

Net pressure differential:

$$
\Delta P = 3.5 - 0.5 = 3.0 \text{ bar} = 3 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}
$$

Nozzle area:

$$
A_{noz} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (1.6 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 2.0106 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

Mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{inj} = 0.85 \cdot 2.0106 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot 750 \cdot 3 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
\sqrt{2 \cdot 750 \cdot 3 \times 10^5} = \sqrt{4.5 \times 10^8} = 2.1213 \times 10^4
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{inj} = 0.85 \cdot 2.0106 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 2.1213 \times 10^4
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{inj} = 0.85 \cdot 0.04265 = 0.03625 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Pulse width:

$$
t_{inj} = \dfrac{34.69 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg}}{0.03625 \text{ kg/s}} = 9.57 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{inj} \approx 0.957 \text{ ms} \approx 0.96 \text{ ms}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** This value is in the realistic MPFI range (0.8 – 4 ms at cruise). At idle (lower air mass), $t_{inj}$ may drop to 1.0 – 1.5 ms, and at WOT it may reach 8 – 12 ms. The ECU's **pulse width map** is a 3-D surface indexed by RPM and load.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: MPFI vs CRDI

| Parameter | MPFI | CRDI |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure generation | Inline electric pump @ 3.5 bar | Radial piston pump @ 1500 – 2000 bar |
| Pressure storage | Fuel rail (small volume) | Common rail (large accumulator) |
| Injection event | Single pulse per cycle (mostly) | Multiple events (pilot + main + after) |
| Atomization driver | Pressure + hot intake valve | Ultra-high pressure alone |
| Nozzle type | Pintle / disc | Multi-hole (5 – 9 holes) |
| ECU feedback | Lambda + MAP | Rail pressure sensor + Lambda + Temp |
| NOx control | 3-way catalyst + EGR (limited) | EGR + DPF + SCR (extensive) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (but falling) |
| Particulate matter | Very low (gasoline) | Higher (needs DPF) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 MPFI System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[ELECTRIC FUEL PUMP]
    B --> C[FUEL FILTER]
    C --> D[FUEL RAIL ASSEMBLY]
    D --> E[PRESSURE REGULATOR]
    E --> F[RETURN LINE TO TANK]
    D --> G1[INJECTOR 1]
    D --> G2[INJECTOR 2]
    D --> G3[INJECTOR 3]
    D --> G4[INJECTOR 4]
    G1 --> H1[CYLINDER 1 INTAKE VALVE]
    G2 --> H2[CYLINDER 2 INTAKE VALVE]
    G3 --> H3[CYLINDER 3 INTAKE VALVE]
    G4 --> H4[CYLINDER 4 INTAKE VALVE]
    I[MAF SENSOR] --> J[ECU]
    K[MAP SENSOR] --> J
    L[O2 SENSOR] --> J
    M[COOLANT TEMP] --> J
    N[CRANKSHAFT POSITION] --> J
    J -->|PULSE WIDTH| G1
    J -->|PULSE WIDTH| G2
    J -->|PULSE WIDTH| G3
    J -->|PULSE WIDTH| G4
    J -.FEEDBACK.-> L
    style A fill:#222,color:#fff
    style J fill:#cc6600,color:#fff
    style D fill:#336699,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Fuel flows from tank → pump → filter → fuel rail.
- The pressure regulator bleeds excess fuel back to the tank.
- The fuel rail feeds four injectors, each spraying onto its intake valve.
- The ECU receives inputs from 5 sensors and commands the injectors.
- The O₂ sensor provides closed-loop feedback (dashed line).

---

## 4.2 CRDI System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[LIFT PUMP 5-7 BAR]
    B --> C[FUEL FILTER FINE]
    C --> D[RADIAL PISTON HIGH PRESSURE PUMP]
    D -->|1500-2000 BAR| E[COMMON RAIL ACCUMULATOR]
    E --> F[RAIL PRESSURE SENSOR]
    F -->|FEEDBACK| M[ECU]
    G[PRESSURE CONTROL VALVE] --> D
    M -->|PWM COMMAND| G
    E --> H1[PIEZO INJECTOR CYL1]
    E --> H2[PIEZO INJECTOR CYL2]
    E --> H3[PIEZO INJECTOR CYL3]
    E --> H4[PIEZO INJECTOR CYL4]
    H1 --> I1[COMBUSTION CHAMBER 1]
    H2 --> I2[COMBUSTION CHAMBER 2]
    H3 --> I3[COMBUSTION CHAMBER 3]
    H4 --> I4[COMBUSTION CHAMBER 4]
    I1 --> J[EXHAUST MANIFOLD]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    J --> K[DPF DIESEL PARTICULATE FILTER]
    K --> L[SCR SELECTIVE CATALYTIC REDUCTION]
    L --> N[TAILPIPE]
    P[CRANKSHAFT SENSOR] --> M
    Q[COOLANT TEMP] --> M
    R[ACCEL PEDAL SENSOR] --> M
    M -->|PILOT+MAIN+AFTER| H1
    M -->|PILOT+MAIN+AFTER| H2
    M -->|PILOT+MAIN+AFTER| H3
    M -->|PILOT+MAIN+AFTER| H4
    style A fill:#222,color:#fff
    style E fill:#990000,color:#fff
    style M fill:#cc6600,color:#fff
    style K fill:#336699,color:#fff
    style L fill:#336699,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Fuel travels through **two pressure stages**: lift pump (low) and radial piston pump (ultra-high).
- The **common rail** is the heart of the system — always pressurized.
- The **pressure control valve (PCV)** regulates rail pressure on ECU command.
- Each injector receives **multi-event (pilot, main, after)** commands from the ECU.
- The after-treatment train (DPF → SCR) is shown as it is integral to BS-VI CRDI vehicles.

---

## 4.3 Multi-Event Injection Timing Diagram (CRDI)

```mermaid
gantt
    title CRDI Multi-Event Injection per Combustion Cycle
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Crank Angle
    Pilot Injection (-15 to -10 deg BTDC)   :a1, 0, 5
    Main Injection (-2 to +8 deg BTDC)     :a2, 5, 12
    After Injection (60 to 90 deg ATDC)    :a3, 12, 17
    section Combustion
    Ignition Delay (a1 to a2 gap)          :crit, 5, 5
    Rapid Pressure Rise (a2 peak)          :crit, 8, 9
    Heat Release Tail                       :done, 12, 17
```

> [!NOTE]
> The pilot injection is a tiny pre-spray (~1 – 2 mm³) that raises local temperature, shortens the **ignition delay** of the main injection, and dramatically reduces **diesel knock** and **NOx formation**.

---

## 4.4 Sensor-ECU-Actuator Closed Loop (MPFI)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DRIVER INPUT ACCEL PEDAL] --> B[TPS]
    A --> C[MAF]
    A --> D[CTS]
    A --> E[CKP CAMSHAFT]
    B & C & D & E --> F[ECU FUEL MAP]
    F -->|PULSE WIDTH| G[FUEL INJECTORS]
    G --> H[ENGINE COMBUSTION]
    H --> I[EXHAUST GAS]
    I --> J[O2 LAMBDA SENSOR]
    J -->|CLOSED LOOP CORRECTION| F
    style F fill:#cc6600,color:#fff
    style H fill:#990000,color:#fff
    style J fill:#336699,color:#fff
```

This is the canonical **closed-loop air-fuel control loop** in MPFI. The O₂ sensor makes MPFI emissions-compliant by continuously trimming fuel delivery around stoich (λ = 1.000 ± 0.01).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]
**List any six advantages of MPFI over a conventional carburetor system.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Better A/F control:** ECU maintains precise air-fuel ratio within ±1% across all operating conditions, vs. ±10% in carburetors.
2. **Improved fuel atomization:** Individual injectors produce finer spray droplets (SMD 100 – 200 μm), improving combustion.
3. **Lower cold-start enrichment:** ECU compensates via coolant temperature input; carburetors need choke mechanisms.
4. **Reduced emissions:** Closed-loop O₂ feedback enables BS-VI compliance with a 3-way catalytic converter.
5. **No altitude/altitude-correction needed:** MAP sensor self-corrects for altitude.
6. **Better driveability:** Faster transient response, no fuel puddle formation issues, no icing in venturi.

> [!TIP]
> For 3-mark questions, list **exactly 6 points** with **one-line justification each** — examiners look for keyword density, not essays.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**Compare MPFI and CRDI on the basis of: (a) injection pressure, (b) injection location, (c) engine type.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | MPFI | CRDI |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Injection pressure | 2.5 – 4.0 bar (gauge) | 1500 – 2000 bar (gauge) |
| (b) Injection location | At the back of the intake valve (port) | Directly into the combustion chamber |
| (c) Engine type | Spark Ignition (SI / petrol) | Compression Ignition (CI / diesel) |

> [!TIP]
> Tabular comparison always scores more in KTU valuation. Reserve the third option in the comparison for a 1-line conceptual note if the question has 4 sub-parts.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### 📝 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 4]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Multiport Fuel Injection (MPFI)** system in a petrol engine. List its main components. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the **mass flow rate of fuel through an injector** using Bernoulli's principle. A 4-cylinder MPFI engine runs at 2800 rpm with $V_{disp} = 480 \text{ cm}^3$ per cylinder and $\eta_{vol} = 88\%$. Injector nozzle diameter is 1.5 mm, $C_d = 0.9$, fuel density = 745 kg/m³, and rail pressure differential = 2.8 bar. Compute the **injector pulse width** at stoich (AFR = 14.7) and standard air density. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

##### Part (a) — MPFI Working Principle [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

*(Sketch the architecture shown in SECTION 4.1 with proper labels: tank → pump → filter → fuel rail → injectors → intake valves; ECU in the middle with sensor inputs and injector output.)*

**Main Components:**

1. **Electric fuel pump** (in-tank, ~3.5 bar)
2. **Fuel filter** (10 – 30 μm)
3. **Fuel rail** (distributes fuel to all injectors at uniform pressure)
4. **Pressure regulator** (controls rail to manifold ΔP)
5. **Fuel injectors** (solenoid type, one per cylinder)
6. **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** with input map memory
7. **Sensors:** MAF, MAP, TPS, O₂, CTS, CKP/CMP
8. **Throttle body** (driver-controlled air inlet)

**Working Explanation (4 logical steps):**

- **Step 1 (Air Intake):** Driver presses accelerator → throttle opens → air flows past MAF/MAP sensor → ECU reads mass air flow.
- **Step 2 (Fuel Pressurization):** Electric pump pushes fuel to fuel rail at constant 3.5 bar; pressure regulator maintains constant ΔP above manifold.
- **Step 3 (Injection):** ECU computes pulse width and energizes each injector individually (sequential mode). Injector sprays atomized fuel on the back of the intake valve.
- **Step 4 (Closed-Loop Correction):** O₂ sensor in exhaust feeds back voltage signal (0.1 – 0.9 V); ECU uses a PI controller to trim fuel with STFT (±10%) and LTFT (±25%).

##### Part (b) — Derivation + Numerical [7 Marks]

**Derivation of $\dot{m}_{inj}$:**

Starting from Bernoulli's equation (refer SECTION 3.1):

$$
P_1 + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2
$$

With $v_1 \approx 0$ in the rail:

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{fuel}}}
$$

Accounting for the discharge coefficient:

$$
v_{actual} = C_d \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{fuel}}}
$$

Multiplying by $A_{noz} \cdot \rho_{fuel}$:

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{inj} = C_d \cdot A_{noz} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{fuel} \Delta P}}
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Air mass per cylinder per second:**

$$
\dot{m}_{air/cyl} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot \dfrac{n_{eng}}{2 \cdot 60}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{air/cyl} = 0.88 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 480 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \dfrac{2800}{120}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{air/cyl} = 0.88 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 480 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 23.33
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{air/cyl} = 0.88 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.0112 = 0.01183 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Fuel mass per cylinder per second (stoich):**

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel/cyl} = \dfrac{0.01183}{14.7} = 8.05 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s} = 805 \text{ mg/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Injector mass flow rate:**

$$
A_{noz} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{inj} = 0.9 \cdot 1.767 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot 745 \cdot 2.8 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
\sqrt{2 \cdot 745 \cdot 2.8 \times 10^5} = \sqrt{4.172 \times 10^8} = 2.043 \times 10^4
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{inj} = 0.9 \cdot 1.767 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 2.043 \times 10^4 = 0.0325 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Pulse width:**

$$
t_{inj} = \dfrac{8.05 \times 10^{-4}}{0.0325} = 0.02477 \text{ s} = 24.77 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{inj} \approx 24.8 \text{ ms}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** At 2800 rpm, the time available per intake event is 2/2800 min = 42.86 ms. An injector pulse width of 24.8 ms is about 58% of available time, which is on the **higher side** — consistent with a high-load (near-WOT) operating point. At idle, the pulse width would be 2 – 3 ms.

---

### 📝 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Explain with a block diagram the working of a **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI)** system. What is meant by **multi-event injection**? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An automotive CRDI engine has a common rail of volume 30 cm³. The high-pressure pump delivers fuel at 1800 bar. If the engine is idling at 800 rpm with a total cycle fuel quantity of 8 mm³/cycle/cylinder across 4 cylinders, determine whether the rail pressure will drop by more than 5% during one injection event. Assume bulk modulus of diesel $K = 1.6 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

##### Part (a) — CRDI Working + Multi-Event Injection [7 Marks]

**Working Principle (refer to flowchart in SECTION 4.2):**

- **Lift pump** transfers fuel from tank to high-pressure pump at low pressure.
- **Radial piston pump** pressurizes fuel to 1500 – 2000 bar and fills the **common rail**.
- **Common rail** (a tubular accumulator) holds this high-pressure fuel continuously, decoupled from engine speed.
- **Rail pressure sensor** sends feedback to ECU; ECU commands the **pressure control valve (DRV)** to maintain the target pressure.
- **ECU** triggers each **piezo/solenoid injector** at precisely timed crank angles; fuel sprays directly into the combustion chamber through multi-hole nozzles.
- **Excess fuel** is returned to tank via a fuel cooler.

**Multi-Event Injection (3-mark dedicated explanation):**

- **Pilot injection** (~1 – 2 mm³) occurs ~15° before TDC, raising local cylinder temperature and reducing the main injection's ignition delay, thus lowering combustion noise and NOx.
- **Main injection** (the bulk of fuel) occurs at -2° to +8° ATDC, producing peak cylinder pressure and torque.
- **After injection** (post-combustion) occurs 60° – 90° ATDC, raising exhaust gas temperature for **DPF regeneration** (burning trapped soot).

##### Part (b) — Rail Pressure Drop Numerical [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $V_{rail} = 30 \text{ cm}^3 = 30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$
- $P_{rail} = 1800 \text{ bar} = 1.8 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa}$
- Engine: 800 rpm, 4 cylinders, 8 mm³/cycle/cylinder
- $K_{bulk} = 1.6 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1 — Total fuel injected per engine cycle:**

$$
Q_{total} = 8 \text{ mm}^3 \times 4 = 32 \text{ mm}^3 = 32 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Volume change as fraction of rail volume:**

$$
\dfrac{\Delta V}{V_{rail}} = \dfrac{32 \times 10^{-9}}{30 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.067 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Pressure drop from bulk modulus definition:**

The bulk modulus relates volumetric strain to pressure change:

$$
K = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta V / V}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\Delta P = K \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_{rail}}
$$

$$
\Delta P = 1.6 \times 10^9 \cdot 1.067 \times 10^{-3} = 1.707 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bar:**

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{1.707 \times 10^6}{10^5} = 17.07 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage drop:**

$$
\% \text{ drop} = \dfrac{17.07}{1800} \times 100 = 0.948\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\% \text{ drop} \approx 0.95\% \; < \; 5\% \; \checkmark \text{Acceptable}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The rail pressure drop of **0.95%** is well within the **5% tolerance**, confirming that the 30 cm³ common rail is **adequately sized** for this idle condition. The high-pressure pump has time to refill the rail between injection events.

> [!TIP]
> This type of problem tests the student's grasp of **hydraulic accumulator design** — a favorite of KTU paper-setters. The bulk modulus relation $K = -\Delta P / (\Delta V / V)$ is the same concept used in hydraulic accumulator sizing in industrial hydraulics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE MARKS in MPFI/CRDI questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Bernoulli derivation:** Students write $\Delta P$ in bar but forget to convert to Pa when applying $\sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$. **Always convert pressure to SI (Pa) before plugging into formulas.**
>
> 2. **Dividing RPM by 60 only once:** For 4-stroke engines, the intake event happens once per **2 revolutions** — so divide by **2 × 60 = 120**, not just 60.
>
> 3. **Confusing MPFI rail pressure with CRDI rail pressure:** Writing "4 bar" for CRDI or "1500 bar" for MPFI will immediately fetch **zero marks** in the comparison part.
>
> 4. **Forgetting $C_d$ in the mass flow equation:** A common trap question asks "what is the *theoretical* flow rate?" — in which case $C_d = 1$ and you must not include it. Read the question carefully.
>
> 5. **In the rail pressure drop numerical:** Students often use Young's modulus (E) instead of bulk modulus (K). **Use bulk modulus for fluids, Young's modulus for solids.**
>
> 6. **Diagram labels:** Examiners award 1 mark just for a **neat, labeled block diagram** with arrows showing flow direction. Always draw the diagram, even if you can explain verbally.
>
> 7. **In the multi-event injection explanation:** Students often forget to mention **DPF regeneration** as the purpose of after-injection — this is a guaranteed 1-mark differentiator.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> **The following is your rapid-revision checklist for this topic — read this 10 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **MPFI = One injector per cylinder + low pressure (2.5 – 4 bar) + sprays at intake valve + used in SI engines.**
- ✅ **CRDI = One shared high-pressure rail (1500 – 2000 bar) + direct injection into cylinder + used in CI engines.**
- ✅ **MPFI injector pulse width = 1 – 12 ms; CRDI events = pilot (1 – 2 mm³) + main (bulk) + after (DPF regen).**
- ✅ **Master the Bernoulli-derived formula:** $\dot{m}_{inj} = C_d A_{noz} \sqrt{2 \rho_{fuel} \Delta P}$. **This is THE most important equation.**
- ✅ **Always convert ΔP to Pascals (Pa) before using in formulas** — common trap.
- ✅ **4-stroke engines → divide RPM by 120** for per-cylinder air flow (intake event per 2 revs).
- ✅ **CRDI's key innovation = pressure-storage decoupling** — rail pressure is independent of engine speed.
- ✅ **Closed-loop control in MPFI** is via the **O₂ (Lambda) sensor**; **CRDI** uses **rail pressure sensor + Lambda** for closed loop.
- ✅ **Bulk modulus of diesel ≈ $1.6 \times 10^9$ Pa** — memorize this for accumulator-sizing problems.
- ✅ **Piezo injectors** in modern CRDI are **7× faster** than solenoid injectors, enabling multiple precise injections.
- ✅ **Multi-event injection** reduces **diesel knock, NOx, and particulates** simultaneously — explain this chain in 2-mark questions.
- ✅ **Nozzle type:** MPFI = pintle / disc; CRDI = multi-hole (5 – 9 holes, ~150 μm each).
- ✅ **For derivation questions:** Always show **all 4 – 5 steps** — KTU valuators give step-marks, not just the final answer.
- ✅ **Tabular answers** > paragraph answers for comparison questions (2 extra marks on average).
- ✅ **The ECU is the brain:** Every sensor input and actuator output in both MPFI and CRDI must be mentioned in any "explain the system" question.
- ✅ **Future-trend linkage:** CRDI is the foundation for **common-rail petrol GDi systems** (Toyota D-4ST, Hyundai Theta II 2.0T) — KTU loves cross-topic linking.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme focus:** Weightage is on **MPFI block diagram (3 marks) + CRDI explanation (4 marks) + numerical on injector pulse width or rail pressure (7 marks)** — practice exactly this pattern.

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 4, TOPIC: FUEL INJECTION SYSTEMS (MPFI & CRDI)**
> *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol. Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Automobile Engineering (PEMRT413).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Vehicle Technology (EVT) - EV Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT413 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electric Vehicle Technology (EVT) - EV Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electric Vehicle (EV) Architecture** refers to the systematic arrangement, integration, and interconnection of the major powertrain and auxiliary subsystems within an electric vehicle. It defines the topological layout of components such as the **energy storage system (ESS)**, **electric traction motor**, **power electronic converters (inverter, DC-DC converter, onboard charger)**, **transmission system**, **vehicle control unit (VCU)**, and **thermal management system**, along with the high-voltage and low-voltage wiring harness networks that bind them into a functional drivetrain.

According to the **SAE J1772** standard, EV architecture is broadly classified based on the **electric energy source** and the **degree of electrification** of the powertrain, ranging from mild hybrid electric vehicles (MHEV) to full battery electric vehicles (BEV).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The 2024 Scheme expects students to identify the major building blocks of an EV, distinguish between BEV, HEV, PHEV, and FCEV topologies, and comprehend the function of each subsystem in delivering traction, energy management, and regenerative braking.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of an EV's architecture like the **circulatory system of the human body**:
- The **battery pack** is the **heart**, pumping energy instead of blood.
- The **high-voltage cables and busbars** are the **arteries and veins**, carrying current to where it is needed.
- The **inverter and power electronics** act as the **lungs**, converting and regulating the "breath" (DC to AC, voltage step-up/down).
- The **electric motor** is the **muscle**, converting that energy into motion.
- The **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** is the **brain**, deciding how much power to deliver, when to regenerate, and how to balance the entire system.

Just as a cardiologist must understand every vessel and chamber, an automobile engineer must understand every component, line, and connection inside the EV to diagnose, design, and optimize the vehicle.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics of an EV Architecture

The performance of any EV architecture is judged by the following **bold-marked standard metrics**:
- **All-Electric Range (AER)** – Distance travelled on a single full charge (km).
- **Battery Energy Capacity (E_bat)** – Total storable energy, typically in **kWh**.
- **Specific Energy** – Energy per unit mass of the battery (**Wh/kg**).
- **Energy Density** – Energy per unit volume of the battery (**Wh/L**).
- **Peak Power Rating (P_peak)** – Maximum deliverable power by the motor (**kW**).
- **State of Charge (SOC)** – Real-time fuel gauge of the battery, expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
- **State of Health (SOH)** – Long-term degradation indicator of the battery (%).
- **C-rate** – Rate at which a battery is charged or discharged relative to its capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** The architecture is not just a parts list — it is a *holistic blueprint* of energy flow, control logic, and mechanical integration that defines the vehicle's character, range, performance, and safety.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks of EV Architecture — Structured Breakdown

A modern electric vehicle architecture comprises the following major subsystems. Each is explained in terms of its **role**, **working principle**, and **engineering rationale**.

### 2.1.1 Energy Storage System (ESS) — The Battery Pack
- **Role:** Primary reservoir of electrical energy; the "fuel tank" of the EV.
- **Common Chemistries:** **Lithium-ion (Li-ion)** dominates the market; variants include **LFP (LiFePO₄)**, **NMC (Li-Ni-Mn-Co)**, and **NCA (Li-Ni-Co-Al)**.
- **Why it matters:** Determines range, weight, charging time, and thermal behavior of the vehicle.
- **Pack Configuration:** Cells → Modules → Pack. A typical 400 V pack uses ~100s96p (100 cells in series, 96 in parallel) for a ~75 kWh capacity.

### 2.1.2 Power Electronics Converters
- **Inverter (DC → AC):** Converts battery DC into 3-phase AC for the traction motor. Uses **IGBT** or **SiC MOSFET** switches.
- **DC-DC Converter (HV → LV):** Steps down 400 V high-voltage to 12 V low-voltage to power accessories (lights, infotainment).
- **Onboard Charger (OBC) (AC → DC):** Converts grid AC to DC for slow/medium charging (Level 1 & 2).
- **Why it matters:** Without power electronics, an EV cannot run motors from a DC battery, charge from AC grid, or supply 12 V loads.

### 2.1.3 Electric Traction Motor
- **Role:** Converts electrical energy into mechanical torque at the wheels.
- **Common Types:** **PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor)**, **Induction Motor (IM)**, **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)**, and **Wound Rotor Synchronous Motor (WRSM)**.
- **Why PMSM dominates:** Highest power density and efficiency (~95–97%).

### 2.1.4 Transmission & Drivetrain
- **Single-speed reducer** is the most common in modern BEVs (e.g., Tesla, Tata Nexon EV).
- **Why single-speed?** Electric motors deliver peak torque from 0 RPM, eliminating the need for multi-gear transmissions.

### 2.1.5 Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)
- **Role:** The master controller — it receives inputs from accelerator pedal, brake pedal, SOC sensor, temperature sensors, and commands the inverter, BMS, and OBC accordingly.
- **Why it matters:** Enables torque vectoring, regenerative blending, fault diagnostics, and OTA (Over-The-Air) updates.

### 2.1.6 Battery Management System (BMS)
- **Role:** Monitors cell voltages, temperatures, currents; balances cells (cell balancing); computes SOC and SOH; protects against overcharge/over-discharge.
- **Why it matters:** Li-ion cells are unforgiving — a 50 mV cell imbalance can reduce pack capacity by 10%.

### 2.1.7 Thermal Management System (TMS)
- **Role:** Maintains battery, motor, and inverter at optimal operating temperature (typically 20–40 °C for batteries).
- **Methods:** Air cooling (cheap), liquid cooling (most modern EVs), refrigerant cooling (premium EVs).

### 2.1.8 Charging Port & Connectors
- **Standards:** **CCS2 (Combined Charging System)**, **CHAdeMO**, **Type 2 (AC)**, **Bharat DC001** (India-specific for low-power DC).
- **Why it matters:** Determines charging speed, compatibility, and user convenience.

## 2.2 Classification of EV Architectures

EV architectures are categorized by **degree of electrification** and **powertrain configuration**:

| **Architecture Type** | **Full Form** | **Energy Source** | **Engine Present?** | **Example Vehicle** |
|------------------------|---------------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| BEV | Battery Electric Vehicle | Battery only | No | Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV |
| HEV (Series) | Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Battery + ICE (generator only) | Yes, no mechanical link to wheels | Nissan e-Power (Kicks) |
| HEV (Parallel) | Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Battery + ICE (both drive wheels) | Yes, mechanically linked | Toyota Hyryder (mild hybrid) |
| HEV (Series-Parallel / Power-Split) | Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Battery + ICE + Planetary gear | Yes | Toyota Prius |
| PHEV | Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Battery (externally chargeable) + ICE | Yes | MG Hector Plus, Volvo XC90 T8 |
| FCEV | Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle | Hydrogen (fuel cell) + small battery | No (fuel cell stack) | Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations form the **core numerical toolkit** for Module 4 EV problems.

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Standard Units** |
|-------------|-------------|--------------------|
| $E_{bat} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$ | Energy stored in battery | Wh |
| $E_{bat} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \times I \times t}{1000}$ | Energy delivered over time $t$ | kWh |
| $E_{bat} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{eq} \times V_{oc}^{2}$ | Energy from equivalent capacitance | Wh |
| $R = \dfrac{E_{bat} \times \eta_{sys}}{E_{c}}$ | All-electric range | km |
| $P_{req} = F_{rr} \times v + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{3} + m a v$ | Tractive power demand at wheels | W |
| $F_{rr} = m g C_{rr}$ | Rolling resistance force | N |
| $F_{ad} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2}$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N |
| $F_{cl} = m g \sin\theta$ | Climbing force (on slope $\theta$) | N |
| $F_{acc} = m a$ | Acceleration force | N |
| $P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{req}}{\eta_{motor} \times \eta_{transmission}}$ | Required motor rated power | W |
| $SOC(\%) = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$ | State of Charge | % |
| $\text{C-rate} = \dfrac{I_{charge/discharge}}{C_{Ah}}$ | Charge / discharge current rate | dimensionless |
| $\eta_{regen} = \dfrac{E_{recovered}}{E_{kinetic}}$ | Regenerative braking efficiency | dimensionless |
| $T_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{motor}}{\omega_{motor}}$ | Motor torque at a given speed | N·m |
| $v_{max} = \dfrac{\omega_{max} \times r_{wheel}}{i_{g}}$ | Maximum vehicle speed | m/s |

Where:
- $m$ = vehicle mass (kg), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $C_{rr}$ = coefficient of rolling resistance
- $\rho$ = air density ≈ **1.225 kg/m³**, $C_{d}$ = drag coefficient, $A$ = frontal area (m²)
- $v$ = velocity (m/s), $a$ = acceleration (m/s²)
- $\eta_{sys}$ = drivetrain efficiency, $E_c$ = energy consumption per km (Wh/km)
- $\omega$ = angular speed (rad/s), $r_{wheel}$ = wheel radius (m), $i_g$ = gear ratio

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

EV architecture knowledge is **non-negotiable** in the following industry domains:
- **OEM Powertrain Design** (Tata Motors, Mahindra Electric, Ola Electric)
- **EV Charging Infrastructure Planning** (Statiq, Ather Energy, Tata Power EZ Charge)
- **Battery Pack Design & BMS Development** (Log9 Materials, Exide Industries)
- **Motor Controller Firmware** (Bosch, Continental, Tata Autocomp)
- **Vehicle Homologation & AIS-156 Compliance** (for Indian EVs)
- **Thermal Simulation & Crash Safety** of high-voltage packs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Tractive Force & Power Demand

The total resistance a vehicle must overcome to move is the sum of four opposing forces. The traction power required at the wheels is derived step-by-step below.

### Step 1 — Identify the Four Resistive Forces

**Step 1.1: Rolling Resistance Force ($F_{rr}$)**
Arises from tire deformation as the wheel rolls. Empirically proportional to vehicle weight.

$$F_{rr} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr}$$

Where $C_{rr}$ is a dimensionless coefficient (~0.012 for passenger car tires on asphalt).

**Step 1.2: Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{ad}$)**
Arises from the relative wind on the vehicle body. Increases with the *square* of velocity.

$$F_{ad} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A \cdot v^{2}$$

**Step 1.3: Climbing Force ($F_{cl}$)**
Required to lift the vehicle against gravity on a slope of angle $\theta$.

$$F_{cl} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 1.4: Acceleration Force ($F_{acc}$)**
Required by Newton's Second Law to accelerate mass $m$ at rate $a$.

$$F_{acc} = m \cdot a$$

### Step 2 — Sum the Forces

$$F_{total} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{cl} + F_{acc}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Force to Power

Power is force times velocity. Therefore, the power demand *at the wheel* is:

$$P_{req} = F_{total} \times v$$

Substituting all four components:

$$P_{req} = (F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{cl} + F_{acc}) \times v$$

Expanding the full expression:

$$P_{req} = \left[ m g C_{rr} + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2} + m g \sin\theta + m a \right] \times v$$

Final expanded form:

$$P_{req} = m g C_{rr} v + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{3} + m g v \sin\theta + m a v$$

### Step 4 — Account for Drivetrain Losses

The motor must deliver more power than the wheels demand because of losses in the motor and transmission:

$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{req}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{transmission}}$$

Typical values: $\eta_{motor} \approx 0.92$, $\eta_{transmission} \approx 0.95$.

## 3.2 Derivation: All-Electric Range from Battery Energy

### Step 1 — Define Specific Energy Consumption

Let $E_c$ be the energy consumed by the vehicle per kilometre (in Wh/km). This is measured empirically and includes auxiliary loads (HVAC, lights).

### Step 2 — Effective Usable Energy

The battery cannot be drained to 0% (cell damage). Only a fraction $DoD$ (Depth of Discharge, typically 0.8–0.9) is usable. Also, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{sys}$ reduces usable traction energy.

$$E_{usable} = E_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{sys}$$

### Step 3 — Range Equation

Dividing usable energy by per-km consumption:

$$R = \dfrac{E_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{sys}}{E_{c}}$$

### Step 4 — Illustrative Numerical Substitution

Suppose an EV has a **50 kWh** battery, **DoD = 0.9**, **$\eta_{sys} = 0.85$**, and **$E_c = 150$ Wh/km**.

$$R = \dfrac{50{,}000 \times 0.9 \times 0.85}{150}$$

$$R = \dfrac{38{,}250}{150} = 255 \text{ km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the textbook method used by KTU examiners to test EV range problems. Always show unit conversion (Wh → km) clearly in your answer script.

## 3.3 Derivation: Battery Pack Sizing for a Target Range

### Step 1 — Set the Target

Given: Target range $R_{target}$, per-km consumption $E_c$, usable fraction $(DoD \times \eta_{sys})$.

### Step 2 — Rearrange the Range Equation

$$E_{bat} = \dfrac{R_{target} \times E_{c}}{DoD \times \eta_{sys}}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Energy to Ah

$$C_{Ah} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{V_{nom}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example

Given: $R_{target} = 300$ km, $E_c = 160$ Wh/km, $DoD = 0.85$, $\eta_{sys} = 0.88$, $V_{nom} = 400$ V.

$$E_{bat} = \dfrac{300 \times 160}{0.85 \times 0.88} = \dfrac{48{,}000}{0.748} = 64{,}171 \text{ Wh} \approx 64.2 \text{ kWh}$$

$$C_{Ah} = \dfrac{64{,}171}{400} = 160.4 \text{ Ah}$$

So, a **~64 kWh, 400 V, 160 Ah** pack would be required to deliver a 300 km range under the stated conditions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Power Demand vs. Speed (Typical Compact EV)

Assume: $m = 1500$ kg, $C_{rr} = 0.013$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.2$ m², $\theta = 0$, $a = 0$.

| **Speed $v$ (km/h)** | **$v$ (m/s)** | **$F_{rr}$ (N)** | **$F_{ad}$ (N)** | **$F_{total}$ (N)** | **$P_{req}$ (kW)** |
|-----------------------|---------------|------------------|------------------|---------------------|--------------------|
| 20 | 5.56 | 191.3 | 11.2 | 202.5 | 1.13 |
| 40 | 11.11 | 191.3 | 44.9 | 236.2 | 2.62 |
| 60 | 16.67 | 191.3 | 101.0 | 292.3 | 4.87 |
| 80 | 22.22 | 191.3 | 179.6 | 370.9 | 8.24 |
| 100 | 27.78 | 191.3 | 280.7 | 472.0 | 13.11 |
| 120 | 33.33 | 191.3 | 404.1 | 595.4 | 19.85 |

**Observations:**
- At low speeds, rolling resistance dominates.
- At high speeds, aerodynamic drag dominates (cubic relation with speed).
- This is why EV designers prioritize low $C_d$ for highway range extension.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — High-Level EV Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Grid AC Supply] --> A2[Onboard Charger AC to DC]
    A2 --> A3[High Voltage DC Bus 400V]
    B1[Battery Pack Li-ion] --> A3
    A3 --> A4[Inverter DC to 3-phase AC]
    A4 --> A5[Electric Traction Motor PMSM]
    A5 --> A6[Single Speed Reducer]
    A6 --> A7[Wheels]
    A3 --> A8[DC-DC Converter 400V to 12V]
    A8 --> A9[12V Auxiliary Loads]
    C1[Vehicle Control Unit VCU] -. Controls .-> A2
    C1 -. Controls .-> A4
    C1 -. Controls .-> A8
    D1[Battery Management System BMS] -. Monitors .-> B1
    D1 -. Reports SOC SOH .-> C1
    E1[Thermal Management System] -. Cools .-> B1
    E1 -. Cools .-> A4
    E1 -. Cools .-> A5
    F1[Accelerator Pedal Sensor] -. Input .-> C1
    F2[Brake Pedal Sensor] -. Input .-> C1
    C1 -. Regen Command .-> A4
    A5 -. Regenerative Energy .-> A4
    A4 -. Recaptured DC .-> B1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted lines (`.->`) represent **signal/control flow**, while the solid lines (`-->`) represent **power flow**. This distinction is critical for KTU exam answers: examiners expect students to differentiate between "power" and "control" arrows.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparison of HEV Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SeriesHEV["Series HEV Architecture"]
        S1[ICE Engine] --> S2[Generator]
        S2 --> S3[Inverter]
        S3 --> S4[DC Bus]
        S4 --> S5[Inverter 2]
        S5 --> S6[Motor]
        S6 --> S7[Wheels]
        S4 --> S8[Battery]
    end

    subgraph ParallelHEV["Parallel HEV Architecture"]
        P1[ICE Engine] --> P2[Clutch]
        P2 --> P3[Transmission]
        P3 --> P4[Wheels]
        P5[Motor] --> P3
        P6[Battery] --> P5
    end

    subgraph SeriesParallelHEV["Series-Parallel Power-Split HEV"]
        SP1[ICE Engine] --> SP2[Planetary Gear Set]
        SP2 --> SP3[Generator]
        SP2 --> SP4[Motor]
        SP2 --> SP5[Wheels]
        SP3 --> SP6[Battery]
        SP4 --> SP6
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Skateboard Platform Architecture (Modern BEV)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Skateboard["Skateboard Chassis Platform"]:::highlight
    Skateboard --> BP["Battery Pack Floor Mounted"]
    Skateboard --> FM["Front Motor Unit"]
    Skateboard --> RM["Rear Motor Unit"]
    Skateboard --> SUSP["Suspension Subframes"]
    Skateboard --> TMS["Integrated Thermal Manifold"]
    BP --- CellMod1["Module 1"]
    BP --- CellMod2["Module 2"]
    BP --- CellModN["Module N"]
    classDef highlight fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **skateboard architecture** is the modern industry standard (used by Tesla Model Y, Hyundai E-GMP, BYD e-Platform 3.0). It places the battery *between the axles*, lowering the center of gravity and freeing up cabin space. KTU examiners frequently test this concept.

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Choosing an EV Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need Zero Tailpipe Emissions?} -->|Yes| Q2{Range > 300 km required?}
    Q1 -->|No, but want fuel savings| HEVChoice
    Q2 -->|Yes, with charging access| BEVChoice[BEV Architecture]
    Q2 -->|No, short urban trips| PHEVChoice[PHEV Architecture]
    Q1 --> HEVChoice{Drivetrain layout?}
    HEVChoice -->|Generator feeds motor only| SeriesHEVChoice[Series HEV]
    HEVChoice -->|Both ICE and motor drive wheels| ParallelHEVChoice[Parallel HEV]
    HEVChoice -->|Best of both worlds| PowerSplitChoice[Series-Parallel Power-Split]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Electric Vehicle (EV) architecture. List any four major subsystems of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV).**

**Model Answer:**
EV architecture refers to the systematic layout and interconnection of the major subsystems in an electric vehicle that enable energy storage, conversion, and delivery to the wheels. It encompasses the topology of the battery, motor, power electronics, transmission, and control systems.

The four major subsystems of a BEV are:
1. **Energy Storage System (Battery Pack)** — stores electrical energy.
2. **Power Electronics Converters** — inverter (DC→AC), DC-DC converter (HV→LV), onboard charger (AC→DC).
3. **Electric Traction Motor** — converts electrical energy to mechanical torque.
4. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** — orchestrates the entire powertrain.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any four subsystems with brief description = 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between BEV, HEV, and PHEV architectures. State one example vehicle for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **BEV** | **HEV** | **PHEV** |
|---------------|---------|---------|----------|
| Energy Source | Battery only | Battery + ICE (no external charging) | Battery + ICE (externally chargeable) |
| Engine Role | None | Drives wheels / generates | Drives wheels / generates |
| Battery Size | Large (30–100 kWh) | Small (1–2 kWh) | Medium (10–20 kWh) |
| External Charging | Yes | No | Yes |
| Example | Tata Nexon EV | Toyota Camry Hybrid | Volvo XC90 T8 |

*(2 marks for table + 1 mark for examples = 3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks in Module 4 because they (a) confuse *power flow* with *signal/control flow* in diagrams, (b) forget to apply drivetrain efficiency when calculating motor power, and (c) omit the unit conversion from Wh to kWh in range problems. Always show units step-by-step in your derivations.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV). Describe the function of each major component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BEV has the following specifications:
- Vehicle mass $m = 1400$ kg
- Coefficient of rolling resistance $C_{rr} = 0.012$
- Aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d = 0.29$
- Frontal area $A = 2.1$ m²
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Target speed $v = 90$ km/h
- Gradient = 0 (flat road)
- Acceleration $a = 0$ (steady speed)
- Motor efficiency $\eta_{motor} = 0.92$, transmission efficiency $\eta_{transmission} = 0.95$

Calculate the **total tractive power required at the motor** to maintain the vehicle at 90 km/h. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

A BEV architecture consists of the following blocks (draw neatly, label all arrows):

1. **Battery Pack** — Li-ion, typically 400 V architecture; supplies DC energy.
2. **Inverter** — Converts DC from battery to 3-phase AC for the motor.
3. **Electric Motor** — PMSM or Induction type; provides traction torque.
4. **Single-Speed Reducer** — Steps down motor RPM to wheel RPM.
5. **DC-DC Converter** — Steps 400 V down to 12 V for auxiliary loads.
6. **Onboard Charger (OBC)** — Charges battery from AC grid.
7. **BMS** — Monitors battery health, SOC, cell balancing.
8. **VCU** — Master controller, reads driver inputs, commands motor/inverter.
9. **Thermal Management** — Liquid cooling loop for battery, motor, inverter.

*(4 marks for neat labeled diagram + 3 marks for functional description = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Convert speed to SI units**
$$v = 90 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{90}{3.6} = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Rolling Resistance Force**
$$F_{rr} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} = 1400 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 164.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Aerodynamic Drag Force**
$$F_{ad} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^{2}$$
$$F_{ad} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.29 \times 2.1 \times (25)^{2}$$
$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.29 \times 2.1 \times 625$$
$$F_{ad} = 233.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Total Tractive Force (flat road, no acceleration)**
$$F_{total} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} = 164.8 + 233.1 = 397.9 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Power at the Wheels**
$$P_{wheels} = F_{total} \times v = 397.9 \times 25 = 9947.5 \text{ W} \approx 9.95 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6: Power at the Motor (after drivetrain losses)**
$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{\eta_{motor} \times \eta_{transmission}}$$
$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{9947.5}{0.92 \times 0.95} = \dfrac{9947.5}{0.874} = 11{,}381.6 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{motor} \approx 11.38 \text{ kW}}$$

*(1 mark each for Steps 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, plus 2 marks for final answer with units = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]
**(a)** Explain the **Series HEV**, **Parallel HEV**, and **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) HEV** architectures with neat diagrams. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electric vehicle has a **battery pack rated at 60 kWh**. The vehicle consumes **170 Wh/km** under standard driving conditions. The depth of discharge is **85%** and overall drivetrain efficiency is **88%**.
Calculate:
(i) The all-electric range of the vehicle. **(3 marks)**
(ii) The required battery capacity in Ah if the nominal pack voltage is **400 V**. **(4 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Series HEV:**
- The ICE does *not* mechanically drive the wheels. It drives a generator to produce electricity.
- The electricity either charges the battery or directly powers the motor that drives the wheels.
- *Advantage:* Simple transmission, ICE can run at constant optimal speed.
- *Disadvantage:* Three energy conversions (mechanical → electrical → mechanical) cause higher losses.
- *Example:* Nissan e-Power.

**Parallel HEV:**
- Both the ICE and the electric motor are mechanically coupled to the wheels (via a clutch/transmission).
- They can drive the wheels *simultaneously or independently*.
- *Advantage:* Higher efficiency at highway speeds (no double energy conversion).
- *Disadvantage:* Complex control, ICE and motor speeds must be synchronized.
- *Example:* Honda Civic Hybrid (older generation).

**Series-Parallel (Power-Split) HEV:**
- Uses a **planetary gear set** to split power between ICE, generator, motor, and wheels.
- Can operate in *series mode, parallel mode, or power-split mode* depending on driving conditions.
- *Advantage:* Best efficiency across all speeds; smooth transition.
- *Disadvantage:* Most complex and expensive; planetary gear requires precise control.
- *Example:* Toyota Prius (signature architecture).

*(3 marks for three neat diagrams + 4 marks for comparison and examples = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Identify Given Data**
- $E_{bat} = 60$ kWh = $60{,}000$ Wh
- $E_c = 170$ Wh/km
- $DoD = 0.85$
- $\eta_{sys} = 0.88$
- $V_{nom} = 400$ V

**Part (i): All-Electric Range**

**Step 2: Apply the Range Formula**
$$R = \dfrac{E_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{sys}}{E_{c}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**
$$R = \dfrac{60{,}000 \times 0.85 \times 0.88}{170}$$

**Step 4: Simplify Numerator**
$$60{,}000 \times 0.85 = 51{,}000$$
$$51{,}000 \times 0.88 = 44{,}880 \text{ Wh}$$

**Step 5: Final Division**
$$R = \dfrac{44{,}880}{170} = 264 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{R = 264 \text{ km}}$$

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final answer with units = 3 marks)*

**Part (ii): Battery Capacity in Ah**

**Step 6: Apply the Energy-to-Charge Formula**
$$C_{Ah} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{V_{nom}}$$

**Step 7: Substitute Values**
$$C_{Ah} = \dfrac{60{,}000 \text{ Wh}}{400 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 8: Final Result**
$$C_{Ah} = 150 \text{ Ah}$$

$$\boxed{C_{Ah} = 150 \text{ Ah}}$$

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for unit clarity, 1 mark for final answer = 4 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — EV Architecture**

- **EV Architecture** = systematic layout of battery, motor, power electronics, BMS, VCU, TMS, and transmission.
- **Major BEV subsystems:** Battery Pack, Inverter, Motor, DC-DC Converter, OBC, BMS, VCU, Thermal Management, Charging Port.
- **Classification by electrification:** BEV, HEV (Series/Parallel/Series-Parallel), PHEV, FCEV.
- **Motor types:** PMSM (most popular), IM, SRM, WRSM.
- **Battery chemistries:** LFP (safe, long life), NMC (high energy), NCA (high power).
- **Power Electronics:** Inverter (DC→AC), DC-DC (HV→LV), OBC (AC→DC).
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $E_{bat} = V \times Ah$
  - $R = \dfrac{E_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{sys}}{E_{c}}$
  - $P_{req} = m g C_{rr} v + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{3} + m g v \sin\theta + m a v$
  - $SOC(\%) = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$
- **Aerodynamic drag dominates at high speed** ($v^3$ dependence) — this is the most asked conceptual point in KTU exams.
- **Skateboard platform** is the modern BEV industry standard (low CG, modular).
- **Always include drivetrain efficiency** when calculating motor power — common 1–2 mark loss.
- **Unit conversions:** km/h → m/s (divide by 3.6); Wh → kWh (divide by 1000).
- **Series HEV:** ICE → Generator → Battery/Motor → Wheels. No mechanical link from ICE to wheels.
- **Parallel HEV:** ICE and Motor both mechanically drive wheels via clutch/transmission.
- **Power-Split HEV:** Uses planetary gear for optimal blending — Toyota Prius signature.
- **BMS** does cell balancing, SOC/SOH estimation, and thermal protection.
- **Cooling methods:** Air (cheap), Liquid (standard in modern EVs), Refrigerant (premium).
- **Standard charging connectors in India:** CCS2, Type 2 (AC), Bharat DC001.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Battery (KTU 2024 Definition):** An automotive battery is a **rechargeable electrochemical energy storage device** that converts stored chemical energy into electrical energy through reversible redox (oxidation-reduction) reactions, providing the necessary cranking current for engine starting, stabilizing voltage in the electrical system, and supplying auxiliary power to accessories when the alternator is inactive.

In the context of **KTU PEMRT413 - Automobile Engineering (Module 4)**, the topic of *"Types of Batteries"* focuses on classifying automotive energy storage systems based on their **electrochemistry, construction, application (SLI vs. EV traction), and maintenance requirements**.

> [!NOTE]
> **SLI Battery** stands for **Starting, Lighting, and Ignition** — the three primary loads a battery handles in a conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle. The cranking current typically demanded is **200 A to 600 A** for 3–10 seconds at **−18 °C** (per BIS/SAE J537 standard).

## 1.2 Classification Overview of Automotive Batteries

Automotive batteries are broadly classified into two functional families:

| Family | Primary Use | Energy Range | Example Chemistry |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SLI Batteries** | Engine cranking + auxiliaries | 30–100 Ah | Lead-Acid (Flooded, VRLA, AGM) |
| **Traction Batteries** | EV/Hybrid propulsion | 10–100+ kWh | Li-ion, NiMH, Solid-State, Na-S |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Energy Piggy Bank"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Think of a battery as an *energy piggy bank* with two coins slots.
> - **The Charging Slot (Discharge reaction):** The bank returns coins (electrons) when you need to buy something (start the engine, light the headlamp).
> - **The Recharging Slot (Charge reaction):** The alternator (or EV charger) pushes the coins back into the bank.
> - **The Electrolyte** is like the *security guard* who escorts the coins through the lobby — it allows ionic transport but does not let electrons sneak through the wrong door (which would short the bank).

A **Lead-Acid cell** uses lead plates and sulfuric acid — a heavy, cheap, dependable "ceramic piggy bank" that is *tough and forgiving* but heavy. A **Lithium-Ion cell** uses a graphite anode, lithium metal-oxide cathode, and a salt-based electrolyte — a *lightweight high-tech piggy bank* that holds much more energy per kg, but is more sensitive to overcharging and temperature.

## 1.4 Historical & Syllabus Highlight

> [!NOTE]
> **Gaston Planté** invented the Lead-Acid battery in **1859** — the oldest rechargeable battery still in commercial automotive use. **Sony** commercialized the Lithium-Ion battery in **1991**, revolutionizing EV design. For the KTU 2024 Automobile Engineering syllabus, the focus is on **construction, working principle, advantages, limitations, and applications** of each battery type.

---

## 1.5 SI Units & Standard Metrics (Bold Constants)

The following physical/electrical constants and standard metrics are used throughout this module:

- **Standard electromotive force of a lead-acid cell:** $\mathbf{2.1 \text{ V per cell}}$ (6 cells in series = **12.6 V** nominal)
- **Nominal voltage of a Li-ion cell:** $\mathbf{3.6 - 3.7 \text{ V}}$ (nominal)
- **Standard temperature for capacity rating:** $\mathbf{27\,°C}$ (IEC 60095) — capacity derates with cold.
- **Specific energy of Lead-Acid:** $\mathbf{30\text{–}40 \text{ Wh/kg}}$
- **Specific energy of Li-ion (NMC):** $\mathbf{150\text{–}220 \text{ Wh/kg}}$
- **Faraday's Constant:** $\mathbf{F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}}$
- **Cranking Amps (CA) / Cold Cranking Amps (CCA):** Measured at $\mathbf{-18\,°C}$ per SAE J537.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle — The Galvanic Cell

Every battery is a **galvanic (voltaic) cell** consisting of:

1. **Anode (Negative electrode):** Site of **oxidation** — loses electrons during discharge.
2. **Cathode (Positive electrode):** Site of **reduction** — gains electrons during discharge.
3. **Electrolyte:** Ionic conductor that completes the internal circuit.
4. **Separator:** Prevents physical contact of electrodes while permitting ion flow.
5. **Current Collector:** Conducts electrons out of the cell to the external circuit.

The **Electromotive Force (EMF)** is governed by the **Nernst Equation**:

$$E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

Where:
- $E^{\circ}_{cell}$ = Standard cell potential (V)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred per mole
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $Q$ = Reaction quotient

## 2.2 Lead-Acid Battery — Deep Working Principle

### 2.2.1 Construction

- **Anode:** Spongy/porous **Lead (Pb)** plate.
- **Cathode:** **Lead Dioxide (PbO₂)** plate.
- **Electrolyte:** **Sulfuric Acid (H₂SO₄)** solution (~37% by weight, density **1.28 g/cm³** at full charge).
- **Separator:** Microporous polyethylene or PVC to prevent shorting.
- **Container:** Polypropylene (PP) with cell partitions.
- **Vent Caps:** Allow gas escape (sealed in VRLA designs).
- **Plates:** Can be **flat pasted**, **tubular**, or **spiral-wound** in AGM designs.

### 2.2.2 Electrochemistry — Discharge Reaction

At the **Anode (Oxidation)**:

$$Pb + SO_4^{2-} \rightarrow PbSO_4 + 2e^{-}$$

At the **Cathode (Reduction)**:

$$PbO_2 + 4H^{+} + SO_4^{2-} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow PbSO_4 + 2H_2O$$

### 2.2.3 Overall Cell Reaction (Discharge)

$$Pb + PbO_2 + 2H_2SO_4 \rightarrow 2PbSO_4 + 2H_2O$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "double-sulfation" principle:** Both electrodes convert to **Lead Sulfate (PbSO₄)**, and **sulfuric acid is consumed (replaced by water)** during discharge. This is the diagnostic signature — a discharged battery has *dilute electrolyte* (low specific gravity) and *sulfated plates*.

### 2.2.4 Charging Reaction (Reversal)

The reactions reverse when external DC is applied (alternator in running vehicle):

$$2PbSO_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow Pb + PbO_2 + 2H_2SO_4$$

## 2.3 Lithium-Ion Battery — Deep Working Principle

### 2.3.1 Construction (for EV traction)

- **Anode:** Graphite (C) on copper current collector; increasingly **Silicon-graphite** composites.
- **Cathode:** Lithium metal oxide — variants include **NMC** (Ni-Mn-Co), **NCA** (Ni-Co-Al), **LFP** (LiFePO₄), **LMO** (Li-Mn-O).
- **Electrolyte:** LiPF₆ salt in organic carbonate solvents (EC, DMC, EMC).
- **Separator:** Microporous polyethylene/polypropylene trilayer with **thermal shutdown** above 130 °C.
- **Form factor:** Cylindrical (18650, 21700), prismatic, or **pouch** cells.

### 2.3.2 Electrochemistry (Discharge)

**Anode (Oxidation):**

$$Li_xC_6 \rightarrow xLi^+ + xe^- + 6C$$

**Cathode (Reduction)** (for NMC cathode $Li_{1-x}Co_{1/3}Ni_{1/3}Mn_{1/3}O_2$):

$$Li_{1-x}MO_2 + xLi^+ + xe^- \rightarrow LiMO_2$$

**Overall:**

$$Li_xC_6 + Li_{1-x}MO_2 \rightarrow 6C + LiMO_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "rocking-chair" battery?** During charge/discharge, **Li⁺ ions shuttle (rock)** between the anode and cathode through the electrolyte — no metallic lithium is plated, which improves safety.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Cheat Sheet — Memorize these formulas for the 14-mark derivations:**

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | Nernst Equation | V | Cell EMF under load |
| 2 | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Charge transferred | C (A·s) | Capacity (Ah) = (A)(h) |
| 3 | $\text{Energy} = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Stored energy | Wh | Battery energy content |
| 4 | $\text{Specific Energy} = \frac{E}{m}$ | Energy per unit mass | Wh/kg | Compare chemistries |
| 5 | $\text{Specific Power} = \frac{P}{m}$ | Power per unit mass | W/kg | Acceleration performance |
| 6 | $\text{Energy Density} = \frac{E}{V}$ | Energy per unit volume | Wh/L | Packaging efficiency |
| 7 | $C\text{-rate} = \frac{I}{C_{nom}}$ | Discharge current ratio | 1/h | Battery loading |
| 8 | $\text{Peukert: } I^{k} \cdot t = const$ | Capacity loss at high rate | — | Lead-acid capacity fade |
| 9 | $\eta_{coulombic} = \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ | Charge efficiency | % | Round-trip efficiency |
| 10 | $V_{cell}^{Pb} = 2.1$ V (nominal) | Lead-acid cell voltage | V | 6 cells $\rightarrow$ 12.6 V |
| 11 | $V_{cell}^{Li} = 3.6 - 3.7$ V | Li-ion cell voltage | V | 100 cells $\rightarrow$ 360 V pack |
| 12 | $R_{internal} = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I}$ | Internal resistance | $\Omega$ | Cranking capability |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** When writing **Nernst's equation** in the exam, students often forget to convert temperature to **Kelvin** ($T_K = T_{°C} + 273.15$) and confuse the **log base** (use $\ln$ — natural log — as per the standard form). The 2.303 conversion factor is for $\log_{10}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vehicle Electrical System:** The 12 V SLI battery smooths alternator ripple, supports idle loads, and provides energy for **stop-start systems** (micro-hybrid).
- **EV Powertrain:** A 400 V or 800 V traction battery pack drives the inverter-motor assembly and supports **regenerative braking** energy recovery.
- **48 V Mild-Hybrid (MHEV):** A **48 V Li-ion** battery replaces the 12 V starter with a **BSG** (Belt-Started Generator), enabling torque-assist and coasting.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Lead-acid has a **round-trip efficiency of 70–85%**; Li-ion achieves **90–95%** — critical for EV range.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: EMF of a Lead-Acid Cell Using the Nernst Equation

We will derive the **open-circuit voltage** of the lead-acid cell from first principles using standard reduction potentials.

### Step 1: Identify the half-reactions and their standard potentials

The standard reduction potentials (at 25 °C = 298.15 K) are:

- Cathode (PbO₂/PbSO₄): $E^{\circ}_{cathode} = +1.685$ V
- Anode (PbSO₄/Pb): $E^{\circ}_{anode} = -0.356$ V

### Step 2: Calculate the standard cell potential

$$E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode}$$

$$E^{\circ}_{cell} = (+1.685) - (-0.356) = 2.041 \text{ V}$$

This matches the experimental value of ~**2.1 V per cell** under standard conditions.

### Step 3: Apply the Nernst Equation for the overall reaction

The overall discharge reaction is:

$$Pb + PbO_2 + 2H_2SO_4 \rightarrow 2PbSO_4 + 2H_2O$$

The number of electrons transferred is $n = 2$ (each Pb atom loses 2 electrons).

The reaction quotient is:

$$Q = \frac{[PbSO_4]^2 \cdot [H_2O]^2}{[Pb] \cdot [PbO_2] \cdot [H_2SO_4]^2}$$

Since solids have unit activity:

$$Q = \frac{[H_2O]^2}{[H_2SO_4]^2}$$

### Step 4: Substitute into Nernst Equation

$$E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

$$E_{cell} = 2.041 - \frac{(8.314)(298.15)}{(2)(96485)} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{[H_2O]^2}{[H_2SO_4]^2}\right)$$

### Step 5: Simplify the logarithm

Using the property $\ln(a^2/b^2) = 2 \ln(a/b)$:

$$E_{cell} = 2.041 - \frac{(8.314)(298.15)}{(2)(96485)} \cdot 2 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{[H_2O]}{[H_2SO_4]}\right)$$

The prefactor simplifies to:

$$\frac{2 \cdot RT}{nF} = \frac{2 \cdot (8.314)(298.15)}{2 \cdot 96485} = 0.02569 \text{ V}$$

### Step 6: Final Compact Form

$$E_{cell} = 2.041 + 0.02569 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{[H_2SO_4]}{[H_2O]}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As the battery discharges, $H_2SO_4$ is consumed and $H_2O$ is produced — the ratio $[H_2SO_4]/[H_2O]$ **decreases**, which **reduces** $E_{cell}$. This explains why the open-circuit voltage of a discharged cell is **~1.8 V** instead of 2.1 V.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Stored in a Battery Pack

### Step 1: Define charge capacity

A battery rated at $C$ ampere-hours can deliver a current $I$ for time $t$:

$$Q = I \cdot t \quad \text{[Coulombs]}$$

Converting Ah to Coulombs:

$$Q \, [C] = C_{Ah} \times 3600$$

### Step 2: Define nominal energy

The stored energy is:

$$E = V_{nom} \cdot Q = V_{nom} \cdot I \cdot t$$

### Step 3: Compute energy in Wh and Joules

$$E \, [\text{Wh}] = V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah}$$

$$E \, [\text{J}] = V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah} \cdot 3600$$

### Numerical Example (KTU-style)

A **Tata Nexon EV** has a 30.2 kWh Li-ion pack with nominal voltage ~350 V. Find the **pack capacity in Ah** and the **specific energy** if the pack mass is ~**300 kg**.

**Solution:**

$$C_{Ah} = \frac{E}{V_{nom}} = \frac{30200 \text{ Wh}}{350 \text{ V}} = 86.3 \text{ Ah}$$

$$\text{Specific Energy} = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{30200}{300} = 100.7 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Note on real values:** Modern EV packs achieve **150–200 Wh/kg at the cell level** but only **100–160 Wh/kg at the pack level** due to housing, BMS, and cooling overheads.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cranking Performance and Internal Resistance

The **terminal voltage** during cranking is:

$$V_{terminal} = V_{OCV} - I \cdot R_{int}$$

Solving for cranking current:

$$I_{crank} = \frac{V_{OCV} - V_{min}}{R_{int}}$$

**Example:** A 12 V battery with $V_{OCV} = 12.6$ V, $R_{int} = 0.01 \, \Omega$, $V_{min} = 8$ V (minimum starter voltage):

$$I_{crank} = \frac{12.6 - 8}{0.01} = 460 \text{ A}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** Some students use $V_{OCV} = 12$ V (rated) instead of the actual open-circuit voltage (12.6 V fully charged). The distinction matters because the rated voltage is the **nominal** value, not the open-circuit value.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Battery Types

| Property | Lead-Acid (Flooded) | Lead-Acid (VRLA/AGM) | NiMH | Li-ion (NMC) | Li-ion (LFP) | Solid-State (Emerging) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nominal Cell Voltage (V)** | 2.1 | 2.1 | 1.2 | 3.6–3.7 | 3.2 | 3.6–3.7 |
| **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** | 30–40 | 35–45 | 60–80 | 150–220 | 90–160 | 250–400 (lab) |
| **Energy Density (Wh/L)** | 60–100 | 80–110 | 140–200 | 250–350 | 200–300 | 350–500 |
| **Cycle Life** | 300–500 | 500–800 | 500–1000 | 1000–3000 | 2000–5000+ | 1000–5000 (target) |
| **Self-Discharge/Month** | 4–6% | 2–3% | 20–30% | 1–3% | 1–3% | <1% |
| **Operating Temp (°C)** | −20 to 60 | −30 to 60 | −20 to 45 | −20 to 60 | −20 to 60 | −20 to 80 |
| **Cost ($/kWh)** | 100–150 | 150–200 | 250–350 | 120–180 | 100–150 | 500+ (R&D) |
| **Toxicity / Recyclability** | High (Pb) — recyclable | High (Pb) | Medium (Ni) | Low | Low | Low |
| **Primary Automotive Use** | SLI | Start-Stop, premium SLI | Hybrid (older Prius) | EV traction, 48V MHEV | EV traction (BYD) | Future EV traction |
| **Thermal Runaway Risk** | Low | Low | Low | High | Medium | Very Low (target) |

## 3.5 Detailed Comparison of Other Battery Types

### 3.5.1 Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH)

- **Anode:** Metal hydride (MH); **Cathode:** Nickel hydroxide NiO(OH); **Electrolyte:** KOH.
- Used in **Toyota Prius (1st–3rd gen)** — robust, tolerant to abuse.
- **Disadvantage:** High self-discharge ("memory effect" is mild), heavy for energy stored.

### 3.5.2 Solid-State Battery

- Replaces liquid electrolyte with **solid ceramic/sulfide electrolyte** (e.g., Li₃PS₄, LLZO garnet).
- Enables **lithium metal anode** — energy density boost.
- **Challenges:** Interfacial resistance, dendrite formation, manufacturing scale-up.

### 3.5.3 Sodium-Sulfur (NaS) Battery

- Operates at **300–350 °C** with molten electrodes and β-alumina solid electrolyte.
- Used in **stationary grid storage** (not automotive) — not in cars.

### 3.5.4 Ultracapacitors (Supplementary)

- Not a battery, but **EDLC** (Electrochemical Double-Layer Capacitor) used in **regenerative braking** of heavy vehicles and **start-stop** to supplement batteries.
