# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** In the End Semester Examination (ESE), Module 4 typically carries **15–20% weightage** (12–15 marks out of 70). Expect a **3-mark short question** in Part A and a **14-mark long answer** in Part B with internal choice.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**"Define the term 'Cold Cranking Amps' (CCA) of an automotive battery. State its typical value for a modern passenger car."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Cold Cranking Amps (CCA)** is defined as the maximum current (in amperes) a fully charged battery can deliver for **30 seconds** at a temperature of **−18 °C** while maintaining a terminal voltage of at least **7.2 V** (for a 12 V battery), as per **SAE J537** standard. **(2 marks)**
>
> For a modern petrol passenger car, the typical CCA rating is **300 A to 600 A**; for diesel cars and commercial vehicles, **600 A to 1000 A** is common. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**"Distinguish between a flooded lead-acid battery and a VRLA (Valve Regulated Lead-Acid) battery in two points."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Flooded Lead-Acid | VRLA Battery |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electrolyte is **liquid**, free to move; **vent caps** are open. | Electrolyte is **absorbed in glass mat (AGM)** or immobilized as **gel**; **sealed** with pressure relief valve. |
| 2 | Requires **regular topping-up** of distilled water. | **Maintenance-free**; recombination cycle keeps water content stable. |
| 3 | Lower cost, higher gassing, position-sensitive. | Higher cost, lower gassing, spill-proof, can be mounted in any orientation. |

*(Any 2 distinctions for full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B Module 4 question will have an internal choice — answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working principle of a Lead-Acid battery** used in automobiles. State the chemical reactions during charging and discharging. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **types of Lead-Acid batteries** based on construction (flooded, VRLA-AGM, VRLA-Gel) and state **two advantages and two disadvantages** of each. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working

**Construction (5 points, 4 marks):**

1. **Container:** Polypropylene (PP) box divided into 6 cells by partitions; each cell produces ~2.1 V.
2. **Positive Plates:** Lead dioxide (PbO₂) pressed onto a lead-antimony grid.
3. **Negative Plates:** Sponge lead (Pb) on a similar grid; more negative plates than positive for uniform current distribution.
4. **Electrolyte:** Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) of specific gravity **1.28 g/cm³** at full charge.
5. **Separators:** Microporous PVC or polyethylene sheets between plates to prevent shorting while permitting ionic flow.
6. **Vent Caps:** Allow escape of gases (H₂ and O₂) generated during overcharge.
7. **Terminal Posts:** Lead-alloy posts connected to the plate groups.

**Working — Discharge Reaction (1 mark):**

$$Pb + PbO_2 + 2H_2SO_4 \rightarrow 2PbSO_4 + 2H_2O$$

**Working — Charge Reaction (1 mark):**

$$2PbSO_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow Pb + PbO_2 + 2H_2SO_4$$

**Sketch** (1 mark): Mermaid block diagram already provided in Section 4.3.

#### Part (b) — Types of Lead-Acid Batteries

**Flooded Lead-Acid (Wet Cell) — Advantages:** (1 mark)
- Lowest cost, robust, tolerant of overcharge, easy to recycle.

**Flooded — Disadvantages:** (0.5 mark)
- Requires water topping up, acid spillage risk, position-sensitive.

**VRLA-AGM (Absorbed Glass Mat) — Advantages:** (1 mark)
- Maintenance-free, spill-proof, low self-discharge, high CCA output, supports stop-start systems.

**VRLA-AGM — Disadvantages:** (0.5 mark)
- Sensitive to overcharge, higher cost, heat dissipation is limited.

**VRLA-Gel — Advantages:** (1 mark)
- Excellent deep-cycle capability, vibration-resistant, ideal for off-road and military vehicles.

**VRLA-Gel — Disadvantages:** (0.5 mark)
- Lower peak current than AGM, more expensive, sensitive to fast charging.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** AGM is the modern standard for start-stop ICE vehicles; Gel is preferred for deep-cycle and leisure applications (RVs, marine).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Describe the **construction and working principle of a Lithium-Ion (Li-ion) battery** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Lead-Acid, NiMH, and Li-ion batteries** in terms of specific energy, cycle life, cost, and typical automotive application. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Li-ion Construction and Working

**Construction (4 marks):**

1. **Anode:** Graphite (C) coated on copper current collector; emerging designs use silicon-blended graphite.
2. **Cathode:** Lithium metal oxide (e.g., NMC: LiNi₁/₃Mn₁/₃Co₁/₃O₂) coated on aluminum current collector.
3. **Electrolyte:** Lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF₆) dissolved in organic carbonates (ethylene carbonate, dimethyl carbonate).
4. **Separator:** Microporous polyethylene (PE) / polypropylene (PP), often with ceramic coating for thermal shutdown above 130 °C.
5. **Form Factors:** Cylindrical (18650, 21700), prismatic, or pouch cells.
6. **Housing & BMS:** Metal/plastic case with integrated Battery Management System for cell balancing, temperature monitoring, and protection.

**Working (3 marks):**

- **Discharge:** Li⁺ ions deintercalate from the anode (graphite), travel through the electrolyte and separator, and intercalate into the cathode (NMC). Electrons flow externally from anode to cathode, providing current to the load.
- **Charge:** External DC source drives Li⁺ ions back from cathode to anode; electrons flow externally from cathode to anode.

**Equations:**

**Anode (during discharge):**

$$Li_xC_6 \rightarrow xLi^+ + xe^- + 6C$$

**Cathode (during discharge):**

$$Li_{1-x}MO_2 + xLi^+ + xe^- \rightarrow LiMO_2$$

> The "rocking-chair" mechanism — no metallic Li deposition — enhances safety.

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | Lead-Acid | NiMH | Li-ion (NMC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** | 30–40 | 60–80 | 150–220 |
| **Cycle Life** | 300–500 | 500–1000 | 1000–3000 |
| **Cost ($/kWh)** | 100–150 | 250–350 | 120–180 |
| **Nominal Cell Voltage (V)** | 2.1 | 1.2 | 3.6–3.7 |
| **Self-Discharge/Month** | 4–6% | 20–30% | 1–3% |
| **Memory Effect** | None | Mild | None |
| **Thermal Runaway Risk** | Low | Low | High (requires BMS) |
| **Recyclability** | Excellent (Pb回收率 >95%) | Moderate | Developing (15–25% Li回收) |
| **Typical Automotive Use** | SLI in ICE cars; start-stop | Toyota Prius (older gen), HEV | Tesla, BYD, modern EVs, 48V MHEV |
| **Maintenance** | Periodic (flooded) or none (VRLA) | Low | None (BMS-controlled) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Lead-acid remains dominant for cost-sensitive SLI applications; NiMH is being phased out in favour of Li-ion; Li-ion is the de-facto choice for modern EVs due to its superior energy density and cycle life.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the stoichiometric coefficients** in the lead-acid discharge equation — many write $Pb + PbO_2 \rightarrow 2PbSO_4$ without the $2H_2SO_4$ and $2H_2O$ — **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Conflating CCA with CA or HCA** — CCA is at −18 °C for 30 s; CA is at 0 °C; HCA is at 27 °C. Examiners specifically look for the **−18 °C** mention. **Lose 1 mark**.
> 3. **Not mentioning "rocking-chair" mechanism** for Li-ion — the *rocking* analogy is the examiner's favourite marker. **Lose 1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Writing "memory effect" for Li-ion** — Li-ion has *no* memory effect; this statement is wrong and attracts negative marking.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical problems** — capacity must be in **Ah**, energy in **Wh**, specific energy in **Wh/kg**. Unit marks are **1 mark** by themselves.
> 6. **Not drawing a neat, labelled diagram** — KTU examiners allocate **1 mark** strictly for a labelled diagram in the 7-mark sub-questions.
> 7. **Writing SLI as "Single Load Ignition"** — the correct expansion is **Starting, Lighting, Ignition**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ **Automotive batteries** are classified into **SLI** (12 V, 30–100 Ah) and **Traction** (400 V/800 V, 50–100+ kWh).
- ✅ **Lead-acid** is the most common SLI battery: **6 cells × 2.1 V = 12.6 V** nominal.
- ✅ **Discharge reaction:** $Pb + PbO_2 + 2H_2SO_4 \rightarrow 2PbSO_4 + 2H_2O$
- ✅ **Charge reaction:** $2PbSO_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow Pb + PbO_2 + 2H_2SO_4$
- ✅ **Specific gravity** of electrolyte falls from **1.28 → 1.12 g/cm³** during full discharge (key SOC indicator).
- ✅ **CCA** is the most important SLI performance metric — current at **−18 °C for 30 s to 7.2 V** (SAE J537).
- ✅ **VRLA types:** **AGM** (absorbed glass mat, high power) and **Gel** (immobilized silica, deep cycle).
- ✅ **Li-ion working principle:** **Li⁺ ions rock** between graphite anode and metal-oxide cathode through the electrolyte.
- ✅ **Li-ion nominal cell voltage:** **3.6–3.7 V**; **NMC, LFP, NCA** are common cathode chemistries.
- ✅ **Energy formula:** $E \, [Wh] = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$
- ✅ **Specific energy order (low → high):** Lead-Acid < NiMH < LFP < NMC < Solid-State.
- ✅ **Round-trip efficiency:** Lead-Acid ~80%, Li-ion ~92–95%.
- ✅ **Nernst equation:** $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ — use **T in Kelvin**, **n = 2 for lead-acid**.
- ✅ **Cycle life order:** Lead-Acid < NiMH < NMC < LFP.
- ✅ **Cost order (lowest → highest):** Lead-Acid < LFP ≈ NMC < NiMH < Solid-State.
- ✅ **Future trends:** **800 V architecture, LFP dominance, solid-state R&D, sodium-ion (Na⁺) for entry-level EVs.**
- ✅ **Toxicity:** Lead-acid batteries are 95%+ recyclable — but lead is highly toxic; dispose responsibly.
- ✅ **BMS (Battery Management System)** is mandatory for all Li-ion automotive packs — it monitors voltage, current, temperature, and SOC/SOH.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Memory Mnemonic:** **"Lead-Acid is HEAVY but HONEST; Li-ion is LIGHT but LEERY."**
> — i.e., Lead-acid is heavy but reliable and forgiving; Li-ion is light and energy-dense but requires careful thermal and voltage management.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT413 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Battery Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of an Automotive Battery
An **automotive battery** is a rechargeable electrochemical energy storage device that converts stored chemical energy into electrical energy through reversible redox reactions. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, it is classified as a **SLI (Starting, Lighting, and Ignition) battery** that supplies the high cranking current required by the starter motor while also acting as a voltage stabilizer for the vehicle's DC electrical system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Battery parameters include — Cell voltage (EMF), Ampere-hour capacity, Watt-hour capacity, Cranking Amps (CA), Cold Cranking Amps (CCA), Reserve Capacity (RC), Internal Resistance, Specific Gravity, and Battery Efficiency. These parameters collectively determine the battery's fitness for a given automotive application.

### 1.2 What are Battery Parameters?
**Battery parameters** are the measurable electrical, chemical, and physical characteristics that quantify a battery's performance, durability, and operational limits. They allow engineers to:
- Select the correct battery for a specific vehicle.
- Predict battery life and reliability.
- Diagnose battery health and State of Charge (SOC).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank" Model
Think of an automotive battery as a **water tank connected to a tap**:
- **Voltage (V)** is like the **water pressure** in the pipes — it pushes current through the electrical circuit.
- **Capacity (Ah)** is the **size of the tank** — how much total charge it can hold.
- **Internal Resistance (R_i)** is the **friction in the pipes** — restricting the smooth flow of current.
- **Specific Gravity (SG)** is a **dipstick reading** of the acid — telling you how "full" the tank is.
- **Cranking Amps (CA/CCA)** is the **maximum tap flow rate** — how much current the battery can deliver in a burst.
- **Reserve Capacity (RC)** is the **time the tap can run at a moderate rate** before the tank empties.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a low water tank (low SOC) with clogged pipes (high R_i) cannot deliver strong flow (high cranking amps), a weak or discharged battery will fail to crank the engine even if its rated capacity looks acceptable on paper.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics
- **Standard Temperature for CCA testing:** **−18 °C (0 °F)**
- **Standard Reference Electrode:** Lead (Pb) anode / Lead dioxide (PbO₂) cathode
- **Electrolyte:** **Sulfuric Acid (H₂SO₄)**, nominal specific gravity **1.265 ± 0.005** at **27 °C**
- **Nominal Cell Voltage (Lead-Acid):** **2.1 V** (6 cells × 2.1 V = 12.6 V for a standard 12 V battery)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Primary Battery Parameters — Logical Breakdown

#### A. Electromotive Force (EMF) and Cell Voltage
- **EMF (E₀)** is the theoretical open-circuit voltage of a cell, governed by the Nernst equation.
- For a lead-acid cell, each cell produces **2.1 V**. A 12 V battery contains **6 cells in series**.
- EMF depends on **electrolyte specific gravity (SG)** and **temperature (T)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Nernst Approximation for Lead-Acid Cell:**
> $$E_0 \approx SG + 0.84 \quad (\text{volts, empirical relation})$$

This is the **industry-standard quick formula** used by KTU examiners and is highly important for numerical problems.

#### B. Ampere-Hour (Ah) Capacity
- Defined as the product of discharge current and the time (in hours) a fully charged battery can deliver it before terminal voltage drops below a cutoff (typically **10.5 V** for a 12 V battery).
- Depends on **discharge rate**, **temperature**, **plate area**, and **active material mass**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Higher discharge current → Lower effective Ah capacity (Peukert Effect, covered in Section 3).

#### C. Cold Cranking Amps (CCA)
- The **number of amps a battery can deliver at −18 °C for 30 seconds** while maintaining a terminal voltage **≥ 7.2 V** (for a 12 V battery).
- Critical for cold-start performance because engine oil viscosity increases at low temperatures, raising cranking load.

#### D. Cranking Amps (CA) / Marine Cranking Amps (MCA)
- Same as CCA but measured at **0 °C (32 °F)** instead of −18 °C.
- Always: **CA > CCA** (typically by ~20–25%).

#### E. Reserve Capacity (RC)
- The **number of minutes a fully charged battery at 27 °C can deliver 25 A** before terminal voltage falls below **10.5 V**.
- Indicates survival time if the alternator fails (e.g., headlight-only driving).

#### F. Internal Resistance (R_i)
- Opposition to current flow **inside** the battery (electrolyte + plate resistance).
- **Increases** as SOC drops and as the battery ages due to sulfation.
- Causes terminal voltage to sag under load: $V_{terminal} = E_0 - I \cdot R_i$

#### G. Specific Gravity (SG)
- Ratio of electrolyte density to water density.
- **Fully charged:** SG = 1.265–1.285
- **Half charged:** SG ≈ 1.190
- **Discharged:** SG ≈ 1.120

> [!NOTE]
> SG is measured using a **hydrometer** (for flooded cells) or inferred from open-circuit voltage in sealed (VRLA/AGM) batteries.

#### H. Battery Efficiency
1. **Ampere-Hour Efficiency (η_Ah):**
   $$\eta_{Ah} = \frac{\text{Ah discharged}}{\text{Ah charged}} \times 100\%$$
   Typical lead-acid: **90–95%**

2. **Watt-Hour Efficiency (η_Wh):**
   $$\eta_{Wh} = \frac{\text{Wh discharged}}{\text{Wh charged}} \times 100\%$$
   Typical lead-acid: **75–85%**
   (Lower than η_Ah because average discharge voltage < average charge voltage.)

3. **Energy Efficiency (η_E):** Same as Watt-Hour Efficiency in KTU context.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Value (12 V Lead-Acid) |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | EMF (empirical) | $E_0 = SG + 0.84$ | V | 2.1 V/cell |
| 2 | Terminal Voltage | $V_t = E_0 - I \cdot R_i$ | V | 12.6 V (open), 10.5 V (cutoff) |
| 3 | Capacity | $C = I \times t$ | Ah | 35–100 Ah (car) |
| 4 | Energy | $E = V \times I \times t$ | Wh | ~1.2 kWh (typical car) |
| 5 | Peukert Capacity | $C_p = I^n \cdot t$ | — | n ≈ 1.1–1.3 (lead-acid) |
| 6 | CCA (standard) | I at −18 °C, 30 s, $V_t \geq 7.2$ V | A | 300–800 A |
| 7 | RC | t at 25 A, 27 °C, $V_t \geq 10.5$ V | min | 60–150 min |
| 8 | η_Ah | $\eta_{Ah} = \frac{Ah_{disch}}{Ah_{ch}} \times 100$ | % | 90–95% |
| 9 | η_Wh | $\eta_{Wh} = \frac{Wh_{disch}}{Wh_{ch}} \times 100$ | % | 75–85% |
| 10 | Specific Gravity Range | $SG = \frac{\rho_{acid}}{\rho_{water}}$ | — | 1.120 to 1.285 |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility
- **CCA** determines cold-start reliability in winter climates (essential in Northern India, Europe, North America).
- **RC** dictates how long a vehicle can run on battery alone (useful for emergency/critical vehicles).
- **Internal Resistance** determines heat dissipation losses — vital for EV/hybrid battery packs where thermal management is a design constraint.
- **Peukert's Law** is critical in sizing battery banks for solar EVs and HEVs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Empirical EMF–Specific Gravity Relationship

**Starting Point:** Nernst Equation for the lead-acid cell reaction:
$$Pb + PbO_2 + 2H_2SO_4 \rightarrow 2PbSO_4 + 2H_2O$$

The general Nernst equation for cell EMF is:
$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 1:** For the lead-acid cell, the number of electrons transferred $n = 2$.

**Step 2:** At standard temperature (25 °C = 298 K), substituting constants $\frac{RT}{F} \approx 0.0257$ V:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{0.0257}{2} \ln Q$$

**Step 3:** The standard reduction potential $E^0$ for the lead-acid cell is **2.04 V** when SG = 1.000 (pure water reference).

**Step 4:** Empirical curve-fitting against experimentally measured cell voltages at various SG values yields the linear approximation used by KTU examiners:

$$E_0 = SG + 0.84 \quad \text{(volts per cell)}$$

**Step 5 — Verification:** For a fully charged cell with SG = 1.265:
$$E_0 = 1.265 + 0.84 = 2.105 \, \text{V} \approx 2.1 \, \text{V} \quad \checkmark$$

For a 12 V battery (6 cells in series):
$$V_{battery} = 6 \times 2.105 = 12.63 \, \text{V} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Terminal Voltage Under Load

**Starting Point:** When current $I$ flows from a battery with internal resistance $R_i$, the terminal voltage $V_t$ is reduced:

**Step 1:** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law to the closed circuit (battery + external load $R_L$):

$$E_0 = I \cdot R_i + I \cdot R_L$$

**Step 2:** Solve for terminal voltage $V_t = I \cdot R_L$:

$$V_t = E_0 - I \cdot R_i$$

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example:**
Given: $E_0 = 12.6$ V, $R_i = 0.01 \, \Omega$, Cranking current $I = 300$ A.

$$V_t = 12.6 - (300)(0.01)$$
$$V_t = 12.6 - 3.0$$
$$V_t = 9.6 \, \text{V}$$

**Interpretation:** During cranking, the terminal voltage sags to 9.6 V — still above the 7.2 V CCA cutoff, so this battery is healthy.

### 3.3 Derivation: Peukert's Law (Capacity vs Discharge Rate)

**Step 1:** Peukert's empirical law states that the usable capacity $C$ depends on discharge rate $I$ as:
$$C_p = I^n \cdot t$$

where $n$ is the **Peukert exponent** (1.1–1.3 for lead-acid).

**Step 2:** Taking logarithms:
$$\log(t) = \log(C_p) - n \cdot \log(I)$$

**Step 3 — Application:** If a 100 Ah battery is rated at the 20-hour rate ($I_1 = 5$ A), find the capacity at a 1-hour rate ($I_2 = 100$ A). Assume $n = 1.2$:

$$\frac{t_1}{t_2} = \left(\frac{I_2}{I_1}\right)^{n-1}$$

**Step 4:** Compute:
$$\frac{t_1}{t_2} = \left(\frac{100}{5}\right)^{1.2-1} = (20)^{0.2}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate $(20)^{0.2}$:
$$20^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \cdot \ln 20} = e^{0.2 \times 3.0} = e^{0.599} \approx 1.82$$

**Step 6:** Since $t_1 = 20$ h (rated), $t_2 = \frac{20}{1.82} \approx 11$ h... *correction* — Peukert's law gives the time at the new rate:

$$t_2 = \frac{C_p}{I_2} = \frac{100 \cdot (5)^{1.2}}{(100)^{1.2}} = \frac{100}{20^{1.2}} = \frac{100}{36.4} \approx 2.75 \, \text{h}$$

So at 100 A discharge, the battery lasts only **~2.75 hours** instead of the 1 hour that a simple 100 Ah / 100 A = 1 h calculation would suggest is *worse*, meaning the **effective capacity is only 275 Ah-hours at the 1-hour rate**... 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Peukert's Key Insight:** Higher discharge currents yield *lower* total deliverable energy — the higher the Peukert exponent $n$, the poorer the high-rate performance.

### 3.4 Derivation: Battery Efficiency Relationships

**Step 1 — Ampere-Hour Efficiency:**
During charge, a fraction of current is lost to **gassing** (electrolysis of water: $2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$).
$$\eta_{Ah} = \frac{\int_0^{t_d} I_d \, dt}{\int_0^{t_c} I_c \, dt} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Watt-Hour Efficiency:**
Average discharge voltage $V_{d,avg}$ < average charge voltage $V_{c,avg}$.
$$\eta_{Wh} = \eta_{Ah} \times \frac{V_{d,avg}}{V_{c,avg}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example:**
A battery receives 120 Ah charge and delivers 110 Ah. Average charge voltage = 14.4 V, average discharge voltage = 12.0 V.

$$\eta_{Ah} = \frac{110}{120} \times 100 = 91.67\%$$

$$\eta_{Wh} = 91.67 \times \frac{12.0}{14.4} = 91.67 \times 0.833 = 76.39\%$$

### 3.5 State of Charge (SOC) Estimation

**Step 1:** SOC (%) using Specific Gravity (empirical lookup):

| SOC (%) | Specific Gravity (SG) |
|---------|----------------------|
| 100 | 1.265 |
| 75  | 1.225 |
| 50  | 1.190 |
| 25  | 1.155 |
| 0   | 1.120 |

**Step 2:** Linear interpolation formula:
$$SOC(\%) = \frac{SG_{measured} - 1.120}{1.265 - 1.120} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Example:** Measured SG = 1.210:
$$SOC = \frac{1.210 - 1.120}{0.145} \times 100 = \frac{0.090}{0.145} \times 100 = 62.07\%$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Battery Parameters and Their Inter-relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BATTERY PERFORMANCE DOMAIN] --> B[ELECTRICAL PARAMETERS]
    A --> C[CHEMICAL PARAMETERS]
    A --> D[THERMAL PARAMETERS]
    A --> E[AGING PARAMETERS]

    B --> B1[EMF E0]
    B --> B2[Terminal Voltage Vt]
    B --> B3[Internal Resistance Ri]
    B --> B4[Capacity Ah]
    B --> B5[Energy Wh]
    B --> B6[CCA CA]

    C --> C1[Specific Gravity SG]
    C --> C2[Electrolyte Concentration]
    C --> C3[Plate Composition]

    D --> D1[Operating Temperature]
    D --> D2[Thermal Runaway Risk]
    D --> D3[Capacity Temperature Coefficient]

    E --> E1[State of Charge SOC]
    E --> E2[Depth of Discharge DOD]
    E --> E3[State of Health SOH]
    E --> E4[Cycle Life]

    B1 --> F1[EMF drives Vt minus IR drop]
    B3 --> F1
    B2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Battery Terminal Behavior]

    C1 --> F2[SG maps to SOC lookup]
    C1 --> F3[SG maps to EMF via E0 = SG plus 0.84]
    F2 --> G2[SOC Estimation Block]
    F3 --> B1

    D1 --> F4[Temperature affects Ri and Capacity]
    F4 --> B3
    F4 --> B4

    E1 --> F5[SOC affects all electrical outputs]
    F5 --> B1
    F5 --> B3
    F5 --> B4

    B6 --> G3[Cold Start Performance]
    E4 --> G4[Battery Lifespan Prediction]

    G1 --> H[VEHICLE ELECTRICAL SYSTEM]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H

    classDef primary fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef secondary fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#b4c7e7,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A primary
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,B6,C1,C2,C3,D1,D2,D3,E1,E2,E3,E4 secondary
    class H output
```

### 4.2 Discharge Characteristic Curve — Terminal Voltage vs Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fully Charged: 12.6 V] -->|Mild load| B[Normal Operation: 12.0 to 12.4 V]
    B -->|Heavy load| C[Sustained Load: 11.5 to 12.0 V]
    C -->|Sustained current| D[Caution Zone: 10.8 to 11.5 V]
    D -->|Critical threshold| E[Cutoff: 10.5 V]
    E --> F[Battery disconnected - prevent deep discharge]

    A -.->|SG = 1.265| A1[HIGH SOC 100 percent]
    B -.->|SG = 1.225| A2[GOOD SOC 75 percent]
    C -.->|SG = 1.190| A3[MEDIUM SOC 50 percent]
    D -.->|SG = 1.155| A4[LOW SOC 25 percent]
    E -.->|SG = 1.120| A5[CRITICAL SOC 0 percent]

    classDef safe fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef warn fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef danger fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    class A,B,A1,A2 safe
    class C,D,A3,A4 warn
    class E,F,A5 danger
```

### 4.3 Battery Parameter Test Procedure Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Battery Test] --> B{Battery Type?}
    B -->|Flooded Lead Acid| C[Hydrometer Test]
    B -->|Sealed VRLA AGM| D[Open Circuit Voltage Test]

    C --> C1[Measure Specific Gravity]
    C1 --> C2{SG within range?}
    C2 -->|No| C3[Cell Replacement Required]
    C2 -->|Yes| C4[Record SOC]

    D --> D1[Measure OCV after 12 hour rest]
    D1 --> D2{OCV greater than 12.4 V?}
    D2 -->|No| D3[Recharge and Retest]
    D2 -->|Yes| D4[Record SOC]

    C4 --> E[Load Test Apply 50 percent CCA for 15 seconds]
    D4 --> E

    E --> F{Vt after 15s greater than 9.6V?}
    F -->|No| G[REPLACE BATTERY]
    F -->|Yes| H[Battery PASSES test]

    G --> I[End - Failed]
    H --> J[End - Serviceable]

    classDef pass fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef fail fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef process fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    class C1,C4,D1,D4,E process
    class H,J pass
    class C3,D3,G,I fail
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]
**Define Specific Gravity of electrolyte. How is it related to the State of Charge of a lead-acid battery?**

**Model Answer:**
Specific gravity (SG) is the ratio of the density of the battery electrolyte (sulfuric acid solution) to the density of pure water at the same temperature.

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{electrolyte}}{\rho_{water}}$$

**Relationship to SOC:** As the battery discharges, sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) is consumed and water is produced, lowering the electrolyte density. Hence SG is directly proportional to SOC:
- Fully charged (100% SOC) → SG ≈ 1.265
- Fully discharged (0% SOC) → SG ≈ 1.120

A hydrometer is used to measure SG and infer SOC.

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]
**Distinguish between Cranking Amps (CA) and Cold Cranking Amps (CCA). State the standard test conditions for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | CA (Cranking Amps) | CCA (Cold Cranking Amps) |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Test Temperature | 0 °C (32 °F) | −18 °C (0 °F) |
| Test Duration | 30 seconds | 30 seconds |
| Minimum Terminal Voltage | 7.2 V (12 V battery) | 7.2 V (12 V battery) |
| Typical Magnitude | Higher (less stringent) | Lower (~75–80% of CA) |
| Application | Marine, mild climates | Automotive, cold climates |

> Both are critical for evaluating cold-start performance, but CCA is the **industry and KTU standard reference** for automotive batteries.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q.A.** **(a)** Explain the following battery parameters with standard test conditions:
**(i)** Cold Cranking Amps (CCA) **(ii)** Reserve Capacity (RC) **(iii)** Ampere-Hour Efficiency **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12 V lead-acid battery has an EMF of 12.6 V and an internal resistance of 0.015 Ω. During engine cranking, the starter draws 280 A. Determine:
**(i)** Terminal voltage during cranking
**(ii)** Power delivered to the starter
**(iii)** Power lost as heat inside the battery
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Parameter Explanations:**

**(i) CCA:** The current a fully charged 12 V battery can deliver at **−18 °C for 30 seconds** while maintaining terminal voltage **≥ 7.2 V**. It indicates cold-start capability.

**(ii) RC:** The number of **minutes** a fully charged 12 V battery at **27 °C** can deliver **25 A** continuously before terminal voltage drops to **10.5 V**. Indicates alternator-failure survival time.

**(iii) Ampere-Hour Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{Ah} = \frac{Ah \, \text{discharged}}{Ah \, \text{charged}} \times 100\%$$
For lead-acid: **90–95%**; loss due to gassing during overcharge.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $E_0 = 12.6$ V, $R_i = 0.015 \, \Omega$, $I = 280$ A.

**(i) Terminal Voltage:**
$$V_t = E_0 - I \cdot R_i$$
$$V_t = 12.6 - (280)(0.015)$$
$$V_t = 12.6 - 4.2 = 8.4 \, \text{V}$$

**(ii) Power Delivered to Starter:**
$$P_{starter} = V_t \times I = 8.4 \times 280 = 2352 \, \text{W} = 2.352 \, \text{kW}$$

**(iii) Power Lost as Heat:**
$$P_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R_i = (280)^2 \times 0.015 = 78400 \times 0.015 = 1176 \, \text{W} = 1.176 \, \text{kW}$$

**Verification (Energy balance):** $P_{total} = E_0 \cdot I = 12.6 \times 280 = 3528$ W; $3528 = 2352 + 1176$ ✓

---

#### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Expected]

**Q.B.** **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the relationship between Specific Gravity (SG) and the State of Charge (SOC) of a lead-acid battery. List the SG values corresponding to 100%, 75%, 50%, 25%, and 0% SOC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A battery is charged at 15 A for 20 hours and then discharged at 25 A for 11 hours. Average charge voltage is 14.0 V and average discharge voltage is 11.8 V. Calculate:
**(i)** Ampere-hour efficiency
**(ii)** Watt-hour efficiency
**(iii)** Energy lost during the complete cycle
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SG-SOC Relationship:**

As the battery discharges, sulfuric acid concentration in the electrolyte reduces (H₂SO₄ is converted to PbSO₄ + H₂O during discharge). The Specific Gravity, being a direct measure of acid concentration, **decreases linearly** with SOC.

| SOC (%) | Specific Gravity |
|---------|------------------|
| 100 | 1.265 |
| 75  | 1.225 |
| 50  | 1.190 |
| 25  | 1.155 |
| 0   | 1.120 |

A linear interpolation formula:
$$SOC(\%) = \frac{SG - 1.120}{0.145} \times 100$$

**Diagram description:** Plot of SG (Y-axis) vs SOC % (X-axis) — a straight line from (0%, 1.120) to (100%, 1.265). Hydrometer reading directly maps to SOC.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Charge current $I_c = 15$ A, Charge time $t_c = 20$ h, $V_{avg,ch} = 14.0$ V
- Discharge current $I_d = 25$ A, Discharge time $t_d = 11$ h, $V_{avg,dis} = 11.8$ V

**(i) Ampere-Hour Efficiency:**
$$Ah_{charged} = I_c \times t_c = 15 \times 20 = 300 \, \text{Ah}$$
$$Ah_{discharged} = I_d \times t_d = 25 \times 11 = 275 \, \text{Ah}$$
$$\eta_{Ah} = \frac{275}{300} \times 100 = 91.67\%$$

**(ii) Watt-Hour Efficiency:**
$$Wh_{charged} = Ah_{charged} \times V_{avg,ch} = 300 \times 14.0 = 4200 \, \text{Wh}$$
$$Wh_{discharged} = Ah_{discharged} \times V_{avg,dis} = 275 \times 11.8 = 3245 \, \text{Wh}$$
$$\eta_{Wh} = \frac{3245}{4200} \times 100 = 77.26\%$$

**(iii) Energy Lost:**
$$E_{lost} = Wh_{charged} - Wh_{discharged} = 4200 - 3245 = 955 \, \text{Wh} = 0.955 \, \text{kWh}$$

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Battery Parameter Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply cell EMF by 6** — Always convert "per cell" to "battery total" (12 V = 6 × 2.1 V). Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up CCA and RC units** — CCA is in **Amperes (A)**, RC is in **minutes (min)**. Writing "CCA = 90 minutes" loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Not stating test conditions** — When defining CCA, always mention **−18 °C, 30 s, V_t ≥ 7.2 V**. Omitting any one costs marks.
> 4. **Watt-hour efficiency without voltage ratio** — Many students compute η_Ah and stop. KTU requires the additional step involving $V_{dis}/V_{ch}$.
> 5. **Peukert's law sign errors** — Remember: $C_p = I^n \cdot t$, and as $I$ ↑, effective capacity ↓.
> 6. **Specific Gravity must include units** — SG is dimensionless, but stating "1.265 g/cc" shows conceptual weakness. Loss: 0.5 mark.
> 7. **Skipping the final verification step** — In numerical problems, validating the answer with energy balance (P_total = P_load + P_loss) earns **bonus awareness marks** in borderline cases.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Battery Parameters**

- ✅ **EMF per cell (lead-acid) = 2.1 V**; 12 V battery = 6 cells in series.
- ✅ **Empirical EMF formula:** $E_0 = SG + 0.84$ (volts/cell) — KTU favorite.
- ✅ **CCA test:** −18 °C, 30 s, $V_t \geq 7.2$ V (for 12 V).
- ✅ **RC test:** 27 °C, 25 A, until $V_t = 10.5$ V; result in **minutes**.
- ✅ **Terminal voltage under load:** $V_t = E_0 - I \cdot R_i$.
- ✅ **Ampere-Hour Efficiency (η_Ah):** **90–95%** for lead-acid.
- ✅ **Watt-Hour Efficiency (η_Wh):** **75–85%** for lead-acid. Always **η_Wh < η_Ah**.
- ✅ **Specific Gravity range:** 1.120 (empty) to 1.265 (full).
- ✅ **Internal resistance increases** with age, sulfation, and low SOC.
- ✅ **Peukert's Law:** Higher discharge rate → Lower effective capacity; exponent $n \approx 1.1–1.3$ for lead-acid.
- ✅ **Energy formula:** $E = V \times I \times t = V \times Ah$ (in Wh).
- ✅ **Power loss in battery:** $P_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R_i$.
- ✅ **Depth of Discharge (DOD) + SOC = 100%**.
- ✅ **SOC estimation:** Hydrometer (flooded) or OCV (sealed VRLA).
- ✅ **Cutoff voltage** for 12 V lead-acid: **10.5 V** (1.75 V/cell).

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** Questions on battery parameters are **formula-driven and numerical-friendly** — perfect scoring territory if the standard test conditions and units are memorized precisely. Always include units in your final answers and **state the standard test conditions** explicitly when defining CCA, CA, or RC.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Super Capacitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT413 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Super Capacitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Super Capacitor** (also known as an **Ultracapacitor** or **Electric Double-Layer Capacitor — EDLC**) is an electrochemical energy storage device that stores energy through electrostatic charge separation at the interface between an electrode and an electrolyte. Unlike conventional batteries that rely on faradaic (redox) chemical reactions, super capacitors store energy predominantly through a non-faradaic electrochemical double-layer capacitance mechanism, allowing them to deliver and absorb very high power densities over hundreds of thousands of charge–discharge cycles.

In the **automotive context**, super capacitors serve as **auxiliary energy storage systems** that complement batteries in hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs), plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVs), battery electric vehicles (BEVs), and 12V/48V start-stop micro-hybrid systems. They are particularly suited for **regenerative braking** and **transient peak power delivery**, where rapid charge acceptance and high current discharge are required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Super capacitors are categorized under *Alternative / Auxiliary Energy Storage Devices* for electric and hybrid vehicles. They are distinct from conventional capacitors (energy stored as electric field) and batteries (energy stored as chemical potential). The defining feature is the **Helmholtz Double Layer** at the electrode–electrolyte interface.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **highway with thousands of toll booths (electrode surface)**, where cars (ions) queue up neatly in two parallel lanes — one lane of positive ions along the negative electrode, and one lane of negative ions along the positive electrode. The "toll booths" are made of **activated carbon with pores so tiny that the surface area, if unfolded, would cover several football fields inside a sugar-cube-sized chunk**.

- **Conventional Capacitor** = a single lane of cars parked in an open lot. Small surface area → small capacity.
- **Battery** = cars driving into a chemical factory, getting dismantled, and rebuilt (slow but energy-dense).
- **Super Capacitor** = a massive multi-lane parking structure at a stadium where millions of ions line up physically (no chemical reaction) — extremely fast in/out, but the "fuel tank" is shallower than a battery.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Think of a super capacitor as a *fast-filling, fast-emptying, shallow water tank* — perfect for capturing short bursts of braking energy or providing a sudden boost during acceleration.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
- **Relative permittivity of electrolyte double layer:** $\varepsilon_r \approx 10$ to $30$ (aqueous/symmetric)
- **Effective double-layer separation distance:** $d \approx 0.3 \text{ nm}$ to $1 \text{ nm}$ (size of solvated ion)
- **Activated carbon specific surface area:** **$1000$ to $3000 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$**
- **Commercial super capacitor specific energy:** **$5$ to $10 \text{ Wh/kg}$**
- **Commercial super capacitor specific power:** **$1000$ to $10000 \text{ W/kg}$**
- **Cycle life:** **$> 500{,}000$ cycles** (vs. $\sim 1000$ for Li-ion)
- **Operating voltage per cell:** **$2.5 \text{ V}$ to $2.7 \text{ V}$** (symmetric carbon/carbon EDLC)
- **Internal resistance (ESR):** **$0.1$ to $10 \text{ m}\Omega$** for large automotive modules

### 1.4 Classification of Super Capacitors

| Type | Charge Storage Mechanism | Electrode Material | Energy Density |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EDLC** (Electric Double-Layer Capacitor) | Pure electrostatic (non-faradaic) | Activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene | High power, moderate energy |
| **Pseudo-capacitor** | Fast reversible redox (faradaic) at/near surface | Metal oxides ($\text{RuO}_2$, $\text{MnO}_2$), conducting polymers (PANI, PPy) | Higher energy, lower cycle life |
| **Hybrid Super Capacitor** | Combination of double-layer + faradaic | Li-ion capacitor (LIC): activated carbon + pre-lithiated graphite | Bridging battery and capacitor |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanism — The Helmholtz Electrical Double Layer

The foundational theory behind EDLCs is the **Helmholtz Double Layer Model (1853)**, later refined by **Gouy-Chapman** and **Stern**. When a solid electrode is immersed in an electrolyte and a potential is applied:

1. **Charge Separation at Interface:** A monolayer of oppositely charged ions from the electrolyte migrates to the electrode surface, forming a layer that balances the electronic charge on the electrode. This creates a **Helmholtz layer** with effective thickness equal to the ionic radius ($\sim 0.3$–$1$ nm).

2. **Formation of Two Capacitors in Series:** Two distinct double layers form — one at the positive electrode and one at the negative electrode — each behaving as an individual capacitor. The cell capacitance is therefore a **series combination** of the two.

3. **Pore-Driven Surface Area Amplification:** Activated carbon has micropores ($< 2$ nm) and mesopores ($2$–$50$ nm). The ions access these pores, increasing the effective interfacial area to **$1000$–$3000 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$** — the primary reason super capacitors have orders of magnitude higher capacitance than conventional capacitors.

4. **No Faradaic Reaction:** Because there is no electron transfer across the electrode–electrolyte interface (unlike a battery), no chemical bond breaking/forming occurs. This is why super capacitors exhibit **$> 500{,}000$ cycles** without degradation.

5. **Electrolyte Window Defines Voltage:** The maximum voltage of a symmetric carbon EDLC is limited by the **decomposition voltage of the electrolyte** (typically $\sim 2.7$ V for organic electrolytes like $\text{TEA-BF}_4$ in acetonitrile; $\sim 0.9$ V for aqueous).

### 2.2 Why and How — Engineering Logic Behind Each Step

- **Why so small a distance $d$?** Capacitance $C = \varepsilon A / d$. Halving $d$ doubles $C$. The double-layer thickness is at the atomic scale — unattainable in conventional parallel-plate capacitors.

- **Why is surface area $A$ so huge?** Activated carbon processing (KOH activation, steam activation) creates a sponge-like network of pores, multiplying interfacial contact between solid and electrolyte.

- **Why is energy lower than a battery?** Energy $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$. Even with enormous $C$, $V$ is limited to $2.5$–$2.7$ V per cell (vs. $3.6$–$4.2$ V for Li-ion), so $V^2$ caps energy density.

- **Why such high power?** Power $P = V^2 / (4 \cdot \text{ESR})$. The extremely low ESR of activated-carbon structures and ionic electrolytes allows currents of hundreds of amperes from a small module.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below are **directly examinable** in the KTU ESE for PEMRT413 — Module 4.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d}$ | Capacitance of parallel plate / double layer | F (Farads) | Theoretical capacitance per electrode |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{C_{\text{cell}}} = \dfrac{1}{C_1} + \dfrac{1}{C_2}$ | Series combination of two EDLC electrodes | F | Total cell capacitance |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Stored energy in a capacitor | J (Joules) | Total energy stored |
| 4 | $E_{\text{spec}} = \dfrac{E}{m} = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{C V^2}{m}$ | Specific (gravimetric) energy | Wh/kg | Compare to battery |
| 5 | $P_{\max} = \dfrac{V^2}{4 \cdot R_{\text{ESR}}}$ | Maximum deliverable power | W (Watts) | Peak power rating |
| 6 | $P_{\text{spec}} = \dfrac{P_{\max}}{m}$ | Specific power | W/kg | Power-to-weight metric |
| 7 | $\tau = R_{\text{ESR}} \cdot C$ | RC time constant | s | Pulse response time |
| 8 | $E = \dfrac{Q \cdot V}{2} = \dfrac{C V^2}{2}$ | Energy from charge $Q$ at voltage $V$ | J | Energy during discharge |
| 9 | $Q = \int I \, dt = C \cdot V$ | Charge stored | C (Coulombs) | Charge capacity |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{coulomb}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | Coulombic efficiency | % | Round-trip efficiency metric |
| 11 | $\text{Ragone: } P = k \cdot E^{-n}$ | Power-Energy trade-off curve | — | Ragone plot coordinates |
| 12 | $V_{\text{module}} = N_s \cdot V_{\text{cell}}$ | Module voltage (series) | V | Pack sizing for EV |
| 13 | $C_{\text{module}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{cell}}}{N_s}$ | Module capacitance (series) | F | Pack capacitance |
| 14 | $E_{\text{module}} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{\text{module}} V_{\text{module}}^2$ | Module energy | J | Pack stored energy |

### 2.4 Real-World Automotive Utility

- **Regenerative Braking:** Captures $100$–$300$ kW of peak braking energy that batteries cannot absorb quickly — the ultracapacitor stores it in seconds.
- **Start-Stop Micro-Hybrids:** Provides cranking current to restart the ICE instantly when the vehicle stops at a traffic signal.
- **Acceleration Boost:** Discharges a high-current pulse to the traction motor during hard acceleration, sparing the battery from high-C-rate stress.
- **Cold-Start Assist:** Operates reliably at $-40 \degree \text{C}$ where Li-ion batteries lose $>50\%$ capacity.
- **Voltage Stabilization:** Buffers the 12V/48V bus in mild-hybrid architectures.
- **Backup Power for EV ECU and ADAS:** Ensures safety-critical systems retain power if the main battery fails.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Why Two Double-Layers Behave as Series Capacitors

In an EDLC cell, there are **two electrodes**, each forming a Helmholtz double layer with the electrolyte. Let:
- $C_a$ = capacitance of the double layer at the anode (positive electrode)
- $C_c$ = capacitance of the double layer at the cathode (negative electrode)

**Step 1 — Apply the definition of capacitance** to each electrode–electrolyte interface:

$$
q = C_a \cdot V_a = C_c \cdot V_c
$$

where $V_a$ and $V_c$ are the potential drops across each double layer, and $q$ is the magnitude of charge stored on each (charges are equal and opposite on the two electrodes — a fundamental property of a capacitor).

**Step 2 — Total cell voltage** is the sum of the two interfacial potential drops:

$$
V_{\text{cell}} = V_a + V_c
$$

**Step 3 — Express the total stored charge** in terms of the cell-level equivalent capacitance $C_{\text{cell}}$:

$$
q = C_{\text{cell}} \cdot V_{\text{cell}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute** $V_a = q/C_a$ and $V_c = q/C_c$:

$$
V_{\text{cell}} = \frac{q}{C_a} + \frac{q}{C_c} = q \left( \frac{1}{C_a} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Equate with the series-capacitance definition** $C_{\text{cell}} = q / V_{\text{cell}}$:

$$
\frac{1}{C_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{1}{C_a} + \frac{1}{C_c}
$$

**Conclusion (Engineering Insight):** The cell capacitance is always **less than the smaller of $C_a$ or $C_c$**. This is why manufacturers use **symmetric carbon electrodes** — to ensure $C_a \approx C_c$ and avoid wasting the smaller side. It also means the EDLC behaves electrically as two capacitors in series, justifying the **per-cell voltage limit of $2.7$ V** (electrolyte decomposition constraint).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Stored in a Super Capacitor (with Numerical Illustration)

**Step 1 — Recall the general energy integral** for a capacitor charged from $0$ to $V$:

$$
E = \int_0^Q V(q) \, dq
$$

**Step 2 — For a linear capacitor** where $V = q/C$:

$$
E = \int_0^Q \frac{q}{C} \, dq = \frac{1}{C} \cdot \frac{Q^2}{2} = \frac{Q^2}{2C}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $Q = CV$** to obtain the standard form:

$$
E = \frac{(CV)^2}{2C} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Watt-hours** (since 1 Wh = 3600 J):

$$
E \,[\text{Wh}] = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{C \,[\text{F}] \cdot V^2 \,[\text{V}^2]}{3600}
$$

**Numerical Illustration (Sample Problem for KTU Practice):**
> *A super capacitor module has $C = 3000$ F rated at $V = 2.7$ V per cell. The pack has 12 cells in series to form a $32.4$ V module. Compute the total stored energy.*

**Step 4a — Cell capacitance** (series reduces capacitance):

$$
C_{\text{module}} = \frac{3000 \, \text{F}}{12} = 250 \, \text{F}
$$

**Step 4b — Module voltage** (series adds voltage):

$$
V_{\text{module}} = 12 \times 2.7 \, \text{V} = 32.4 \, \text{V}
$$

**Step 4c — Energy in Joules:**

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} \times 250 \times (32.4)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 250 \times 1049.76 = 131220 \, \text{J}
$$

**Step 4d — Convert to Watt-hours:**

$$
E = \frac{131220}{3600} \approx 36.45 \, \text{Wh}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A 36.45 Wh super capacitor module weighs about 5–8 kg — a specific energy of $\sim 5$–$7$ Wh/kg, which is about **$1/30^{th}$ of a Li-ion battery** but offers **$10\times$ the specific power**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Power Transfer Theorem Applied to EDLC

**Step 1 — Model the EDLC** as an ideal capacitor $C$ in series with its Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR) $R_{\text{ESR}}$, driving an external load $R_L$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Maximum Power Transfer Theorem:** Maximum power is delivered to $R_L$ when $R_L = R_{\text{ESR}}$.

**Step 3 — At the matched condition**, the voltage divides equally, so $V_L = V/2$ across the load.

**Step 4 — Power into the load:**

$$
P_{\max} = \frac{V_L^2}{R_L} = \frac{(V/2)^2}{R_{\text{ESR}}} = \frac{V^2}{4 R_{\text{ESR}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values** for our 12-cell module (each cell: $V = 2.7$ V, $R_{\text{ESR,cell}} = 0.5$ m$\Omega$):

$$
R_{\text{ESR,module}} = 12 \times 0.5 \, \text{m}\Omega = 6 \, \text{m}\Omega
$$

$$
P_{\max} = \frac{(32.4)^2}{4 \times 0.006} = \frac{1049.76}{0.024} = 43740 \, \text{W} \approx 43.7 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Even a small ultracapacitor pack can deliver $\sim 44$ kW of peak power — sufficient to assist the traction motor of a mild-hybrid vehicle during acceleration bursts.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Super Capacitor vs. Battery vs. Conventional Capacitor

| Parameter | Conventional Capacitor | Super Capacitor (EDLC) | Li-ion Battery |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Storage mechanism** | Electrostatic (dielectric) | Electrostatic (double layer) | Chemical (faradaic) |
| **Specific energy (Wh/kg)** | $0.01$ – $0.1$ | $5$ – $10$ | $150$ – $250$ |
| **Specific power (W/kg)** | $> 10^5$ | $1000$ – $10000$ | $250$ – $1000$ |
| **Cycle life** | $> 10^6$ | $> 500{,}000$ | $500$ – $2000$ |
| **Charge time** | $\mu$s – ms | $1$ – $10$ s | $30$ min – few hr |
| **Cell voltage** | Up to $\sim 1000$ V | $2.5$ – $2.7$ V | $3.6$ – $3.7$ V |
| **Operating temperature** | Wide | $-40 \degree \text{C}$ to $65 \degree \text{C}$ | $0 \degree \text{C}$ to $45 \degree \text{C}$ |
| **Self-discharge** | Low | Moderate (days–weeks) | Low (months) |
| **Cost (per kWh)** | Low | High | Moderate |
| **Best use in EV** | Filtering, snubbing | Regenerative braking, peak power | Sustained energy supply |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Helmholtz Double-Layer Formation in a Single Pore

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["POSITIVE ELECTRODE"]:::anode
    node2["ACTIVATED CARBON SURFACE"]:::carbon
    node3["NEGATIVE ION LAYER (ANION)"]:::anion
    node4["BULK ELECTROLYTE"]:::electrolyte
    node5["POSITIVE ION LAYER (CATION)"]:::cation
    node6["ACTIVATED CARBON SURFACE 2"]:::carbon2
    node7["NEGATIVE ELECTRODE"]:::cathode
    node8["DOUBLE LAYER d approx 0.3 to 1 nm"]:::gap

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node3 -.-> node8
    node5 -.-> node8

    classDef anode fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef carbon fill:#34495e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef anion fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef electrolyte fill:#f1c40f,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef cation fill:#3498db,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef carbon2 fill:#34495e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef cathode fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef gap fill:#ecf0f1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Super Capacitor Module in a HEV

```mermaid
graph TB
    A1["BRAKING EVENT / ACCELERATOR PEDAL"]:::input
    A2["ENERGY MANAGEMENT ECU"]:::ecu
    A3["BIDIRECTIONAL DC DC CONVERTER"]:::dcdc
    A4["SUPER CAPACITOR MODULE 32 to 100 V"]:::supcap
    A5["HIGH VOLTAGE LITHIUM BATTERY"]:::batt
    A6["TRACTION MOTOR / STARTER"]:::motor
    A7["12V / 48V VEHICLE BUS"]:::bus
    A8["TELEMETRY STATE OF CHARGE MONITOR"]:::tele

    A1 --> A2
    A2 -->|PEAK POWER DEMAND| A3
    A2 -->|SUSTAINED ENERGY| A5
    A3 --> A4
    A3 --> A5
    A4 --> A6
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7
    A4 --> A8
    A5 --> A8
    A8 -->|FEEDBACK| A2

    classDef input fill:#16a085,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef ecu fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef dcdc fill:#d35400,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef supcap fill:#c0392b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef batt fill:#27ae60,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef motor fill:#2980b9,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef bus fill:#7f8c8d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef tele fill:#f39c12,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Charge-Discharge Cycle Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    B1["VEHICLE STATE"]:::state
    B2["BRAKING DETECTED"]:::brake
    B3["REGEN ON: DC DC ROUTES ENERGY TO SUPERCAP"]:::route1
    B4["SUPERCAP STATE OF CHARGE 100 PERCENT"]:::full
    B5["EXCESS ENERGY DIVERTED TO LITHIUM BATTERY"]:::excess
    B6["ACCELERATION DEMAND"]:::accel
    B7["SUPERCAP DISCHARGES FIRST"]:::discharge
    B8["BATTERY SUPPLEMENTS REMAINING DEMAND"]:::battassist
    B9["IDLE / CONSTANT SPEED"]:::idle
    B10["SUPERCAP AT FLOATING SOC BATTERY POWERS BUS"]:::float

    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B6
    B1 --> B9
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 -->|YES| B5
    B4 -->|NO| B3
    B6 --> B7
    B7 --> B8
    B9 --> B10

    classDef state fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef brake fill:#c0392b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef route1 fill:#e67e22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef full fill:#f1c40f,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef excess fill:#27ae60,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef accel fill:#2980b9,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef discharge fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```
